challenge_prompt:
A 19-year-old Caucasian male student presented with a marked cognitive decline within 6 months, anhedonia, ambivalence, social withdrawal, poverty of speech, and brief limited intermittent psychotic symptoms (BLIPS), including delusions and hallucinations. After an initial psychological assessment, further examinations to clarify the initial diagnosis were recommended. Two months later, he presented for more detailed examinations. Clinical and neurological examination, brain MRI, EEG, and extensive blood and CSF testing were all within normal limits. He had an uncle with bipolar disorder but no other family history of psychiatric or neurological diseases. He had no psychiatric history, was antipsychotic-naïve, and had not received psychological treatment. He self-reported cannabis use four times in his lifetime at age 15 in minor to moderate doses; urine drug screening was negative.

Based on the information provided, what is the most likely diagnosis or clinical stage? What would be the most appropriate next diagnostic or therapeutic step?
gold_diagnosis: Clinical High Risk (CHR) mental state for psychosis (prodromal psychosis)

answer_key.diagnosis: Clinical High Risk (CHR) mental state for psychosis (prodromal psychosis)
answer_key.etiology: Unknown; likely multifactorial with genetic predisposition (family history of bipolar disorder) and possible environmental factors.
answer_key.next_management_step: Confirm the diagnosis with a structured clinical assessment for CHR (e.g., SIPS/SPI-A) and provide evidence-based psychosocial interventions such as cognitive behavioral therapy and family psychoeducation. Close monitoring for conversion to psychosis is essential. Off-label antipsychotic medication may be considered if symptoms are severe or persistent.
required_findings: 19-year-old male
Marked cognitive decline within 6 months
Anhedonia, ambivalence, social withdrawal, poverty of speech
Brief limited intermittent psychotic symptoms (delusions and hallucinations)
Normal neurological examination, brain MRI, EEG, blood, and CSF investigations
Negative urine drug screening
No prior psychiatric history, antipsychotic-naïve
Family history of bipolar disorder in an uncle
optional_findings: Self-reported limited cannabis use in the past, remote and low dose
Family history limited to bipolar disorder in uncle

provenance.title: Cannabidiol enhances cerebral glucose utilization and ameliorates psychopathology and cognition: A case report in a clinically high-risk mental state

challenge_prompt:
A 29-year-old right-handed man suffered an electrical injury after coming into contact with a 220 V wire in a staff dormitory. He was found unconscious and incontinent of urine. In the emergency department he had partial exfoliation of the epidermis of both great toes and subcutaneous congestion around the left orbit and left shoulder. He was intubated and admitted to the burn unit with a gastric tube and urinary catheter.

Initial neurological examination: coma, bilateral pupils were equal, round, and 8 mm in diameter with no light reflex, and Babinski sign was positive bilaterally.

Cervical spine MRI: normal spinal cord size, shape, and signal; cervical disc herniation compressing the dural sac; decreased flow void signal in the left vertebral artery; right vertebral artery was thin.

MRA: localized stenosis of the P1 segment of both posterior cerebral arteries; poor visualization of the C5 segment of both internal carotid arteries; basilar artery ring normal in size and shape.

MRI of the head: abnormal signals in the midbrain and bilateral thalamus; soft tissue swelling in the bilateral frontal, left parietal temporal, and left orbit areas.

Two days after admission a tracheotomy was performed because the patient could not spontaneously expectorate. Over the next 10 days his consciousness improved from coma to drowsiness and he could follow simple commands. Repeat MRI of the head 10 days later again showed abnormal signals in the bilateral thalamic region and midbrain. Repeat MRA of the head showed: localized stenosis in the clinoid segment of both internal carotid arteries; slender right vertebral artery; localized stenosis in the A2 segment of both anterior cerebral arteries.

Based only on the information above, what is the most likely diagnosis and etiology, and what is the most appropriate next step in management to address the suspected underlying pathophysiology?
gold_diagnosis: Thalamencephalic and mesencephalic injury (top of the basilar syndrome) secondary to electrical trauma

answer_key.diagnosis: Thalamencephalic and mesencephalic injury (top of the basilar syndrome) secondary to electrical trauma
aliases: Ischemic stroke involving thalamus and midbrain
Cerebral infarction due to vasospasm after electrical injury
answer_key.etiology: Vasospasm‐induced ischemia caused by low‐voltage electrical injury
answer_key.next_management_step: Transcranial Doppler (TCD) to evaluate for vasospasm; if confirmed, consider intra‑arterial nimodipine and supportive neurological care
required_findings: 29-year-old man with 220 V electrical injury
Coma on admission
Bilateral pupils 8 mm and unreactive to light
Positive Babinski sign bilaterally
MRI head: abnormal signals in midbrain and bilateral thalamus
MRA: localized stenosis of P1 segment of bilateral posterior cerebral arteries
MRA: slender right vertebral artery and decreased flow void in left vertebral artery
Repeat MRI confirms persistent abnormal signal in thalamus and midbrain, with additional stenoses on MRA
optional_findings: Partial epidermis exfoliation of both great toes
Subcutaneous congestion around left orbit and shoulder
Cervical MRI: cord normal, disc herniation compressing thecal sac
Initial consciousness improved from coma to drowsiness within 10 days
Soft tissue swelling on head MRI
Poor visualization of C5 segment of bilateral internal carotid arteries on initial MRA
Localized stenosis in clinoid segment of ICA and A2 of ACA on repeat MRA

provenance.title: Acupuncture for the treatment of thalamencephalic and mesencephalic injury secondary to electrical trauma: A case report

challenge_prompt:
A right-handed 7-year-old boy presented with signs and symptoms of increased intracranial pressure for several months, and gait disturbance and dysmetria for 1 month. Clinical examination revealed a cerebellar syndrome with multidirectional nystagmus and difficulties in walking with enlargement of the sustentation polygon. No behavioral or speech impairments were noted. Brain MRI showed a large posterior fossa mass invading the vermis, the lower part of the floor of the fourth ventricle, left cerebellar peduncles, and causing obstructive hydrocephalus. After endoscopic third ventriculostomy to relieve hydrocephalus, the patient underwent total resection of the mass via a telovelar approach. Histopathological examination revealed medulloblastoma. The patient's clinical status was assessed independently. One day after surgery, new symptoms emerged: severe loss of spontaneous speech, loss of motivation, mood changes, irritability, and a cerebellar syndrome (hypotonia, dysmetria, and left ataxia). The patient showed no spontaneous speech but could respond to yes/no questions if strongly stimulated. Language comprehension appeared preserved. Nine days postoperatively, the symptoms had not improved.

Based on the postoperative presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?
gold_diagnosis: Postoperative cerebellar mutism syndrome (pCMS) / posterior fossa syndrome

answer_key.diagnosis: Postoperative cerebellar mutism syndrome (pCMS) / posterior fossa syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Postoperative complication of medulloblastoma resection, likely due to disruption of dentato-thalamo-cortical pathways
answer_key.next_management_step: Administration of zolpidem (a short-acting non-benzodiazepine hypnotic/GABA-A agonist) to promote paradoxical recovery of speech and behavior
required_findings: severe loss of spontaneous speech
loss of motivation
mood changes
irritability
cerebellar syndrome (hypotonia, dysmetria, and left ataxia)
preserved language comprehension
onset approximately 1 day after posterior fossa medulloblastoma resection
no spontaneous speech but responds to yes/no questions if strongly stimulated
optional_findings: signs and symptoms of increased intracranial pressure for several months
gait disturbance and dysmetria for 1 month
cerebellar syndrome with multidirectional nystagmus and difficulties in walking with enlargement of sustentation polygon
MRI large posterior fossa mass invading vermis, floor of fourth ventricle, left cerebellar peduncles
endoscopic third ventriculostomy performed before tumor resection

provenance.title: Case Report: Zolpidem’s paradoxical restorative action: A case report of functional brain imaging

challenge_prompt:
We present the case of an 80-year-old man referred for brain perfusion SPECT imaging for cognitive disorders. A dynamic SPECT acquisition (temporal sampling of 15 frames × 3 s, 10 frames × 15 s, 14 frames × 30 s) was performed utilizing a 360°CZT camera (VERITON 200 series, Spectrum Dynamics®) immediately after the injection of 615 MBq of 99mTc-hexamethylpropyleneamineoxime (HMPAO), followed by a conventional static acquisition of 15 min. CZT-SPECT acquisitions were performed with no zoom and a radius of gyration of 11.5 cm. Dynamic and static images were reconstructed with an ordered-subset expectation maximization (OSEM)-3D algorithm (4 iterations with 8 subsets and 28 iterations with 8 subsets, respectively), with a temporal kernel filter for dynamic images. All dynamic and static images were corrected for attenuation and scatters, and displayed with voxels of 4.92 × 4.92 × 4.92 mm and 2.46 × 2.46 × 2.46 mm, respectively.

All SPECT images from 30 s to 10 min post-injection were spatially normalized to the MNI space using a dedicated template. Seed based correlation analyses were performed to identify within the set of SPECT images the well-known resting-state networks: default mode, executive, salience, sensory-motor and visual. The coordinates of one voxel-seed for each network have been previously reported. A statistical level of significance of p < 0.001 uncorrected, corrected for the cluster volume was applied. Results are expressed according to the Automated Anatomical Labelling (AAL) atlas. Figure 1 depicts the results of voxel-wise analyses obtained for each network and Table 1, details the identification of brain regions associated to each network.

The visual and semi-quantitative analyses of conventional static images according to the current guidelines did not reveal any argument for a neurodegenerative condition for the cognitive disorders in this patient.

Based only on the presentation above, what is the most likely interpretation of these imaging findings, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Normal brain perfusion SPECT with identification of resting-state networks via dynamic fSPECT imaging; no evidence of neurodegenerative hypoperfusion.

answer_key.diagnosis: Normal brain perfusion SPECT with identification of resting-state networks via dynamic fSPECT imaging; no evidence of neurodegenerative hypoperfusion.
aliases: Feasibility of fSPECT for resting-state network identification
answer_key.etiology: Cognitive disorder of uncertain etiology; perfusion SPECT did not show a pattern typical of Alzheimer's disease or other neurodegenerative conditions.
answer_key.next_management_step: Proceed with comprehensive neuropsychological evaluation, laboratory tests for reversible causes of cognitive impairment, and consider FDG-PET or MRI if clinical suspicion for neurodegeneration persists.
required_findings: Seed based correlation analyses were performed to identify default mode, executive, salience, sensory-motor, and visual networks.
Figure 1 and Table 1 demonstrate the identified resting-state networks.
The visual and semi-quantitative analyses of conventional static images did not reveal any argument for a neurodegenerative condition.
Dynamic SPECT acquisition with temporal sampling 15x3s, 10x15s, 14x30s using 360°CZT camera.
Injection of 615 MBq 99mTc-HMPAO radiotracer.
Statistical threshold p<0.001 uncorrected, corrected for cluster volume.
Patient is an 80-year-old man referred for cognitive disorders.
Images spatially normalized to MNI space and expressed via AAL atlas.
optional_findings: CZT-SPECT acquisitions with no zoom and radius of gyration 11.5 cm.
Images reconstructed with OSEM-3D (4 iterations, 8 subsets dynamic; 28 iterations, 8 subsets static).
Temporal kernel filter applied to dynamic images.
Voxel sizes: 4.92x4.92x4.92 mm dynamic, 2.46x2.46x2.46 mm static.
Co-registration of frames to correct movement artefacts before normalization.
Proportional scaling and 12 mm FWHM Gaussian post-filter applied.
Template created from averaged late dynamic frames.

provenance.title: Identification of resting-state networks using dynamic brain perfusion SPECT imaging: A fSPECT case report

challenge_prompt:
Clinical Presentation:
A 79-year-old woman with advanced Parkinson's disease (PD) and BMI 32.3 underwent elective replacement of her deep brain stimulator (DBS) implantable pulse generator (IPG) one month ago. She originally had bilateral globus pallidus interna (GPi) DBS with a non-rechargeable IPG placed in the abdomen for cosmetic reasons. During replacement, a rechargeable neurostimulator was implanted in a new, more superficial pocket to facilitate charging. Intraoperatively, the device showed normal impedances and full wireless pairing with the external charger. The IPG was anchored with 2-0 silk at two sites to the underlying tissue. The patient had no history of bleeding diathesis and was not on antiplatelet or anticoagulant medications. She had no significant psychiatric comorbidities, including obsessive-compulsive disorder, and no dementia on prior screening. She had not experienced any recent weight loss.

One month after replacement, she reported being unable to communicate with or charge her device for one week. In clinic, the device could not be interrogated and programming was impossible. Physical examination revealed no abnormal findings. Plain film X-rays of the abdomen showed the IPG flipped 180° along its long axis. Manual palpation of the pulse generator site could not induce any flipping. Upon further questioning, the patient recalled swelling at the surgical site appearing a few days after the replacement procedure, but she denied any significant bruising, trauma, or manipulation of the device. She had not sought care for the swelling at that time.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next appropriate management step?
gold_diagnosis: Pocket hematoma causing IPG flipping (Twiddler-like phenomenon)

answer_key.diagnosis: Pocket hematoma causing IPG flipping (Twiddler-like phenomenon)
answer_key.etiology: Postoperative pocket hematoma resulting in device flipping and failure.
answer_key.next_management_step: Surgical exploration, evacuation of hematoma, pocket revision, and re-anchoring of IPG.
required_findings: History of recent IPG replacement surgery one month prior
Patient reports swelling at surgical site a few days after surgery
Presenting complaint of inability to communicate with or charge the device for one week
Device cannot be interrogated or programmed in clinic
Plain film X-ray reveals IPG flipped 180° along its long axis
Unable to manually flip device on palpation of the pulse generator site
No history of device manipulation, picking, or twiddling by patient
optional_findings: Abdominal IPG placement (as opposed to subclavicular)
No bleeding diathesis or anticoagulant use
Absence of significant psychiatric comorbidities such as OCD
Normal intraoperative device impedances and wireless pairing during replacement surgery

provenance.title: Hematoma-induced Twiddler-like phenomenon as a presentation of DBS hardware failure: Case report

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely etiology of the patient's ongoing seizures, and what next management step would be most appropriate?

A 12-year-old girl was referred for attention and school difficulties, poor peer relationships, irritability, and poor adherence to a classic ketogenic diet (CKD). She was diagnosed at age 11 with a rare genetic epilepsy syndrome for which CKD is the gold standard treatment. She has mild intellectual disability with generalized academic and attentional problems. Despite treatment with valproic acid, ethosuximide, clobazam, and topiramate, she continues to have poorly controlled absence seizures, and EEG shows multiple generalized epileptiform discharges. The family has low socio-economic status. Since initiation of CKD, adherence has been poor, with very low blood ketone bodies (<1 mmol/L vs therapeutic 2–5 mmol/L). A 7-day food diary and semi-structured psychological interviews reveal that the family has inadequate knowledge about CKD: they often rely on weight-loss websites for recipes, incorrectly choose ‘light’ or ‘zero added sugar’ foods that may contain carbohydrates, and the father does not know the rationale for the diet. The patient acknowledges that when teased by peers about her appearance and different dietary habits, she secretly eats forbidden foods. She shows limited understanding of why she must follow the diet.
gold_diagnosis: Poor adherence to classic ketogenic diet

answer_key.diagnosis: Poor adherence to classic ketogenic diet
answer_key.etiology: Psychosocial stressors (peer teasing, emotional distress), low health literacy, and inadequate family knowledge about the diet leading to intentional and unintentional non-adherence
answer_key.next_management_step: A structured multidisciplinary psychoeducation program (e.g., KETOLAND) combining patient and family education, cognitive-behavioral strategies, and therapeutic storytelling to improve dietary knowledge and adherence.
required_findings: Very low blood ketone bodies (<1 mmol/L, therapeutic 2–5 mmol/L)
Poor adherence to classic ketogenic diet since initiation
Inadequate family knowledge about CKD: using weight-loss websites, choosing inappropriate "light" or "zero added sugar" foods
Father does not know the rationale for the diet
Patient secretly eats forbidden foods when teased by peers
Ongoing absence seizures and multiple generalized epileptiform discharges despite four anti‑seizure medications
7‑day food diary and interviews confirm dietary non‑adherence
Referral for attention/school difficulties, poor peer relationships, and irritability
optional_findings: Treated with valproic acid, ethosuximide, clobazam, topiramate
Patient has limited understanding of why she needs the diet
Peer teasing about appearance and dietary habits
EEG shows multiple generalized epileptiform discharges

provenance.title: Case report: KETOLAND the psychoeducation program for ketogenic diet

challenge_prompt:
A 60-year-old right-handed white male with 8 years of education presented with gradual onset of language production difficulties over one year. He reported effortful speech and word-finding problems (anomia). He lived alone and continued working with task adjustments. Family history was notable for cerebrovascular disease in his mother.

At initial speech-language evaluation one year after symptom onset, he exhibited anomia and phonemic paraphasias, mild difficulties understanding syntactically complex sentences, and mild dyscalculia. After a six-month speech-language training program, re-evaluation showed progression to agrammatism.

At 18 months after onset, neurological examination was normal with no focal motor or sensory deficits; he was oriented but could only understand syntactically simple commands. FDG-PET revealed hypometabolism in the left fronto-insular, fronto-temporal, and temporo-lateral regions, as well as in the basal ganglia, with left predominance.

At 24 months, brain CT demonstrated hypodensity of the periventricular white matter, primarily at the left frontal horn. EEG was normal. CSF analysis showed total tau 489 pg/mL (slightly elevated), phosphorylated tau 34 pg/mL (normal), and amyloid-β42 1,114 pg/mL (normal). The patient was then referred to a tertiary center for comprehensive evaluation.

Neuropsychological assessment at the tertiary center confirmed predominant language impairment characterized by apraxia of speech, agrammatism in speech, and impaired sentence comprehension. Mild non-linguistic deficits included orofacial apraxia and difficulty interpreting others' intentions and emotions. Insight was preserved, and no behavioral disturbances were present except moderate anxiety. Brain 3-Tesla MRI demonstrated left sylvian-perisylvian, fronto-opercular, parietal, and striatal atrophy, with prominent left frontal periventricular white matter hyperintensities.

At 31 months after onset, language deficits had worsened, now involving single-word comprehension, object knowledge, and sentence repetition. Extra-language cognitive domains, except visuospatial abilities, were impaired, with severe deficits in attention and executive functions. Behavioral changes included moderate apathy, aspontaneity, hyperorality, and a preference for sweet foods. Repeat MRI showed progressive ventricular enlargement predominantly on the left, increased atrophy of left frontal-opercular and temporo-mesial regions, and an increased burden of left frontal white matter hyperintensities.

Genetic testing for common frontotemporal lobar degeneration (FTLD) mutations has not yet been performed.

Given the clinical history, examination, and imaging findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

Genetic testing revealed a heterozygous GRN mutation (c.1018delC, p.H340TfsX21).
gold_diagnosis: Non-fluent variant primary progressive aphasia (nfvPPA) due to a heterozygous GRN mutation (c.1018delC, p.H340TfsX21), causing frontotemporal lobar degeneration.

answer_key.diagnosis: Non-fluent variant primary progressive aphasia (nfvPPA) due to a heterozygous GRN mutation (c.1018delC, p.H340TfsX21), causing frontotemporal lobar degeneration.
answer_key.etiology: Progranulin haploinsufficiency from a novel GRN gene mutation (c.1018delC, p.H340TfsX21)
answer_key.next_management_step: Genetic testing for GRN mutations (or serum progranulin level measurement) to confirm the suspected genetic etiology.
required_findings: Progressive non-fluent speech with effortful speech and anomia evolving to agrammatism and apraxia of speech.
Impaired sentence comprehension with relatively preserved single-word comprehension early on.
Left fronto-insular, fronto-temporal, and basal ganglia hypometabolism on FDG-PET with left predominance.
Hypodensity/hyperintensity of left frontal periventricular white matter on CT and MRI.
Asymmetric atrophy of left sylvian-perisylvian, fronto-opercular, parietal, and striatal regions on MRI.
CSF total tau 489 pg/mL (elevated), normal phosphorylated tau (34 pg/mL), and normal amyloid-β42 (1,114 pg/mL).
Preserved insight and absence of early behavioral disturbances, with later development of apathy, aspontaneity, hyperorality, and sweet food preference.
Progressive ventricular enlargement and worsening left frontotemporal atrophy on follow-up MRI.
optional_findings: Difficulties in attributing others' intentions and emotions (theory of mind deficit).

provenance.title: A novel GRN mutation in an Italian patient with non-fluent variant of primary progressive aphasia at onset: a longitudinal case report

mend_provenance.added: Genetic testing revealed a heterozygous GRN mutation (c.1018delC, p.H340TfsX21).
missing_discriminators: GRN mutation status, specifically c.1018delC

challenge_prompt:
Case 1 is a 20-year-old male. No family history of neurological disease was reported. Pregnancy ended in cesarean section at 35 weeks of gestation due to premature membrane rupture. His Apgar score was 7 at 1′, 9 at 5′, and 10 at 10′. At the age of 2 years, the child was first evaluated for developmental delay. No signs suggestive of autism spectrum disorder were noticed. MRI and EEG were normal. Array CGH detected a 5p15.2 microdeletion inherited from his asymptomatic father. At the age of 5 years, the child began experiencing daily episodes of reduced awareness and mild oro-mandibular automatisms. Video EEG documented absence seizures at 3-Hz spike and wave discharges. The EEG background activity and sleep organization were normal. Absences were controlled by combined valproic acid and ethosuximide treatment. Initial genetic screening, including karyotyping and FMR1 and SLC2A1 sequence analyses failed to detect variants. Given the history of mild intellectual disability and typical absence seizures without a known cause, what is the most appropriate next step in genetic evaluation to identify the underlying etiology?

At 17 years of age, a multigene epilepsy panel revealed a de novo variant in the SLC6A1 gene (NM_003042.3): c.1648G>A/p.Gly550Arg, (rs886042046). Segregation analysis ruled out the presence of the variant in both of the healthy parents.
gold_diagnosis: SLC6A1-associated neurodevelopmental disorder with epilepsy (pathogenic variant c.1648G>A/p.Gly550Arg)

answer_key.diagnosis: SLC6A1-associated neurodevelopmental disorder with epilepsy (pathogenic variant c.1648G>A/p.Gly550Arg)
aliases: Myoclonic-atonic epilepsy due to SLC6A1, mild phenotype with isolated absence seizures
answer_key.etiology: De novo heterozygous missense mutation in SLC6A1 (NM_003042.3): c.1648G>A/p.Gly550Arg (rs886042046), encoding the GABA transporter GAT-1, leading to impaired inhibitory neurotransmission.
answer_key.next_management_step: Order a multigene epilepsy panel (which includes SLC6A1) or whole exome sequencing; if panel is targeted, consider SLC6A1 sequencing specifically. Confirm variant with segregation analysis in parents and provide genetic counseling.
required_findings: Developmental delay noticed at age 2 years
Normal brain MRI and normal EEG at initial evaluation
Array CGH showing 5p15.2 microdeletion inherited from asymptomatic father (likely benign)
Onset at age 5 of daily absence seizures with reduced awareness and oro-mandibular automatisms
Video EEG with 3-Hz spike-and-wave discharges typical of absence seizures, normal background
Seizures controlled with valproic acid and ethosuximide
Negative targeted genetic tests: karyotype, FMR1, SLC2A1 sequencing
optional_findings: Prematurity at 35 weeks with premature membrane rupture
Seizure freedom on dual anti-seizure medications

provenance.title: Case report: SLC6A1 mutations presenting with isolated absence seizures: description of 2 novel cases

mend_provenance.added: At 17 years of age, a multigene epilepsy panel revealed a de novo variant in the SLC6A1 gene (NM_003042.3): c.1648G>A/p.Gly550Arg, (rs886042046). Segregation analysis ruled out the presence of the variant in both of the healthy parents.
missing_discriminators: multigene epilepsy panel result revealing SLC6A1 pathogenic variant c.1648G>A/p.Gly550Arg

challenge_prompt: A 30-year-old female with a 12-year history of bipolar II disorder presents with a hypomanic episode. She was diagnosed with bipolar II after a first hypomanic episode at age 22; previously she had recurrent depressive episodes beginning at age 18. During this admission, which occurred after she discontinued mood-stabilizing medications due to pregnancy, she exhibited elevated mood, pressured speech, decreased need for sleep, irritability, and mood swings. Physical examination was unremarkable. Complete blood count, electrolytes, liver and renal function tests, and thyroid function tests were all within normal limits. Brain MRI revealed an isolated, well-circumscribed lesion in the splenium of the corpus callosum. The lesion was hyperintense on T2-weighted and T2-FLAIR sequences and isointense to slightly hypointense on T1-weighted imaging. No neurological symptoms or deficits were noted. What is the most likely diagnosis for the splenial lesion, and what is the next most appropriate step?
gold_diagnosis: Transient splenial lesion syndrome (RESLES)

answer_key.diagnosis: Transient splenial lesion syndrome (RESLES)
answer_key.etiology: Cytotoxic edema of the splenium, possibly triggered by abrupt medication withdrawal and the hypomanic episode
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate mood-stabilizing treatment (e.g., lithium and quetiapine) and obtain a repeat brain MRI after 6–8 weeks to confirm lesion reversibility.
required_findings: 30-year-old female with bipolar II disorder
Current hypomanic episode after medication cessation during pregnancy
Normal physical examination and blood tests
Brain MRI shows isolated, well-circumscribed lesion in the splenium of the corpus callosum
Hyperintense on T2WI and T2 FLAIR, isointense to slightly hypointense on T1WI
No neurological deficits
optional_findings: YMRS score of 4, HCL-32 score of 21
First hypomanic episode at age 22 triggered by venlafaxine

provenance.title: Transient splenial lesion syndrome in bipolar-II disorder: a case report highlighting reversible brain changes during hypomanic episodes

challenge_prompt:
A 35-year-old incarcerated Korean-American male with a history of unspecified psychosis presented to the hospital for extrapyramidal symptoms after administration of haloperidol decanoate. He had received haloperidol decanoate 100 mg IM at a jail outside the county, was transferred the same day, and started on risperidone 6 mg PO daily. He was also taking valproic acid. Ten days after the initial haloperidol dose, he received a second dose of haloperidol decanoate 100 mg IM. The day after this second dose, he presented with bradykinesia, stooped posture, mask-like facies, cogwheel rigidity, diaphoresis, drooling, and suicidal ideation. Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale (BFCRS) score was 17. Vital signs: temperature 97.1°F (36.2°C), pulse 115 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, blood pressure 158/109 mmHg. Laboratory findings: creatine kinase 1335 U/L, anion gap 20, total bilirubin 1.3 mg/dL, AST 57 IU/L, ALT 35 IU/L, WBC 6.0 × 10³/μL, platelets 194 × 10³/μL. ECG showed sinus tachycardia with QTc 403 ms. Poison Control was contacted and noted low concern for neuroleptic malignant syndrome given absence of hyperthermia and lead-pipe rigidity. Antipsychotics were held, and the patient received intramuscular diphenhydramine and intravenous fluids per Poison Control recommendation. Scalp examination revealed lice infestation, which was treated with permethrin.

Based on the information available at this point, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next management step is most appropriate?
gold_diagnosis: Antipsychotic-induced catatonia

answer_key.diagnosis: Antipsychotic-induced catatonia
answer_key.etiology: Iatrogenic, due to administration of high-potency antipsychotics (haloperidol and risperidone) with high D2 receptor affinity.
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue all antipsychotics. Administer a benzodiazepine (e.g., lorazepam 1–2 mg IV or IM) as first-line treatment. Consider electroconvulsive therapy if inadequate response.
required_findings: Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale (BFCRS) score of 17
Bradykinesia, stooped posture, mask-like facies, cogwheel rigidity
Diaphoresis and drooling
Recent exposure to haloperidol decanoate and risperidone
Elevated creatine kinase (1335 U/L)
Autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension)
Absence of hyperthermia and lead-pipe rigidity (low concern for neuroleptic malignant syndrome)
Poison Control assessment of low concern for NMS
optional_findings: Total bilirubin 1.3 mg/dL, AST 57 IU/L, ALT 35 IU/L

provenance.title: Case report on antipsychotic induced catatonia in an incarcerated patient

challenge_prompt:
A 42-year-old right-handed female electronic engineer presented with progressive cognitive and behavioral decline over several years. She had a history of fibromyalgia diagnosed at age 28 and depressive symptoms treated as bipolar affective disorder type 1. At age 37, after a pregnancy, she discontinued oral contraceptives (drospirenone/ethinyl estradiol) and a levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) was inserted. One year later, she developed work-related difficulties, irritability, social withdrawal, anhedonia, forgetfulness, poor concentration, apathy, and neglect of household and childcare responsibilities. Notably, during her prior use of oral contraceptives, there had been no clear relationship with depressive symptoms. Her chronic pain worsened, leading to multiple hospitalizations and treatment with intravenous ketamine and muscle blocks with poor response.

At age 39, she was diagnosed with seronegative spondyloarthropathy and started treatment, experiencing partial pain improvement; however, psychomotor symptoms accelerated, and anxiety increased. Laboratory testing and lumbar puncture ruled out metabolic and infectious diseases. Brain MRI with contrast was normal. Neuropsychological assessment showed mild frontal-predominant cognitive impairment, anxiety, and severe depression. An extensive autoimmune panel, including anti-NMDA receptor antibodies, was negative. Despite meeting criteria for probable antibody-negative autoimmune encephalitis, six sessions of plasmapheresis yielded no improvement.

The patient met International Consensus Criteria for possible behavioral variant frontotemporal dementia (bvFTD). Neurological examination revealed no pyramidal, extrapyramidal, cerebellar, or peripheral nerve signs. She had no history of traumatic brain injury, alcohol or drug abuse, or family history of dementia. A second neuropsychological evaluation showed deterioration in visual and verbal memory, comprehension, and executive functions, with persistent severe depression and anxiety.

Brain 18F-FDG PET scan demonstrated hypometabolism in the frontal lobes, most pronounced in the superior orbital gyrus and inferior opercular, triangular, and opercular gyri; the temporal lobes were less affected. The findings were considered consistent with a frontal-predominant neurodegenerative process.

The clinical course was marked by late onset of psychiatric symptoms, progressive deterioration, and fluctuating symptoms. The case was discussed at a multidisciplinary board meeting to determine further management.

Based on the available evidence, what is the most likely underlying etiology, and what next step should be considered?

These symptoms improved after the removal of the device.
gold_diagnosis: Major depression associated with a levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS)

answer_key.diagnosis: Major depression associated with a levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS)
answer_key.etiology: Levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS)
answer_key.next_management_step: Remove the LNG-IUS
required_findings: One year after insertion of LNG-IUS, she developed cognitive and depressive symptoms.
During prior use of oral contraceptives, there had been no clear relationship with depressive symptoms.
Brain MRI with contrast was normal.
Autoimmune panel, including anti-NMDA receptor antibodies, was negative; six sessions of plasmapheresis yielded no improvement.
Neuropsychological assessment showed mild frontal-predominant cognitive impairment, anxiety, and severe depression.
FDG PET showed frontal hypometabolism, most pronounced in superior orbital gyrus and opercular gyri.
She met criteria for possible bvFTD, but course was marked by late onset of psychiatric symptoms, progressive deterioration, and fluctuating symptoms.
No history of traumatic brain injury, alcohol or drug abuse, or family history of dementia; neurological exam normal.
optional_findings: History of fibromyalgia and bipolar affective disorder type 1.
Negative lumbar puncture for metabolic/infectious disease.
IV ketamine and muscle blocks for pain with poor response.
Neurological exam: no pyramidal, extrapyramidal, cerebellar, or peripheral nerve signs.
Second neuropsychological evaluation showed deterioration in visual/verbal memory, comprehension, and executive functions.
No improvement in depression/anxiety between first and second neuropsychological evaluations.
Occupational and social decline with apathy and neglect of responsibilities.

challenge_prompt:
A 40-year-old male presented with a 3-year history of progressive motor symptoms. He exhibited diffuse muscle atrophy, fasciculations, weakness, and hyperreflexia. Both upper and lower motor neuron signs were present. There was no evidence of cognitive or behavioral disorders. Brain MRI with quantitative susceptibility mapping (QSM) was performed. QSM analysis revealed the following magnetic susceptibility values (mean ± SD) in the putamen:
- Left putamen: 0.068 ± 0.008 (age-matched control left: 0.034 ± 0.005)
- Right putamen: 0.062 ± 0.007 (control right: 0.041 ± 0.005)
gold_diagnosis: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)

answer_key.diagnosis: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)
answer_key.etiology: Sporadic ALS; iron dysregulation shown by QSM may contribute to pathogenesis
answer_key.next_management_step: Refer for nerve conduction studies/electromyography to confirm diffuse active and chronic denervation, rule out mimics (cervical MRI, serum B12, CK, etc.), and initiate multidisciplinary ALS care including riluzole.
required_findings: Progressive motor symptoms over 3 years
Diffuse muscle atrophy
Fasciculations
Weakness
Hyperreflexia
Presence of both upper and lower motor neuron signs
No cognitive or behavioral disorder
Increased QSM susceptibility in bilateral putamen compared to control
optional_findings: Putamen volumes slightly lower than control
Control subject matched for age and sex

provenance.title: Iron quantification in basal ganglia using quantitative susceptibility mapping in a patient with ALS: a case report and literature review

challenge_prompt:
Mr. B is a Caucasian man born in 1961. He had unspecified learning difficulties since age 8, requiring special school with a 2-year delay, but later attended ordinary school at 14 without graduating. He worked as a store manager for 13 years and then as a delivery boy until 2008. Medical history: cranial trauma with peri-circumstantial amnesia at age 8. Family history: brother with slight intellectual disability, institutionalized; no family history of movement disorders or psychosis. In 2012, without a provoking factor, he suffered a psychotic episode with bizarre mystic and persecutory delusions and congruent hallucinations, requiring hospitalization for psychomotor agitation and lack of insight. Diagnosed as an idiopathic brief psychotic episode, treated with olanzapine 10 mg with complete remission in 7 days, continued for relapse prevention. After 2 years, following olanzapine withdrawal for 4 months per psychiatrist, a second brief psychotic episode occurred: he was arrested while walking barefoot, explaining he would find a wife that day, with delusions mixed with mystic content from prior episode. Hospitalization for 7 days and resumption of olanzapine 10 mg led to full remission. After discharge, neurological assessment revealed a predominant right hypokinetic rigid extrapyramidal syndrome.

Brain CT demonstrated bilateral calcifications in the basal ganglia, thalami, and dentate nuclei.
gold_diagnosis: Fahr's disease (idiopathic bilateral basal ganglia calcification)

answer_key.diagnosis: Fahr's disease (idiopathic bilateral basal ganglia calcification)
answer_key.etiology: Sporadic; genetic testing negative for SLC20A2, PDGFB, PDGFRB, XPR mutations
answer_key.next_management_step: Brain MRI without contrast to detect basal ganglia calcifications
required_findings: Age 51 at first psychotic episode
Two brief psychotic episodes with bizarre delusions and hallucinations
Complete remission with olanzapine, relapse on withdrawal
Right hypokinetic rigid extrapyramidal syndrome
History of learning difficulties since childhood
No family history of movement disorders or psychosis
Psychotic episodes atypical for schizophrenia (brief, full remission, no residual decline)
Neurological exam reveals extrapyramidal signs disproportionate to olanzapine use
optional_findings: Initial diagnosis of idiopathic brief psychotic episode
Personality described as tame and naïve
No cognitive decline between psychotic episodes

provenance.title: Case report: 10 years follow-up of psychosis due to Fahr’s disease complicated by a left temporal stroke

mend_provenance.added: Brain CT demonstrated bilateral calcifications in the basal ganglia, thalami, and dentate nuclei.
missing_discriminators: Brain imaging (CT or MRI) showing bilateral basal ganglia calcifications

challenge_prompt:
A 73-year-old male presents with a 20-day history of progressive left-sided limb weakness. He denies headache, fever, vomiting, vision changes, or seizures. Physical examination reveals decreased muscle strength in the left limbs but is otherwise normal. His medical history is unremarkable; specifically, there is no history of otitis media, sinusitis, head trauma, bowel malignancy, or pulmonary arteriovenous malformation. Blood tests show a leukocyte count of 3.71 × 10^9/L (normal reference range: 3.5–9.5 × 10^9/L), 57.8% neutrophils (40–75%), and 31.8% lymphocytes (20–50%). Body temperature is within normal limits. Brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) demonstrates an occupying lesion in the right frontal lobe with long T1 and T2 signals; contrast-enhanced T1-weighted imaging reveals heterogeneous ring enhancement and surrounding edema, with no remarkable midline shift. Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) and apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping were not performed. A lumbar puncture yields cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) that is negative for bacteria or fungi on culture; metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS) of the CSF detects no microbial DNA fragments. Given the patient’s age and imaging, a brain glioma is initially suspected. The patient undergoes robot-assisted stereotactic biopsy. Intraoperative frozen pathological examination confirms an abscess; white-yellow, viscous pus is aspirated, and the capsule wall is removed for histopathology. Pathological examination of the excised tissue shows inflammation with neutrophil infiltration, consistent with an abscess. The patient is started on empirical intravenous meropenem (2 g every 8 hours) and vancomycin (1 g every 12 hours). The aspirated pus is cultured on chocolate agar under anaerobic conditions, yielding creamy white, moist, and confluent colonies. Gram staining of these colonies shows gram-negative filamentous rods. What is the most likely causative organism, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

The isolated strain was identified as Fusobacterium nucleatum by 16S rRNA gene sequencing.
gold_diagnosis: Brain abscess caused by Fusobacterium nucleatum

answer_key.diagnosis: Brain abscess caused by Fusobacterium nucleatum
aliases: Anaerobic brain abscess due to Fusobacterium
answer_key.etiology: Fusobacterium nucleatum
answer_key.next_management_step: Switch antimicrobial therapy to metronidazole combined with ceftriaxone (or an equivalent regimen targeting anaerobic gram-negative bacteria) and initiate a search for a dental or oral source of infection.
required_findings: Brain MRI shows a right frontal lobe lesion with ring enhancement and perilesional edema
Intraoperative frozen section and histopathology confirm an abscess with pus and neutrophil infiltration
CSF culture and mNGS are negative for pathogens
Pus cultured on anaerobic chocolate agar yields creamy white, moist, confluent colonies
Gram stain of the colonies shows gram-negative filamentous rods
Normal peripheral leukocyte count and differential
Age 73
Progressive left-sided limb weakness
optional_findings: No history of otitis media, sinusitis, or head trauma
Lesion located in the right frontal lobe
Pus described as white-yellow and viscous
Initial empirical antibiotics meropenem and vancomycin

provenance.title: Case report: Two case reports of cryptogenic brain abscess caused by Fusobacterium nucleatum and literature review

mend_provenance.added: The isolated strain was identified as Fusobacterium nucleatum by 16S rRNA gene sequencing.
missing_discriminators: specific identification of the organism from culture (e.g., 16S rRNA sequencing, MALDI-TOF)

challenge_prompt: A 17-year-old male with no prior psychiatric or medical history (except myopia) presented with acute disorganized and aggressive behavior, delusions of persecution, auditory and visual hallucinations, and confusion. He had recently migrated and was detained for 4 weeks, during which he had a mild febrile illness with nasal congestion, cough, headache, positive rapid SARS-CoV-2 and streptococcal antigen tests, normal chest x-ray, and was treated with amoxicillin. After detention, he had 2–3 weeks of normal behavior, then developed a generalized tonic-clonic seizure with tongue biting, loss of consciousness, and postictal paresis. He was brought to an outside hospital, where basic labs, urine toxicology, and non-contrast head CT were unremarkable. He was started on risperidone for presumed primary psychotic disorder but transferred to a tertiary center due to lack of improvement. On arrival, he had labile affect, disorientation, fluctuating confusion, paranoia, and endorsed visual and auditory hallucinations. His presentation was consistent with delirium with psychotic features. Neurological examination during lucid periods showed attention and delayed recall deficits, no focal deficits. Over the following 2–3 weeks, he developed hypersomnolence and fluctuating catatonia: verbigeration, stupor, minimal verbal responsiveness, staring, posturing, negativism, waxy flexibility, Mitgehen, and mild autonomic instability (Bush-Francis score 23). He also developed dysarthria and gait ataxia. A maculopapular rash on chest and back had appeared 2–3 weeks before admission. Brain MRI with contrast showed no structural abnormalities or abnormal enhancement. EEG demonstrated mild diffuse polymorphic slowing without epileptiform activity. Lumbar puncture revealed normal cell count, protein, and glucose. CT chest/abdomen and testicular ultrasound showed no malignancy. Extensive serum and CSF studies for infectious, metabolic, and other autoimmune causes were negative. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
gold_diagnosis: Anti-N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor autoimmune encephalitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Anti-N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor autoimmune encephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune, possibly triggered by recent SARS-CoV-2 infection
answer_key.next_management_step: Send CSF and serum for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies; consider starting empiric immunotherapy with high-dose corticosteroids and IVIG while awaiting results.
required_findings: Acute onset of psychotic symptoms (disorganized behavior, delusions, hallucinations) in a previously healthy adolescent
Fluctuating mental status with delirium and catatonic features (verbigeration, stupor, posturing, negativism, waxy flexibility)
Generalized seizure with tongue biting, loss of consciousness, and postictal paresis
Memory and attention deficits on neurological exam without focal findings
Brain MRI with contrast normal
CSF analysis: normal cell count, protein, and glucose
EEG: mild diffuse slowing, no epileptiform activity
Development of dysarthria and gait ataxia during hospitalization
optional_findings: History of mild COVID-19 2–3 weeks prior to symptom onset
Maculopapular rash on chest and back
Unremarkable chest/abdominal CT and testicular ultrasound
Failure to respond to antipsychotics (risperidone, chlorpromazine)
Initial presentation misdiagnosed as primary psychotic disorder
Social isolation and detention prior to symptom onset

provenance.title: Case report: Anti N-methyl-D-aspartate autoimmune encephalitis following a mildly symptomatic COVID-19 infection in an adolescent male

challenge_prompt:
A 72-year-old right-handed man with 12 years of education (Patient #1) and a 69-year-old right-handed woman with 12 years of education (Patient #2) were referred for neuropsychological evaluation due to reported memory concerns. Neither patient complained of memory difficulties. Patient #1 stated he did not experience any unusual difficulty with memory but recognized his wife held a different opinion; he appeared perplexed as to why he needed the examination. Patient #2 also reported no memory difficulties, but family members described an insidious development of memory problems over the preceding 2 years; she fully understood the reason for evaluation.

Detailed neuropsychological testing was performed. On the WAIS-IV, both patients performed similarly on three composite indices; Patient #1 scored in the impaired range on the Processing Speed Index, while Patient #2 scored in the average range. Memory testing (WMS-IV, RAVLT, BVMT-R) revealed impaired performance for both patients, similar in degree. Patient #1 had significant difficulty with category word fluency, whereas Patient #2 did not. Confrontational naming (Boston Naming Test) was comparable and intact. Trail Making Test Part B was significantly more challenging for Patient #1 than for Patient #2. Perseverative errors on the Wisconsin Card Sorting Test (64-card version) were in the impaired range for Patient #1 but not for Patient #2. Finger tapping speed was similar.

During testing, both patients were cooperative. Each acknowledged difficulty with some memory tasks but appeared unconcerned, stating that at their age such difficulty was expected. Neither appeared defensive; both seemed dismissive of memory failures.

Self-reported competency on the Patient Competency Rating Scale fell in the normal range for age for both, and both denied any difficulties in daily functioning or memory. However, spousal reports diverged. Patient #1’s spouse rated his daily functioning at 98/150; Patient #2’s spouse rated hers at 119/150. Patient #1’s spouse noted that he could not handle finances and could not recognize when something he said or did upset someone else; Patient #2’s spouse reported no difficulties in either area. When asked to rate the patient’s level of concern about everyday difficulties on a zero-to-ten scale (10 = extreme concern), the spouse of Patient #1 rated him 1, and the spouse of Patient #2 rated her 4. On a similar scale for anxiety or anger/agitation when discussing their difficulties, the spouse of Patient #1 rated him 2, while the spouse of Patient #2 rated her 8.

What phenomenon best accounts for each patient’s lack of reported memory symptoms, and what next step would help distinguish such patients?
gold_diagnosis: Patient #1: anosognosia for memory impairment; Patient #2: denial of memory impairment (psychological coping mechanism)

answer_key.diagnosis: Patient #1: anosognosia for memory impairment; Patient #2: denial of memory impairment (psychological coping mechanism)
aliases: Patient #1: unawareness of deficit, impaired self-awareness; Patient #2: conscious avoidance, resistance to change
answer_key.etiology: Patient #1: likely underlying neurodegenerative disease (MCI) disrupting self-awareness networks (e.g., medial prefrontal/posterior cingulate). Patient #2: psychological denial driven by anxiety about cognitive decline.
answer_key.next_management_step: Apply a brief screening tool targeting emotional/behavioral markers: (1) anxiety/anger when discussing limitations, (2) ability to perceive when one upsets others (PCRS item), (3) resistance to feedback. Score ≥4 suggests denial; absence suggests anosognosia. Follow with comprehensive denial questionnaire (e.g., Illness Denial Questionnaire) if denial suspected.
required_findings: Patient #1 appears perplexed about need for evaluation and reports no memory difficulty.
Patient #1’s spouse rates his function 98/150, notes he cannot handle finances or recognize upsetting others.
Patient #1’s spouse rates his concern 1/10 and anxiety/agitation 2/10.
Patient #1 shows impaired processing speed, category fluency, TMT-B, and WCST perseverative errors.
Patient #2 reports no memory difficulty but family notes insidious decline; she understands reason for evaluation.
Patient #2’s spouse rates her function 119/150, no difficulty with finances or perceiving upsetting others.
Patient #2’s spouse rates her concern 4/10 and anxiety/agitation 8/10.
Patient #2’s executive functions (category fluency, TMT-B, WCST) are intact.
optional_findings: Both patients show similar impaired memory scores on WMS-IV, RAVLT, BVMT-R.
Both are cooperative and dismissive of memory test failures.
Patient #1’s spouse notes he cannot handle finances.
Both have 12 years of education and are right-handed.
Patient #2’s memory decline was insidious over 2 years.
Both self-reported normal daily functioning on PCRS.
Finger tapping speed is similar between patients.
Patient #1’s WAIS-IV Processing Speed Index is impaired; Patient #2’s is average.

provenance.title: Studying lack of awareness of cognitive decline in neurodegenerative diseases requires measures of both anosognosia and denial

challenge_prompt:
A fit and autonomous female patient in her mid-80s, known for arterial hypertension, was admitted to the hospital for pyelonephritis with progressive asthenia and urinary symptoms. Clinical examination was unremarkable except for moderate dehydration and a fever of 39.2°C. Laboratory investigations at the emergency room showed leucocytosis (12 ×10⁹/L), elevated C-reactive protein (122 mg/L), mild hyponatraemia (130 mmol/L), and normal renal function (creatinine 73 µmol/L, CKD-EPI 64 mL/min/1.73 m²). Ceftriaxone was started at 2 g/day; fever and urinary complaints resolved within two days. On day 1, creatinine rose slightly to 88 µmol/L (CKD-EPI 51) without meeting acute kidney injury criteria; renal function then normalised with intravenous fluids and remained stable (creatinine 54–66 µmol/L, CKD-EPI 72–82) throughout hospitalisation. On day 4, the patient developed fluctuating and progressively worsening neurological symptoms: transitory disorientation and incomprehensible speech. Blood tests on day 4 showed normal glucose, stable sodium (130 mmol/L), slight hypophosphataemia (0.69 mmol/L), normal liver enzymes, liver function tests, and coagulation. Bladder ultrasound revealed urinary retention (catheterised, no acute postrenal kidney injury). Neurological examination was non-focal, without signs of meningitis. Cerebral angio-CT was normal except for mild atrophy. On day 5, fluctuating aphasia, three short episodes of loss of contact, and brief low-amplitude myoclonic movements in all four extremities appeared. Non-convulsive status epilepticus was suspected; intravenous clonazepam 0.5 mg was administered, leading to complete resolution of symptoms for approximately 12 hours. Symptoms returned on day 6 and worsened on day 7: aphasia periods lasting up to one hour, gripping and not releasing objects, and chorea-like tongue movements. Lumbar puncture yielded normal, clear cerebrospinal fluid: 1 erythrocyte/µL, 1 leucocyte/µL, normal glucose and lactate. Brain MRI showed cortico-subcortical atrophy with parietal predominance, no acute changes. Electroencephalogram (EEG) demonstrated unstable background activity with superimposed diffuse fast activity (beta rhythm) and generalised discharges with triphasic morphology, consistent with encephalopathy. Blood and urine cultures grew a multisensitive Escherichia coli. Although non-convulsive status was suspected again, intravenous clonazepam 0.5 mg ×2 only partially improved symptoms, and subcutaneous haloperidol 0.5 mg had no effect. At this point, structural brain lesions, central nervous system infection, epileptiform activity, and major electrolyte disturbances had been excluded.

Ceftriaxone was discontinued on day 7, and symptoms gradually resolved over the following days, confirming the diagnosis of ceftriaxone-induced encephalopathy.
gold_diagnosis: Ceftriaxone-induced encephalopathy

answer_key.diagnosis: Ceftriaxone-induced encephalopathy
answer_key.etiology: Ceftriaxone neurotoxicity
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue ceftriaxone and switch to an alternative antibiotic (e.g., amoxicillin-clavulanic acid)
required_findings: Female, mid-80s
Received ceftriaxone 2 g/day for pyelonephritis
Onset of neurological symptoms on day 4, progressing to aphasia, myoclonus, and chorea-like movements by day 7
EEG: unstable background with diffuse fast beta rhythm and generalised triphasic discharges, indicative of encephalopathy
Normal cerebrospinal fluid (1 erythrocyte/µL, 1 leucocyte/µL, normal glucose/lactate)
Brain MRI and CT angiography without acute pathology (only atrophy)
Exclusion of stroke, intracranial bleed, meningitis/encephalitis, and major electrolyte disturbances
Blood and urine cultures grew E. coli; ceftriaxone continued but neurological deterioration persisted
optional_findings: Transient complete resolution of symptoms with clonazepam 0.5 mg (12 hours)
Normal liver enzymes and liver function tests
Renal function normal throughout hospitalisation (creatinine 54–66 µmol/L, CKD-EPI 72–82)

provenance.title: Ceftriaxone-induced encephalopathy in a patient with a normal renal function

mend_provenance.added: Ceftriaxone was discontinued on day 7, and symptoms gradually resolved over the following days, confirming the diagnosis of ceftriaxone-induced encephalopathy.
missing_discriminators: confirmation that ceftriaxone was discontinued and symptoms resolved

challenge_prompt: A 25-year-old woman with no significant medical history presented with acute onset neuropsychiatric symptoms. Nine days before admission, she developed lack of response to her mother’s calls, impaired executive function (e.g., inability to open a pencil case), disorientation, tremors in the upper limbs, visual hallucinations of suicide victims, incontinence, and wandering. Seven days prior to admission, she developed a slight fever and was hospitalized. On admission, she was agitated, restless, with incoherent thoughts, disorientation, poor conversational fluency, generalized athetoid-like involuntary movements, and lower limb weakness. Vital signs: fever 39.5°C, heart rate 156/min. Tendon reflexes and pupillary responses were normal, no nuchal rigidity or pathological reflexes. She expressed Cotard delusions: “I am dead,” “I have no uterus,” “my heart is not beating.” Laboratory blood tests: WBC 18,000/μL, total bilirubin 1.70 mg/dL, LDH 410 U/L, CK 1,705 U/L. CRP 0.01 mg/dL. Thyroid function normal. Lumbar puncture: opening pressure 15 cm H2O, 2 WBC/μL, normal protein and glucose, IgG index 0.43. CSF PCR negative for HSV DNA. CSF antibodies (IgG and IgM) negative for HSV, VZV, CMV. CSF screening negative for NMDA receptor, LGI1, Caspr2, AMPA receptor, GABA-B receptor, and DPPX antibodies. Serum antibodies negative for ANA, NAE, thyroglobulin, thyroid peroxidase. Serum anti-recoverin antibodies: initially slightly positive (BML Inc., Tokyo, Japan). Contrast-enhanced brain MRI unremarkable. EEG showed diffuse nonspecific slowing without epileptiform activity. Whole-body CT and MRI of spine, pelvis, ovaries unremarkable; tumor markers (CEA, AFP, CA125, CA19-9, SLX, SCC antigen, SYFRA21-1, ProGRP, NSE, CA15-3, sIL-2L, BCA225) all negative. Ophthalmological exam: no retinopathy. She was suspected of autoimmune encephalitis and treated with two cycles of IVIG (20,000 mg/day for 5 days) and three cycles of steroid pulse therapy (methylprednisolone 1,000 mg/day for 3 days). Levetiracetam and valproate were given for seizure prophylaxis. At one month, neurological symptoms (disorientation, poor conversational fluency) improved, but psychiatric symptoms persisted. Olanzapine (up to 20 mg/day), risperidone (up to 6 mg/day), lorazepam (up to 6 mg/day), quazepam (20 mg/day), lemborexant (5 mg/day) were tried without improvement. Two months after admission, she developed worsened psychiatric symptoms: pessimistic thoughts, suicidal ideation, Capgras delusions (“Other persons, who have the same facial features as my parents but who have been replaced, live in my home. My real parents are dead.”), and persistent Cotard delusions. She attempted suicide by jumping/hanging because she believed her family was dead. SPECT imaging showed decreased rCBF in bilateral frontal and occipital lobes, increased rCBF in bilateral basal ganglia and pons. Repeat serum testing at 2 and 3 months after admission showed strongly positive anti-recoverin antibodies. At this point, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?
gold_diagnosis: Anti-recoverin antibody-positive encephalitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Anti-recoverin antibody-positive encephalitis
aliases: Autoimmune encephalitis associated with anti-recoverin antibodies
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune encephalitis associated with antibodies targeting recoverin, a neuron-specific calcium-binding protein; no tumor identified
answer_key.next_management_step: Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT)
required_findings: Acute (<9 days) neuropsychiatric syndrome with disorientation, hallucinations, and Cotard delusions
Movement disorder: tremors, athetoid-like involuntary movements, lower limb weakness
Autonomic symptoms: fever (39.5°C), tachycardia (156/min)
Cerebrospinal fluid negative for viral and common autoimmune encephalitis antibodies
Serum anti-recoverin antibodies positive (initially slightly positive, then strongly positive on repeat testing)
Brain MRI unremarkable, EEG with diffuse slowing
Poor response to immunotherapies (IVIG, steroids) and antipsychotics, with worsening psychiatric symptoms and severe suicide risk
SPECT showing decreased frontal/occipital rCBF and increased basal ganglia/pons rCBF
optional_findings: Leukocytosis (WBC 18,000/μL) and elevated CK, LDH, total bilirubin
Normal CSF opening pressure, cell count, protein, glucose, IgG index
Negative serum ANA, NAE, thyroglobulin, thyroid peroxidase antibodies
Negative tumor markers and whole-body imaging for occult malignancy
Absence of retinopathy on ophthalmological exam
Normal tendon reflexes and pupillary responses, no nuchal rigidity
Symptom onset with executive dysfunction and visual hallucinations of suicidal theme
Treatment history: failed trials of olanzapine, risperidone, benzodiazepines

provenance.title: Case report: A case of anti-recoverin antibody-positive encephalitis exhibiting Cotard and Capgras delusions that was successfully treated with electroconvulsive therapy

challenge_prompt:
A 39-year-old female patient was admitted to our hospital complaining of headaches, pain, and paralysis in the left hemifacial region, along with diplopia. The patient had previously experienced pain and diplopia and was admitted to another healthcare facility where treatment with corticosteroids resulted in partial clinical improvement. Following discharge, the patient reported a recurrence of symptoms after tapering off the corticosteroids, albeit with a milder presentation. 3 days prior to her most recent admission, the patient's symptoms worsened, prompting her to seek medical attention at our institution. Notably, the patient denied experiencing any sensory or motor dysfunction or symptoms related to infectious diseases. Her headaches were persistent throughout the day and intensified with positional changes. On physical examination, the patient exhibited no dysarthria, and her pupils were normal in size, with a normal reaction to light. Additionally, her eye movements were regular in all directions, with no observed ptosis, lid lag, or nystagmus. She suffered from left-sided facial plegia. No evidence of meningeal irritation was observed, and other clinical examinations were unremarkable.

Initial screening tests, including complete blood count, renal and liver function tests, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, C-reactive protein, fasting blood sugar, arterial blood gas, urine analysis, and fasting blood sugar, were performed and reported within the normal range. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain, thyroid, and abdominopelvic sonography and toxicology tests were also conducted and showed no pathological findings. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis was also performed and reported within the normal range. Additionally, the patient's CSF sample was tested for oligoclonal bands, which was negative. However, thyroid peroxidase antibody (TPO-Ab) levels were significantly elevated, and a positron emission tomography scan (PET-Scan) revealed diffuse increased metabolic activity in the thyroid gland due to autoimmune thyroiditis.
gold_diagnosis: Autoimmune thyroid disease-associated focal central neurological disorder (focal neurological deficit without encephalopathy)

answer_key.diagnosis: Autoimmune thyroid disease-associated focal central neurological disorder (focal neurological deficit without encephalopathy)
aliases: Steroid-responsive focal neurological disorder associated with autoimmune thyroiditis
Focal neurological manifestation of Hashimoto's thyroiditis
answer_key.etiology: Elevated thyroid peroxidase antibodies leading to immune-mediated focal central nervous system involvement
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate high-dose corticosteroids (e.g., intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 5 days) and consider rituximab or other immunosuppressive therapy.
required_findings: left-sided facial plegia
headaches, pain, and paralysis in the left hemifacial region, along with diplopia
MRI of the brain showed no pathological findings
Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis was also performed and reported within the normal range. Additionally, the patient's CSF sample was tested for oligoclonal bands, which was negative.
significantly elevated thyroid peroxidase antibody (TPO-Ab) levels
a positron emission tomography scan (PET-Scan) revealed diffuse increased metabolic activity in the thyroid gland due to autoimmune thyroiditis
treatment with corticosteroids resulted in partial clinical improvement
the patient denied experiencing any sensory or motor dysfunction or symptoms related to infectious diseases
optional_findings: persistent headaches intensified with positional changes
no dysarthria, pupils normal size/reaction, eye movements regular
initial labs normal (CBC, renal, liver, ESR, CRP, glucose, ABG, UA, toxicology)
thyroid MRI and abdominopelvic sonography normal

provenance.title: Focal central neurological disorder in patients with autoimmune thyroid disease without encephalopathy

challenge_prompt: A 32-year-old female patient presented with feverish feelings and joint pain, followed by a sudden onset of unclear speech and unsteady gait. Initial management by a physician did not lead to improvement. On examination, she appeared fatigued, with a low-grade fever (temperature 99.5°F / 37.5°C, heart rate 90 bpm, blood pressure 120/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 16 bpm). Neurological examination revealed an unsteady gait with mild ataxia (wide-based stance, slight swaying, difficulty maintaining balance) and dysarthria (slurred and unclear speech). No focal weakness or sensory deficits were noted; deep tendon reflexes were symmetric and normal. Joint examination revealed mild tenderness on palpation in both knees without swelling, redness, effusion, or synovitis; range of motion was normal. The remainder of the physical exam (cardiovascular, respiratory, abdominal, skin) was unremarkable. Laboratory investigations were negative. MRI of the brain demonstrated lesions in the left frontal lobe. On T2-weighted/STIR images, the lesions were hyperintense. Diffusion-weighted imaging showed vivid diffusion restriction. T1-weighted images revealed multiple well-defined lesions that were hypointense, with the largest measuring 21 × 20 mm and exhibiting concentric rings. What is the most likely diagnosis?
gold_diagnosis: Balo's concentric sclerosis (Baló's concentric sclerosis, Balo disease)

answer_key.diagnosis: Balo's concentric sclerosis (Baló's concentric sclerosis, Balo disease)
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune demyelinating disorder, considered a rare variant of multiple sclerosis.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate high-dose intravenous corticosteroids (e.g., methylprednisolone) to reduce inflammation and mitigate disease progression.
required_findings: 32-year-old female
feverish feelings and joint pain
sudden onset of unclear speech and unsteady gait
ataxic gait and dysarthria on neurological exam
MRI left frontal lobe lesions hyperintense on T2-weighted/STIR
vivid diffusion restriction on DWI
well-defined hypointense lesions on T1 with concentric rings
negative laboratory investigations
optional_findings: mild bilateral knee tenderness without arthritis
no focal weakness or sensory deficits
heart rate 90 bpm, blood pressure 120/80 mmHg
lack of improvement with initial physician management

provenance.title: A rare case of Balo's disease in a young adult: A clinical presentation and management

challenge_prompt:
A 12-year-old male presented with a 2-day history of high-grade fever and severe polyarticular joint pain involving the wrists, knees, and ankles. Over the subsequent 4 days, he developed progressive decline in consciousness, becoming lethargic and disoriented. He had been engaging in outdoor activities during the evening in a tropical region, with potential mosquito exposure. He had a history of seasonal allergies and mild asthma. On admission, temperature was 38.7°C, pulse 92/min, respiratory rate 18/min, blood pressure normal. He was lethargic, with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 12 (E3V3M6). Neurologic examination revealed no focal deficits, and muscle strength and reflexes were normal. Musculoskeletal exam showed tenderness and swelling in the wrists, knees, and ankles, without erythema or effusion. Skin exam showed no rash. Cardiovascular and respiratory exams were unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed leukopenia and thrombocytopenia. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis demonstrated lymphocytic pleocytosis and elevated protein. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain with contrast revealed scattered bilateral white matter lesions with periventricular diffusion-restricted spots on diffusion-weighted imaging; there was no involvement of the posterior fossa, cortical, or deep gray matter, and no contrast enhancement. T2-weighted, FLAIR, and susceptibility-weighted images were unremarkable.

Serology for chikungunya virus was positive for IgM antibodies.
gold_diagnosis: Chikungunya encephalitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Chikungunya encephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Chikungunya virus (CHIKV)
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) therapy and supportive care; send serum and CSF for CHIKV RT-PCR.
