challenge_prompt:
The 36-year-old patient had OCD since adolescence, starting at 17, characterized by obsessions and compulsions related to symmetry accompanied by checking compulsions, collecting, ordering, arranging, and repeating rituals where doubt was omnipresent. The main treatment at the time of surgery was paroxetine (60 mg/day). Therapies combining antidepressants, antipsychotics, benzodiazepines, cognitive behavioral therapy and electroconvulsive therapy had proven ineffective. A neurosurgical intervention was performed in two stages on the same day, involving stereotactic bilateral implantation of two electrodes (Activa RC) at the level of the BNSTand subsequent tunneling of extensions to the neurostimulator implanted subcutaneously in the right hypochondrium. The procedure was without complications, and postoperative brain scans confirmed the correct electrode positioning.

The psychometric and biological data were collected by a psychiatrist specialized in OCD and DBS, with time points aligned to clinical evaluations of DBS efficacy. The data collection took place at the interventional Psychiatry Unit of the University Hospital of Liège.

Blood samples were drawn at 08:00 h in the morning and centrifuged within 2 hours, with the serum immediately frozen and stored at -181°C until analysis. OT levels were measured using a double antibody radioimmunoassay, with intra-assay variability of 3.41% and interassay variability of 2.84%, and a sensitivity of 1 pmol/l.

The patient was first stimulated 11 days after surgery, and data were collected at 12 separate time points: T-1: (Just before surgery), T0 (day of first stimulation), T1 (T0 + 6days), T2 (T0 + 19days), T3 (T0 + 29 days), T4 (T0 + 63 days), T5 (T0 + 3 months), T6 (T0 + 9 months), T7 (T0 + 1year), T8 (T0 + 603 days), T9 (T0 + 616 days), T10 (T0 + 693 days), T11 (T0 + 2 years), T12 (T0 + 3years). Stimulator variables (amplitude left, amplitude right, frequency left, frequency right, pulse left, pulse right) were measured on all time points. YBOCS (Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale) scores were measured on T-1, T1, T3, T4, T5,T6,T7,T8,T9,T10, T11, T12, Plasma Oxytocin levels were measured on T0,T3,T4,T8, T9,T10.

For the longitudinal analysis of change over time, all available data points were used for each variable. For the correlational analysis between longitudinal changes only complete data sets were considered (T3,T4,T8, T9,T10). As during the treatment only adaptations of the DBS amplitude were done, only these were taken into account for longitudinal and correlational analysis. All statistical analysis were carried out in Matlab R2023a.

Based only on the presentation above, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?
gold_diagnosis: Non-linear evolution of plasma oxytocin levels positively correlated with YBOCS changes post-DBS

answer_key.diagnosis: Non-linear evolution of plasma oxytocin levels positively correlated with YBOCS changes post-DBS
aliases: Oxytocin as a biomarker of DBS efficacy in OCD
answer_key.etiology: DBS of BNST likely reinforces GABAergic inhibitory networks, modulating oxytocin levels
answer_key.next_management_step: Monitor serial oxytocin levels to track DBS response and guide stimulation adjustments

provenance.title: Case report: Non-linear evolution of oxytocin informs YBOCS changes post-DBS of the bed nucleus of the stria terminalis for treatment resistant OCD

challenge_prompt:
A 74-year-old woman with a history of left breast neoplasia and hypokinetic dilated cardiomyopathy (left ventricular ejection fraction 30% by Teicholz, pulmonary artery pressure elevated to 55 mmHg) presents acutely with proportional left hemiparesis and speech impairment. Blood pressure 110/76 mmHg, oxygen saturation 98% on room air. ECG shows sinus tachycardia at 105 bpm, atrial and left ventricular hypertrophy. Initial head CT reveals a small hypodensity in the posterior arm of the right internal capsule and diffuse hypodensity of the semioval centers and periventricular white matter, consistent with ischemic stroke in the right anterior choroidal territory with cortico-subcortical atrophy. She is started on enoxaparin, aspirin, and fluoxetine. On day 3, she deteriorates, developing proportional left hemiplegia and dysarthria. Repeat CT brain shows a large hypodensity (24 Hounsfield units) in the left parieto-temporo-occipital and right capsulo-lenticulo-thalamic regions, with mass effect causing partial collapse of the lateral ventricle and a 4 mm midline shift; no intra- or extra-axial hemorrhage. Doppler ultrasound of the supra-aortic trunks demonstrates total thrombotic occlusion of the right internal bulbar and post-bulbar carotid arteries. Angioscan of the supra-aortic trunks shows right carotid atherosclerosis with an occlusive thrombus beginning at the bulb, extending to the internal carotid artery and supra-cavernous portion, with no vascular malformation.

Based on the available information, what is the most likely etiology of her stroke, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Ischemic stroke due to total thrombotic occlusion of the right internal carotid artery secondary to atherosclerosis

answer_key.diagnosis: Ischemic stroke due to total thrombotic occlusion of the right internal carotid artery secondary to atherosclerosis
answer_key.etiology: Atherosclerosis (atherothrombosis)
answer_key.next_management_step: Continue parenteral anticoagulation with enoxaparin (or low molecular weight heparin) for 10 days, then transition to aspirin and high-intensity statin (e.g., atorvastatin); consider carotid endarterectomy if surgical candidacy and resources allow.

Angioscan showing right carotid atherosclerosis with occlusive thrombus from bulb to supra-cavernous ICA
No vascular malformation on angioscan
Absence of intra- or extra-axial hemorrhage on CT
Large hypodense lesions in capsulolenticulothalamic and parieto-temporo-occipital regions
Proportional left hemiplegia and dysarthria
Hypokinetic dilated cardiomyopathy with LVEF 30%
History of left breast neoplasia
optional_findings: Initial CT: small hypodensity in right internal capsule and periventricular white matter
ECG: sinus tachycardia 105 bpm, atrial and left ventricular hypertrophy
Initial treatment with enoxaparin, aspirin, fluoxetine

challenge_prompt: A 77-year-old woman with a history of hemorrhagic stroke 50 days prior presents with a prolonged disorder of consciousness. Head CT shows cerebral hemorrhages in the left parietal, occipital, and temporal lobes, injury to the splenium of the corpus callosum, and multiple bilateral infarcts in the basal ganglia, pontine tegmentum, thalamus, and corona radiata. On examination, she has eyes open but no spontaneous movement, no visual fixation or pursuit, no verbal output, no command following. She blinks to loud sounds and withdraws the left lower extremity to noxious stimuli. The Coma Recovery Scale-Revised (CRS-R) score is 2. She has received 30 days of pharmacological and central nervous system interventions without improvement. To evaluate residual cortical function, functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) is performed during auditory command tasks (told to open and close her eyes). The fNIRS shows statistically significant increases in oxygenated hemoglobin concentration in the left Wernicke area, right prefrontal cortex, and right primary motor cortex during the commands, but she shows no behavioral response. What is the most likely state of consciousness diagnosis suggested by these findings, and what is the next most appropriate step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Cognitive motor dissociation (covert consciousness)

answer_key.diagnosis: Cognitive motor dissociation (covert consciousness)
answer_key.etiology: Hemorrhagic stroke with disruption of motor output pathways while preserving cortical networks supporting consciousness
answer_key.next_management_step: Repeat behavioral assessments with CRS-R and consider adjunctive neuromodulation therapy such as transcutaneous auricular vagus nerve stimulation (taVNS) with ongoing fNIRS monitoring.

fNIRS during auditory commands shows increased HbO in left Wernicke area, right prefrontal cortex, and right primary motor cortex
No behavioral response to the same commands
History of hemorrhagic stroke with extensive lesions sparing some cortical regions
optional_findings: Multiple infarcts in basal ganglia, thalamus, brainstem
No improvement after 30 days of standard therapy

provenance.title: Case report: Monitoring consciousness with fNIRS in a patient with prolonged reduced consciousness following hemorrhagic stroke undergoing adjunct taVNS therapy

challenge_prompt:
A 33-year-old man with a college degree presented to a psychiatric outpatient clinic with a 3-4 year history of increasing distraction, attention deficits, and impaired judgment, leading to job instability. He was referred for suspected adult ADHD. He reported lifelong mild inattention, but the recent decline was progressive. Additional symptoms included mild lethargy, generalized itching, misplacing items, and psychomotor agitation. His Full-Scale IQ was 65, and he screened positive on the Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale (4 of 6 criteria). He had a history of Graves' disease treated with methimazole 5 years earlier. Due to atypical progression and concerns for organic etiology, neurological consultation was obtained.

Neurological examination initially showed mild gait instability, generalized hyperreflexia, and bilateral Babinski signs. There was no definite ataxia or parkinsonism. Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) score was 28. Electroencephalogram and nerve conduction studies were normal. Brain MRI revealed symmetrical high signal intensities on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences in the posterior limb of the internal capsule, hypothalamus, cerebral peduncles, and cerebellar white matter, including the dentate nucleus. Laboratory tests showed elevated antithyroid, anti-microsomal, and thyroglobulin antibodies, raising suspicion for Hashimoto's encephalopathy. However, cerebrospinal fluid analysis was unremarkable, and a trial of intravenous steroid pulse therapy yielded no improvement.

Over the next three months, the patient's cognitive function deteriorated further (MMSE dropped to 22), and he developed a spastic, foot-dragging gait with bilateral leg tremors. Neuropsychological testing demonstrated amnestic mild cognitive impairment involving multiple domains. Physical examination now revealed bilateral Achilles tendon xanthomas as painless soft tissue masses. Concurrently, bilateral cataracts and osteoporosis were diagnosed, and the patient reported intermittent diarrhea.

Based on the findings, a specific rare metabolic disorder was suspected. Genetic testing was initiated to confirm the diagnosis.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next best step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Cerebrotendinous Xanthomatosis

answer_key.diagnosis: Cerebrotendinous Xanthomatosis
answer_key.etiology: Autosomal recessive CYP27A1 mutations leading to bile acid synthesis defect and cholestanol accumulation
answer_key.next_management_step: Genetic testing for CYP27A1 mutations (or plasma cholestanol levels if available) to confirm the diagnosis; if confirmed, initiate chenodeoxycholic acid therapy.
required_findings: Bilateral Achilles tendon xanthomas
Brain MRI showing symmetrical T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in dentate nucleus and cerebellar white matter
Progressive cognitive decline with amnestic mild cognitive impairment
Generalized hyperreflexia and bilateral Babinski signs
Bilateral cataracts
Osteoporosis
Intermittent diarrhea
Onset of neurological symptoms in early 30s with childhood ADHD-like traits
optional_findings: Normal EEG and nerve conduction studies

provenance.title: Case report: Cerebrotendinous Xanthomatosis masquerading as adult ADHD in psychiatric practice

challenge_prompt:
A 9-year-old child with a history of celiac disease and dermatitis herpetiformis presented to the emergency room with a 2-month history of vomiting and loose stools, and a recent seizure lasting 20 minutes. On examination, the child appeared pale, emaciated, and drowsy, with a generalized rash. Glasgow Coma Scale was 10/15. Increased muscle tone and heightened reflexes were noted. The patient was tachycardic and hypertensive, with oxygen saturation of 65%. Supplemental oxygen was started, and intravenous ceftriaxone, acyclovir, and hydralazine were administered. The patient was transferred to the pediatric intensive care unit, intubated, and a CT brain showed intraparenchymal and subarachnoid hemorrhages. Subsequent MRI of the brain demonstrated multifocal areas of parenchymal hemorrhages in the supratentorial region, predominantly at the gray-white matter junctions in the bilateral parieto-occipital and parasagittal frontal regions, with subarachnoid hemorrhage components. MRA and MRV were unremarkable, with no significant vessel stenosis or filling defects.

Based on the presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?
gold_diagnosis: Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome (PRES)

answer_key.diagnosis: Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome (PRES)
answer_key.etiology: Multifactorial, likely precipitated by acute hypertension in the setting of underlying celiac disease and pro-inflammatory state, leading to endothelial dysfunction and vasogenic edema.
answer_key.next_management_step: Aggressive blood pressure control (reduce mean arterial pressure by 25% from baseline) and seizure management with anticonvulsants, while addressing underlying triggers (e.g., infection, autoimmune activity).

challenge_prompt: A 5-month-old male infant, delivered at term by cesarean section to a healthy mother, had been followed since a prenatal ultrasound at 19 weeks of gestation revealed cystic dilation of the fourth ventricle, borderline ventriculomegaly (9 mm bilaterally), and absence of the cerebellar vermis. The family history was negative for congenital anomalies, neurological disorders, or genetic syndromes. At delivery, the infant weighed 3.3 kg, presented with a weak cry and signs of respiratory distress, and had Apgar scores of 7 at 1 minute and 8 at 5 minutes. He was admitted to the NICU for further evaluation and management. Physical examination revealed a head circumference above the 95th percentile, an anterior fontanelle approximately four fingers wide, tachypnea with mild subcostal retractions, generalized hypotonia, a weak Moro reflex, diminished sucking reflex, recurrent hiccups, and occasional suspected seizures. Cardiovascular examination was initially unremarkable, but a mild systolic murmur was later detected. The infant was placed on 2 L/min of nasal cannula oxygen to maintain SpO₂ ≥ 92%, and nasogastric feeding was initiated. A comprehensive laboratory panel, including a full septic workup and serial labs, yielded unremarkable results. Cardiac evaluation identified a small muscular ventricular septal defect with a left-to-right shunt and a patent foramen ovale, both hemodynamically insignificant. Renal ultrasound was normal. A non-contrast brain CT demonstrated cystic dilation of the posterior fossa, absence of the cerebellar vermis, enlargement of the fourth ventricle, and positioning of the straight sinus and torcula superior to the lambdoid suture. There was moderate dilation of the right lateral ventricle without transependymal CSF permeation. No intracranial hemorrhage, midline shift, or white-gray matter differentiation abnormalities were observed. Intracranial pressure remained persistently elevated. Based on the presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?
gold_diagnosis: Dandy-Walker Malformation

answer_key.diagnosis: Dandy-Walker Malformation
answer_key.etiology: Congenital malformation of the posterior fossa with cerebellar vermis hypoplasia/agenesis, cystic dilation of the fourth ventricle, and enlarged posterior fossa; often sporadic, can be associated with genetic syndromes or chromosomal anomalies.
answer_key.next_management_step: Placement of a ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt to relieve hydrocephalus and elevated intracranial pressure.

challenge_prompt:
A 71-year-old right-handed man with a history of hypertension and diabetes mellitus presented to the emergency department with 4 days of sudden-onset urinary incontinence, inability to speak or understand speech, and involuntary movements of his right upper limb. Neurological examination: alert, but mixed aphasia; right central facial palsy; mild weakness of right upper limb (remaining limbs normal); Babinski sign present on the right; normal sensory function and deep tendon reflexes. Continuous involuntary movements of the right hand were observed, which seemed against his will; his left hand frequently attempted to grasp or restrain his right hand. Brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed acute infarction involving the left frontoparietal lobe, cingulate gyrus, and corpus callosum. Computed tomography angiography (CTA) demonstrated occlusion of the left anterior cerebral artery. Electrocardiogram and complete blood count and coagulation studies were unremarkable.

Based on the information available at this point, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next most appropriate step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Alien hand syndrome (callosal subtype) secondary to acute ischemic stroke from left anterior cerebral artery occlusion

answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate secondary stroke prevention with dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin and clopidogrel) and high‑dose statin (atorvastatin); consider memantine for aphasia, as the patient is beyond the thrombolysis/thrombectomy window.

challenge_prompt:
We recorded neural data from a 57-year-old right-handed engineer and avid skier with a 10-year history of a progressive neurodegenerative movement disorder that began with reduced right arm swing. He had motor fluctuations, rigidity, bilateral tremor (right>left), and gait disorder. Motor severity scores, off and on medication, were 34 and 9 respectively. Lateralized motor scores during his off state were 10 for the left and 17 for the right body, indicating that the left brain and right body were more affected. Recordings were performed using two bidirectional implanted pulse generators (IPGs) (Medtronic model Summit RC + S), each connected to an ipsilateral subthalamic cylindrical lead (Medtronic model 3389) and an ipsilateral subdural paddle-type lead (Medtronic model Resume II) over the sensorimotor cortex, approximately 25 mm from the midline. Two-channel time series of bipolar local field potential recordings from the dorsal subthalamic nucleus and motor cortex, sampled at 500 Hz, were wirelessly transferred from each IPG to a tablet computer. The tablet computer was carried in a small backpack by the skier during all recordings. Successful data streaming was confirmed on the tablet computer before and after each skiing run, and recording sessions were kept under 10 min. Recordings were done in two different settings (i) in-clinic 10 days after surgery, on-medication, during a computer-controlled arm movement task consisting of a touchscreen-based center-out reaching task. Each trial began with a 'hold' period of 5–7 s, during which the subject was instructed to rest their hand on a desk while maintaining gaze on a central target. The patient was then instructed to touch the target with his index finger, performing a continuous movement lasting approximately 1–2 s; and (ii) outdoors, 6 weeks after surgery, during downhill skiing and at rest on the chairlift, off-medication (4 h after early morning dose) and on-medication (30 min after habitual dose). During the ski runs, the patient made continuous wide turns, occasionally stopping for about 30 s between turns. Two skiing runs and one chairlift resting period were recorded for each medication state. Each skiing run lasted approximately 4–5 min, while each resting period on the chairlift lasted approximately 4 min.

All recordings were performed prior to initiation of chronic therapeutic stimulation. While skiing, video was aligned to neural activity by using a synchronization trigger that created a variable pulse width analog signal that was captured by both IPGs and recorded by the camera. Data were analyzed in 30 s segments, and segments containing movement artifacts were manually removed after visual inspection. We calculated power spectral density (PSD) and coherence using the multitaper method (1,000 ms windows with 500 ms overlap, 3 tapers and a time-half bandwidth product of 5). For the in-clinic movement task, 40 trials were aligned relative to the onset of movement and averaged. The averaged amplitude was normalized by the mean over the 1 s window prior to cue presentation. Data were z-scored by subtracting the average baseline amplitude and dividing by the baseline standard deviation, separately for each frequency. The postoperative localization of subthalamic electrodes was performed using Lead-DBS toolbox and integrated FieldTrip-SimBio pipeline in MATLAB.
gold_diagnosis: Parkinson's disease

answer_key.diagnosis: Parkinson's disease
answer_key.etiology: Neurodegenerative disorder involving loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate chronic deep brain stimulation (DBS) therapy with the implanted neurostimulator.
required_findings: 57-year-old with progressive movement disorder beginning with right arm swing
motor fluctuations, rigidity, bilateral tremor (right>left), gait disorder
off-medication motor severity score 34, on-medication 9 (dramatic levodopa response)
asymmetric motor signs: off-state lateralized scores left body 10, right body 17
subthalamic and cortical leads implanted for DBS
recordings performed prior to initiation of chronic therapeutic stimulation
optional_findings: use of bidirectional IPGs Medtronic Summit RC+S and cylindrical/paddle leads
data streaming during downhill skiing and chairlift rest
postoperative CT/MRI fusion for lead localization

challenge_prompt:
A 45-year-old married Caucasian female presents with recurrent generalized tonic-clonic seizures. She was first diagnosed with depression at age 18 and over the years received escitalopram, trazodone, and fluoxetine. Her mood had been stable until recently, when she began having seizures that did not respond to multiple antiepileptic medications. A sleep-deprived EEG captured one of these episodes and showed no abnormal electrical activity. She also had an episode of rapidly resolving speech deficits and loss of peripheral vision, for which she was diagnosed with optic neuropathy. Brain MRI was within normal limits.

The burden of seizures triggered a recurrence of severe depression, with poor sleep, decreased interest, decreased energy, poor concentration, decreased appetite with weight loss from 260 to 191 lbs, psychomotor retardation, crying spells, and hopelessness (she felt like a burden on her family). Her primary care physician prescribed fluoxetine and alprazolam without significant benefit.

She was admitted to an inpatient psychiatric facility for suicidal ideation and stabilized on fluoxetine 60 mg daily, trazodone 150 mg at night, and hydroxyzine 50 mg twice daily as needed. After discharge, seizures continued; she was readmitted following a suicide attempt by overdose. She was discharged on fluoxetine 80 mg daily, quetiapine 100 mg at night, and clonazepam 0.25 mg twice daily. Despite these changes, she reported worsening depression and frequent generalized tonic-clonic seizures.

She was evaluated by an outpatient psychiatrist, to whom she disclosed a history of severe emotional and physical abuse by her ex-husband. She reported ongoing trauma-related symptoms, including avoidance behaviors, heightened awareness, startle response, and nightmares. She was given an additional diagnosis of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) and referred for regular therapy sessions. Bupropion 300 mg daily was added to her regimen. Given her history of poor response to more than two antidepressants, she meets criteria for treatment-resistant depression (TRD).

What is the most likely diagnosis for her seizure episodes, and what is the most appropriate next management step?
gold_diagnosis: Psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES) / functional seizures

answer_key.diagnosis: Psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES) / functional seizures
answer_key.etiology: Psychological distress and trauma, in the context of major depressive disorder (treatment-resistant depression) and post-traumatic stress disorder; the seizures are a physical manifestation of psychological conflict or stress, not due to abnormal electrical brain activity.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate psychotherapy, specifically cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) for PNES; avoid antiepileptic medications. Continue management of underlying depression and PTSD. For treatment-resistant depression, consider further options such as esketamine or electroconvulsive therapy if standard antidepressants remain ineffective.

Generalized tonic-clonic seizures unresponsive to multiple antiepileptic medications
Normal brain MRI
History of severe emotional and physical abuse and PTSD symptoms (avoidance, heightened awareness, startle response, nightmares)
Recurrent severe depression with weight loss from 260 to 191 lbs, psychomotor retardation, crying spells, hopelessness
Episode of rapidly resolving speech deficits and loss of peripheral vision (other functional neurological symptoms)
Seizures triggered recurrence of depression
Met criteria for treatment-resistant depression (failed at least two antidepressants)
optional_findings: Depression diagnosed at 18, prior treatment with escitalopram, trazodone, fluoxetine
Inpatient psychiatric hospitalization for suicidal ideation and later suicide attempt
Current regimen details: fluoxetine 80 mg, quetiapine 100 mg, clonazepam 0.25 mg, bupropion 300 mg
Primary care prescription of fluoxetine and alprazolam without benefit
Optic neuropathy diagnosed with loss of peripheral vision

challenge_prompt:
A 9-year-old Bangladeshi boy presents with a few months of blurry distant vision in both eyes. His father reports that he has watched television from a close distance and has had a history of convulsions several times since age 4, with unknown frequency. He has irregular follow-up and no documented medical history. Prenatal, perinatal, and vaccination histories are unremarkable. He has no developmental delay but has limited, disconnected speech, indicative of intellectual handicap, and is unable to attend school. He is the only child of non-consanguineous parents, with no family history of neurological or skin disorders.

On examination, the boy is anxious and irritable. There are numerous facial angiomas (adenoma sebaceum) over the face and forehead. Further skin examination reveals an ash leaf spot over the right hypochondriac region and multiple Shagreen patches on the back.

Ophthalmic evaluation reveals best-corrected visual acuity of 6/6 in both eyes with mild myopic correction, intraocular pressure of 14 mmHg, and no anterior segment abnormalities. Dilated fundus examination shows a retinal astrocytic hamartoma approximately 2 mm × 2.5 mm above the optic disc in the left eye.

Neurological examination shows normal tone and reflexes, with bilateral negative Babinski sign. Other systems are unremarkable.

Laboratory investigations including complete blood count, serum electrolytes, renal and liver function tests are within normal limits. Echocardiography and abdominal ultrasonography show no abnormalities.

Axial MRI of the brain shows multiple well-circumscribed cortical lesions that are hypointense on T1, hyperintense on T2 and FLAIR, consistent with cortical tubers. There are multiple subependymal nodules (SENs) along the ventricular surfaces of the lateral ventricles, which are hyperintense on T1 and iso- to hyperintense on T2 and FLAIR. FLAIR images also demonstrate small, cyst-like, well-demarcated hyperintense lesions near the lateral ventricles, suggestive of white matter lesions (WMLs), which are hypointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2. Additionally, there are thin, straight bands of radial linear WMLs, hyperintense on T2 and iso- to hypointense on T1.

Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?
gold_diagnosis: Tuberous sclerosis complex

answer_key.diagnosis: Tuberous sclerosis complex
answer_key.etiology: Autosomal dominant mutation in TSC1 or TSC2 genes
answer_key.next_management_step: Referral to neurology for seizure management and multidisciplinary care, including genetic counseling

Ash leaf spot over the right hypochondriac region

Brain MRI: multiple subependymal nodules (hyperintense on T1, iso- to hyperintense on T2/FLAIR) along lateral ventricles
Brain MRI: white matter lesions (cyst-like hyperintense on FLAIR, hypointense on T1) and radial linear WMLs

challenge_prompt:
A 35-year-old male with a history of chronic insomnia, fatigue, and emotional stress presented with a one-week history of severe headaches, generalized arthralgias, and myalgias. He was initially diagnosed with an upper respiratory tract infection and treated with IV fluids, which provided temporary relief. He later developed a severe, throbbing headache with visual disturbances, photophobia, and phonophobia; neurological examination was unremarkable, and a brain MRI showed nonspecific findings. He was diagnosed with complex migraine and discharged with temporary improvement.

However, his symptoms recurred and progressively worsened over the following days, with new onset of difficulty sleeping, tongue heaviness, hypoactivity, numbness in the right hand, bluish discoloration of the lips, speech difficulties, and episodes of disorientation. On the day of admission, he had a fever (38.2°C), tachycardia (104 bpm), blood pressure 133/80 mmHg, oxygen saturation 95%. He was confused, hypoactive, and poorly responsive, with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 10/15. Physical examination revealed swelling in the right eye, tachycardia, and decreased breath sounds. Neck motion elicited pain, suggesting meningeal irritation.

An initial brain CT scan and limited brain MRI did not reveal acute ischemia or hemorrhage. A follow-up brain MRI showed newly developed areas of high T2-weighted fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) signal intensities in the left hemisphere, right parietal, and temporal lobes, as well as mild leptomeningeal enhancement.

Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis revealed elevated protein levels and lymphocytic pleocytosis. PCR testing for viral pathogens, including herpes simplex virus, was negative. CSF cultures showed no bacterial growth. A pathergy test and eye examination were normal.

Based on the clinical presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM)

answer_key.diagnosis: Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM)
answer_key.etiology: Post-viral immune-mediated demyelination (suspected viral trigger)
answer_key.next_management_step: High-dose intravenous methylprednisolone (e.g., 20–30 mg/kg/day, max 1 g/day, for 3–5 days)

challenge_prompt:
A 6-month-old female infant, born to a nonconsanguineous Chinese couple, presented with profound global developmental delay, intractable epilepsy, and progressive microcephaly. The family history was unremarkable, with a healthy older sibling.

Prenatal course: Ultrasound at 24 weeks gestation detected microcephaly and cleft palate. Fetal MRI at 24 weeks showed no brain structural abnormalities. Amniocentesis revealed a normal karyotype and absence of pathogenic copy number variants on chromosomal microarray analysis. Serial ultrasound monitoring confirmed progressive microcephaly at 27 and 30 weeks gestation. At 35 weeks, intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR) was detected together with diminished peak systolic velocity in the middle cerebral artery.

Birth: Vaginal delivery at 37 weeks. Birth weight 2.4 kg (−2.5 SD), length 45.0 cm (−3.2 SD), head circumference 30.0 cm (−3.5 SD).

Postnatal course: At 3 months of age the infant exhibited profound global developmental delay, failing to achieve head control. She had hypotonia and feeding difficulties. Anthropometric measurements showed weight 4.74 kg (−2.8 SD), length 55 cm (−2.3 SD), and head circumference 32 cm (<−6 SD). At 4 months she developed frequent seizures, each consisting of upturned eyes, clenched teeth, clenched fists and twitching limbs, lasting 1–5 minutes with 5–10 episodes daily. Electroencephalography showed extensive abnormalities with remarkable epileptic discharge, including large amounts of multifocal low-medium-voltage sharp spikes and polyspike-and-slow waves. Seizures were not controlled with levetiracetam and sodium valproate. Brain MRI performed at 4 months revealed corpus callosum hypoplasia, cerebellar hypoplasia, delayed myelination, cortical atrophy, enlarged ventricles and gyral simplification. The infant made no eye contact and did not achieve any developmental milestones. She died at 6 months of age from respiratory failure and status epilepticus.

Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next best diagnostic step?

Whole exome sequencing revealed a novel homozygous variant c.4 T > G (p.Cys2Gly) in ASNS.
gold_diagnosis: Asparagine synthetase deficiency (ASNSD)

answer_key.diagnosis: Asparagine synthetase deficiency (ASNSD)
answer_key.etiology: Biallelic pathogenic variants in the ASNS gene; autosomal recessive inheritance.
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform genetic testing (whole exome sequencing or targeted ASNS gene sequencing) to confirm the diagnosis.

challenge_prompt:
On November 7th, a 71-year-old male with a history of Parkinson’s disease, depression, and somatization disorder was admitted to the emergency department in a comatose state. His regular medications included idebenone 30 mg three times daily, escitalopram 10 mg once daily, trazodone 50 mg nightly, levodopa/carbidopa 250 mg nightly, levodopa/benserazide 125 mg three times daily, pramipexole 0.5 mg three times daily, and clonazepam 1 mg nightly. He had no history of hypertension, diabetes, smoking, or alcohol use. According to his family, the patient was suspected to have ingested over 30 tablets (1 mg) of clonazepam, and he was found unconscious approximately 2.5 hours later.

Upon arrival, gastric lavage was attempted twice but was unsuccessful due to both attempts entering the respiratory tract and was abandoned. Laboratory evaluation showed no abnormalities in blood counts, CRP, coagulation profile, electrolytes, liver and renal function. Brain MRI revealed no abnormalities. Vital signs were: body temperature 36.5°C, respiratory rate 18/min, heart rate 72 bpm, blood pressure 110/73 mmHg, oxygen saturation 99%. Flumazenil 0.01 mg/kg was administered intravenously, but the patient remained comatose. The family declined renal replacement therapy at that time.

The next day (November 8th), the patient developed a fever of 39.5°C. Investigations showed: WBC 10.92 x10⁹/L (ref. 3.5–9.5), CRP 13.2 mg/L (ref. 0–8), positive influenza B IgM. Chest CT revealed lung inflammation. Serum toxicology levels were: clonazepam 121 ng/mL (ref. 0–20), alprazolam 135 ng/mL (ref. 0–20), olanzapine 758 ng/mL (ref. 0–100). The patient was transferred to the ICU.

On the following day (November 9th), coagulation studies showed: PT 20.30 s (ref. 11.8–15.1), aPTT 45.60 s (ref. 28–42.5), fibrinogen 4.39 g/L (ref. 2–4), D-dimer 2.47 μg/mL (ref. 0–0.5), fibrin degradation products 9.56 μg/mL (ref. 0–5.0). A sputum culture grew Acinetobacter baumannii; susceptibility testing indicated sensitivity to tigecycline and minocycline.

What are the most likely diagnoses, and what is the most appropriate next management step?
gold_diagnosis: Mixed drug overdose (clonazepam, alprazolam, olanzapine) complicated by Acinetobacter baumannii pneumonia and mild coagulopathy.

answer_key.diagnosis: Mixed drug overdose (clonazepam, alprazolam, olanzapine) complicated by Acinetobacter baumannii pneumonia and mild coagulopathy.
answer_key.etiology: Self-ingestion of supratherapeutic doses of clonazepam, alprazolam, and olanzapine leading to prolonged CNS depression, with secondary hospital-acquired pneumonia from Acinetobacter baumannii.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate targeted antibiotic therapy for Acinetobacter pneumonia (e.g., tigecycline or minocycline based on susceptibility) and start renal replacement therapy (hemodialysis) to enhance clearance of the overdosed drugs, alongside continued supportive care.

Serum alprazolam 135 ng/mL (ref. 0–20)

challenge_prompt: A 55-year-old nonsmoker, nonalcoholic male with type 2 diabetes mellitus, chronic vertigo, and a prior mid‑brain ischemic stroke 6 years ago (residual left‑side weakness) presented to the emergency room with a 2‑day history of visual disturbance in the left eye, burning sensation in the left hemi‑body, occipital headache, polyuria, severe dry mouth, vomiting, productive yellow cough, fever, and abdominal pain. On admission: temperature 38.0°C, blood pressure 121/74 mm Hg, pulse 110 bpm regular, respiratory rate 25/min, oxygen saturation 70% on room air, BMI 34.39. Physical exam: confused, fruity odor on breath, coarse crackles at both lung bases. Neurologic exam: GCS 14/15, upper and lower limb weakness, diplopia, left homonymous hemianopsia; pupils equal and reactive, right‑side reflexes +3, left‑side +1, meningeal signs and Babinski negative. Initial laboratory tests were consistent with diabetic ketoacidosis (markedly elevated blood glucose, ketonemia, metabolic acidosis). Chest X‑ray showed mild basilar right opacities suggestive of aspiration pneumonia. Electrocardiogram demonstrated sinus tachycardia. Non‑contrast brain CT at admission revealed global sulci widening with infarctions and foci in the right posterior and middle cerebral artery territories (temporal and occipital lobes), and bilateral calcifications along both vertebral arteries. Magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) disclosed stenosis of the right posterior cerebral artery and an anomalous artery connecting the two vertebral arteries at the level of the medulla. Prior MRI from 6 years earlier confirmed an old mid‑brain infarct. Based on the above, what is the most likely underlying etiology for the recurrent posterior circulation strokes, and what is the next appropriate management step?
gold_diagnosis: Recurrent posterior circulation ischemic strokes due to a rare congenital vertebral artery anomaly (V4 segment connecting artery) and decompensated diabetic ketoacidosis

answer_key.diagnosis: Recurrent posterior circulation ischemic strokes due to a rare congenital vertebral artery anomaly (V4 segment connecting artery) and decompensated diabetic ketoacidosis
aliases: Congenital vascular malformation of the vertebro‑basilar system
answer_key.etiology: Hemodynamically significant anomalous artery connecting both vertebral arteries at the medullary level leads to turbulent flow, accelerated atherosclerosis, and recurrent embolic/thrombotic events in the posterior circulation, compounded by diabetic ketoacidosis as an acute precipitant.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate aspirin 81 mg daily and atorvastatin 40 mg daily, manage diabetic ketoacidosis with intravenous insulin and fluids, treat aspiration pneumonia with appropriate antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone), and consider long‑term anticoagulation (later rivaroxaban); obtain vascular surgery consultation for the anomalous vessel.

challenge_prompt:
A 45-year-old woman presented with a 2-month history of tingling pain in the left middle and ring fingers. Symptoms were aggravated at and after work (assembly line packing 5-6 kg boxes) and partially relieved by rest. Initially intermittent, the symptoms became constant, interfering with sleep. She had two steroid injections at the carpal tunnel with partial and temporary relief lasting less than a week.

Examination: positive Phalen sign on the left, no Tinel sign at the wrist, mild paresthesia at the volar tips of the left index, middle, and ring fingers. Sensory and motor function of ulnar and radial nerves intact. No atrophy or weakness. During manipulation of the arm to position for nerve conduction study, she experienced shooting pain from the medial arm through the anterior forearm to the middle finger. Additional history revealed mild left medial arm pain just proximal to the elbow over several months. Palpation revealed an intense tender point with a positive Tinel sign over the median nerve at the left medial distal arm.

Electrodiagnostic testing: Distal motor and sensory nerve conduction studies of the median, ulnar, and radial nerves were normal, including sensitive tests for carpal tunnel syndrome. Needle electromyography of the pronator teres, flexor pollicis longus, pronator quadratus, and abductor pollicis brevis showed no abnormalities. An inching study of the median nerve with recording at the abductor pollicis brevis was performed in 2-cm segments along the distal arm. A segment located 3–5 cm proximal to the elbow crease demonstrated a decreased conduction velocity of 27 m/s, while adjacent segments had a conduction velocity of 64 m/s.

A quick ultrasonographic evaluation at the site revealed an echogenic structure superficial to the median nerve and brachial artery.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next best step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Supracondylar process syndrome (median nerve compression by supracondylar process of the humerus)

answer_key.diagnosis: Supracondylar process syndrome (median nerve compression by supracondylar process of the humerus)
aliases: Median nerve entrapment by supracondylar process
answer_key.etiology: Compression of the median nerve by a bony supracondylar process (anatomic variant) of the distal humerus, possibly with an associated ligament of Struthers.
answer_key.next_management_step: Obtain plain radiographs of the left humerus/elbow to visualize the supracondylar process and confirm the diagnosis.

Positive Phalen sign.
Tender point and positive Tinel sign at the left medial distal arm.
Normal distal median motor and sensory nerve conduction studies and negative sensitive tests for carpal tunnel syndrome.
Normal needle electromyography in median-innervated forearm muscles including pronator teres.
Inching study reveals focal conduction slowing (27 m/s) in a 2-cm segment 3–5 cm proximal to the elbow crease, with adjacent normal conduction (64 m/s).
Ultrasonography shows an echogenic structure superficial to the median nerve and brachial artery at that level.
optional_findings: History of work-related repetitive manual labor (packing boxes).
Temporary partial relief from carpal tunnel steroid injections.
Shooting pain provoked by arm manipulation.
Mild chronic medial arm pain proximal to the elbow.
No Tinel sign at the wrist.
No sensory or motor deficits in ulnar/radial nerves.

challenge_prompt:
A 37-year-old man presented with a 6-month history of sudden onset weakness of right wrist dorsiflexion and finger extension. He initially had no pain, but developed pain in the extensor side of the upper arm after 1 month. On examination, right wrist volar-flexion was 5/5, dorsiflexion was 2/5 with radial deviation. Metacarpophalangeal joint flexion was 5/5, extension was 0/5. Right forearm supination was 4/5. Tendon reflexes were normal at the biceps brachii but decreased at the right triceps brachii. Sensory examination revealed hypoesthesia over the dorsum of the radial side of the right hand. Nerve conduction studies showed that sensory nerve action potential and compound muscle action potential of the right radial nerve were not evoked. Needle electromyography revealed spontaneous denervation potentials and neuropathic motor unit action potentials (MUAPs) on volition in the right brachioradialis, extensor carpi radialis longus, and supinator. Denervation potentials were seen in the right extensor digitorum communis, extensor indicis proprius, and extensor carpi ulnaris; no volitional MUAPs were noted in these muscles. The triceps muscle showed normal MUAPs without denervation potentials. MRI of the right arm demonstrated a well-defined, intra-neural, round mass measuring about 0.8 cm × 0.5 cm within the radial nerve. The mass exhibited iso-intensity on T1-weighted images and slight hyper-intensity on T2-weighted images compared to adjacent muscles, with homogeneous signal intensity on both. On T1-weighted images with fat saturation and contrast enhancement, the mass showed homogeneous mild enhancement. A well-demarcated hypointense rim surrounded the mass on both T1- and T2-weighted images.

Histopathological examination of the excised mass revealed a well-demarcated intraneural leiomyoma, composed of smooth muscle cells without atypia, and immunohistochemistry was positive for smooth muscle actin and desmin.

answer_key.diagnosis: Intraneural leiomyoma of the radial nerve
answer_key.etiology: Benign smooth muscle tumor arising from vascular smooth muscle within the nerve
answer_key.next_management_step: Surgical exploration with complete excision and nerve grafting as necessary

Intraneural mass with homogeneous iso-to-hyperintensity and hypointense rim on MRI

Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

A 31-year-old previously healthy female presented to a community health center in the Napu Valley, Central Sulawesi, Indonesia, with tonic-clonic convulsions. She had a 6-month history of headaches not relieved by an analgesic prescribed by a midwife. She reported working on a plantation for 15 years; the plantation is a habitat for snails, intermediate hosts for Schistosoma species. A CT scan of the head showed a hypodense area in the left parieto-occipital region with perifocal edema. CSF analysis was not performed.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

A 64-year-old female with a university degree was hospitalized because of a year-long progression of walking difficulties, which had intensified over the past 6 months. She was fully conscious, communicated effectively, exhibited no speech impairment, and could walk independently. She presented with a slightly wide-based gait and impaired mobility alongside a 10-year history of auditory impairment; tinnitus had gradually developed 3 years prior. She had suffered a traumatic lumbar fracture in her mid-30s, underwent surgical intervention. The patient was admitted for additional diagnostic evaluation.

Neurological examination: Hyperactive tendon reflexes in all four limbs. Bilateral alternating movement, heel-knee-tibia, and finger-nose tests were inaccurate. Romberg’s sign was positive. Maintaining a straight line while walking was difficult; broad-based gait observed. Hoffmann’s sign was positive, whereas Oppenheim’s, Gordon’s, Chaddock’s, and bilateral Babinski signs were negative. Upper-limb postural tremors were observed.

Cognitive assessment: Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) score 26. Laboratory analyses revealed normal results.

Pure tone audiometry: profound sensorineural hearing impairment in the right ear; left ear normal. Video head impulse testing: positive for the left horizontal canal without evidence of otolith involvement. Electromyography: damage to the left ulnar nerve at the elbow level and right median nerve at the wrist suggestive of mild carpal tunnel syndrome; decreased sensory nerve action potential amplitude in the right ulnar nerve. No evidence of polyneuropathy.

Cervical and craniocerebral MRI: extensive hemosiderin deposition, highlighting involvement of the left temporal lobe, brainstem, and cervical pulp. Hemosiderin deposits were visible on standard T2-weighted MRI of the cervical spinal cord as linear hypointensities. Susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) enhanced visibility of these signals across the left temporal lobe, bilateral insula, temporal cortex sulci, and the brainstem. CT angiography of the head and neck showed no anomalies. Chest, abdominal, and pelvic CT showed no abnormalities.

The patient refused all invasive procedures such as lumbar puncture, CT myelography, or lumbar vascular digital subtraction angiography.
gold_diagnosis: Central nervous system superficial siderosis (classical type)

answer_key.diagnosis: Central nervous system superficial siderosis (classical type)
answer_key.etiology: Chronic subarachnoid hemorrhage (exact source undetermined)
answer_key.next_management_step: Attempt to locate the bleeding source (e.g., digital subtraction angiography) and consider surgical repair; if patient declines or source not found, initiate medical therapy (iron chelation and supportive care).

Chronic progressive sensorineural hearing loss (profound right ear, 10-year history)
Pyramidal signs: hyperactive tendon reflexes in all limbs and positive Hoffmann sign

Absence of large vascular anomalies on CT angiography of head and neck
History of traumatic lumbar fracture and surgery (possible source of chronic bleeding)
optional_findings: Electromyography showing focal peripheral nerve damage (left ulnar, right median) without polyneuropathy
Left ear normal hearing on pure tone audiometry
Normal chest, abdominal, and pelvic CT
Patient refusal of invasive diagnostic procedures
Positive video head impulse test for left horizontal canal without otolith involvement

challenge_prompt:
A 59-year-old female was referred for persistent left limb incoordination of 6 months duration. Six months prior, she suddenly developed inability to lift the left upper limb, inability to grasp, gait instability, nausea, and vomiting. Cranial CT showed right thalamic hemorrhage. After acute management, she regained ability to move the left limbs and walk independently, but incoordination persisted, especially in the upper limb. Despite 5 months of systematic rehabilitation, she remained unable to perform daily activities like holding a cup or eating with a spoon using the left hand. Past medical history: hypertension, not systematically treated, maximum recorded BP 185/110 mm Hg. No diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or peripheral vascular disease. challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most appropriate next step in management?

In March 2023, a 1-year-old Chinese male infant was admitted due to frequent seizures and developmental delay. Since birth, he had gradual developmental delay. At age 1 year, he started experiencing two types of seizures. One type: limb stiffness, unresponsiveness, staring, cyanosis, without limb tremors or drooling; each episode ~20 seconds, about twice daily. Second type: nodding or cessation of movement in clusters, each cluster 10 or more episodes, lasting seconds to minutes, up to 7–8 times per day. He could sit alone but not crawl, walk, or speak words. Gesell Developmental Scale showed moderate delays in adaptive behavior, language, personal-social, gross motor, fine motor. Physical exam: mild hypotonia in extremities. Lab tests: blood glucose, plasma ammonia, lactic acid, electrolytes, blood and urine metabolic screening all normal. Cranial MRI: reduced medullary volume, fewer gyri, focal cortical thickening in both hemispheres, consistent with malformations of cortical development. EEG: interictal discharges in right parietal, occipital, meso-posterior temporal regions; recorded epileptic spasms and focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizures. Seizure types classified as epileptic spasms and focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizures; epilepsy syndrome diagnosed as infantile epileptic spasms syndrome (IESS). He was treated with topiramate, ACTH, levetiracetam, and sodium valproate sequentially, but seizures remained refractory, with persistent daily clusters. He is now 2 years old and continues to have frequent seizures.

Genetic testing revealed a novel heterozygous missense variant in the DYNC1H1 gene.

answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate ketogenic diet

Failure of multiple antiseizure medications (topiramate, ACTH, levetiracetam, sodium valproate)
Ongoing refractory daily seizures at age 2 years

Normal blood glucose, ammonia, lactic acid, electrolytes

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

On January 28, 2024, a 46-year-old Chinese woman with no prior history of mental illness presented with abnormal behavior, cognitive impairment, altered consciousness, and epileptic seizures. Thirteen days prior to admission, she exhibited unexplained crying, nonsensical speech, irritability, and physical aggression. She was treated at a psychiatric facility with oral medication, but there was no improvement. Seven days prior to admission, she developed fever and unresponsiveness, progressing to impaired consciousness, accompanied by facial twitching, a rightward gaze, stiff limbs, and intermittent involuntary shaking of the upper limbs. On examination, she had increased muscle tone in the limbs, positive bilateral Babinski signs, and did not cooperate with the remainder of the exam.

Initial complete blood count showed elevated white blood cell counts. CSF analysis revealed a significant increase in WBC count, predominantly lymphocytes. Rheumatologic and immunological markers, as well as tumor markers, were normal. High-throughput sequencing of CSF for pathogenic microorganisms detected Epstein-Barr virus (sequence number 31, confidence 99%) and herpes simplex virus type 1 (sequence number 3, confidence 70%). Tests for HIV, SARS-CoV-2, influenza A/B, Treponema pallidum, Mycobacterium tuberculosis, Brucella, Aspergillus, and other pathogens were negative.

Initial autoimmune encephalitis antibodies and paraneoplastic antibody tests (performed on January 30, 2024) were negative. EEG showed moderate abnormalities, with background beta wave changes during coma and frequent partial seizures originating from the left frontal pole and anterior temporal region. Electroneurography revealed abnormal H-reflex responses in both tibial nerves and abnormal F-wave patterns in bilateral median and tibial nerves. Cranial MRI (performed twice) showed no abnormalities. CT of the chest and abdomen and ultrasound of the reproductive system showed no tumors.

Based on the symptoms, signs, and auxiliary examinations, a diagnosis of viral encephalitis was suspected. The patient was started on ganciclovir for antiviral therapy, and a combination of sodium valproate and levetiracetam for seizure management, along with antipsychotic medications. After initiating treatment, she had no further epileptic seizures, and follow-up EEGs showed no epileptic activity. However, her consciousness did not improve, involuntary movements increased, and she developed autonomic dysfunction.

Given the lack of improvement with antiviral therapy and the new symptoms, the clinical team suspected a possible alternative diagnosis and considered further workup.

Repeat autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel on February 10, 2024, revealed positive NMDAR antibodies in CSF.
gold_diagnosis: Anti-N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor (NMDAR) encephalitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Anti-N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor (NMDAR) encephalitis
aliases: autoimmune encephalitis associated with NMDAR antibodies
answer_key.etiology: Triggered by Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) and herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1) co-infection
answer_key.next_management_step: Repeat cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and serum testing for anti-NMDAR antibodies; initiate first-line immunotherapy with intravenous methylprednisolone and/or human immunoglobulin.
required_findings: CSF analysis: significant increase in WBC count, predominantly lymphocytes
High-throughput sequencing of CSF detected Epstein-Barr virus (sequence number 31, confidence 99%) and herpes simplex virus type 1 (sequence number 3, confidence 70%)
Initial autoimmune encephalitis antibodies and paraneoplastic antibody tests (January 30, 2024) were negative
Cranial MRI (performed twice) showed no abnormalities
After antiviral treatment, consciousness did not improve, involuntary movements increased, and autonomic dysfunction developed
EEG showed moderate abnormalities with frequent partial seizures from left frontal pole and anterior temporal region
No evidence of tumors on CT chest/abdomen and ultrasound of reproductive system
Despite seizure control, neurologic worsening characterized by increased involuntary movements and dysautonomia
optional_findings: 46-year-old Chinese woman with no prior psychiatric history
Prodromal psychiatric symptoms: unexplained crying, nonsensical speech, irritability, physical aggression
Fever and unresponsiveness 7 days before admission
Facial twitching, rightward gaze, stiff limbs, intermittent shaking of upper limbs
Increased muscle tone in limbs and positive bilateral Babinski signs
Electroneurography: abnormal H-reflexes in both tibial nerves, abnormal F-waves in median and tibial nerves
No further seizures after starting valproate and levetiracetam

challenge_prompt:
A previously healthy 50-year-old man presented in December 2021 with worsening seizures and progressive cognitive impairment. He had experienced his first generalized tonic-clonic seizure during sleep in August 2021; an initial brain MRI and routine EEG were normal. He later had another seizure and was started on levetiracetam without significant improvement, continuing with monthly convulsions and memory decline. He was admitted for further evaluation.

Initial workup: Neurological exam revealed disorientation to time and place. MMSE 22 (normal ≥22), MoCA-B 19 (normal ≥22). Cranial nerves, motor, cerebellar functions normal. Routine labs, autoimmune panels negative. Brain MRI showed bilateral hippocampal hyperintensities, MTA score 1 (slight atrophy). Video EEG showed left temporal spike-wave activity. CSF: normal opening pressure, 4 leukocytes/μL, protein 0.88 g/L (normal 0.2–0.4), glucose 3.72 mmol/L (normal). Autoantibody panel using fixed-cell-based assays (CBA): positive LGI1-IgG in both CSF (1:100) and serum (1:100). Negative for NMDAR, AMPAR1/2, GABAB, GABAA, CASPR2, IgLON5, DPPX, GlyR1, mGluR5, GAD65, AQP4, MOG, GFAP, Yo, Hu, Ri, Ma2, amphiphysin. No evidence of thymoma or other tumor on imaging.

Diagnosis and treatment: The patient was diagnosed with anti-LGI1 encephalitis and treated with high-dose methylprednisolone and intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG). After one month, his cognitive scores improved to MMSE 27, MoCA-B 22, and he was discharged on low-dose oral glucocorticoids.

Three months later, the patient returned with a new complaint of hyperhidrosis. Blood tests, liver/kidney function, systemic autoantibodies, and EEG were unchanged. Brain MRI now showed hippocampal sclerosis (instead of previous hyperintensity). Repeat CSF analysis: protein 1.19 g/L (elevated from prior), glucose 2.98 mmol/L. Repeat antibody testing for neuronal and glial antibodies: LGI1-IgG titers 1:32 in serum, 1:3.2 in CSF (lower than initial). Notably, MOG-IgG was now positive: serum titer 1:32 (dilution 1:10), CSF titer 1:32 (dilution 1:1). No antibodies against NMDAR, AMPAR, GABA, CASPR2, etc. were detected. Whole-spine MRI with T2 Flair showed no abnormal signals. The patient had no symptoms of optic neuritis, myelitis, or other typical MOGAD features.

Based on this new presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step?
gold_diagnosis: Relapse of anti-LGI1 encephalitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Relapse of anti-LGI1 encephalitis
aliases: Recurrence of limbic encephalitis due to LGI1-IgG
Anti-LGI1 encephalitis relapse with incidental MOG-IgG positivity
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune (LGI1-IgG mediated), with secondary low-titer MOG-IgG positivity of uncertain significance (likely false-positive or bystander immune response).
answer_key.next_management_step: Resume high-dose immunosuppression with intravenous methylprednisolone and IVIG (as previously effective).
required_findings: Previously confirmed anti-LGI1 encephalitis with positive LGI1-IgG (CSF 1:100, serum 1:100) and negative MOG-IgG.
Initial response to immunotherapy (cognitive improvement) and later new symptom hyperhidrosis.
New MRI finding of hippocampal sclerosis.
CSF protein elevation to 1.19 g/L (increasing from 0.88).
Persistent LGI1-IgG positivity (serum 1:32, CSF 1:3.2) on repeat testing.
New low-titer MOG-IgG positivity (serum 1:32, CSF 1:32).
Absence of clinical features of MOGAD (optic neuritis, myelitis).
Normal whole-spine MRI (no demyelinating lesions).
optional_findings: Initial bilateral hippocampal hyperintensities on MRI.
Left temporal spike-wave activity on EEG.
MMSE 22, MoCA-B 19 initially, improving to 27 and 22 post-treatment.
No evidence of tumor on initial imaging.
Three-month interval before recurrence of symptoms.

challenge_prompt:
A 14-year-old girl presents with a history of paroxysmal headaches starting at age 5. At age 11, she was admitted due to developmental regression and behavioral changes, including personality shifts, panic attacks with limb shaking, involuntary laughter, prolonged slurred speech, and although logic normalized after remission, speech remained slurred. These symptoms progressively worsened, interfering with daily life and school attendance. At age 14, she began experiencing episodes of sudden loss of consciousness, inability to call out, shaking of the head and upper limbs, or staring without limb twitching, lasting more than 10 seconds and occurring several times per day. She occasionally reports pain at the top of her head but cannot clearly describe its nature.

She was born at term via cesarean section with a normal Apgar score and no perinatal hypoxia. Early development was normal. Her parents are non-consanguineous; her father and 11-year-old brother are healthy. Her mother had a mandibular hemangioma since childhood that gradually enlarged, and at age 20 developed dryness, fatigue, discomfort, swelling, and pain in the left eye with decreased vision; she was diagnosed with glaucoma and cataract, underwent surgery, but is now blind. She also had an intracranial tumor surgically removed (postoperative pathology: microscopically visible vascular malformation in the left temporal lobe).

Physical examination: the patient is conscious, responsive, normally developed, and well nourished. Multiple café au lait spots of varying sizes are scattered on her trunk. No spinal deformity, limbs move freely, joints normal, no lower limb edema. Muscle strength grade V, normal tone, normal abdominal wall reflexes.

At age 11, transcranial Doppler ultrasound suggested increased blood flow velocity in the left internal carotid artery system, raising suspicion of intracranial vascular malformation. Cranial MRI at age 11: T2-weighted imaging showed patchy high signal intensities in the bilateral basal ganglia, thalamus, brainstem, and bilateral cerebellar hemispheres with fuzzy boundaries; no diffusion restriction; no other parenchymal signal abnormalities; ventricular system and midline structures normal. Magnetic resonance angiography showed tortuous intracranial vertebral arteries but normal courses of internal carotid arteries, basilar artery, and anterior/middle/posterior cerebral arteries, with no abnormal vessel clusters. At age 14, MRI again demonstrated patchy T1 low signal and T2 high signal lesions in the bilateral basal ganglia, thalamus, corpus callosum, brainstem, and cerebellum; no diffusion restriction; ventricles, sulci, and midline structures normal; pituitary normal.

Video EEG at age 11 showed abnormal childhood patterns: focal bilateral central, parietal, and central midline spike waves, sharp waves, and spike-and-slow-wave complexes during wakefulness and sleep, with significant sleep activation. At age 14, video EEG was borderline: alpha dissociation in sleep, increased slow-wave activity, normal vertex waves and sleep spindles, and a slow-wave sleep phase noted. A clinical diagnosis of symptomatic epilepsy was made.

Urine organic acid measurements and blood genetic metabolic tests at age 11 were unremarkable; no other laboratory abnormalities were detected.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next most appropriate diagnostic step?
gold_diagnosis: Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) with epilepsy

answer_key.etiology: Heterozygous pathogenic mutation in the NF1 gene (c.6640dupA, p.R2214Kfs*7 inherited from mother; second variant c.7395-3C>G of uncertain significance inherited from father)
answer_key.next_management_step: Genetic testing for NF1 (whole-exome or targeted sequencing of NF1) to confirm the diagnosis and characterize the mutation

Focal seizures (sudden loss of consciousness with head/upper limb shaking or staring lasting >10 sec, several per day)
Video EEG: focal spike-and-sharp-wave and spike-and-slow-wave complexes with sleep activation
Normal urine organic acids and metabolic genetic testing (ruling out many inborn errors of metabolism)
Normal early development with later regression and cognitive/behavioral changes
Transcranial Doppler suspicion of intracranial vascular malformation (left ICA increased flow)

challenge_prompt:
A 32-year-old previously healthy woman was admitted to the hospital because of fever and sleep disturbances, accompanied by memory loss. Ten days before hospitalization, the patient developed a fever, nasal congestion, and a runny nose, along with obvious difficulty in sleeping, generalized weakness, and nighttime irritability. Furthermore, the patient exhibited memory loss of recent events and verbal expression problems 1 week prior to hospitalization. The neurological examination showed fuzzy consciousness, impaired verbal expression, memory deficit for recent events, and slow reaction time, but essentially normal comprehension and computation abilities, as well as normal muscle strength and tone in the extremities. During hospitalization, the fuzzy consciousness and speech disorder of the patient progressively worsened, with the development of involuntary movement of the right limbs. Ultimately, the patient lost the ability to walk and speak. The patient was incapable of cooperating with the Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) and the Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) scale examinations.

Lumbar puncture revealed an initial pressure of 240 mmH2O, with colorless and clear CSF, and normal levels of white cell count (WCC), protein, glucose, and chloride. The albumin quotient (QALB) was 4.11. Serum and CSF tests for autoimmune encephalitis-related antibodies revealed positive anti-AMPAR2 antibodies: serum titer 1:1000+, CSF titer 1:100+. Next-generation sequencing of CSF detected herpes simplex virus (HSV). Brain MRI showed patchy abnormal signals in the left basal ganglia region and swelling in some cerebral gyri of the bilateral cerebral hemispheres, left caudate nucleus head, and bilateral hippocampus. CT scans of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis and whole-body PET/CT showed no evidence of malignancy.

What is the most likely diagnosis? What is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Anti-AMPAR2 encephalitis (autoimmune encephalitis associated with AMPAR2 antibodies, likely triggered by HSV infection)

answer_key.diagnosis: Anti-AMPAR2 encephalitis (autoimmune encephalitis associated with AMPAR2 antibodies, likely triggered by HSV infection)
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune, with autoantibodies targeting the GluA2 subunit of AMPA receptors, possibly triggered by herpes simplex virus infection.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate immunotherapy with high-dose corticosteroids (e.g., intravenous methylprednisolone) and intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG), and start antiviral therapy with intravenous acyclovir. Continue tumor surveillance given the association with malignancies.

challenge_prompt:
An 87-year-old man with a past medical history of lacunar cerebral infarction (on clopidogrel and atorvastatin) and a 50-year history of heavy smoking (at least 50 cigarettes daily) presented after a fall. He reported dizziness and loss of balance, landing on the left parieto-occipital region. He developed headache and vomited twice, without loss of consciousness or seizures. Physical examination showed left parieto-occipital scalp bruising and swelling, pupils equal and reactive (2.5 mm), no neck stiffness, normal limb motor function, and preserved reflexes. Cranial CT demonstrated subdural hemorrhages in the right frontotemporal and left frontotemporal regions, right frontal lobe contusion with hemorrhage, traumatic subarachnoid hemorrhage, fracture of the left parietal bone, and scalp soft tissue swelling. Pulmonary CT showed bilateral pulmonary infection, small right pleural effusion, emphysema, and pleural adhesions. ECG revealed atrial fibrillation. Laboratory tests showed leukocytosis (16.61 ×10⁹/L), neutrophilia (93.11%), normal hemoglobin (145.4 g/L) and platelets (150 ×10⁹/L), mild hypoalbuminemia (33.7 g/L), and otherwise normal coagulation, liver, kidney, cardiac enzymes, tumor markers, and negative infectious serologies. HbA1c was 6.58%, random blood glucose 7.5 mmol/L. He was treated with ceftazidime, nimodipine, mannitol, levetiracetam, and metoprolol, with gradual improvement; follow-up CT showed absorption of hemorrhage with a small residual subdural effusion. After one month, he had clinically recovered with resolution of headache, nausea, vomiting, cough, and sputum, and regained normal mobility.

During his entire hospitalization and over 6-month follow-up, the patient reported no desire to smoke. He did not use any smoking cessation medications or behavioral therapy. Clinical staff and family members noted no signs of nicotine withdrawal, such as irritability, anxiety, restlessness, or somatic symptoms. This cessation was confirmed by the patient and his daughter at 1, 3, and 6 months post-injury. Cranial MRI during follow-up showed right hemispheric malacia, cerebral atrophy, leukoaraiosis; MRA indicated cerebral atherosclerosis.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology underlying the patient’s loss of nicotine dependence, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?
gold_diagnosis: Reduced nicotine dependence following traumatic brain injury (TBI-induced disruption of dopaminergic reward pathways)

answer_key.diagnosis: Reduced nicotine dependence following traumatic brain injury (TBI-induced disruption of dopaminergic reward pathways)
aliases: Spontaneous smoking cessation after traumatic brain injury
answer_key.etiology: Severe traumatic brain injury (subdural hematoma, frontal contusion, subarachnoid hemorrhage, skull fracture) likely disrupted neural circuits involved in reward and craving, particularly mesolimbic dopaminergic pathways, leading to loss of nicotine dependence.
answer_key.next_management_step: Continue monitoring for recurrence of craving or withdrawal; no acute pharmacological intervention is required; consider supportive counseling if needed.
required_findings: Decades-long heavy smoking (≥50 cigarettes/day) indicating severe nicotine dependence
Severe TBI with right frontal lobe contusion, bilateral subdural hematomas, subarachnoid hemorrhage, skull fracture
Spontaneous and complete cessation of cigarette craving post-injury
Absence of nicotine withdrawal symptoms (no irritability, anxiety, restlessness, somatic symptoms)
No pharmacological or behavioral smoking cessation interventions used
Abstinence confirmed by patient and family at 1-, 3-, and 6-month follow-ups
Follow-up MRI showing right hemispheric malacia, cerebral atrophy, leukoaraiosis
Preserved physical recovery with normal mobility
optional_findings: Bilateral pulmonary infection and emphysema on CT
Residual subdural effusion on follow-up CT
Past lacunar infarction on antiplatelet therapy

provenance.title: Reduced nicotine dependence following traumatic brain injury in an elderly patient: a case report and literature review

challenge_prompt: A 77-year-old woman presented with a one-year history of progressive forgetfulness, confusion, difficulty recalling the names of objects (anomia), and an inability to navigate familiar places (e.g., locating the restroom). On examination, she was alert (GCS 15). Cognitive assessment revealed an MMSE score of 4/30 and a Nishimura Dementia Scale score of 25 (severe impairment). Specific deficits included acalculia, agraphia, finger agnosia, and mild left-right disorientation, consistent with incomplete Gerstmann syndrome. Brain CT showed ventricular enlargement with an Evans index of 0.33, widening of the Sylvian fissures, pronounced left posterior dominant ventricular dilation, and bilateral hippocampal atrophy. Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next appropriate management step?

challenge_prompt:
A 75-year-old male patient had recurrent erythematous macules and papules with itching mainly on the flexed side of the body for more than 2 years. Combined history of asthma and allergic rhinitis for more than 20 years. ALS was diagnosed according to the revised El Escorial criteria 3 years prior to presentation; he was currently on riluzole (50 mg), administered twice daily. The pharyngeal reflex was weakened, and the muscle strength of the extremities was 0. The body was covered with dry skin, with a few visible fine white scales; red spots of varying sizes were scattered on the back, and millet grain to mung bean-sized papules were distributed. The erythema on the instep is eroded with yellow exudate and crusts. Laboratory tests revealed that the blood C-reactive protein was 81.92 mg/L (normal: 0–5 mg/L), immunoglobulin E was 258 IU/mL (normal: 0–100 IU/mL), and erythrocyte sedimentation rate was 65 mm/h (normal: 0–15 mm/h). Routine blood tests revealed no abnormalities in the liver and kidney functions, and blood lipids.

Based only on the presentation above, what is the most likely diagnosis for the skin condition, and what is the next best step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Moderate atopic dermatitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Moderate atopic dermatitis
answer_key.etiology: Chronic inflammatory skin disease associated with skin barrier dysfunction and type 2 immune activation, in a patient with a personal history of atopy.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate dupilumab therapy (600 mg loading dose, then 300 mg subcutaneously every 2 weeks) with topical corticosteroids as needed for flares.
