challenge_prompt:
A 58-year-old right-handed, monolingual English-speaking male with a history of recurrent right temporal lobe glioblastoma (MGMT promoter methylated, TERT mutation positive) presented with an intermittent speech disturbance and severe headache. He had undergone a right-sided craniotomy for tumor resection 5 years earlier under general anesthesia without postoperative aphasia, followed by adjuvant radiotherapy and chemotherapy; his last chemotherapy was completed 4 years previously. Surveillance MRIs had been stable until the current presentation. In the month before evaluation, he reported “slurred speech,” left-sided facial drooping, and intermittent twitching of his left upper and lower extremities.

MRI at presentation showed new nodular enhancement in the right temporal lobe measuring 2.5 × 1 cm, with elevated relative cerebral blood volume on perfusion imaging, consistent with disease progression. The enhancement was located near the resection cavity in the right superior temporal lobe along the Sylvian fissure.

Neuropsychology consultation revealed intermittent response latency indicative of effortful lexical retrieval; spontaneous speech and language were otherwise intact. The Edinburgh Handedness Inventory – Short Form (LQ = 75) confirmed right-hand preference, with mixed handedness for sports. There was no history of developmental delay.

Given the recurrent tumor in an eloquent region and the patient’s new speech difficulties, a multidisciplinary preoperative evaluation was performed to guide surgical planning.

**Magnetoencephalography (MEG)**
Receptive language was assessed with a word-listening paradigm: the patient heard five target words and then identified them from a series of 105 written lexical distractors. This paradigm produced significant activation in the right posterior temporal lobe, consistent with Wernicke’s area. Expressive language was assessed with an object-naming task combined with written word matching; this elicited activation in the right hemisphere with language production consistent with Broca’s area. Overall, the MEG scan demonstrated that both the right temporal and right frontal lobes were highly eloquent for language, while some activation was also observed in the left hemisphere.

**High-Definition Fiber Tracking (HDFT)**
Tractography showed potential edema-related distortion of the right arcuate fasciculus but no evidence of tumor infiltration into critical subcortical pathways.

**Neuropsychological Testing**
The Quick Aphasia Battery (QAB) was largely error-free: a single object-naming failure and one minor repetition error on a complex phrase yielded a technically normal score of 9.86. Writing ability was intact. Mild impairments were noted in higher-level executive-linguistic skills and bilateral fine motor skills. Overall, the testing indicated greater decline in verbal compared with non-verbal functions, with mild anomia suggesting possible involvement of semantic language networks.

To establish a baseline for intraoperative monitoring, the patient completed language tasks used during awake surgery. Performance was as follows: picture naming 76/90 (84% correct), word reading 40/40 (100% correct), number reading 30/30 (100% correct), auditory comprehension 84/89 (94% correct). A 25% error-rate threshold was used to determine task viability intraoperatively.
gold_diagnosis: Atypical right-hemisphere language representation (bilateral language dominance) explaining speech disturbance from recurrent glioblastoma in the right temporal lobe

answer_key.diagnosis: Atypical right-hemisphere language representation (bilateral language dominance) explaining speech disturbance from recurrent glioblastoma in the right temporal lobe
answer_key.etiology: Recurrent right temporal glioblastoma causing mass effect and focal seizures, with pre-existing atypical language lateralization
answer_key.next_management_step: Proceed with awake craniotomy and intraoperative language mapping using direct electrical stimulation (DES)
required_findings: Right-handed (EHI-SF LQ = 75) patient with recurrent right temporal glioblastoma
New onset slurred speech, left facial drooping, and left arm/leg twitching
MRI shows 2.5×1 cm nodular enhancement in right superior temporal lobe near Sylvian fissure with elevated rCBV
MEG receptive language paradigm: right posterior temporal activation (Wernicke's area)
MEG expressive language paradigm: right frontal activation (Broca's area)
NPT reveals effortful lexical retrieval and greater verbal than non-verbal deficits, with mild anomia
HDFT shows no tumor infiltration into critical subcortical pathways, making gross total resection feasible
Baseline intraoperative monitoring tasks: picture naming 84% correct, reading 100%, auditory comprehension 94% correct
optional_findings: Prior craniotomy under general anesthesia without aphasia
MGMT promoter methylated, TERT mutation positive
Edema-related distortion of right arcuate fasciculus on HDFT
QAB score 9.86 with single object-naming failure
Mild impairment in higher-level executive-linguistic and bilateral fine motor skills
No developmental delay, high school diploma

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most appropriate deep brain stimulation (DBS) target configuration for this patient?

A 71-year-old male was diagnosed with Parkinson's disease 5 years ago after developing a right upper limb tremor that progressively spread to the right lower limb, left upper limb, and left lower limb, resulting in generalized tremor. His symptoms were initially controlled with carbidopa-levodopa (half tablet daily). Over the past 2 years, his condition worsened. Despite increasing medications to carbidopa-levodopa (half tablet QID), amantadine (1 tablet QN), and pramipexole (1 tablet TID), he developed prominent right-sided limb tremor (6 Hz frequency), left-sided rigidity with tremor (3 Hz frequency), bradykinesia, turning difficulty, impaired nocturnal turning, dysphagia, and medication-induced dyskinesias. Non-motor symptoms included depression, anxiety, cognitive impairment, and constipation.

Physical examination: masked facies, high neck muscle tension, limb muscle strength 5/5, increased right limb tone with obvious tremor, increased left limb tone with stiff knee joint and slow joint activity, poor postural stability, forward-leaning posture, unstable gait, slow turning, and bilateral finger-nose test positive. UPDRS-III = 73, Hoehn & Yahr stage = 4, PDQ-39 = 118. Acute levodopa challenge test showed 57% improvement. Brain MRI was normal. DBS surgery is recommended.

Assume the surgeon has access to standard DBS targets: subthalamic nucleus (STN), globus pallidus internus (GPi), ventral intermediate nucleus (VIM), and posterior subthalamic area (PSA). Which target configuration (e.g., left X, right Y) is most appropriate based on the asymmetric symptom profile?

answer_key.etiology: Neurodegenerative loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta leading to basal ganglia dysfunction
answer_key.next_management_step: Combined left posterior subthalamic area (PSA) and right globus pallidus internus (GPi) deep brain stimulation

Normal brain MRI

challenge_prompt:
A 73-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension and coronary heart disease and had been on long-term clopidogrel anticoagulation therapy presented to the hospital with weakness and numbness in the right limb for over 1 hour. According to the patient, she had undergone acupuncture treatment on the neck and shoulder at a community hospital for dizziness 1 hour prior. Half an hour after the acupuncture session, she noticed weakness and numbness in her right limb and was urgently sent to the hospital. The right limb exhibited decreased sensation with numbness, hyperreflexia, and positive Babinski and Hoffman signs. No significant abnormalities were observed in the left limb. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?

challenge_prompt:
A 37-year-old right-handed woman with a 37-year history of drug-resistant focal epilepsy is referred for pre-surgical evaluation. At 5 months of age, following hypoxia due to dehydration from diarrhea, she began experiencing absence seizures (frequency ~5/day, duration 2–3 s). At age 34, seizure manifestations evolved to include loss of consciousness, a greeting-like vocalization, and automatisms such as slapping of the right hand or bilateral hand clapping, lasting 5–6 s and occurring almost daily. By age 36, she developed psychiatric symptoms including auditory hallucinations and self-talking.

Video EEG (VEEG) monitoring revealed interictal sharp wave discharges in the bilateral temporal regions and the right frontal lobe, with a predominant focus in the right temporal region (T8, F8). Ictal EEG recordings demonstrated bilateral temporal sharp waves, maximal over the right temporal lobe, with propagation to the right frontal lobe. MRI revealed cystic degeneration involving the right hippocampus. 18F-FDG PET showed reduced metabolism in the right temporal lobe and right prefrontal lobe.

Based on a preliminary diagnosis of right temporal lobe epilepsy, SEEG electrodes were implanted. Interictal SEEG identified rhythmic phase inversion in the right anterior temporal lobe, hippocampus-amygdala complex, and anterior insular cortex, along with persistent slow-wave discharges in the left frontal lobe. Ictal SEEG recordings demonstrated seizure onset in the right anterior temporal lobe, with propagation to the right dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (dlPFC) within 3 s, coinciding with the emergence of right slapping and then clapping automatism.

Based on these findings, what is the most likely seizure onset zone and propagation network, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Right temporal lobe epilepsy (TLE) with ictal propagation to the right dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC), manifesting as slapping automatism (temporal-plus epilepsy).

answer_key.diagnosis: Right temporal lobe epilepsy (TLE) with ictal propagation to the right dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC), manifesting as slapping automatism (temporal-plus epilepsy).
aliases: Right mesial temporal lobe epilepsy with extratemporal frontal involvement
Focal epilepsy due to right hippocampal lesion with rapid frontal propagation
answer_key.etiology: Remote symptomatic focal epilepsy; likely due to hypoxic-ischemic injury in infancy resulting in right hippocampal cystic degeneration.
answer_key.next_management_step: Right anterior temporal lobectomy including resection of the anterior insula and frontal operculum, with SEEG-guided radiofrequency thermocoagulation (RF-TC) of the right insula.

challenge_prompt:
A 19-year-old, right-handed male was admitted due to recurrent absence seizures over a period of 6 months. Initially, the episodes occurred 2–3 times per day and lasted approximately 1 minute. Gradually, the seizures became more severe, reaching 6–7 attacks per day and lasting 2–3 minutes each. He was born via uneventful caesarean section at 39 weeks; no perinatal complications were noted. Medical and family history were unremarkable. His mother reported that, from age 6, he consistently showed weaker learning abilities compared to peers. Physical examination revealed no remarkable abnormalities, with no hemiparesis or facial asymmetry. The Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) score was 20, indicating mild cognitive impairment with predominant deficits in language and delayed recall. Electroencephalogram showed interhemispheric asymmetry and increased sharp slow wave complexes in the left occipital and left middle-posterior temporal regions. Cranial CT and MRI revealed left hemisphere atrophy, thickening of the ipsilateral cranial vault, dilatation of the ipsilateral frontal sinus, and enlargement of the left lateral ventricle. CT angiography showed hypoplasia of the right vertebral artery; the left posterior cerebral artery (PCA) originated from the ipsilateral internal carotid artery with a hypoplastic P1 segment, a slender subsequent artery stem, and sparse branches. CT perfusion imaging confirmed hypoperfusion in the areas supplied by the left PCA. Additional investigations—psychological assessment, magnetic resonance venography, ultrasonography of the heart and other organs, chest CT, blood routine, blood biochemistry, coagulation analysis, urinalysis, and autoimmune disease parameters—were all within normal limits.

What is the most likely diagnosis?
gold_diagnosis: Dyke-Davidoff-Masson syndrome (congenital)

answer_key.diagnosis: Dyke-Davidoff-Masson syndrome (congenital)
answer_key.etiology: Congenital cerebral hemispheric hypoperfusion due to hypoplasia of the left PCA (P1 segment) and ipsilateral fetal-type posterior communicating artery (FTP), resulting in ipsilateral cerebral atrophy, ventriculomegaly, and compensatory calvarial changes.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate appropriate antiepileptic therapy (e.g., lamotrigine) and provide cognitive rehabilitation training; titrate medication to achieve seizure control and regular multidisciplinary follow-up.

Learning difficulties since age 6 relative to peers.
MoCA score 20 with deficits in language and delayed recall.
EEG: interhemispheric asymmetry, sharp slow wave complexes in left occipital and left middle-posterior temporal regions.
CT/MRI: left hemisphere atrophy, thickening of ipsilateral cranial vault, dilatation of ipsilateral frontal sinus, enlargement of left lateral ventricle.
CTA: left PCA origin from ipsilateral ICA with hypoplastic P1 segment, slender artery stem, sparse branches; hypoplastic right vertebral artery.
CT perfusion: hypoperfusion in left PCA territory.
Physical exam without hemiparesis or facial asymmetry.
optional_findings: Delivered by caesarean section at 39 weeks.
Right vertebral artery hypoplasia on CTA.
All other laboratory and imaging investigations normal.
Mild cognitive improvement at 12-month follow-up (reported in outcome).
Seizure freedom on lamotrigine monotherapy (reported in outcome).

challenge_prompt:
A 30-year-old woman presents with headache 4 weeks after cesarean delivery performed under spinal anesthesia. During the spinal anesthesia procedure, multiple attempts at dural puncture were required. She initially experienced a postural headache that improved with lying flat, but over the last 3 days the headache has worsened significantly, becoming constant and non-postural, and is now accompanied by vomiting. She has no history of recent trauma, coagulation disorders, or use of anticoagulant or antiplatelet medications.

CT head revealed a hyperdense crescentic extra-axial collection over the right cerebral hemisphere, consistent with acute subdural hematoma.

answer_key.next_management_step: Non-contrast brain CT to confirm, followed by urgent burr hole evacuation if subdural hematoma is present.

challenge_prompt:
A 32-year-old Chinese male presented with a 2-month history of progressive bilateral upper and lower limb muscle weakness. The weakness initially involved the proximal lower limbs, causing difficulty squatting, and progressed to difficulty standing up and lifting his arms. He also reported inflammatory joint pain over the fingers and wrists, a rash over the bilateral lower limbs, loss of appetite, and weight loss. He denied trauma, back pain, prolonged fever, facial rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, alopecia, chronic cough, hemoptysis, or urinary/bowel incontinence. He had no significant past medical or family history. He was a cigarette smoker and worked as a delivery driver.

Physical examination revealed multiple flat, nontender, dark brown hyperpigmented rashes with distinct borders (1 cm × 2 cm) on the forearms and lower legs. There was no facial rash, photosensitivity, or oral ulcers. Cardiovascular, respiratory, and abdominal examinations were normal. Neurological examination showed no muscle fasciculations. Tone and reflexes were normal. Muscle strength testing revealed proximal myopathy: bilateral shoulder power 3/5, elbow power 3/5, wrist power 5/5, hand grip 5/5; bilateral hip power 3/5, knee power 5/5, ankle plantar flexion and dorsiflexion 5/5. Sensation was normal in all dermatomes. Babinski signs were downgoing, proprioception normal, and cerebellar signs negative.

Initial blood investigations showed anemia (hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL), leukopenia (total white cell count 2.96 × 10³/µL), and lymphopenia (0.92 × 10³/µL); platelet count 148 × 10³/µL. Renal profile showed sodium 130 mmol/L, potassium 3.5 mmol/L, urea 2.70 mmol/L, and creatinine 45 µmol/L (normal 62–106). Creatine phosphokinase was normal at 47 U/L (normal 39–308). Liver function tests, serum cortisol (512 nmol/L, normal 133–537), and thyroid function (TSH 2.550 mIU/L, free T4 18.80 pmol/L) were normal. Complement levels were low: C3 0.32 g/L (normal 0.90–1.80) and C4 0.07 g/L (normal 0.10–0.40). Antinuclear antibody (ANA) was positive at >200 CU. Anti-dsDNA antibody was positive at 50.6 IU/mL (negative <27). Extractable nuclear antibody panel showed anti-Smith (anti-Sm) 125.7 CU (<20), anti-RNP 21.5 CU (<20), anti-Ro-60 21.7 CU (<20), and anti-ribosomal P >362 CU (<20). These were performed by chemiluminescent immunoassay. Nerve conduction studies and electromyography demonstrated axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy and irritative myopathy. The patient declined a lumbar puncture.

A connective tissue disease was considered, and he was started on high-dose corticosteroids and hydroxychloroquine. Two days after admission, he developed urinary incontinence. Examination revealed weak anal tone and reduced perianal sensation. Cauda equina syndrome was suspected. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the spine with contrast showed leptomeningeal enhancement at the cauda equina region, with no cord compression and no features of transverse myelitis or multiple sclerosis. There was no evidence of malignancy.

What is the most likely underlying diagnosis, and what is the next best management step?
gold_diagnosis: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) with neuropsychiatric manifestations: polyneuropathy and noncompressive cauda equina syndrome

challenge_prompt:
A 56-year-old female presented with a 12-month history of insidiously progressive uncoordinated gait and slurred speech. She also had a 6-year history of rapid eye movement sleep behavior disorder (RBD) diagnosed elsewhere. There was no family history of ataxia or neurodegenerative disorders, and she denied alcohol or tobacco use. On examination, she had dysarthria, exaggerated bilateral tendon reflexes, positive bilateral Babinski and Chaddock signs, dysmetria on finger-to-nose and heel-knee-shin testing, wide-based gait, impaired tandem gait, and Romberg instability with eyes both open and closed. Muscle strength was normal. Blood pressure did not change significantly between supine, seated, and standing positions.

Laboratory studies showed positive antinuclear antibodies (titer 1:320) and positive anti-SSA antibodies, though the patient had no related symptoms. Routine blood tests including liver and kidney function, thyroid function, vitamin B12, folic acid, anti-thyroid antibodies, ANCA, and rheumatoid factor were normal. Tests for syphilis, hepatitis, and HIV were negative. Serum and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel (NMDA-R, CASPR2, AMPA1, AMPA2, LGI1, GABA-B, GAD65) and paraneoplastic panel (CV2/CRMP5, Ma2/Ta, Ri, Yo, Hu, Amphiphysin) were sent: anti-CASPR2 IgG was positive in serum at a titer of 1:100 (fixed CBA), while all other antibodies were negative in serum and CSF. CSF analysis showed normal white cell count, protein, glucose, and immunoglobulins; oligoclonal bands were absent. CSF bacterial and viral studies were negative. Serum tumor markers were negative, and genetic testing for spinocerebellar ataxia was unrevealing.

Brain MRI demonstrated atrophy of the brainstem and cerebellum; the putamen was normal. External anal sphincter electromyography (EAS-EMG) revealed increased duration of motor unit action potentials and satellite potentials. Bladder ultrasonography for post-void residual was normal. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed no abnormalities.

Based on this initial evaluation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what would be the most appropriate next step in management?

CSF testing for CASPR2 antibodies was negative.

answer_key.etiology: Synucleinopathy
answer_key.next_management_step: Monitor for development of autonomic dysfunction (orthostatic hypotension, urinary symptoms) and repeat brain imaging to assess progression; avoid immunosuppressive therapy as clinical and EMG findings suggest a neurodegenerative rather than autoimmune process despite positive CASPR2 antibodies.

challenge_prompt:
A 44-year-old female with a 9-year history of rheumatoid arthritis (RA) previously managed with methotrexate, sulfasalazine, prednisolone, and hydroxychloroquine was switched to infliximab (no washout period) due to inadequate disease control. She received nine doses of infliximab over one year. Two months before presentation, she developed headache and neck pain, followed by numbness and weakness in the right upper limb. A nerve conduction study suggested carpal tunnel syndrome, and she underwent carpal tunnel surgery. Post-operatively, her symptoms persisted and extended to her right lower limb. Brain MRI without and with contrast was performed. The initial MRI revealed a left frontoparietal periventricular white matter lobulated cystic lesion measuring 22 × 16 × 16 mm, with peripheral T2/FLAIR hyperintensity and diffusion restriction in the wall and septa. Post-contrast sequences demonstrated both closed ring and open ring enhancement, with the open side of the ring facing gray matter, and an enhancing prominent medullary vein (central vein sign). There was mild vasogenic edema without mass effect. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis showed no oligoclonal bands. Serum and CSF were negative for myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein IgG (MOG-IgG) and aquaporin-4 (AQP4) antibodies. Toxoplasma serology: IgM negative, IgG positive. challenge_prompt: A 32-year-old man with a history of seizures since childhood was diagnosed with structural focal epilepsy with focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizures (ILAE 2017 classification). Despite prolonged pharmacotherapy, seizures remained drug-resistant. Scalp EEG recorded two distinct seizure onset patterns: one originating from right frontotemporal leads with diffuse right-sided propagation; the second from left temporal leads, terminating with bilateral synchronization of bioelectrical activity. Semiology during these events included ipsilateral head version, and clinical features were discordant with EEG lateralization. Most recent MRI showed bilateral hippocampal sclerosis. PET-CT was unavailable due to financial constraints. Given the electro-clinical discordance and the high risk of severe anterograde amnesia with bilateral resection, invasive evaluation with stereoelectroencephalography (SEEG) was performed. Electrodes were implanted bilaterally in the hippocampus, amygdala, and cingulate gyrus; the left hemisphere additionally covered the insular gyrus, basal/orbitofrontal cortex, and occipital lobe. SEEG recorded three seizures. The first began in the right hippocampus, propagated to the left hippocampus, then involved the right amygdala and left hemisphere with bilateral synchronization. The second originated in the left hippocampus, rapidly followed by bilateral synchronization, indicating an independent focus. The third seizure arose from the left orbitofrontal region. Background activity showed bilateral theta accentuation in temporal regions, sporadic sharp waves and spikes in mediotemporal and anterotemporal leads, and paroxysmal activity in orbitofrontal areas. Clinically, the first seizure (right hippocampal onset) presented with behavioral arrest, leftward gaze deviation, right-hand dystonia, evolving to right-hand automatisms, disorientation, aphasia, and impaired consciousness. The second mirrored this pattern. The third seizure (left orbitofrontal onset) exhibited sudden grimaces, facial twitching, hyperexcitability, and transient vocalizations. Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis or description of the epileptogenic network, and what is the most appropriate next management step?
gold_diagnosis: Multifocal drug-resistant epilepsy with independent seizure onset zones in the right hippocampus, left hippocampus, and left orbitofrontal cortex

answer_key.etiology: Bilateral hippocampal sclerosis with additional epileptogenic focus in the left orbitofrontal cortex, likely related to structural/metabolic changes and network reorganization
answer_key.next_management_step: Deep brain stimulation of the anterior nucleus of the thalamus (ANT-DBS) or any form of neuromodulation targeting the distributed epileptogenic network

challenge_prompt:
A 25-year-old right-handed female with hypothyroidism and moderate depression presented with weakness in all four limbs for 10 days. It started in the right hand with difficulty in daily activities, pain, tingling, and numbness, then progressed over three days to the right lower limb, left lower limb, and left upper limb. Decreased sensation in all limbs. Cranial nerves including optic nerve normal. Cerebellar examination could not be performed due to quadriparesis; no nystagmus.

challenge_prompt:
A 42-year-old man sustained a traumatic brain injury in a traffic accident in May 2021. The injury resulted in a subdural hematoma, subarachnoid hemorrhage, and intracerebral hemorrhage. He remained in a vegetative state with a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score of 6 (E1, M4, V1) and a Coma Recovery Scale–Revised (CRS-R) score of 6. He exhibited sleep–wake cycles without awareness. Motor responses were purposeless movements, posturing, or withdrawal to noxious stimuli. Auditory and visual reactions were limited to startle reflexes. No communication was evident, and emotional responses were reflexive. He occasionally groaned in response to pain. He underwent rehabilitation, traditional Chinese acupuncture, and hyperbaric oxygen therapy without significant improvement. A brain CT scan revealed bilateral diffuse white matter hyperintensities consistent with diffuse axonal injury and a right-sided ventricular drainage catheter. Tc-99m ECD brain perfusion SPECT showed reduced activity in the bilateral inferior and superior frontal lobes, cingulate, precuneus, and left occipital areas, interpreted as hypoperfusion. His medications—piracetam 2400 mg/day, acetaminophen 2000 mg/day, bisacodyl 10 mg/day, bisoprolol 2.5 mg/day, and tizanidine 6 mg/day—had been unchanged for more than 3 months. He had no history of seizures and no contraindications to brain stimulation. He was referred to a brain stimulation clinic for evaluation of potential neuromodulation to improve his level of consciousness.

Based on the presentation and available findings, what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Vegetative state (unresponsive wakefulness syndrome) secondary to traumatic brain injury

answer_key.etiology: Traumatic brain injury with subdural hematoma, subarachnoid hemorrhage, intracerebral hemorrhage, and diffuse axonal injury
answer_key.next_management_step: Bilateral high-frequency (10 Hz) repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) targeting the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) to promote consciousness recovery

No contraindications to brain stimulation

challenge_prompt: A 31-year-old right-handed male sustained a headfirst impact during an electric bicycle accident, with immediate loss of consciousness and unresponsiveness to verbal stimuli. challenge_prompt: A 27-year-old woman presented with significant excessive daytime sleepiness (EDS) for over 10 years, worsening in recent months. challenge_prompt:
A 73-year-old man with a history of rectal cancer (diagnosed May 2020, treated with radical resection and 6 cycles of XELOX: oxaliplatin 150 mg plus capecitabine) presented to the Acupuncture Department in April 2023 with a 1-month history of persistent facial numbness. He had developed lung metastases in January 2023, underwent thoracoscopic lobectomy in February 2023, and then started salvage chemotherapy with XELOX (oxaliplatin 150 mg plus capecitabine every 3 weeks) on March 7, 2023. After that first salvage cycle, he developed recurrent hand and foot numbness (also experienced since 2020 but previously mild) along with new-onset facial numbness. The facial numbness involved the periorbital, lateral nasal, preauricular, and perioral regions bilaterally. He reported a mild restriction of facial movements and a tightening sensation. The symptoms persisted and severely impaired his daily activities, including tooth brushing and eating, and caused insomnia. After the second cycle on March 30, 2023, the symptoms worsened.

On examination, voluntary facial movements (eye closure, eyebrow elevation, nose twitching, cheek puffing) were intact, though the patient reported mild subjective restriction. Tactile and pain sensations were preserved on facial sensory testing. Limb muscle strength and tone were normal. Head CT was unremarkable. The patient declined facial nerve electromyography. Hand and foot numbness were more severe in the lower extremities, consistent with a glove-and-stocking distribution. Using Levi's Oxaliplatin-Specific Sensory Neurotoxicity Scale, his symptoms were graded as 3.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

challenge_prompt: A 43-year-old male with a history of resistant hypertension, medication noncompliance, cystitis cystica, and smoking presented with severe bitemporal headache, blurring of vision, and dizziness for 3 days. He stopped his antihypertensives 3 months ago. Previous workup for secondary hypertension (including aldosterone, renin ratio, and genetic panel for Liddle syndrome) was negative. Family history: father with hypertension. On examination: BP 225/141 mm Hg, HR 77 bpm, O2 97% on room air. Fundoscopy showed Grade IV hypertensive retinopathy with papilledema. Neurological exam: no focal deficit, GCS 15, normal power. Labs: creatinine 132 μmol/L, potassium 3.2 mmol/L, magnesium 0.6 mmol/L, glucose 6.1 mmol/L; CBC, other electrolytes, troponin, liver function normal. CT head showed chronic white matter microvascular ischemic changes, no acute bleeding or infarct. He was given oral nifedipine and valsartan, but BP remained 219/148, so IV hydralazine and oral potassium/magnesium supplements were added. BP still high (201/134), labetalol infusion started. On the second day, he had two generalized tonic-clonic seizures within 15 min without regaining full consciousness (status epilepticus), preceded by systolic BP >200. Lorazepam and levetiracetam were given. Mental status declined: GCS 13, confusion. MRI brain: T2/FLAIR showed extensive patches of increased signal intensity in the left cerebellar hemisphere, bilateral periventricular and subcortical regions, predominantly posteriorly. DWI/ADC: no restricted diffusion. Postcontrast T1: left parieto-occipital leptomeningeal enhancement. MRA: no vasospasm. Lumbar puncture: CSF analysis normal (glucose, protein, cell count within normal limits). What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.next_management_step: Aggressive blood pressure control (gradual reduction with IV agents like labetalol), continuation of antiepileptic therapy (levetiracetam), and correction of electrolyte abnormalities (especially magnesium).

challenge_prompt:
An 11-year-old boy was born without complications. At 5 months of age, during a febrile illness, he experienced impaired consciousness and generalized tonic-clonic seizures. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis at that time showed a white blood cell count of 10 × 10⁶/L and protein level of 0.46 g/L, leading to a diagnosis of viral encephalitis. The episode resolved within 2 days.

He remained seizure-free until 2.3 years of age, when he began experiencing monthly focal seizures with preserved consciousness, characterized by either left leg twitching or left-sided facial twitching. Seizure frequency increased progressively. At 2.8 years, EEG captured atypical absence seizures and revealed spike-and-wave complexes in the centroparietal midline and Rolandic regions, with a spike-wave index (SWI) of 80% during sleep. Concurrently, he showed neurodevelopmental regression: he lost the ability to stand or walk independently, had language regression, and cognitive decline.

Multiple anti-seizure medications (levetiracetam, sodium valproate, lamotrigine, topiramate, clonazepam, sultiame, clobazam) were tried with poor efficacy. Three courses of pulsed corticosteroids at ages 2.9, 3.3, and 4 years produced transient improvement, but relapses followed each course.

At age 4.8 years, pre-surgical evaluation showed approximately 30 focal motor seizures per month, inability to stand or walk independently, markedly reduced speech output, and impaired cognition. Brain MRI demonstrated bilateral atrophy of the putamen and caudate nucleus. A 4-hour ambulatory EEG again showed spike-and-wave complexes in the centroparietal midline and Rolandic regions, with a SWI of 80% during sleep. Griffiths mental development scales confirmed severe global developmental delay (developmental quotient/intelligence quotient ≤ 35). Current medications are valproate, clobazam, and levetiracetam.

What is the most likely epilepsy syndrome/diagnosis, and what next therapeutic step is most appropriate to consider at this time?
gold_diagnosis: Developmental and epileptic encephalopathy with spike-and-wave activation in sleep (DEE/EE-SWAS), also known as electrical status epilepticus during slow-wave sleep (ESES) or epilepsy with continuous spike-and-wave during slow-wave sleep (CSWS).

answer_key.diagnosis: Developmental and epileptic encephalopathy with spike-and-wave activation in sleep (DEE/EE-SWAS), also known as electrical status epilepticus during slow-wave sleep (ESES) or epilepsy with continuous spike-and-wave during slow-wave sleep (CSWS).
answer_key.etiology: Structural (bilateral basal ganglia atrophy) likely secondary to remote viral encephalitis, with a possible inflammatory component.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate vagus nerve stimulation (VNS) therapy.
required_findings: Impaired consciousness and generalized tonic-clonic seizures during febrile illness at 5 months; CSF WBC 10 × 10⁶/L, protein 0.46 g/L consistent with viral encephalitis.
Focal seizures with preserved consciousness (left leg or facial twitching) starting at 2.3 years and progressive increase.
EEG at 2.8 years: spike-and-wave complexes in centroparietal midline and Rolandic regions with SWI 80% during sleep, and atypical absence seizures.
Neurodevelopmental regression: loss of independent standing/walking, language regression, cognitive decline coinciding with EEG findings.
Failure of multiple anti-seizure medications (levetiracetam, valproate, lamotrigine, topiramate, clonazepam, sultiame, clobazam).
Three courses of pulsed corticosteroids provided transient benefit with subsequent relapse.
Brain MRI showing bilateral atrophy of the putamen and caudate nucleus.
Repeat 4-h ambulatory EEG at 4.8 years: same spike-and-wave pattern, SWI 80% during sleep.
optional_findings: Spike-and-wave location: centroparietal midline and Rolandic regions.
No new ASMs or corticosteroids introduced between evaluations.
Ambulatory EEG was 4 hours in duration.

provenance.title: Vagus nerve stimulation for spike-and-wave activation in sleep in a pediatric patient: a case report

challenge_prompt:
An 8-year-old boy presents with a chief complaint of hand tremors and inability to write, gradually worsening over the past year. He is the second son, with an 11-year-old older brother; mother is a farmer, father is a goldsmith. He completed Grade 1 with excellent results and had age-appropriate reading, writing, and math skills. The symptoms began in June 2022, after a two-month summer vacation filled with play, when he started additional tutoring to prepare for second grade. His teacher and family observed frequent hand tremors and slight jerking movements during writing, leading to deteriorating handwriting and eventual inability to write with his right hand.

At school, he was trained to write with his left hand, which he managed temporarily. During this period, his mother reported recurrent episodes of abdominal pain lasting several hours, occurring at any time, causing intense crying and occasional fainting, with elevated blood pressure and pulse. Extensive hospital evaluations found no organic cause, and the pain resolved within one day of admission without specific treatment. Subsequently, tremors developed in his left hand, causing inability to write with that hand as well. The school accommodated this by exempting him from written assignments, allowing oral responses, and arranging for a peer to transcribe his dictated answers during exams in Vietnamese, mathematics, and social-nature. He achieved outstanding grades in both semesters of second grade using this method.

In June 2023, after completing his summer vacation following Grade 2, the boy continued to experience writing difficulties. He was admitted to several general hospitals where he underwent brain and spinal MRI, electromyography (EMG), and electroencephalography (EEG), all of which revealed no abnormalities. Similar investigations in the previous year had also been normal. He had no history of epilepsy, traumatic brain injury, or other medical conditions; developmental milestones were normal; family history was negative for psychiatric, medical, and neurological disorders.

On clinical evaluation, he was a lively, agile child who responded promptly to questions and showed a strong attachment to his mother. Physical examination revealed normal muscle strength (5/5) and deep tendon reflexes. When asked to draw a picture under observation, he exhibited significant tremors but completed a car drawing when unobserved. Apart from writing inability and occasional tremors during eating (spilling food, requiring both hands to stabilize a spoon), fine motor examination showed preserved function: he could hold a mobile phone securely, catch and throw a ball effectively, and use scissors to cut out complex shapes. The “three wishes” technique revealed desires to excel academically, for his parents’ health, and for better treatment from classmates.

During interview, the boy resisted his mother leaving the room and often preferred to hold her hand. He reported feeling anxious when she left the house, but did not describe nightmares, fears of abandonment, or separation-related distress. He simply stated he wanted her to stay with him. He described classmates as “unkind and selfish,” giving examples: he often lent them materials but they did not reciprocate when he needed help. He spent recess coloring or drawing, rarely interacting with peers. He was affectionate and generous, frequently sharing with friends, and became tearful when praise was not given.

The Spence Children’s Anxiety Scale (SCAS) was administered. He responded ‘sometimes’ to items 1, 3, 12, 18, 33; ‘often’ to items 5, 8, 31, 43; and ‘always’ to items 17, 26, 38; with ‘never’ to all others. These responses did not indicate clinically significant anxiety. A structured clinical interview based on DSM-5 criteria confirmed the absence of comorbid anxiety disorders.

Based on the above, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Conversion Disorder (Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder)

answer_key.diagnosis: Conversion Disorder (Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder)
answer_key.etiology: Psychological stressors including academic pressure, peer relationship difficulties, and secondary gains from symptom accommodation
answer_key.next_management_step: Referral to child psychiatry for play therapy and cognitive-behavioral therapy addressing secondary gains and psychosocial stressors
required_findings: frequent hand tremors and slight jerking movements during writing, leading to deteriorating handwriting and eventual inability to write with his right hand
tremors developed in his left hand, causing inability to write with that hand as well
brain and spinal MRI, electromyography (EMG), and electroencephalography (EEG), all of which revealed no abnormalities
he could hold a mobile phone securely, catch and throw a ball effectively, and use scissors to cut out complex shapes
recurrent episodes of abdominal pain lasting several hours … no organic cause was identified
normal muscle strength (5/5) and deep tendon reflexes
The Spence Children’s Anxiety Scale … did not indicate clinically significant anxiety
structured clinical interview based on DSM-5 criteria confirmed the absence of comorbid anxiety disorders
optional_findings: desires to excel academically, for his parents’ health, and for better treatment from classmates
spilling food, requiring both hands to stabilize a spoon
mother is a farmer, father is a goldsmith
He reported feeling anxious when she left the house, but did not describe nightmares, fears of abandonment, or separation-related distress
He described classmates as ‘unkind and selfish’
Exempted from written assignments, allowing oral responses, and a peer transcribed his dictated answers during exams
Family history was negative for psychiatric, medical, and neurological disorders

provenance.title: Agraphia due to Conversion Disorder (Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder) in an 8-year-old boy: a case report

challenge_prompt:
A 72-year-old male with type 2 diabetes and hypertension presented with a 7-day history of fever (38.8 °C), headache, and vomiting. Blood glucose was not routinely monitored. He had discontinued smoking and alcohol 5 years earlier. Initial head computed tomography revealed a right cerebellar hematoma with multiple hypodense lesions showing partial rim enhancement; CT angiography excluded vascular anomalies. The patient was alert with dysarthria, dysphagia, and unsteady gait but preserved cognition. Neurological exam showed bilaterally responsive 3-mm pupils, normal muscle strength/tone, and no pathological reflexes. Blood tests demonstrated mild leukocytosis (10.0 × 10⁹/L). 

Brain MRI (1.5 T) with diffusion-weighted imaging and contrast enhancement confirmed multiple brain abscesses, a hemorrhagic cerebellar lesion, meningitis, and subdural empyema. A diagnosis of complicated intracranial infection was established. Management included supportive care (oxygen, intravenous fluids, expectorants, electrolyte correction, glycemic and hypertensive control), antiemetics (dolasetron 12.5 mg IV bid, ondansetron 8 mg IV bid), and antimicrobials (cefoperazone 4 g IV q12h, linezolid 600 mg IV q12h). Symptoms resolved within 7 days. He was discharged with a plan for 1‑month follow‑up MRI but was lost to follow‑up.

Seven months later, the patient was readmitted because of persistent cognitive‑behavioral changes: memory deterioration, personality alteration, disorganized speech, tangentiality, and emotional lability with irritability. Activities of daily living were preserved; there was no urinary incontinence, dysphagia, or dysarthria. Neurological examination revealed isolated cognitive decline without motor, sensory, or cranial nerve deficits. Cognitive assessments showed: Mini‑Mental State Examination 27/30, Montreal Cognitive Assessment 16/30, Clinical Dementia Rating 0.5/3.

Laboratory work‑up for autoimmune encephalitis was negative: normal immunoglobulins (IgA, IgG, IgM) and complement (C3, C4); negative antinuclear antibody, anti‑double‑stranded DNA, and extractable nuclear antigen antibodies. Apolipoprotein E genotyping showed ε3/ε3 homozygosity.

Multimodal 3‑T brain MRI at readmission demonstrated: communicating hydrocephalus; persistent hemosiderin deposition in the right cerebellar hemisphere; and a patchy T2‑hyperintense lesion with enlargement in the left anterior medulla oblongata (region of the inferior olivary nucleus). Diffusion‑weighted imaging showed no restricted diffusion. Lumbar drainage combined with gait assessment excluded normal pressure hydrocephalus.

Based on these data, what is the most likely diagnosis for the left medullary lesion, and what is the next most appropriate management step?
gold_diagnosis: Hypertrophic olivary degeneration (HOD)

answer_key.diagnosis: Hypertrophic olivary degeneration (HOD)
answer_key.etiology: Secondary to right cerebellar hemorrhagic cerebellitis/brain abscess disrupting the dentato‑rubro‑olivary pathway (Guillain‑Mollaret triangle)
answer_key.next_management_step: Supportive care with serial neuroimaging and monitoring for development of palatal tremor, ataxia, or other movement disorders; symptomatic treatment (e.g., clonazepam, levodopa) if manifestations arise.
required_findings: Initial presentation with fever (38.8 °C), headache, vomiting, and mild leukocytosis (10.0 × 10⁹/L); CT showed right cerebellar hematoma and multiple hypodense lesions.
First‑admission MRI confirmed multiple brain abscesses, a hemorrhagic cerebellar lesion, meningitis, and subdural empyema.
Seven months later, readmission with cognitive‑behavioral changes: memory deterioration, personality alteration, disorganized speech, emotional lability; MMSE 27/30, MoCA 16/30, CDR 0.5.

Readmission 3‑T MRI revealed communicating hydrocephalus, persistent hemosiderin deposition in the right cerebellar hemisphere, and a patchy T2‑hyperintense lesion with enlargement in the left anterior medulla oblongata (inferior olivary nucleus region).
DWI showed no restricted diffusion, ruling out acute medullary infarction.
Lumbar drainage and gait assessment excluded normal pressure hydrocephalus.
Autoimmune encephalitis panel negative: normal immunoglobulins, complement, and negative ANA, anti‑dsDNA, ENA antibodies.
Neurological exam demonstrated isolated cognitive deficits without motor, sensory, or cranial nerve abnormalities.
optional_findings: Blood glucose was not routinely monitored.
Five years prior, the patient had discontinued smoking and alcohol consumption.
Initial CT angiography excluded vascular abnormalities.
Initial symptoms resolved within 7 days of antimicrobial therapy (cefoperazone, linezolid).
No prior history of unconsciousness, visual abnormalities, aspiration, seizures, or facial nerve dysfunction.

provenance.title: Hypertrophic olivary degeneration secondary to brain abscess: a case report and literature review

challenge_prompt:
A 66-year-old man presented with persistent pain beginning 6 months after a cerebral infarction that had resulted in left-sided hemiplegia. The pain was a burning sensation at rest, predominantly on the left (hemiplegic) side, and was exacerbated by light touch from clothing or manual skin palpation. He rated the pain intensity as 4 on a 0–10 visual analog scale. Comprehensive evaluation excluded nociceptive and peripheral neuropathic etiologies of pain.

What is the most likely diagnosis?
gold_diagnosis: Central post-stroke pain (CPSP)

answer_key.diagnosis: Central post-stroke pain (CPSP)
answer_key.etiology: Ischemic stroke (cerebral infarction) causing a right-hemispheric lesion with left-sided hemiplegia and subsequent development of central neuropathic pain.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate gabapentin 300 mg three times daily (or alternative first-line agent such as pregabalin or amitriptyline) for central neuropathic pain; consider multidisciplinary pain management if refractory.
required_findings: 66-year-old man
History of cerebral infarction with left-sided hemiplegia
Pain onset 6 months after the stroke
Burning quality of pain
Predominantly on the hemiplegic (left) side
Pain present at rest
Exacerbated by light touch (clothing/palpation) indicating allodynia
Nociceptive and peripheral neuropathic causes excluded
optional_findings: Pain initially also reported on the non-paralyzed side but later resolved
No cutaneous trophic changes or autonomic signs
No new motor or sensory deficits beyond prior hemiplegia
Absence of infection or trauma as triggers
Pain refractory to initial simple analgesics
Normal blood biochemistry at initial assessment

provenance.title: A clinical case report on transcranial low-intensity focused ultrasound neuromodulation for central post-stroke pain

challenge_prompt: An 69-year-old male presented with sudden onset of right-sided foot drop, not related to any recent direct trauma, accompanied by numbness and paresthesia over the lateral leg and foot, without significant pain. He reported a 2-week history of frequent right ankle sprains and limping. Physical examination revealed muscle strength of BMRC grade 1 in dorsiflexion and toe extension, grade 2 in eversion, preserved ankle plantar flexion, and full active range of motion at the knee. Sensory examination showed reduced sensation over the lateral leg and dorsum of the foot. Tinel sign was positive at the fibular head. Lumbar MRI demonstrated a mild herniated intervertebral disc and degenerative disc changes at the L3–4 and L4–5 levels, without significant radicular compression. Lower leg MRI performed on the fourth day after symptom onset revealed increased T2 signal intensity in the tibialis anterior muscle, suggestive of early denervation changes, along with hyperintensity of the common peroneal nerve at the level of the fibular head. Nerve conduction velocity and electromyography demonstrated findings consistent with acute involvement of the right common peroneal nerve. Based on the above presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?
gold_diagnosis: Common peroneal nerve entrapment neuropathy (nontraumatic)

answer_key.diagnosis: Common peroneal nerve entrapment neuropathy (nontraumatic)
aliases: Foot drop due to common peroneal nerve compression
answer_key.etiology: Compression of the common peroneal nerve by a tight intermuscular septum at the fibular head (identified intraoperatively)
answer_key.next_management_step: Surgical decompression and neurolysis of the right common peroneal nerve

Numbness and paresthesia over the lateral leg and foot
Muscle strength BMRC grade 1 in dorsiflexion and toe extension, grade 2 in eversion
Tinel sign positive at the fibular head
Lower leg MRI: increased T2 signal in tibialis anterior and hyperintensity of common peroneal nerve at fibular head
NCV and EMG: findings consistent with acute involvement of the right common peroneal nerve
Lumbar MRI: mild herniated disc at L3-4 and L4-5 without significant radicular compression
optional_findings: History of frequent right ankle sprains over 2 weeks
Reduced sensation over lateral leg and dorsum of foot

provenance.title: Recurrent nontraumatic common peroneal neuropathy after surgical decompression: Case report and literature review

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

A 37-year-old male factory worker in furniture assembly presented with two nights of distressing auditory hallucinations and severe anxiety. He had just completed four consecutive night shifts (22:00–06:00) followed by one voluntary daytime overtime shift (08:00–18:00). He heard accusatory voices commenting on his work performance and felt paranoid about colleagues’ intentions, with symptoms peaking during attempted daytime rest. He denied any prior psychiatric history, substance use (alcohol, cannabis, stimulants), head trauma, or family mental illness. Burnout and workplace mobbing were denied. The overtime was initially voluntary but became non‑cancelable due to scheduling constraints. He occasionally used over‑the‑counter caffeine tablets. On examination, he appeared exhausted with dark circles under the eyes and mild psychomotor agitation (fidgeting, rapid speech), but was fully oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Vital signs were stable. Neurological examination showed no focal deficits, tremors, or ataxia; pupils were equal and reactive without nystagmus. Mental status revealed anxious affect but intact cognition (Mini‑Mental State Examination (MMSE) score of 28/30), insight into the symptoms as “unreal,” and no suicidal ideation. Mood was euthymic at baseline with superimposed acute anxiety. No signs of intoxication or withdrawal were evident. Laboratory investigations: complete blood count normal (hemoglobin 15.1 g/dL, white blood cell count 6.2 × 10⁹/L), electrolytes normal, glucose 92 mg/dL, liver function tests normal, thyroid‑stimulating hormone 1.8 mIU/L. A urine toxicology screen was considered but not performed at this visit due to patient refusal; however, a routine urine toxicology screen performed four months prior at the factory’s occupational prevention service was negative. No additional symptoms suggestive of other etiologies (e.g., fever, seizures, cognitive decline beyond acute fatigue) were present.
gold_diagnosis: Transient psychotic symptoms induced by acute sleep deprivation (consistent with brief psychotic disorder DSM‑5 F23; acute and transient psychotic disorder ICD‑11 6A23)

answer_key.diagnosis: Transient psychotic symptoms induced by acute sleep deprivation (consistent with brief psychotic disorder DSM‑5 F23; acute and transient psychotic disorder ICD‑11 6A23)
answer_key.etiology: Cumulative acute sleep deprivation from consecutive night shifts and overtime, leading to reversible neurochemical imbalances
answer_key.next_management_step: Recommend immediate rest, suspend night shifts for two weeks, and refer to primary care for follow‑up; avoid antipsychotic medication
required_findings: Cumulative sleep restriction (4 consecutive night shifts plus daytime overtime)
Acute auditory hallucinations and paranoia, peaking during daytime rest
MMSE 28/30 with preserved insight into symptoms
No prior psychiatric history or family history of mental illness
Normal laboratory investigations (CBC, glucose, LFTs, TSH)
Negative urine toxicology screen four months prior
Normal neurological examination without focal deficits
Absence of fever, seizures, or cognitive decline beyond fatigue
optional_findings: Appearance of exhaustion with dark circles under eyes
No signs of intoxication or withdrawal
Patient denial of burnout or workplace mobbing
Euthymic baseline mood with superimposed acute anxiety

provenance.title: Transient Psychotic Symptoms Induced by Acute Sleep Deprivation in a Factory Worker: A Case Report

challenge_prompt: A 48-year-old woman with a 27-year history of treatment-resistant schizophrenia (DSM-5, diagnosed by SCID) and metabolic syndrome (type 2 diabetes, hypertension, medication-induced) was started on a new inpatient dietary intervention for 5 weeks. Her antipsychotic regimen consisted of clozapine and olanzapine at stable doses; other medications included divalproex sodium, metformin, amlodipine, oxybutynin, propranolol, and a multivitamin. No antipsychotic dose changes occurred during the 5 weeks. The dietary intervention provided 2500–3800 kcal/day with macronutrient composition of approximately 70% fat, 20% protein, and ≤10% carbohydrate (initial fat to combined protein and carbohydrate ratio 2.5:1; increased to 2.65:1 during week 2 due to inadequate objective response). Twice-daily fingerstick monitoring of β-hydroxybutyrate (βHB) and glucose was performed, with a target of βHB ≥0.5 mmol/L. Consistent ketosis was achieved only during the final 5 days of the intervention. After 5 weeks, the following changes from baseline were observed: HOMA-IR decreased by 69% (from prediabetic to optimal); fasting insulin decreased by 64%; C-peptide decreased by 41%; CRP decreased by 61% (from high to average cardiac risk); IL-6 decreased by 20%; Simpson-Angus Scale (SAS) for extrapyramidal symptoms improved from 15 (severe) to 3 (mild); negative symptoms showed a slight improvement; total BPRS score did not change significantly. Weight and BMI were unchanged. What is the most likely etiology for these metabolic, inflammatory, and neurological improvements, and what is the next best management step?
gold_diagnosis: Ketogenic diet therapy

answer_key.diagnosis: Ketogenic diet therapy
answer_key.etiology: Institution of a ketogenic diet led to the observed metabolic, inflammatory, and extrapyramidal improvements.
answer_key.next_management_step: Continue the ketogenic diet, maintain βHB levels ≥0.5 mmol/L, and monitor metabolic and neurological parameters.
required_findings: Macronutrient composition ~70% fat, 20% protein, ≤10% carbohydrate
Fat to combined protein and carbohydrate ratio adjusted from 2.5:1 to 2.65:1
Twice-daily βHB monitoring with target ≥0.5 mmol/L
HOMA-IR decreased by 69% (from prediabetic to optimal)
Fasting insulin decreased by 64%
CRP decreased by 61% (high to average cardiac risk)
SAS improved from 15 (severe) to 3 (mild) without change in antipsychotics
Weight and BMI remained stable despite high calorie intake
optional_findings: Concurrent medications: clozapine, olanzapine, divalproex, metformin, others
Consistent ketosis only in final 5 days
Diet was implemented in an inpatient setting

provenance.title: Case Report: Metabolic, inflammatory, and neurological improvements after a ketogenic diet in a woman with treatment-resistant schizophrenia and metabolic syndrome

challenge_prompt:
An 8-year-old boy presented with 5 days of abnormal mood, primarily irritability, followed by 2 days of speech disorder and involuntary movements. He had a fall and vomiting 5 days prior. He developed incoherent speech, slurred articulation, involuntary head shaking, flailing limbs, impaired recognition of individuals, confusion, and sleep disturbances. Past medical and family history unremarkable. On admission: temperature 36°C, pulse 86/min, RR 18/min, BP 115/70 mm Hg. He was confused with Glasgow Coma Scale score of 4, poor responsiveness, and uncooperative. Babinski sign positive on right, negative on left. After admission, he had progressively worsening consciousness, inability to speak, involuntary limb movements, worsening sleep disturbances, and frequent seizures. Brain MRI showed no significant abnormalities. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis: white blood cell count 26 × 10⁶/L (other parameters not reported). Electroencephalogram: background activity significantly slowed for age, occasional theta rhythm in occipital region, diffuse slow wave activity; one electroclinical seizure was recorded: focal seizure with impaired consciousness.

Based on the clinical presentation and initial workup, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next best diagnostic step?

Cerebrospinal fluid anti-NMDA receptor antibody testing was positive.
gold_diagnosis: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis
aliases: Autoimmune encephalitis due to NMDA receptor antibodies
answer_key.etiology: Autoantibodies against the glutamate receptor (NMDA type), likely triggered by a preceding infection or unknown cause.
answer_key.next_management_step: Test for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies in cerebrospinal fluid and serum; consider initiating immunotherapy (e.g., high-dose steroids, IVIG) while awaiting results.

Speech disorder with incoherent speech and slurred articulation
Involuntary movements including head shaking and flailing limbs
Frequent seizures captured on EEG as focal seizure with impaired consciousness
Cerebrospinal fluid white blood cell count of 26 × 10⁶/L
EEG showing background slowing, occasional theta, diffuse slow waves, and electroclinical seizure
Normal brain MRI
Impaired consciousness with Glasgow Coma Scale score of 4

challenge_prompt: A 68-year-old male Macau resident with a known history of ankylosing spondylitis (AS) diagnosed 10 years ago, not on disease-modifying therapy, presented to the emergency department with neck pain and bilateral lower extremity weakness that began 4 hours after a fall. Physical examination revealed loss of cervical lordosis, restricted cervical range of motion, and tenderness over the cervicoscapular region. Sensory exam showed hypesthesia below the T2/T3 dermatomal level and perianal anesthesia (S3–S5 distribution). Motor testing: right wrist extensors, biceps, brachioradialis, triceps, and wrist flexors were grade 4/5; other right upper extremity muscles were grade 5/5; left upper extremity was grade 5/5 throughout; bilateral lower extremities were grade 1+/5. Muscle tone was normal in all limbs. Hoffmann signs were negative bilaterally; cremasteric and anal reflexes were absent; neurogenic bladder and bowel dysfunction were noted. Past medical history: seropositive AS, essential hypertension; no diabetes, cardiovascular disease, prior spinal issues, or surgeries. Laboratory studies: HLA-B27 positive; C-reactive protein 11.99 mg/L; D-dimer >20.00 μg/mL. Cervical CT scan: comminuted C7 vertebral body fracture; multi-level posterior element fractures from C5 to C7; hyperextension deformity; C6 segmental instability; syndesmophyte formation and facet joint ankylosis consistent with advanced AS. Cervical MRI: longitudinal posterior epidural hematoma extending from cervical to upper thoracic regions, causing significant spinal cord compression with T2 signal changes indicative of contusion and edema at C6–C7 levels. Cardiac workup (ECG, echocardiogram, troponins) and head CT were unremarkable. The patient’s motor function rapidly declined after admission, with bilateral lower extremity strength progressing to grade 0/5. Based on the presentation and available data, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next appropriate management step?
gold_diagnosis: Ankylosing spondylitis complicated by traumatic cervical spine fracture (C5–C7) and cervicothoracic spinal epidural hematoma causing incomplete spinal cord injury

answer_key.diagnosis: Ankylosing spondylitis complicated by traumatic cervical spine fracture (C5–C7) and cervicothoracic spinal epidural hematoma causing incomplete spinal cord injury

Cervical fracture-dislocation in AS with epidural hematoma

answer_key.next_management_step: Emergent combined anterior cervical corpectomy/fusion and posterior laminectomy with evacuation of the epidural hematoma and instrumented stabilization.

challenge_prompt:
A 56-year-old Asian female farmer with no significant past medical history (except chronic hepatitis B surface antigen carriage without prior treatment) and no family history of autoimmune disease presented with recurrent high fever, sore throat, and diffuse joint pain initially without swelling since early 2023. Over time, joint swelling developed and pain worsened. In March 2023, labs showed anemia, elevated inflammatory markers, negative ANA, and markedly elevated ferritin (>33,511 ng/mL). Bone marrow biopsy was reactive without malignancy. Ultrasound showed multiple enlarged superficial lymph nodes; left axillary lymph node biopsy confirmed reactive lymphadenitis. She was treated with prednisone and discharged, but did not follow up. She was re-hospitalized in October 2023 for recurrent fever, arthralgia, fatigue; persistent hyponatremia, anemia, positive EBV antibodies; bone marrow and PET-CT unremarkable. Discharged after symptomatic treatment.

In March 2024, she was admitted after a seizure with altered consciousness. On examination: temperature 39.2°C, BP 97/64, HR 137, RR 24, O2 sat 87% on room air. Coarse breath sounds with bilateral moist rales. Both wrists and knees swollen, tender, limited range of motion. Neurologic: GCS 12 (E3V4M5), pupils equal round 2mm with sluggish light reflexes, symmetrically decreased muscle strength grade 4, positive meningeal signs.

Laboratory (March 2024): leukocytosis (WBC elevated), anemia, elevated ferritin. Serum electrolytes: potassium 3.09 mmol/L, sodium 128.7 mmol/L, chloride 95.4 mmol/L, calcium 1.80 mmol/L. LDH 877.5 U/L, albumin 19.9 g/L, AST 44 U/L. BNP 3921 pg/mL. Inflammatory markers: IL-6 105 pg/mL, procalcitonin 0.645 ng/mL. IgG 23.94 g/L. Autoimmune markers: ANA negative, RF negative, anti-CCP negative, ANCA negative, ENA panel negative. Anti-SP100 positive. Endocrine: markedly decreased serum ACTH, other endocrine parameters normal. HBV DNA 9.86 × 10² IU/mL.

CSF: opening pressure 140 mmH2O; routine analysis normal; bacterial culture negative. mNGS of CSF: no detectable DNA-based pathogens. Autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel (21 antibodies) negative. Anti-GFAP and anti-MBP negative. CSF flow cytometry: 500 nucleated cells, predominantly T lymphocytes, no CD19+ B cells, CD4/CD8 ratio 1.56.

Imaging: Chest CT: bilateral pulmonary infiltrates with multifocal ground-glass opacities and interstitial thickening, multiple enlarged mediastinal, supraclavicular, and axillary lymph nodes. Brain MRI: diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement. Pituitary MRI: partially empty sella. ECG: sinus tachycardia. Echo: left atrial enlargement, mild mitral and tricuspid regurgitation. Abdominal and urinary tract ultrasound normal.

Additional workup: multiple blood cultures negative, respiratory pathogen nucleic acid tests negative, CMV DNA and EBV DNA negative, TB screening (PPD, T-SPOT) negative. Bone marrow aspiration and lymph node biopsy from earlier showed no malignancy. PET-CT (external) unremarkable.
gold_diagnosis: Adult-onset Still’s disease (AOSD) with aseptic meningitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Adult-onset Still’s disease (AOSD) with aseptic meningitis
answer_key.etiology: Autoinflammatory disorder of unknown etiology, likely driven by IL-1 and IL-6 dysregulation
answer_key.next_management_step: Start intravenous methylprednisolone and consider intrathecal methotrexate/dexamethasone for CNS involvement

Arthritis (swollen, tender wrists and knees)

Negative ANA, RF, ANCA, anti-CCP

Sore throat
Negative infectious workup (blood cultures, CSF cultures/mNGS negative)

challenge_prompt: A 48-year-old male with a history of untreated hypertension and 30 pack-year tobacco use presented with 48 hours of fluctuating right-sided weakness and speech difficulty. He took no medications and had no prior history of hypercholesterolemia, coronary artery disease, or surgeries; he had not seen a primary care physician in three years. In the emergency department, the patient had right-sided weakness, aphasia, and right homonymous hemianopsia. Vital signs: blood pressure 140/85 mmHg, pulse 80 bpm, respirations 16/min, temperature 37.1°C, oxygen saturation 97%. Neurologic examination in the sitting position revealed right facial droop, expressive aphasia, 1/5 muscle strength in the right upper and lower extremities, 1/4 brachioradial, biceps, and patellar reflexes, diminished pinprick sensation on the right, downgoing right toe, and right homonymous hemianopsia. NIHSS score was 15. Laboratory results: white blood cell count 7400/mm³, hemoglobin 15.7 g/dL, hematocrit 47%, platelet count 360,000/mm³, BUN 17 mg/dL, creatinine 1.0 mg/dL, potassium 4.1 mEq/L, sodium 138 mEq/L, chloride 97 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, PTT 27 sec, PT 11.4 sec, INR 1.1, D-dimer 1.4 ng/mL, fibrinogen 227 mg/dL. Lipid panel: total cholesterol 200 mg/dL, LDL-C 170 mg/dL, HDL-C 22 mg/dL, triglycerides 160 mg/dL. Albumin, ALT, AST, alkaline phosphatase, and urinalysis were normal. While in the CT scanner, the patient noted complete resolution of symptoms upon assuming a supine position; symptoms returned when he sat up. CT brain without contrast showed no acute hemorrhage. CT angiography revealed a cervical left internal carotid artery occlusion with retrograde flow through the left ophthalmic artery into the internal carotid terminus where it joins the left middle cerebral artery. Additional findings included severe atherosclerotic narrowing of the right vertebral artery, a congenitally narrow right anterior cerebral artery, left A1 segment dominant, and right A1 segment hypoplastic. CT cerebral perfusion demonstrated a large penumbra of at-risk brain involving the entire left cerebrum (mean transit time 12–14 seconds on the left vs normal on the right), affecting the left middle cerebral artery territory and bilateral anterior cerebral artery territories.
gold_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke due to left cervical internal carotid artery occlusion (large vessel occlusion) with positional cerebral hypoperfusion.

answer_key.diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke due to left cervical internal carotid artery occlusion (large vessel occlusion) with positional cerebral hypoperfusion.
answer_key.etiology: Atherosclerotic plaque rupture/thrombosis leading to occlusion of the left internal carotid artery.
answer_key.next_management_step: Carotid artery stenting to restore antegrade flow and resolve the flow-limiting lesion.
required_findings: Complete resolution of neurologic symptoms when lying flat (supine) and recurrence upon sitting.
Right-sided weakness, aphasia, and right homonymous hemianopsia with NIHSS 15.
CT angiography showing cervical left internal carotid artery occlusion with retrograde flow through the ophthalmic artery.
CT perfusion demonstrating a large left hemispheric penumbra with prolonged mean transit time (12–14 seconds).
Absence of acute intracranial hemorrhage on non-contrast CT.
Left A1 segment dominance and right A1 hypoplasia.
Severe atherosclerotic narrowing of the right vertebral artery.
Congenitally narrow right anterior cerebral artery.
optional_findings: History of untreated hypertension and 30 pack-year smoking.
No prior primary care in 3 years.
Normal complete blood count and metabolic panel.
Right toe downgoing on plantar stimulation.
Diminished pinprick sensation on right side.

provenance.title: Heads Down for Acute Stroke: Supine Position for Acute Large Vessel Occlusion in Ischemic Stroke

challenge_prompt:
A 25-year-old man presented to the emergency department with agitation, disorganized behavior, confusion, and dysarthria. He had previously undergone non-contrast cerebral MRI and EEG as an outpatient for seizures that were deemed nonepileptic; both were normal. He had been prescribed escitalopram up to 20 mg daily by an outpatient psychiatrist without improvement. The parents denied any history of drug misuse. Initial differential diagnosis was brief psychotic disorder. He was started on risperidone 2 mg daily, diazepam 15 mg daily, and chlorpromazine 100 mg daily and admitted to the psychiatric ward. Routine blood work (including CBC, renal, hepatic, thyroid function) was normal except for dyslipidemia, hyperuricemia, and elevated ESR.

During days 1–6, his condition worsened: agitation and disorganization persisted, dysarthria continued, and he developed perioral dyskinesia that affected his speech. He remained disoriented to time, place, and family members. His family reported memory problems for months: he would call them repeatedly and ask to speak to his mother without remembering he had already done so. He also experienced auditory and visual hallucinations. Risperidone was increased to 8 mg daily without response, and multiple injectable antipsychotics were given for agitation.

An EEG on day 7 showed artifacts and no epileptic discharges. CPK level was 2459 U/L (with normal kidney function). Hydration was started. Diazepam and chlorpromazine were discontinued on day 8 as his consciousness level began to decrease (GCS dropped, but not comatose), and CPK fell to 1430 U/L.

By day 14, he developed mutism, stupor, waxy flexibility, posturing, catalepsy, rigidity, mannerisms, stereotypes, and other catatonic signs. Risperidone was discontinued, and a session of electroconvulsive therapy was performed without clinical improvement.

A second EEG on day 15 revealed moderate diffuse cerebral dysfunction with generalized slowing and attenuation of the basal rhythm, consisting of theta and delta waves; no epileptic seizures were recorded. An injected cerebral CT scan showed no abnormalities; a cerebral MRI could not be performed due to a technical issue.

That night, the patient had a first-ever generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting 10 minutes. No medications were given.

CSF analysis revealed lymphocytic pleocytosis and was positive for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies; serum also tested positive for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies.
gold_diagnosis: Anti-NMDA receptor autoimmune encephalitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Anti-NMDA receptor autoimmune encephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Autoantibodies against the GluN1 subunit of the NMDA receptor
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis (IgG index, oligoclonal bands, and anti-NMDA receptor antibodies); if consistent with probable autoimmune encephalitis, start high-dose corticosteroids (e.g., intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily) and consider plasma exchange or IVIG.
required_findings: 25-year-old male
Subacute onset of agitation, disorganized behavior, confusion, and dysarthria
Development of perioral dyskinesia
Catatonia (mutism, stupor, waxy flexibility, posturing, catalepsy, rigidity, mannerisms, stereotypes)
Generalized tonic-clonic seizure
Memory problems (repeated calling without remembering)
Worsening despite high-dose risperidone (8 mg) and multiple injectable antipsychotics
EEG showing moderate diffuse cerebral dysfunction with generalized slowing (theta and delta waves)
optional_findings: Normal outpatient cerebral MRI and EEG
Normal injected cerebral CT scan during admission
Elevated CPK 2459 U/L after antipsychotic injections, later dropping to 1430 U/L
Gradual loss of consciousness with decreased GCS
Dyslipidemia, hyperuricemia, elevated ESR on admission blood work

provenance.title: Autoimmune Psychosis Exists: Early Diagnosis of an Anti‐NMDA Receptor Autoimmune Encephalitis Presenting as First‐Episode Psychosis in a 25‐Year‐Old Male—A Case Report

mend_provenance.added: CSF analysis revealed lymphocytic pleocytosis and was positive for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies; serum also tested positive for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies.
missing_discriminators: Anti-NMDA receptor antibody test result in serum or CSF

challenge_prompt: A 61-year-old male with a 19-year history of relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (MS) and severe eosinophilic asthma presents for evaluation. His MS treatment includes dimethyl fumarate from 2016 to 2017 and teriflunomide since 2019. For asthma, he has been on mepolizumab 100 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks since 2020, achieving good control with only one oral corticosteroid-requiring exacerbation in the past year. Pulmonary monitoring with spirometry and Asthma Control Test is stable. In August 2024, he experienced an acute MS relapse with postural instability, only partially responsive to intravenous corticosteroids. Neurological examination reveals an EDSS score of 4.0. Cognitive assessment using BICAMS shows a decline in episodic memory and lexical access compared to prior assessments. Brain MRI shows no new T2-weighted lesions and no gadolinium-enhancing lesions. Baseline laboratory studies show normal serum IgG levels and a B-lymphocyte count within the normal range. The patient has received standard recommended immunizations. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis with active disease (relapse and cognitive decline) despite teriflunomide therapy

answer_key.diagnosis: Relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis with active disease (relapse and cognitive decline) despite teriflunomide therapy
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune demyelination of the central nervous system
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate ofatumumab, a human anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody, while continuing mepolizumab for asthma control.

Current DMT: teriflunomide (since 2019)

Cognitive decline in episodic memory and lexical access on BICAMS
Brain MRI with no new T2 or gadolinium-enhancing lesions
