Baseline normal IgG and B-lymphocyte count
Up-to-date immunizations
optional_findings: Mepolizumab started in 2020 for frequent asthma exacerbations
Only one asthma exacerbation in the past year

provenance.title: Combined biologic therapy targeting B cells and eosinophils in relapsing multiple sclerosis and severe asthma: a case report of ofatumumab and mepolizumab

challenge_prompt:
A 46-year-old woman presented with a sudden-onset fall accompanied by forced rightward gaze deviation, nausea, and vertigo. She had no history of hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, smoking, alcohol, or liver disease. NIHSS was 3 (gaze deviation 2, mild ataxia 1). Oculocephalic maneuver overcame the gaze preference, confirming cortical origin. MRI brain revealed multiple acute infarcts: diffusion restriction in bilateral cerebellar hemispheres, left parieto-occipital, and right parieto-frontal regions, involving anterior and posterior circulations. CT angiography showed only moderate stenosis (<50%) in the V4 segment of the vertebral artery, deemed insufficient to explain the multifocal infarcts. Transthoracic echocardiogram was unremarkable. A 30-day outpatient cardiac event monitor detected no paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. Transesophageal echocardiogram with bubble study was negative for intracardiac thrombus, vegetation, shunt, or patent foramen ovale. Laboratory studies: D-dimer 2.5 mg/L FEU (reference <0.5), AST 120 U/L, ALT 145 U/L, ALP 220 U/L, total bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 1.5 mg/dL. Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated multiple hypoechoic foci with interspersed hyperechoic areas and poorly defined borders in the right hepatic lobe, the largest measuring 50 × 45 mm. Contrast-enhanced abdominal CT showed mass-like lesions with arterial phase hyperenhancement and washout on venous phase.

Based on the above presentation and workup, what is the most likely diagnosis?
gold_diagnosis: Hepatocellular carcinoma (occult malignancy presenting as embolic stroke of undetermined source)

answer_key.diagnosis: Hepatocellular carcinoma (occult malignancy presenting as embolic stroke of undetermined source)
answer_key.etiology: Cancer-associated hypercoagulability from hepatocellular carcinoma leading to multifocal embolic strokes
answer_key.next_management_step: Referral to hepatobiliary surgery/oncology for staging and treatment planning of hepatocellular carcinoma.
required_findings: Multifocal acute infarcts in multiple vascular territories without a large-vessel or cardioembolic source (ESUS)
D-dimer markedly elevated at 5× upper limit of normal (2.5 mg/L FEU)
Elevated liver enzymes (AST 120, ALT 145, ALP 220) and hyperbilirubinemia (total 2.1, direct 1.5)
Abdominal ultrasound showing multiple hypoechoic foci with interspersed hyperechoic areas in right hepatic lobe, largest 50×45 mm
Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrating mass-like lesions with arterial hyperenhancement and washout
Absence of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation on 30-day monitor, negative TEE for thrombus/vegetation/shunt
CT angiography with only moderate vertebral artery stenosis (<50%), insufficient to cause multifocal infarcts
Non-contrast MRI showing diffusion-restricted lesions in bilateral cerebellar and cerebral hemispheres
optional_findings: Age 46 without traditional vascular risk factors
NIHSS 3 with conjugate gaze deviation and mild ataxia
Fall described as sudden loss of postural control (drop attack)
Oculocephalic maneuver confirming cortical origin of gaze preference
FLAIR hyperintensity and ADC hypointensity corresponding to DWI lesions
Bubble study negative for right-to-left shunt
No headache or focal weakness on presentation

provenance.title: Stroke as an Initial Presentation of Hepatocellular Carcinoma: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 78-year-old male with a 6-year history of type 2 diabetes on Glimepiride presents to the emergency department after a 30-minute loss of consciousness, with 1-week history of dizziness and fever, and 4-month history of lower urinary tract symptoms. Examination reveals an ill-looking man with bilateral pitting pedal edema. Chest auscultation reveals crepitations over the right infra-axillary region. Notable features include coarse facial features: prominent forehead, protruding jaw, increased interdental spacing, macroglossia, and thick, rough skin. Arm span is 166.4 cm and height is 165.1 cm.

Laboratory findings: random blood sugar 13.84 mg/dL (low). White blood cell count 15.2 × 10^3/µL (82% lymphocytes), hemoglobin 10.22 g/dL, platelets 504 × 10^3/µL. Liver function tests: total bilirubin 0.85 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 0.28 mg/dL, ALT 15.20 U/L, AST 52.46 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 136.03 U/L. Renal function: urea 25.02 mg/dL, creatinine 1.23 mg/dL. Fasting blood sugar 100.2 mg/dL, postprandial blood sugar 142.6 mg/dL, HbA1c 6.1%. Tumor markers: CEA 121 ng/mL (normal <3.8), AFP 1000 IU/mL (normal <5.8), CA 19-9 154.3 U/mL (normal <39).

Hormonal workup: growth hormone 1.82 ng/mL (normal 0.02–3.893), IGF-1 218.6 ng/mL (normal 59–181), ACTH 19.80 pg/mL (normal 4.70–48.80), 8 AM serum cortisol 36.27 µg/dL (normal 8.7–22.4), prolactin 8.16 ng/mL (normal 2.6–13.1).

Ultrasound abdomen/pelvis: heterogeneously hypoechoic, lobulated solid lesion in the right lobe of the liver measuring 9.4 × 7.3 cm with minimal vascularity, suggestive of neoplasm. Simple hepatic cyst in left lobe and left renal cortical cyst also noted.

Given acromegaly suspicion, brain MRI with contrast was performed: abnormal enhancing thickening of the pituitary infundibulum with post-gadolinium enhancement; no evidence of pituitary macroadenoma or microadenoma. Differential considerations included pituicytoma of the infundibulum and pituitary granular cell tumor.

Contrast-enhanced CT of abdomen and pelvis: large lobulated liver lesion measuring 16.1 × 13.7 × 15.3 cm (AP × TR × CC) with an enhancing capsule, along with multiple enhancing subpleural-based nodules in both lung parenchyma, consistent with pulmonary metastases. Adrenal glands appeared normal.

Based on the clinical presentation and above findings, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?
gold_diagnosis: Paraneoplastic acromegaloid syndrome due to hepatic malignancy (likely hepatocellular carcinoma) with pulmonary metastases

answer_key.diagnosis: Paraneoplastic acromegaloid syndrome due to hepatic malignancy (likely hepatocellular carcinoma) with pulmonary metastases
aliases: Ectopic IGF-1 syndrome from hepatocellular carcinoma
answer_key.etiology: Ectopic secretion of insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) or IGF-1-like peptides by hepatocellular carcinoma, leading to acromegaloid features; associated ACTH-independent hypercortisolism possibly from ectopic cortisol or cortisol-stimulating factors from the tumor.
answer_key.next_management_step: Obtain tissue biopsy of the hepatic lesion (e.g., ultrasound-guided liver biopsy) for histopathological confirmation and management.
required_findings: IGF-1 218.6 ng/mL (elevated) with normal growth hormone 1.82 ng/mL
Large liver mass (16.1 × 13.7 × 15.3 cm) with enhancing capsule and elevated tumor markers (AFP 1000 IU/mL, CEA 121 ng/mL, CA 19-9 154.3 U/mL)
Multiple pulmonary nodules consistent with metastases
Clinical acromegaloid features: coarse facies, prominent forehead, prognathism, interdental spacing, macroglossia, thick skin
Elevated 8 AM cortisol 36.27 µg/dL with normal ACTH 19.80 pg/mL
MRI brain: pituitary infundibular thickening without macro- or microadenoma
Normal adrenal morphology on CT
Arm span to height ratio >1 (166.4 cm vs 165.1 cm) suggesting acromegalic growth
optional_findings: Normal liver enzymes (ALT 15.20 U/L) with slightly elevated AST (52.46 U/L)

provenance.title: Acromegaly in an elderly male with hepatic malignancy: a case report of a diagnostic dilemma

challenge_prompt:
Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

An 82-year-old man with a history of arterial hypertension and a previous episode of herpes zoster infection two years earlier presented with high fever (38.7°C), confusion, and reduced responsiveness that progressively worsened over 24 hours. He had no recent vaccinations, respiratory or gastrointestinal prodromal symptoms, or travel abroad; there was no known exposure to infectious diseases.

On admission, he was somnolent but arousable to painful stimuli, disoriented, and unable to follow commands. Pupils were isochoric and reactive to light; cranial nerve examination was unremarkable. Muscle tone was diffusely reduced, deep tendon reflexes were decreased, and plantar responses were bilaterally extensor. No meningeal signs were present. Cardiovascular, pulmonary, and abdominal examinations were normal.

Initial laboratory tests revealed leukocytosis (12.4 × 10⁹/L) and elevated C-reactive protein (8.6 mg/dL). Renal and hepatic function tests, electrolytes, and blood glucose were within reference ranges. Empirical antibiotic therapy with piperacillin–tazobactam was started for a suspected urinary tract infection. However, his level of consciousness deteriorated rapidly to a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 3 (E1V1M1) within 24 hours, requiring transfer to the intensive care unit, endotracheal intubation, and mechanical ventilation.

Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed mild lymphocytic pleocytosis (25 cells/mm³), elevated protein (112 mg/dL), and normal glucose (64 mg/dL). Polymerase chain reaction for herpes simplex virus types 1 and 2, varicella-zoster virus, cytomegalovirus, enteroviruses, and JC virus were all negative. An extensive serum and CSF autoimmune panel was negative, including antibodies against NMDA receptor, MOG, GQ1b, AQP4, LGI1, CASPR2, and paraneoplastic antibodies (anti-Hu, anti-Yo, anti-Ri, anti-Ma2, anti-amphiphysin). Whole-body computed tomography revealed no neoplastic lesions. Blood and urine cultures remained sterile.

Brain magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated bilateral thalamic hyperintensities on T2-weighted and fluid-attenuated inversion recovery sequences, with restricted diffusion on diffusion-weighted imaging and corresponding hypointensity on apparent diffusion coefficient maps, consistent with cytotoxic edema. No contrast enhancement was observed. Magnetic resonance angiography showed normal patency of the main intracranial vessels, excluding vascular occlusion or vasculitis. A follow-up MRI 10 days later showed persistent bilateral thalamic lesions with no new abnormalities.
gold_diagnosis: Epstein-Barr virus encephalitis (bilateral thalamic encephalitis due to Epstein-Barr virus)

answer_key.diagnosis: Epstein-Barr virus encephalitis (bilateral thalamic encephalitis due to Epstein-Barr virus)
answer_key.etiology: Epstein-Barr virus
answer_key.next_management_step: Send cerebrospinal fluid for Epstein-Barr virus DNA by PCR and serum for EBV serology; consider initiating acyclovir and corticosteroids empirically.
required_findings: Bilateral thalamic hyperintensities on T2/FLAIR with restricted diffusion (DWI) and corresponding hypointensity on ADC maps, consistent with cytotoxic edema, and no contrast enhancement.
Normal magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) excluding vascular occlusion or vasculitis.
CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis (25 cells/mm³) with elevated protein (112 mg/dL) and normal glucose (64 mg/dL).
Negative PCR for herpes simplex virus types 1 and 2, varicella-zoster virus, cytomegalovirus, enteroviruses, and JC virus.
Negative extensive autoimmune and paraneoplastic panels (anti-NMDA receptor, MOG, GQ1b, AQP4, LGI1, CASPR2, anti-Hu, anti-Yo, anti-Ri, anti-Ma2, anti-amphiphysin).
Rapid deterioration to deep coma (GCS 3) within 24 hours of presentation.
Elderly (82 years) immunocompetent patient with no history of immunosuppression.
No evidence of neoplastic, vascular, or systemic infection on whole-body CT and cultures.
optional_findings: Previous episode of herpes zoster two years prior.
Normal renal, hepatic, electrolyte, and glucose levels.
Persistent bilateral thalamic lesions on follow-up MRI at 10 days.
No contrast enhancement on brain MRI.

provenance.title: Bilateral thalamic encephalitis due to Epstein–Barr virus in an elderly patient: a case report and literature review

challenge_prompt: A 22-year-old Caucasian male with no significant past medical or family history presents with 5 days of progressive neurological symptoms. He initially developed sharp, band-like thoracic pain at the T6-T8 dermatomes. Within 48 hours, ascending paresthesias appeared in both feet and spread to the umbilical level. By day three, he experienced bilateral lower limb weakness that rapidly progressed, requiring assistance for ambulation. On examination, vital signs are stable. Upper limb strength is normal (MRC 5/5). Lower limbs demonstrate weakness (MRC 3/5) with increased tone and spasticity. A well-defined sensory level is noted at T10, with absent light touch and pinprick sensation below this level. Vibration sense is reduced in the lower extremities, and proprioception is impaired in the toes. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk in the lower limbs (3+ to 4+) with bilateral Babinski signs, while upper limb reflexes are normal. Abdominal reflexes are absent below T10. Cranial nerves are intact, and there are no visual or cerebellar deficits. Gait is spastic and paraparetic, requiring bilateral support. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis shows oligoclonal bands restricted to CSF (not present in serum). Serum studies: negative for aquaporin-4 and myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (MOG) antibodies. Spinal MRI: T2-weighted imaging reveals two short-segment intramedullary hyperintense lesions—an ovoid lesion at T1-T2 in the left posterolateral cord and another at T11 in the right posterolateral cord. Both lesions enhance with gadolinium. There is no cord swelling or longitudinally extensive lesion. Brain MRI: small periventricular lesion near the left frontal horn. Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Multiple sclerosis

answer_key.diagnosis: Multiple sclerosis
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune demyelinating disease of the central nervous system
answer_key.next_management_step: Administer high-dose intravenous corticosteroids (e.g., methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days)

challenge_prompt:
A 38-year-old woman with a history of depression on sertraline was admitted following an acute psychotic episode with severe agitation and aggression. She received intramuscular haloperidol 5 mg. Routine ECG showed inferolateral ST-segment elevation. She recalled a burning chest discomfort three days prior but was currently asymptomatic. She denied cardiovascular risk factors, recreational drug use, or family history of premature heart disease. Vital signs: BP 108/76 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, temperature 36.9°C, O2 saturation 97% on room air. Physical examination unremarkable. During admission, she had a second psychotic episode managed with lorazepam, promethazine, and aripiprazole. Laboratory investigations: troponin I 23,625 ng/L (peak 24,565 ng/L), creatine kinase 5,931 U/L, WCC 12.3 ×10^9/L, D-dimer 1,019 µg/L. Bedside echocardiography showed severe left ventricular systolic dysfunction with apical akinesia and aneurysmal morphology, estimated ejection fraction ~35%. Right ventricular function was preserved, no apical thrombus. Coronary CT angiography revealed a calcium score of 0, right coronary dominance, and no evidence of coronary plaque or stenosis in the LAD, LCx, or RCA.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy (stress-induced cardiomyopathy)

answer_key.diagnosis: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy (stress-induced cardiomyopathy)
answer_key.etiology: Catecholamine surge triggered by acute psychosis
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate guideline-directed medical therapy for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (e.g., beta-blocker and SGLT2 inhibitor such as bisoprolol and dapagliflozin), supportive care, continuous telemetry monitoring, and psychiatric follow-up

Coronary CT angiography: calcium score 0, no coronary plaque or stenosis

No cardiac symptoms at time of presentation (chest discomfort resolved)
Preserved right ventricular function and no apical thrombus on echo
optional_findings: Initial management with haloperidol 5 mg IM

challenge_prompt:
A 48-year-old male with a history of chronic low back pain with left lower extremity radiculopathy, and chronic abdominal pain from chronic pancreatitis, gastritis, esophagitis, and splenic venous thrombosis, and prior opioid addiction, underwent a spinal cord stimulation (SCS) trial and subsequent permanent implant. The two percutaneous leads were placed with the 0-position at the superior endplate of T5. Intraoperative findings included thoracic rotational scoliosis. Post-implant, he reported over 50% reduction in both abdominal and low back/leg pain, with some asymmetry in stimulation. Seven months later, after moving out of state, he experienced an abrupt loss of pain relief, with return of severe symptoms. Outside imaging studies showed interval caudal migration of both SCS leads down two vertebral levels, now at T7, and rightward displacement. Attempts at reprogramming by the manufacturer team found high impedance on the left-sided lead at contact positions 5-7 and 13-15. Reprogramming could not recapture the pain relief.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next best step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Spinal cord stimulator lead migration with resultant device failure

answer_key.diagnosis: Spinal cord stimulator lead migration with resultant device failure
answer_key.etiology: Mechanical complication of spinal cord stimulator due to lead migration, possibly exacerbated by thoracic rotational scoliosis
answer_key.next_management_step: Refer for surgical revision with paddle leads (or neurosurgeon consultation for lead replacement/repositioning)

Imaging shows interval caudal migration of both leads two vertebral levels (from T5 to T7) with rightward displacement

optional_findings: History of initial >50% pain reduction post-implant

challenge_prompt:
A 36-year-old male with multiple sclerosis on fingolimod therapy for 8 years presented with numerous painless gingival lesions. Intraoral examination revealed multiple sessile and pedunculated mucosal-colored lesions with a mildly papillomatous surface on the labial gingiva of both maxilla and mandible, and on the lingual anterior and posterior right mandibular gingiva. Lesions ranged from small nodules to coalescent plaques. No cutaneous, systemic, or syndromic abnormalities were noted. The clinical differential diagnosis included HPV-related papillomatosis, nevus unius lateris, acanthosis nigricans, and focal dermal hypoplasia syndrome.

An incisional biopsy of a mandibular gingival lesion was performed. Histopathology demonstrated parakeratinized stratified squamous epithelium with exophytic and acanthotic epithelial proliferation forming finger-like projections supported by thin fibrovascular cores. Koilocytosis was observed in the superficial spinous layer. In situ hybridization for high-risk HPV type 16 was negative. p16 immunohistochemistry was negative. PCR-based HPV DNA genotyping on FFPE tissue detected low-risk HPV types 6/11, and was negative for high-risk types 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 58. Of note, the patient had a history of small asymptomatic nodules on the lower lip and floor of mouth six years prior; biopsies at that time showed squamous papilloma (floor of mouth) and acanthosis with koilocytes (labial mucosa), but no medication history was documented then.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: HPV-related oral mucosal epithelial proliferation associated with fingolimod therapy (low-risk HPV types 6/11)

answer_key.diagnosis: HPV-related oral mucosal epithelial proliferation associated with fingolimod therapy (low-risk HPV types 6/11)
answer_key.etiology: Immunosuppression from fingolimod therapy leading to reactivation and proliferation of latent low-risk HPV 6/11 infection
answer_key.next_management_step: Consider discontinuation of fingolimod or switching to an alternative disease-modifying therapy, and monitor for lesion regression; lesion-directed therapy (e.g., surgical excision) may be considered if needed.

painless gingival lesions
multiple sessile and pedunculated mucosal-colored lesions with a mildly papillomatous surface on the gingiva
histopathology: parakeratinized stratified squamous epithelium with exophytic and acanthotic epithelial proliferation forming finger-like projections supported by thin fibrovascular cores
koilocytosis in the superficial spinous layer
in situ hybridization for high-risk HPV type 16 negative
p16 immunohistochemistry negative
PCR-based HPV DNA genotyping detected low-risk HPV types 6/11 and was negative for high-risk types 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 58
optional_findings: prior biopsies 6 years ago showing squamous papilloma and koilocytosis
lesions on labial and lingual gingiva of maxilla and mandible
clinical differential diagnosis included HPV-related papillomatosis

challenge_prompt: A 31-year-old Melanau lady, primigravida at 26 weeks of gestation, presented with sudden bilateral binocular diplopia upon waking, dizziness, nausea, and vomiting for 3 days. No diurnal variation. One week prior, she had an upper respiratory infection. She denied limb weakness, sensory loss, speech difficulty, or incontinence. She had diet-controlled gestational diabetes. Examination: uncomfortable, eyes closed, alert, BP 125/73 mmHg, HR 82, SpO2 100%, afebrile. Bilateral eye abduction was significantly restricted; mild restriction of adduction. Binocular diplopia in all gazes. No ptosis, proptosis, or nystagmus. Pupils equal and reactive; fundi normal with no papilledema. Facial strength and sensation normal. Limb tone, bulk, and power normal. Gait stable with one eye closed. Fatiguability test negative. Obstetric scan showed a singleton pregnancy at appropriate gestational age. CT brain/orbit at 60 hours from symptom onset showed no significant abnormality. Full blood count, renal profile, liver function, CRP, thyroid function, RPR, and HIV were all normal or negative; urine dipstick negative. She received intravenous thiamine without improvement. The next day, ophthalmoplegia worsened; she reported bilateral shoulder and arm weakness. Neurological exam then revealed reduced upper limb power, areflexia, and prominent ataxia. CSF analysis on day 5 of illness showed normal cell counts and protein, with an isolated elevated chloride level; no cytoalbuminologic dissociation. Nerve conduction studies and electromyography were unavailable. Based on the presentation and available findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?
gold_diagnosis: Miller Fisher syndrome

answer_key.next_management_step: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) therapy

Normal CT brain/orbit at 60 hours from symptom onset
No improvement with intravenous thiamine
Negative fatiguability test

challenge_prompt:
A 32-year-old Han Chinese male farmer presents with a 6-year history of progressive neurological and psychiatric symptoms. At age 26, he developed resting tremor and bradykinesia in the right limb. One year later, the tremor affected the left limb, and levodopa was started with good motor control. By age 28, gait instability and mild cognitive decline appeared. Brain MRI at that time showed mild cortical atrophy (Global Cortical Atrophy [GCA] score 1 bilaterally in frontal and temporal lobes). At age 29, he developed depression, anxiety, obsessive thoughts, severe psychomotor agitation, and violent behaviors. He was treated with levodopa/benserazide and rasagiline, but benefit was only temporary. By age 32, cognitive deterioration progressed, gait instability worsened with frequent falls, and he required bed rest or wheelchair. He also developed dysarthria, urinary incontinence, and myoclonus. There was no orthostatic hypotension, hyposmia, or rapid eye movement sleep behavior disorder. He is the second child of non-consanguineous healthy parents; his older sister is healthy, and there is no family history of neurological disorders.

Neurological examination: severe dysarthria; impaired orientation, memory, calculation, and comprehension; limited upward vertical gaze; rigidity in all limbs and trunk; normal muscle strength; inability to perform bilateral heel-knee-shin test; resting tremor and myoclonus in both limbs; unsteady, wide-based, slow gait; positive pull test; brisk tendon reflexes in upper limbs, active in lower limbs; bilateral Babinski sign negative; meningeal irritation signs negative. MDS-UPDRS score: 89. MMSE: 7/30.

Laboratory tests: normal complete blood count, urinalysis, stool routine, biochemical profile, homocysteine, ceruloplasmin, hepatitis B/C, syphilis, HIV, coagulation, and residual urine volume. Serum superoxide dismutase-1 (SOD1) was 236 U/mL (normal 110–215 U/mL). Interleukin-6 was 1.8 pg/mL (normal 0–7 pg/mL). Brain MRI at age 32: generalized brain atrophy, especially bilateral frontal, temporal, and parietal lobes, cortex, and hippocampus. GCA score was 2 bilaterally in frontal lobes and 3 bilaterally in temporal lobes. Susceptibility-weighted imaging was normal. Electroencephalography showed a moderately diffuse slow wave. Lumbar puncture: normal cell counts, protein, and negative autoimmune encephalitis antibodies.

Whole-exome sequencing identified compound heterozygous variants in the VPS13C gene: a nonsense variant c.1699C>T (p.Arg567*) on chromosome 15, and a large deletion (chr15:62156504–62352664) encompassing exons 13, 14, and 35. Both variants were novel and not found in population databases. The mother was an asymptomatic carrier of the nonsense variant, and the father was an asymptomatic carrier of the deletion, confirmed by Sanger sequencing and quantitative PCR, respectively.

Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next appropriate management step?
gold_diagnosis: VPS13C-related early-onset Parkinson disease (autosomal recessive)

answer_key.diagnosis: VPS13C-related early-onset Parkinson disease (autosomal recessive)
answer_key.etiology: Loss-of-function mutations in VPS13C leading to defective mitochondrial function and lysosomal lipid transport
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate symptomatic pharmacotherapy including levodopa/benserazide, rasagiline, donepezil, and quetiapine, and provide genetic counseling.
required_findings: Resting tremor and bradykinesia onset at age 26
Rapid progression with cognitive decline, psychiatric symptoms, myoclonus, and loss of motor benefit from levodopa
Brain MRI showing progressive cortical atrophy (GCA score 2 frontal, 3 temporal)
MMSE score 7/30
Serum SOD1 elevated at 236 U/mL (normal 110–215)
Whole-exome sequencing identified compound heterozygous VPS13C variants: c.1699C>T (p.Arg567*) and a large deletion (chr15:62156504–62352664)
Family history negative and parents non-consanguineous
Normal ceruloplasmin, negative autoimmune encephalitis antibodies, ruling out Wilson disease and immune-mediated encephalopathy
optional_findings: Brisk tendon reflexes in upper limbs

provenance.title: Two novel variants of VPS13C gene related Parkinsonism: A case report and literature review

challenge_prompt:
A 28-year-old female patient presented with unilateral facial numbness and tenderness involving the left midface and oral region. She reported that a miniscrew-assisted rapid palatal expansion (MARPE) appliance had been placed at an external dental clinic approximately 6 weeks before her initial visit and activated at 2 turns (approximately 0.27 mm) per day. About 5 weeks after appliance placement, she developed sensory disturbances in the left infraorbital region. The direction of activation was reversed at the same rate approximately 1 week later, leading to gradual symptom improvement. She noted partial relief in the palate and upper lip, but hypesthesia persisted in the left philtrum and zygomatic area, accompanied by palpation-induced pain in the left buccal mucosa and lower lip. Total expansion amount and screw specifications were not available.

At the initial visit, she reported numbness in the left zygomatic region, upper lip, philtrum, and palate, with palpation-induced pain in the left buccal mucosa and lower lip. The discomfort was dull and throbbing, triggered by touch but not thermal stimuli, rated 2–3 on the visual analog scale. She denied spontaneous pain, trauma, nocturnal awakening, or relief from NSAIDs. Medical, family, and psychosocial histories were unremarkable.

Clinical examination revealed normal oral function with painless maximum mouth opening of 55 mm. Palpation of temporomandibular joints and masticatory muscles was non-tender bilaterally, and no facial asymmetry or muscle weakness was noted.

Standard panoramic and Waters’ view radiographs showed no bony abnormalities or asymmetry; no cone-beam CT was performed. Quantitative sensory testing (QST) was conducted using a standardized orofacial neurosensory protocol, including contact threshold (CTH), direction discrimination (DIR), 2-point discrimination (2PD), pin-prick (PP), and cold perception. Compared with the contralateral side, the patient had marked sensory asymmetry in the left infraorbital nerve distribution: DIR accuracy was 30% (6/20) on the left vs 100% (20/20) on the right; 2PD was abnormal on the left (a single point was perceived as two points, while the right side had a normal threshold of 10 mm); PP threshold was elevated on the left (60 g) compared to the right (30 g); cold perception was reduced on the left; CTH showed no significant asymmetry.

Other possible causes of facial numbness were excluded: no history of recent local anesthesia near the infraorbital region, no systemic metabolic disorders, no evidence of infection or sinus pathology on examination or imaging, and no temporomandibular joint or masticatory muscle dysfunction.

Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis/etiology, and what is the next best management step?
gold_diagnosis: Infraorbital nerve neuropathy (trigeminal neuropathy) secondary to MARPE

answer_key.diagnosis: Infraorbital nerve neuropathy (trigeminal neuropathy) secondary to MARPE
aliases: Mechanical compressive neuropathy of infraorbital nerve
answer_key.etiology: Mechanical stress or compression of the infraorbital nerve from rapid palatal expansion forces
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate pharmacotherapy for neuropathic pain (e.g., gabapentin 300 mg once daily, titrated to three times daily, with or without a short course of corticosteroids such as prednisolone 20 mg/day for 5 days), and schedule close follow-up to monitor symptom progression. Consider adding low-dose amitriptyline if inadequate response.
required_findings: 28-year-old female with MARPE appliance placed 6 weeks prior
Activation at 2 turns/day; onset of left infraorbital sensory disturbances 5 weeks after placement
Reversal of activation led to partial improvement but persistent hypesthesia and pain
Numbness in left zygomatic, upper lip, philtrum, palate; dull throbbing pain, VAS 2-3
Normal panoramic and Waters’ view radiographs (no bony abnormalities)
QST: marked asymmetry, DIR 30% on left vs 100% on right, 2PD abnormal (single point felt as two), PP threshold elevated 60g vs 30g, cold perception reduced, CTH symmetric
No other identifiable cause: no trauma, infection, metabolic disease, TMD, or local anesthetic injury
Symptoms localized to distribution of infraorbital nerve (maxillary branch of trigeminal nerve)
optional_findings: Bilateral non-tender TMJ and masticatory muscles
No spontaneous pain or thermal trigger
Reversal of activation direction 1 week after symptom onset

provenance.title: Neuropathy of the infraorbital area following rapid palatal expansion: A case report

challenge_prompt:
A 69-year-old man presented with several months of progressive behavioral changes, including increasing irritability and difficulty organizing tasks. He also had intermittent headaches. Initial blood tests were normal, and cognitive-behavioral therapy was tried without improvement. One week prior to presentation, he developed left-sided facial weakness, left upper limb numbness, and mouth deviation. On the day of presentation, he had new left eyelid drooping and was more anxious and withdrawn. In the emergency department, he was alert and oriented with mild disinhibition. Cranial nerve examination showed left facial asymmetry and ptosis. There was mild sensory reduction in the left upper limb. The remainder of the neurological exam was non-focal. A non-contrast CT head showed a large right frontal mass with surrounding vasogenic edema and features of raised intracranial pressure. MRI confirmed a right frontal space-occupying lesion consistent with a primary intracranial neoplasm. What is the most appropriate next step in establishing the diagnosis?

A stereotactic brain biopsy revealed diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, consistent with primary central nervous system lymphoma.
gold_diagnosis: Primary central nervous system lymphoma (PCNSL)

answer_key.diagnosis: Primary central nervous system lymphoma (PCNSL)
answer_key.etiology: Primary extranodal non-Hodgkin lymphoma confined to brain/spinal cord/eyes without systemic involvement
answer_key.next_management_step: Brain biopsy for histopathological confirmation
required_findings: Progressive behavioral changes over months
Left-sided facial weakness and ptosis
Left upper limb numbness
Non-contrast CT head showing large right frontal mass with vasogenic edema
MRI confirming right frontal space-occupying lesion consistent with primary neoplasm
Lack of response to cognitive-behavioral therapy
optional_findings: Mild sensory reduction left upper limb

provenance.title: Behavioral Changes as the Initial Presentation of Primary Central Nervous System Lymphoma: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 46-year-old woman with a history of anxiety and scoliosis presents with progressive episodes of sleep paralysis, during which she remains aware, and intermittent gasping and choking with sleep. She endorses muscle spasticity and tightening. Certain lights, noises, and big open spaces trigger her muscle symptoms. She experiences fear with returning to sleep and increased anxiety leading up to sleep. She reports excessive daytime sleepiness. Three months prior, she had similar symptoms but without daytime sleepiness. She has difficulty maintaining balance and uses a walker. On examination, there is no muscle rigidity or spasms at rest. An overnight polysomnography shows some snoring but no evidence of sleep apnea. A multiple sleep latency test reveals she fell asleep quickly (mean latency 8 minutes) but did not enter rapid eye movement (REM) sleep in at least two naps, thus not meeting criteria for narcolepsy. Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next most appropriate diagnostic step?

Anti-GAD antibody testing was positive.
gold_diagnosis: Stiff person syndrome

answer_key.diagnosis: Stiff person syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Autoantibodies against glutamic acid decarboxylase (GAD) leading to impaired GABAergic inhibition
answer_key.next_management_step: Test for serum anti-GAD antibodies
required_findings: Progressive sleep paralysis with awareness
Muscle spasticity and tightening with triggers (lights, noises, open spaces)
Excessive daytime sleepiness and hypersomnia
Short sleep latency on MSLT without REM entry (mean 8 min, no REM in ≥2 naps)
No sleep apnea on polysomnography
Use of walker for balance impairment
History of anxiety and scoliosis
Gasping/choking episodes during sleep
optional_findings: Similar symptoms 3 months earlier without daytime sleepiness
Examination shows no resting muscle rigidity or spasms

provenance.title: Stiff Person Syndrome: A Case Report

mend_provenance.added: Anti-GAD antibody testing was positive.
missing_discriminators: anti-GAD antibody serology result

challenge_prompt:
A 5-year-old girl with consanguineous parents presented with a syndromic neurocutaneous syndrome of pharmacoresistant seizures, developmental regression, and intractable xerosis from infancy. She was born at term with normal birth weight and no perinatal complications. Family history: a brother with seizures and global developmental delay, and two other brothers who died in early infancy with ichthyotic skin changes. Parents are first-degree cousins.

Physical examination: dry scaly skin on trunk and extremities, most marked on extensor surfaces; scales fine to coarse, adherent, non-inflammatory. Hyperlinearity of palms and mild plantar keratoderma. Neurological examination: microcephaly, axial hypotonia with peripheral hypertonia, brisk deep tendon reflexes, poor visual tracking; fundoscopy unremarkable.

Developmental history: never developed expressive speech; unable to control head at 4 months, sat at 11 months. By age 3, loss of social interaction and increasing aggressive conduct. Remains non-verbal and unable to walk independently.

Seizure history: first seizure at 13 months; generalized tonic–clonic seizures; multiple antiepileptic drugs (clobazam, levetiracetam, valproate) failed to provide long-lasting seizure control.

EEG: multifocal epileptiform discharges with frontal-central preponderance. Brain MRI: high signal intensity adjacent to posterior horns of lateral ventricles; brainstem and cerebellum appeared normal, with no evidence of Molar Tooth Sign or hindbrain malformations.

Whole exome sequencing identified:
- Homozygous missense variant in CC2D2A: c.1268G>A (p.Arg423Gln)
- Heterozygous variant in ABCA12: c.5779-1G>A
- Heterozygous variant in DOCK6: c.2520dupT
- A 1 Mbp heterozygous interstitial deletion at 14q31.3–q32.11 encompassing FOXG1, BCL11B, and other genes.

Based on the above, what is the most likely primary diagnosis?
gold_diagnosis: CC2D2A-related Joubert syndrome (Joubert syndrome type 9)

answer_key.diagnosis: CC2D2A-related Joubert syndrome (Joubert syndrome type 9)
answer_key.etiology: Homozygous CC2D2A c.1268G>A (p.Arg423Gln) variant, with modifying heterozygous variants in ABCA12 and DOCK6, and a 14q31.3–q32.11 deletion.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate multidisciplinary supportive care (seizure management, skin care, developmental therapies) and provide genetic counseling.

Ichthyosis (intractable xerosis from infancy with dry scaly skin)
Pharmacoresistant generalized tonic–clonic seizures starting at 13 months
Severe global developmental delay (non-verbal, unable to walk, microcephaly)
Brain MRI: high signal adjacent to posterior horns, normal brainstem/cerebellum, no Molar Tooth Sign
EEG: multifocal epileptiform discharges with frontal-central preponderance
Family history of sibling with seizures/developmental delay and two infant deaths with ichthyosis
WES: homozygous CC2D2A c.1268G>A variant
optional_findings: Hyperlinearity of palms and mild plantar keratoderma
Axial hypotonia with peripheral hypertonia and brisk reflexes
Poor visual tracking with normal fundoscopy

challenge_prompt:
Based on the following clinical scenario, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next best step in management?

A 36-year-old African American female with a known history of SLE (not following with a rheumatologist and off medication for years) presented with a several-day history of fever, malaise, cough, arthralgia, and myalgia. She was febrile and tachycardic and had leukocytosis (WBC 21 K/UL). Chest X-ray revealed right lower lobe consolidation, consistent with sepsis secondary to focal pneumonia. She was started on antibiotics, and her condition initially improved.

However, on hospital day 3, she developed acute confusion, hallucinations (describing creatures crawling up the walls), and worsening mental status, eventually requiring intubation. CT of the head was unremarkable, MRI of the brain was unremarkable, EEG was negative, and antibiotics were adjusted for possible medication-induced encephalopathy. Though she was successfully extubated, her mental status remained impaired despite radiographic resolution of pneumonia.

Rheumatology was consulted due to her SLE history. Labs revealed mildly elevated dsDNA, positive anti-Smith antibodies, positive RNP antibodies, and hypocomplementemia. CSF analysis showed elevated protein, increased oligoclonal bands, and elevated IgG synthesis rate; cultures were negative, and further CSF studies ruled out any infectious source, with full viral CSF panel including VDRL, varicella zoster virus, herpes simplex virus, Epstein-Barr virus, cytomegalovirus, rapid plasmin reagin, and JC virus all negative.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next appropriate step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Neuropsychiatric systemic lupus erythematosus (NPSLE) presenting as lupus psychosis

answer_key.diagnosis: Neuropsychiatric systemic lupus erythematosus (NPSLE) presenting as lupus psychosis
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune-mediated neuropsychiatric manifestation of systemic lupus erythematosus, likely involving autoantibodies and inflammatory cytokines disrupting neuronal function.
answer_key.next_management_step: Start pulse-dose corticosteroids (e.g., methylprednisolone) and consider adding cyclophosphamide if inadequate response.
required_findings: Known history of SLE and not on immunosuppression
Acute onset of confusion and visual hallucinations requiring intubation
Normal brain CT, MRI, and EEG
Mildly elevated anti-dsDNA antibodies
Positive anti-Smith and RNP antibodies
Hypocomplementemia
CSF with elevated protein, increased oligoclonal bands, elevated IgG synthesis rate, and negative infectious panel
optional_findings: Radiographic resolution of pneumonia before mental status changes
Successful extubation but persistent mental status impairment

challenge_prompt:
A 61-year-old African American female presented with progressive proximal muscle weakness, generalized myalgias, and dysphagia to solids and liquids of 2 months duration. She reported difficulty combing her hair and rising from a seated position, and had lost 15 pounds over the same period. She denied fever, rash, Raynaud’s phenomenon, or arthralgia. Past medical history included hypertension, uterine fibroids, and diabetes mellitus.

Physical examination revealed proximal muscle weakness: 3/5 in the upper extremities and 4/5 in the lower extremities. Palpable non-tender inguinal lymphadenopathy was noted. No cutaneous lesions were present, and the remainder of the systemic examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory investigations showed markedly elevated creatinine phosphokinase (10,510 IU/L), aspartate aminotransferase (400 IU/L), lactate dehydrogenase (912 IU/L), and aldolase (68.1 IU/L). Antinuclear antibody (ANA) was positive at a titer of 1:1280 with a homogeneous/speckled pattern. Myositis-specific antibody panel detected anti‑Mi‑2α and anti‑EJ antibodies. Other autoimmune markers (anti‑dsDNA, anti‑RNP, SSA/SSB, Scl‑70) were non‑reactive.

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) with contrast of the thighs performed on day 4 demonstrated diffuse, symmetric muscle edema and enhancement throughout all compartments, consistent with active myositis. Abdominal ultrasound revealed fatty hepatomegaly, cholelithiasis, and multiple midline masses most consistent with lymphadenopathy. Computed tomography of the chest, mammography, and echocardiography were normal.

Electromyography (EMG) on day 16 showed short‑duration, short‑amplitude motor unit action potentials and spontaneous fibrillation potentials, consistent with an inflammatory myopathy.

A core biopsy of a right groin lymph node, performed on day 10 and reported on day 28, confirmed follicular lymphoma, grade 1–2 of 3. Immunohistochemistry was positive for CD20, CD10, BCL‑2, BCL‑6, and PAX‑5; Ki‑67 proliferation index was low (1–2+ out of 4+). Flow cytometry of peripheral blood and lymph node aspirate identified a monoclonal B‑cell population (9%) expressing CD19, CD20, CD10, and CD200, and lacking CD5 and CD23, consistent with a follicle center B‑cell lymphoproliferative disorder.

Positron emission tomography (PET) showed extensive hypermetabolic lymphadenopathy in the cervical, thoracic, abdominal, and pelvic regions, with diffuse splenic involvement.

A muscle biopsy was deferred as the patient declined.

Based on the available information, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next appropriate step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Paraneoplastic inflammatory myopathy associated with follicular lymphoma (immune myositis complicating follicular lymphoma)

answer_key.diagnosis: Paraneoplastic inflammatory myopathy associated with follicular lymphoma (immune myositis complicating follicular lymphoma)
aliases: Cancer-associated myositis due to follicular lymphoma
answer_key.etiology: Paraneoplastic immune-mediated myositis triggered by follicular lymphoma
answer_key.next_management_step: Oncology referral for lymphoma-directed therapy (rituximab-based immunotherapy) and continuation of immunosuppression with methotrexate for myositis.
required_findings: Progressive proximal muscle weakness and dysphagia
Creatine phosphokinase 10,510 IU/L, AST 400 IU/L, LDH 912 IU/L, aldolase 68.1 IU/L
ANA 1:1280 with homogeneous/speckled pattern
Anti-Mi-2α and anti-EJ antibodies positive
MRI thighs: diffuse symmetric muscle edema and enhancement consistent with active myositis
EMG: short-duration, short-amplitude motor unit potentials and fibrillation potentials
Core lymph node biopsy: follicular lymphoma, grade 1–2/3, CD20+, CD10+, BCL-2+
PET: extensive hypermetabolic lymphadenopathy and diffuse splenic involvement
optional_findings: Weight loss of 15 pounds over 2 months
Absence of rash, Raynaud’s phenomenon, or arthralgia
Abdominal US: fatty hepatomegaly and midline masses consistent with lymphadenopathy
Flow cytometry: CD19+, CD20+, CD10+, CD200+, CD5‑, CD23‑ monoclonal B cells

challenge_prompt:
A 77-year-old woman presented with recurrent episodes of vertigo, nausea, vomiting, and brief binocular diplopia. Symptoms began in January 2022, worsened in supine position, and did not improve with eye closure. Initial neurological examination revealed rapidly fatigable fine nystagmus without other focal deficits. Non-contrast head CT showed no acute focal abnormalities.

In July–August 2022, contrast-enhanced brain MRI demonstrated diffuse thickening of the dura mater up to 9 mm with slightly nodular contours and pathological, mildly heterogeneous enhancement after intravenous contrast. The cerebellar tonsils extended approximately 4 mm below the McRae line into the spinal canal. Cervical spine MRI without contrast suggested a small degree of dural thickening in the upper cervical region.

She was admitted in September 2022 for further evaluation. Neurological examination showed an unsteady Romberg test with tendency to fall backward and to the left. She reported weight loss of 3–5 kg over several months.

Extensive laboratory workup included:

- Autoimmune/inflammatory: rheumatoid factor, anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP), antinuclear antibodies (ANA), anti-double-stranded DNA (anti-dsDNA), antimitochondrial (AMA), anti-smooth muscle (ASMA), liver-kidney microsomal (LKM) antibodies, perinuclear and cytoplasmic antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (pANCA, cANCA), and angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) were all negative or normal.

- Infectious: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) from two lumbar punctures showed no evidence of infection (normal cell count, protein, glucose). Serum testing for syphilis (FTA, VDRL) was negative. Lyme disease serology (IgM and IgG by ELISA and Western blot) was negative. Tuberculosis (Quantiferon) was negative. HIV and hepatitis B/C serologies were negative.

- Neoplastic and paraneoplastic: Tumor markers (alpha-fetoprotein, carcinoembryonic antigen, CA19-9, ROMA test) were within normal limits. Onconeural antibodies (anti-amphiphysin, anti-CV2, anti-PNMA2, anti-Ri, anti-Yo, anti-Hu, anti-recoverin, anti-SOX1, anti-titin, anti-Zic4, anti-GAD65, anti-Tr) were negative. Whole-body iodine-131 scintigraphy later showed only minimal tracer uptake in the right thyroid bed with no abnormal accumulation.

- Endocrine: Thyroid function tests initially showed hypothyroidism (elevated TSH). Anti-thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) antibodies were 451 IU/mL (reference <34 IU/mL) and anti-thyroglobulin (anti-Tg) antibodies were 748.31 IU/mL (reference <115 IU/mL). Other endocrine tests were unremarkable except for mild hyperprolactinemia and elevated ACTH later.

- Miscellaneous: Vitamin D, vitamin B12, folic acid, calcium-phosphate metabolism, alkaline phosphatase, and kappa/lambda light chains (kappa 29.47 mg/L, lambda 12.83 mg/L) were checked; no significant deficiencies. Total IgG was 1003 mg/dL (reference 700–1600 mg/dL); IgG subclasses were within normal limits.

- Other: CT chest showed an irregular 7 mm opacity in the right lung, considered likely non-neoplastic. EEG was normal. CSF opening pressure measured in May 2025 was 80 mm H2O. Beta-2 globulin was elevated in CSF electrophoresis.

Symptomatic treatment with oral methylprednisolone was initiated at 64 mg/day (1.03 mg/kg) with gradual tapering. At 3 months, she reported complete resolution of vertigo and reduction in balance disturbances, maintained on 8 mg/day. Attempts to taper to 4 mg/day led to worsening symptoms, which improved when the dose was increased again. She experienced intermittent sleep disturbances, low mood, and mild transient psychotic symptoms while on corticosteroids, which did not require discontinuation.

In December 2023, she underwent total thyroidectomy for a multinodular goiter. Histopathology revealed a 1 mm encapsulated follicular variant of papillary thyroid carcinoma (microcarcinoma papillare) with intrathyroidal capsule involvement, no extrathyroidal extension or vascular invasion (pT1a NX, LVI-0). She was not given radioiodine. Post-thyroidectomy, anti-Tg antibodies remained persistently elevated (1130 IU/mL, 1000 IU/mL, 975 IU/mL, 1022 IU/mL, 1159 IU/mL on serial measurements; reference <115 IU/mL), while anti-TPO declined to 78.6 and 69.7 IU/mL. Repeat oncologic surveillance including neck ultrasound, whole-body iodine-131 scintigraphy, and clinical follow-up showed no evidence of recurrence or metastasis. Serial contrast-enhanced brain MRIs over the next two years consistently showed stable diffuse dural thickening without progression or regression. CT angiography of cerebral arteries was normal. Abdominal and pelvic CT revealed multiple hepatic cysts, a renal cyst, and a small pancreatic cyst. Pituitary MRI showed a subtle bulging of the superior pituitary gland without meningeal thickening in that region.

After self-discontinuation of corticosteroids, symptoms recurred six months later, but responded again to reintroduction of methylprednisolone.

Based on the above presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?
gold_diagnosis: Idiopathic hypertrophic pachymeningitis (IHPM)

answer_key.diagnosis: Idiopathic hypertrophic pachymeningitis (IHPM)
answer_key.etiology: Unknown; suspected autoimmune mechanism with persistently elevated anti-thyroglobulin and anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate or continue oral corticosteroid therapy (e.g., methylprednisolone) with planned gradual taper, and monitor for symptom recurrence; consider steroid-sparing immunosuppressants if frequent relapses or adverse effects.
required_findings: Diffuse thickening of the dura mater up to 9 mm with mildly heterogeneous enhancement on contrast-enhanced brain MRI.
Anti-thyroglobulin antibodies 748.31 IU/mL (reference <115 IU/mL), persistently elevated on serial measurements (up to 1159 IU/mL).
Anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies 451 IU/mL (reference <34 IU/mL).
Comprehensive autoimmune panel (RF, anti-CCP, ANA, anti-dsDNA, ANCA, ACE) all negative.
Infectious workup negative: CSF analysis normal, syphilis (FTA, VDRL) negative, Lyme serology negative, tuberculosis (Quantiferon) negative, HIV/hepatitis negative.
Neoplastic and paraneoplastic workup negative: tumor markers normal, onconeural antibodies negative, whole-body iodine-131 scintigraphy only minimal thyroid bed uptake.
CSF opening pressure 80 mm H2O (not diagnostic of intracranial hypotension).
Symptomatic improvement with oral corticosteroid therapy, with relapse upon dose reduction.
optional_findings: Weight loss of 3–5 kg over several months.
Cerebellar tonsils extending 4 mm below McRae line.
Positive Romberg test and unsteady gait.
Thyroid histopathology: 1 mm follicular variant of papillary thyroid carcinoma, pT1a NX, without extrathyroidal extension.

provenance.title: Idiopathic Hypertrophic Pachymeningitis with Elevated Anti-Thyroglobulin Antibodies—A Case Report
provenance.journal: Reports - Clinical Practice and Surgical Cases

challenge_prompt: Patient: 44-year-old woman with asthma and history of thyroidectomy for multinodular goiter. Diagnosed with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (RRMS) in 2008 based on clinical and MRI findings, CSF oligoclonal bands, and negative workup for other conditions. Over the years, she experienced multiple relapses with partial recovery despite sequential disease-modifying therapies (interferon beta-1a, fingolimod, dimethyl fumarate). By April 2019, EDSS was 5.5. She started alemtuzumab (an immune reconstitution therapy) in April 2019, with a second cycle in July 2020. In April 2021—nine months after the second alemtuzumab cycle—she presented with severe neurological deterioration: gait ataxia, dysarthria, mild dysphagia, incontinence, and concurrent cutaneous fungal infection (Tinea corporis, Microsporum canis positive). EDSS worsened to 6.5. Brain MRI showed a large demyelinating lesion extending from the globose nucleus to the middle and superior cerebellar peduncle and fourth ventricle, with incomplete peripheral enhancement. She received intravenous methylprednisolone 500 mg/day for 11 days without significant improvement. A short course of intravenous immunoglobulins was attempted but discontinued due to side effects and lack of benefit. Lymphocyte subset analysis by flow cytometry (performed before corticosteroid administration) showed: CD19+ B cells: 39.6% (515 cells/µL; normal range 7–23%); with relative CD4+ and CD8+ T-cell depletion. No other acute infections or metabolic derangements were identified. Based only on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?
gold_diagnosis: Post-alemtuzumab B-cell–dominant severe MS relapse (paradoxical disease reactivation)

answer_key.diagnosis: Post-alemtuzumab B-cell–dominant severe MS relapse (paradoxical disease reactivation)
answer_key.etiology: Dysregulated B-cell reconstitution after alemtuzumab, with disproportionate CD19+ B-cell expansion and relative T-cell depletion, leading to corticosteroid-resistant CNS inflammation.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate B-cell-depleting therapy with an anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody (e.g., ocrelizumab or rituximab).

Prior treatment with two cycles of alemtuzumab (April 2019 and July 2020)

Neurological exam: gait ataxia, dysarthria, mild dysphagia, incontinence
MRI: large demyelinating lesion with incomplete peripheral enhancement in brainstem/cerebellum
No improvement after 11 days of high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone
Lymphocyte subset analysis: CD19+ B cells 39.6% (515 cells/µL; normal 7–23%)
Relative CD4+ and CD8+ T-cell depletion on flow cytometry
optional_findings: Lack of response to intravenous immunoglobulin
History of thyroidectomy for multinodular goiter
CSF oligoclonal bands at original diagnosis
Prior use of interferon beta-1a and dimethyl fumarate

challenge_prompt: A 60-year-old Black African male with a history of post-polycythemia vera myelofibrosis presents with acute psychosis. He initially developed PV at age 40 (hemoglobin 183 g/L, hematocrit 0.525 L/L, platelets 602 × 10^9/L, normal WBC, JAK2 V617F positive with VAF 30%, undetectable EPO). He was initially managed with venesection and hydroxycarbamide for 7 years, then anagrelide for 10 years. At approximately 57 years, due to rising platelets (667 × 10^9/L) and loss of response, a bone marrow biopsy showed WHO grade 3 fibrosis, consistent with post-PV MF. Genetic testing revealed persistent JAK2 V617F (VAF 48%) and additional TET2 mutations. Anagrelide was stopped, and oral ruxolitinib was started at 15 mg twice daily, increased to 25 mg twice daily after 3 months. After a total of 5 months on ruxolitinib, the patient developed insomnia, sudden onset persecutory delusions, and auditory hallucinations. There was no history of fever, headache, or neurological deficits. He had no past or family history of psychiatric illness, no use of tobacco, cannabis, or illicit drugs, and aside from mild work-related stress, no identified precipitating factors. Physical examination was unremarkable. A head CT scan showed no acute abnormality. Serology for HIV and syphilis was negative. Autoimmune antibody testing for LGI1, NMDAR, and CASPR2 was negative. He was admitted to psychiatry and started on oral risperidone 2 mg daily, but continued to experience florid psychotic symptoms. Ruxolitinib was the only recently started medication. Based on the information provided, what is the most likely etiology of the patient's acute psychosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Ruxolitinib-induced acute psychotic disorder

answer_key.diagnosis: Ruxolitinib-induced acute psychotic disorder
answer_key.etiology: Ruxolitinib therapy
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue ruxolitinib

no use of tobacco, cannabis, or illicit drugs
head CT scan showed no acute abnormality
Serology for HIV and syphilis was negative
Autoimmune antibody testing for LGI1, NMDAR, and CASPR2 was negative
Ruxolitinib was the only recently started medication
patient developed ... acute psychosis ... after a total of 5 months on ruxolitinib
presented with ... persecutory delusions and auditory hallucinations

persistent JAK2 V617F (VAF 48%) and additional TET2 mutations
treated with oral risperidone 2 mg daily without improvement

provenance.title: Acute psychosis following initiation of ruxolitinib in post-polycythaemia vera myelofibrosis case report

challenge_prompt: An 18-year-old male with no significant past medical history presented with an 18-month history of focal motor-onset seizures, attributed to the right frontal lobe. The episodes consistently occurred in fasting states and were preceded by a prodrome of malaise, weakness, and profuse sweating. He had been treated with carbamazepine 200 mg every 8 hours without adequate seizure control. His records documented two prior hospitalizations where severe hypoglycemia was recorded (35 and 45 mg/dL), with complete symptom remission after intravenous dextrose administration; however, no endocrine workup had been performed. The current admission was prompted by progressive cognitive decline and an increase in seizure frequency. On examination, the patient was disoriented but otherwise had a normal physical exam. Initial laboratory tests showed preserved renal and liver function and normal electrolyte levels. Random serum glucose, hemoglobin, serum calcium, and liver enzymes were within normal limits.
gold_diagnosis: Insulinoma

answer_key.diagnosis: Insulinoma
answer_key.etiology: Autonomous insulin secretion from a pancreatic beta-cell tumor not suppressed by hypoglycemia
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform supervised fasting test to confirm hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia, with measurement of insulin, C-peptide, and sulfonylurea screen during hypoglycemia

treated with carbamazepine 200 mg every 8 hours without adequate seizure control

progressive cognitive decline and an increase in seizure frequency

challenge_prompt:
A 42-year-old Caucasian woman presents with chronic pelvic pain (CPP), vulvodynia, and dyspareunia for the past 8 years, exacerbated by upright posture. She has a history of multiple comorbidities:

- Neurologic: chronic migraines requiring frequent emergency department visits and intravenous treatment, leading to opioid dependence; seizure disorder; declining cognition noted by her neurologist – neuropsychiatric testing revealed superior intelligence but inattention, with short-term memory at the 47th percentile, resulting in a diagnosis of attention deficit disorder (ADD). She became completely disabled, dropped out of graduate school, and was unable to drive due to severe disorientation. Numerous medications were prescribed but provided limited benefit.
- Cardiovascular: postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) with frequent traumatic syncopal episodes.
- Gastrointestinal: severe constipation and bloating with a diagnosis of irritable bowel syndrome; spontaneous colon perforation 8 years ago requiring 13 abdominal surgeries including total colectomy; surgeons noted “poor healing”.
- Musculoskeletal: back pain from lumbar spondylosis treated with spine surgeries; spontaneous cerebrospinal fluid leak.
- Endocrine: pituitary failure, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency.
- Hematologic: pulmonary embolism related to a peripherally inserted central catheter placed for difficult venous access.
- Psychiatric: anxiety and depression.
- No electrolyte imbalance, normal renal function.

Physical examination: tall, muscular, anxious, confused, with speech trailing off and incomplete sentences. Beighton score exceeds the threshold for generalized joint hypermobility. External pelvic exam reveals exquisite vulvar tenderness and scattered vulvar varicose veins.

Questionnaire responses: International Pelvic Pain Society (IPSS) Pelvic Pain Assessment documents chronic pelvic pain; Orthostatic Hypotension Questionnaire (OHQ) qualifies as “moderate to extreme” orthostatic hypotension.

Magnetic resonance venography reveals >50% diameter compression of the left common iliac vein between the right common iliac artery and the lumbar vertebral body.

Based on the above, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next best step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Venous origin chronic pelvic pain (VO-CPP) due to left common iliac vein compression (May-Thurner syndrome) in a patient with Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (hypermobility type)

answer_key.diagnosis: Venous origin chronic pelvic pain (VO-CPP) due to left common iliac vein compression (May-Thurner syndrome) in a patient with Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (hypermobility type)
answer_key.etiology: Extrinsic compression of the left common iliac vein by the overlying right common iliac artery against the lumbar spine, causing pelvic venous congestion and associated symptoms.
answer_key.next_management_step: Venography with intravascular ultrasound (IVUS) and possible iliac vein stenting
required_findings: Chronic non-cyclic pelvic pain >6 months, associated with upright posture
Vulvar varicose veins on external pelvic examination
Exquisite vulvar tenderness
MR venography showing >50% diameter compression of the left common iliac vein
Orthostatic hypotension classified as moderate to extreme on OHQ
Beighton score positive for generalized joint hypermobility
History of poor wound healing and multiple abdominal surgeries
Neurocognitive impairment (inattention, poor short-term memory, diagnosed ADD)
optional_findings: Chronic migraines refractory to multiple medications
Irritable bowel syndrome with severe constipation

provenance.title: Case Report: Neuropsychiatric improvement after treatment of pelvic venous disorder in a multisyndromic patient

challenge_prompt: A 29-year-old woman with no prior psychiatric or medical history and no family history of mental illness was brought to the emergency department by her family due to acute behavioral and perceptual changes. Over the preceding week, she developed persecutory delusions toward her brother, prominent religious preoccupations, delusional guilt, and auditory hallucinations consisting of unfamiliar voices making derogatory and religiously themed comments. She had episodes of disorganized behavior without evidence of manic, depressive, or cognitive symptoms. There was a three-month prodrome of gradual decline in social interaction and personal hygiene, punctuated by inappropriate smiling and staring episodes. On mental state examination, she appeared thin and anxious, with disheveled appearance and poor hygiene. She was cooperative with fair eye contact and fully oriented. Speech was coherent but occasionally tangential. Affect was restricted, mood was anxious. Thought content revealed persecutory and guilt-related delusions with overvalued religious ideas; no thought blocking or loosening of associations. Insight and judgment were impaired. Initial laboratory tests performed before psychiatric evaluation showed TSH <0.005 mIU/L, free T3 30.5 pmol/L, anti-thyroglobulin 20 IU/mL, and anti-thyroid peroxidase 141 IU/mL. Endocrinological examination identified mild exophthalmos and a diffuse non-tender goiter; there was no tremor, tachycardia, heat intolerance, weight loss, or palpitations. Thyroid ultrasonography demonstrated an enlarged heterogeneous gland with increased vascularity and bilateral low-risk nodules (TI-RADS categories 2 and 3) without suspicious cervical lymphadenopathy. Brain MRI, EEG, and cerebrospinal fluid analysis were unremarkable. There were no fluctuations in consciousness or attention. Based on the presentation and initial workup, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Psychotic disorder due to Graves' disease (thyrotoxicosis)

answer_key.diagnosis: Psychotic disorder due to Graves' disease (thyrotoxicosis)
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune hyperthyroidism due to Graves' disease, characterized by stimulating TSH receptor antibodies leading to excessive thyroid hormone production.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate antithyroid therapy (carbimazole or methimazole) and beta-blocker (atenolol), with adjunctive low-dose antipsychotic (olanzapine 5 mg daily) for psychotic symptoms.
required_findings: TSH <0.005 mIU/L
Free T3 30.5 pmol/L (elevated)
Positive anti-thyroglobulin (20 IU/mL) and anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies (141 IU/mL)
Mild exophthalmos and diffuse non-tender goiter
Thyroid ultrasound: enlarged heterogeneous gland with increased vascularity, bilateral low-risk nodules
Absence of classical thyrotoxic symptoms (no tremor, tachycardia, weight loss, heat intolerance)
Acute psychosis: persecutory delusions, auditory hallucinations, religious preoccupation, disorganized behavior
Normal brain MRI, EEG, and CSF analysis
optional_findings: Three-month prodrome of social withdrawal, poor hygiene, inappropriate smiling/staring
No substance use or psychosocial stressors
No family history of psychiatric illness
Absence of thought blocking or formal thought disorder

provenance.title: Psychosis as the Initial and Main Presentation of Graves’ Disease: A Case Report from Saudi Arabia

challenge_prompt:
A 70-year-old female with a history of ovarian cancer (diagnosed in May 2021, treated with radical debulking surgery and adjuvant chemotherapy; histopathology: high-grade serous carcinoma) presented with a 6-month history of insidious onset intermittent diplopia, limb weakness, and transient visual obscuration. She denied headache, nausea, vomiting, convulsions, facial asymmetry, dysphagia, or loss of consciousness. Initial outside hospital evaluation led to a diagnosis of 'ataxia syndrome.' Cranial MRI at that time showed no significant abnormalities. Electronystagmography demonstrated reduced low-frequency responses in the left horizontal semicircular canal. Despite medical management, her symptoms progressed: she developed intermittent head and bilateral upper extremity tremors, exacerbated by emotion and intentional movements, absent during sleep, and accompanied by dizziness. She was re-evaluated at another hospital and diagnosed with 'metabolic encephalopathy.' CSF analysis revealed WBC 10 × 10^6/L, protein 0.67 g/L. Autoantibody screening was positive for ANA (with a nuclear pattern), strongly positive for Ro-52 (+++), and weakly positive for ds-DNA (+/−). Tumor marker CEA was 1.56 ng/mL (normal <3.4 ng/mL). Her condition deteriorated, causing slurred speech, dysarthria, bradykinesia, impaired fine motor skills, and eventually a bedridden state. She was referred to your hospital for further evaluation. On physical examination, the patient was alert but had a poor mental state with slurred speech and dysarthria; her responses were slow but appropriate. Pupils were equal and round with brisk light reflexes (3 mm), convergence uncooperative, ocular posture and eye movements unremarkable. Bilateral hearing and olfactory function were grossly normal. Facial symmetry was preserved, tongue midline, no neck stiffness. Intermittent tremors were noted in the head and both upper extremities. Muscle tone was decreased globally, and strength was grade III in all limbs. Pathological reflexes were positive in the left lower limb; tendon reflexes were diminished in all extremities; pharyngeal reflex was weak. Repeat cranial MRI at your hospital (approximately 3 months after the initial outside MRI) revealed significant atrophy of the cerebellar cortex compared to previous imaging. No other intracranial abnormalities were identified. Given the presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what next diagnostic step is most appropriate to confirm it?

Subsequently, the patient was ultimately diagnosed with anti-Yo antibody-positive paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration.
gold_diagnosis: Anti-Yo antibody-positive paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration

answer_key.diagnosis: Anti-Yo antibody-positive paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration
answer_key.etiology: Paraneoplastic autoimmune response targeting cerebellar antigens (Yo antigen) in the setting of previous ovarian cancer
answer_key.next_management_step: Test for anti-Yo antibodies in serum and cerebrospinal fluid (paraneoplastic antibody panel)
required_findings: History of high-grade serous ovarian cancer treated with debulking surgery and chemotherapy
Subacute onset (6 months) of diplopia, limb weakness, and transient visual obscuration
Progression to head and upper extremity tremors, dizziness, dysarthria, and bedridden state
CSF analysis showing white blood cell count 10 × 10^6/L and protein 0.67 g/L
Autoantibody screen positive for ANA, strongly positive for Ro-52 (+++), and weakly positive for ds-DNA (+/−)
Cranial MRI progression from normal to significant cerebellar cortex atrophy
Physical exam findings of decreased muscle tone, grade III strength, diminished reflexes, and positive pathological reflexes in left lower limb
Negative CEA tumor marker (1.56 ng/mL) and no evidence of tumor recurrence
optional_findings: Intermittent diplopia and transient visual obscuration
Initial cranial MRI with no significant abnormalities
Electronystagmography showing reduced low-frequency responses in left horizontal semicircular canal
Preserved cranial nerves (pupils, eye movements, facial symmetry)
Bilateral hearing and olfaction grossly normal

challenge_prompt:
Patient is a 49-year-old male with a history of metastatic renal carcinoma diagnosed in December 2021, currently on treatment with pembrolizumab and lenvatinib. One and a half years after diagnosis, he presented to the emergency department with a one-week history of left-sided hemibody and facial weakness. Initial workup including ECG, blood gases, coagulation studies, CBC, biochemistry panel, and chest X-ray were unremarkable. Cranial CT revealed a hypodense area in the right corona radiata suggestive of metastasis. He was transferred to an oncology center where CT chest/abdomen/pelvis showed a reduction in supraclavicular lymph node size, stable bone metastases and renal mass, and mild small bowel dilation. Supra-aortic trunk ultrasound and transthoracic echocardiography were normal. Bone scan showed no significant abnormalities. Brain MRI demonstrated a subacute infarction in the territory of the right choroidal and lenticulostriate arteries.

One month later, the patient was admitted to the neurology department due to progressive neurological worsening. Over the previous month, he had developed gait instability, liquid dysphagia, hiccups, worsening speech, and diplopia. Neurological examination revealed mild dysarthria, left eye esotropia, vertical nystagmus, moderate left lower facial paresis, and severe left hemiparesis. Brain MRI showed two areas of vasogenic edema with contrast enhancement: one on the slope of the left middle cerebellar peduncle and another in the right corona radiata, caudate body/head, and upper half of the ipsilateral lenticular nucleus, suggesting intraparenchymal and leptomeningeal tumor dissemination. Dexamethasone was administered with clinical improvement. The case was discussed in the neuro-oncology committee, and it was decided to transfer the patient back to the oncology hospital for suspected tumor involvement.

During this second admission, an inflammatory etiology was considered. A lumbar puncture was performed, which was negative for tumor cells. An autoimmunity panel was ordered and revealed the following: positive anti-MOG antibodies with a titer of 1:160 (detected via indirect immunofluorescence) and positive anti-NMO IgG at 0.15 (detected via ELISA). What is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Double anti-AQP4 and anti-MOG antibody positivity, consistent with overlapping neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD) and MOG antibody disease (MOGAD), likely paraneoplastic or immune checkpoint inhibitor-related.

answer_key.diagnosis: Double anti-AQP4 and anti-MOG antibody positivity, consistent with overlapping neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD) and MOG antibody disease (MOGAD), likely paraneoplastic or immune checkpoint inhibitor-related.
aliases: Paraneoplastic demyelinating disease with AQP4 and MOG antibodies
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune, possibly triggered by underlying malignancy and/or immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone (e.g., 1 g daily for 3–5 days) followed by oral prednisone taper, and consider rituximab for long-term immunosuppression.
required_findings: History of metastatic renal carcinoma on pembrolizumab and lenvatinib.
Progressive neurological deficits including hemiparesis, dysarthria, ocular motility abnormalities, and brainstem signs (hiccups, dysphagia).
MRI showing multifocal areas of vasogenic edema with contrast enhancement in brain and brainstem, initially suggestive of tumor dissemination.
Lumbar puncture negative for malignant cells.
Positive anti-MOG antibodies (1:160, indirect immunofluorescence).
Positive anti-NMO IgG (0.15, ELISA).
Clinical improvement with dexamethasone.
optional_findings: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis showing stable metastatic disease.
Normal supra-aortic trunk ultrasound and echocardiography.
Initial CT head hypodensity in right corona radiata.

provenance.title: Double Anti‐NMO and Anti‐MOG Positivity in a Patient With Metastatic Renal Carcinoma: First Reported Case

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

A 36-year-old woman in the 11th week of her first pregnancy was referred for new onset of hyperthyroidism. She had a history of multiple sclerosis, diagnosed at age 27, treated with alemtuzumab administered in 3 courses at ages 27, 28, and 34 years. She had a positive family history for thyroid disorders. She denied smoking. At presentation she reported fine distal tremors of the upper extremities. On physical examination, she had normal blood pressure and a heart rate of 120 beats per minute. She showed no signs of active ophthalmopathy. Her body weight was 58 kg and height 153 cm. Laboratory assessment showed free T4 27.3 pg/ml (nonpregnancy reference range 7-14.8 pg/ml) and suppressed TSH (nonpregnancy reference range 0.47-4.78 mIU/L). Anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies (TPOAb) and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies (TgAb) were negative. TRAb level was 20.6 IU/L (reference range <1.75). Thyroid ultrasound showed a normal-sized thyroid gland with a heterogeneous and pseudonodular echotexture and increased, uniform vascularization.
gold_diagnosis: Graves' disease

answer_key.diagnosis: Graves' disease
answer_key.etiology: Alemtuzumab-induced autoimmune thyroid disease (Graves' disease) due to immune reconstitution targeting the TSH receptor.
answer_key.next_management_step: Start antithyroid drug therapy (methimazole or propylthiouracil); given 11 weeks gestation (past the teratogenic window), methimazole is preferred initially at 10 mg/day, with close monitoring of thyroid function and fetal growth.
