TSH suppressed
TRAb 20.6 IU/L (elevated)
negative anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies (TPOAb)
negative anti-thyroglobulin antibodies (TgAb)
thyroid ultrasound: normal size, heterogeneous pseudonodular echotexture, increased uniform vascularization
history of alemtuzumab treatment for multiple sclerosis
tachycardia (heart rate 120 bpm) and fine distal tremors
optional_findings: positive family history for thyroid disorders

provenance.title: Management of alemtuzumab-induced Graves’ disease in pregnancy: a case report and literature review

challenge_prompt:
A 57-year-old woman presented with a 4-day history of atypical behavior: excessive spending, delusional thinking, hypomania, and confusion. She had a history of depression, irritable bowel syndrome, psoriasis, and actively smoked 20 cigarettes/day since age 20, and drank 20–30 units of alcohol per week. Physical and neurological examinations were normal. Laboratory studies showed CRP 15.7 mg/L (normal 0–5), WBC 12.65 × 10⁹/L (4–11), neutrophils 8.10 × 10⁹/L (1.6–7.5). Urine culture grew E. coli; urine toxicology was negative. She was treated with nitrofurantoin and Pabrinex. CT head and MRI brain showed no significant abnormalities. She was admitted to a psychiatric unit and treated for bipolar disorder with antipsychotics, benzodiazepines, and antidepressants (clonazepam, fluoxetine, olanzapine).

Seven weeks later, she re-presented after two generalized tonic-clonic seizures. Examination remained unremarkable. Blood tests and chest X-ray were normal. Repeat CT head was negative; MRI head was inconclusive. EEG demonstrated bilateral frontal epileptiform discharges and encephalopathic changes. She was started on lamotrigine and acyclovir for suspected encephalitis. Lumbar puncture yielded clear, colorless CSF with normal glucose and protein, no organisms, and no oligoclonal bands. CSF PCR was negative for HSV 1/2, VZV, enterovirus, parechovirus, and CMV. Acyclovir was stopped. She was treated with prednisolone 60 mg daily for probable autoimmune encephalitis, and her neurological symptoms improved transiently.

Olanzapine was tapered and then discontinued. Repeat MRI head was normal. Repeat EEG showed mixed-frequency, slow and disorganized background with a persistent epileptiform focus in the right posterior temporal region consistent with focal non-convulsive status epilepticus. Her condition deteriorated: she became agitated, irritable, and uncooperative. Olanzapine was restarted. CT of the thorax, abdomen, and pelvis (CT TAP) revealed extensive mediastinal lymphadenopathy without lung parenchymal changes; lymphoma was considered, and endobronchial ultrasound (EBUS) was recommended. Another EEG showed improvement with no status epilepticus. She received plasma exchange and showed some behavioral improvement. Steroids were weaned.

Later, CSF autoantibody results returned negative for NMDA receptor, LGI1, CASPR2, AMPA1, AMPA2, and a paraneoplastic antibody panel (Hu, Yo, Ri, Ma2, Ta, CV2, amphiphysin). She developed sepsis from a central line infection, causing worsening of neuropsychiatric symptoms. Plasma exchange was halted after 3 of 5 planned sessions, and EBUS was postponed. She received IVIG for 5 days with some improvement.

She then reported a 2-week history of progressive dyspnea and fluctuating hoarseness. Flexible nasal endoscopy and FEES revealed left vocal cord immobility (recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy) and severe bilateral laryngeal edema. Over the next few days, she developed right-sided neck swelling, facial plethora, and prominent superficial chest veins. Neck ultrasound showed a patent and compressible right subclavian and right jugular vein; superior to the medial clavicle, there was a mixed-echo lesion measuring 4.1 × 2.4 × 4.0 cm. A CT neck demonstrated multiple bilateral lower cervical lymph nodes and progressive confluent mediastinal lymphadenopathy compared to previous imaging.

Based on the above, what is the most likely diagnosis and what confirmatory test is indicated next?

Endobronchial ultrasound-guided biopsy of the mediastinal lymph nodes revealed small-cell lung carcinoma.
gold_diagnosis: Small-cell lung carcinoma

answer_key.diagnosis: Small-cell lung carcinoma
answer_key.etiology: Malignant neuroendocrine tumor of the lung, strongly associated with smoking
answer_key.next_management_step: Endobronchial ultrasound (EBUS)-guided biopsy for histological confirmation
required_findings: Heavy smoking history (20 cigarettes/day since age 20)
Subacute neuropsychiatric symptoms (excessive spending, delusions, hypomania, confusion)
Two generalized seizures with EEG showing bilateral frontal epileptiform discharges and encephalopathic changes
Normal brain MRI on serial scans
CSF analysis: no pleocytosis, normal glucose/protein, negative infectious PCR, absent oligoclonal bands
Negative paraneoplastic/autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel in CSF (NMDA, LGI1, CASPR2, AMPA1/2, Hu, Yo, Ri, Ma2, Ta, CV2, amphiphysin)
CT TAP revealing extensive mediastinal lymphadenopathy without lung mass

Development of superior vena cava obstruction signs: dyspnea, hoarseness (vocal cord palsy), neck/facial swelling, prominent chest veins, with imaging confirming mediastinal mass and cervical lymphadenopathy
optional_findings: Partial response to steroids and IVIG
Repeat EEG later showed focal non-convulsive status epilepticus

provenance.title: Paraneoplastic Encephalitis Unmasking Small-Cell Lung Cancer With Impending Superior Vena Cava Obstruction: A Case Report

mend_provenance.added: Endobronchial ultrasound-guided biopsy of the mediastinal lymph nodes revealed small-cell lung carcinoma.

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

A 31-year-old male presented with a five-day history of progressively worsening dizziness and gait instability. He had been diagnosed with MOG antibody-associated optic neuritis nine months prior, following an episode of monocular visual loss (serum MOG antibody titer 1:100), which resolved after corticosteroid therapy. Due to poor adherence, he did not initiate any long-term maintenance immunotherapy. A follow-up serum titer one month before this presentation was 1:32. Neurological examination revealed horizontal gaze-evoked nystagmus with left gaze predominance, as well as left-sided limb dysmetria accompanied by intention tremor. Brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a large, mass-like, “fluffy” lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere extending into the middle cerebellar peduncle. The lesion was hypointense on T1-weighted imaging (T1WI) and hyperintense on both T2-weighted and fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) sequences. No restricted diffusion was observed on diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI), and there was a notable absence of mass effect or perilesional edema. Contrast-enhanced T1WI showed minimal, scattered patchy and linear enhancement, while perfusion-weighted imaging (PWI) demonstrated reduced relative cerebral blood volume (rCBV) within the lesion. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis demonstrated opening pressure 130 mmH2O (normal range: 80~180 mmH2O), leukocytosis 9 cells/μL (normal range: 0~8/μL) with 100% mononuclear, normal protein levels, and negative oligoclonal bands. Serum testing for MOG-IgG (live cell-based assay) was positive at a titer of 1:100, and AQP4-IgG was negative.

answer_key.diagnosis: MOG antibody-associated disease presenting as tumefactive demyelinating lesion

answer_key.next_management_step: High-dose intravenous methylprednisolone (1 g daily for 3-5 days) and initiation of maintenance immunotherapy (e.g., oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day with gradual taper)

Brain MRI revealed a large, mass-like, “fluffy” lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere extending into the middle cerebellar peduncle
Notable absence of mass effect or perilesional edema
No restricted diffusion was observed on diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI)
Contrast-enhanced T1WI showed minimal, scattered patchy and linear enhancement
Perfusion-weighted imaging (PWI) demonstrated reduced relative cerebral blood volume (rCBV) within the lesion
Serum testing for MOG-IgG (live cell-based assay) was positive at a titer of 1:100, and AQP4-IgG was negative

challenge_prompt:
A 15-year-old male without significant past medical history presented with an 8-day history of progressive neuropsychiatric symptoms. He initially developed irritability, resistance to instructions, crying spells, and illogical speech, but could answer simple questions and recognize family. Three days before admission, symptoms worsened with nocturnal sleeplessness and agitation, requiring sedation at a local psychiatric hospital; an EEG under sedation was moderately abnormal. On the day of admission, he had a generalized tonic-clonic seizure with limb rigidity (flexed upper, extended lower limbs) and loss of consciousness lasting 5 minutes, followed by stupor (GCS 8). Neurological examination revealed sluggishly reactive pupils (2.5 mm), diffusely increased muscle tone, suspected positive left Babinski sign, and no meningismus. Emergency head CT showed a right temporal low-density lesion, possibly a choroidal fissure cyst.

CSF analysis on day 1 was clear, opening pressure 140 mm H2O, with WBC 0, RBC 0.001×10^12/L, and protein normal at 445.92 mg/L. Acid-fast bacillus smear and India ink stain were negative. Blood tests showed leukocytosis (13.56×10^9/L) with neutrophilia (92%), mildly elevated transaminases (ALT 41 U/L, AST 93 U/L), normal renal function. Serum tumor markers (NSE, CA19-9, CA125, CEA) on day 7 were normal. Ultrasonography of abdomen, heart, and lower limbs, and chest X-ray showed no masses. Brain MRI on day 9 revealed subtle T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in bilateral insular and periventricular regions, without abnormal signal in cerebral or cerebellar structures.

Serum and CSF samples were tested for neuronal autoantibodies using a commercial cell-based assay. Anti-Drebrin antibody was detected in serum (titer 1:1000) and CSF (1:1). Anti-mGluR2 antibody was also detected in serum (titer 1:32) and CSF (1:1).

Based on the above information, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Autoimmune encephalitis with dual anti-Drebrin and anti-mGluR2 antibodies

challenge_prompt: A 47-year-old Moroccan male with a history of smoking and drug abuse presented with status epilepticus lasting 30 minutes. His family reported a one-year history of progressive lethargy, polyuria, polydipsia, significant weight loss, and polyphagia. On arrival, he was obtunded with generalized tonic-clonic movements, requiring airway management and intravenous phenobarbital for 24 hours. Initial laboratory studies revealed severe hyperglycemia, marked ketonuria, and a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. Brain CT was normal. Brain MRI showed multiple white-matter hyperintensities on T2/FLAIR in the subcortical and periventricular regions, with microbleeds on SWI, interpreted as consistent with metabolic encephalopathy. EEG after seizure control demonstrated a normal background rhythm without paroxysmal abnormalities or epileptiform discharges. CSF analysis: glucose 2.88 g/L, normal cell counts, negative cultures. Further evaluation: HbA1c 15.2%. There was no family history of epilepsy or diabetes mellitus. Psychiatric assessment ruled out drug withdrawal as a precipitant.

challenge_prompt:
A 34-year-old Chinese woman presented in November 2024 with one-month progressive neurological symptoms. Initial manifestations included bilateral fingertip numbness extending to palms and buttocks, accompanied by taste disturbance. She developed proximal muscle weakness (MRC grade 3 proximally, 4+ distally in lower limbs; 4+ in upper limbs), bilateral ptosis, facial asymmetry, and mild dysphagia. She denied rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, or Raynaud phenomenon. Medical and family histories were unremarkable.

Neurological examination confirmed the muscle weakness pattern and revealed diminished deep tendon reflexes throughout. Cranial nerve examination revealed: bilateral partial ptosis with intact pupillary responses and full extraocular movements; bilateral facial weakness with incomplete eye closure and asymmetric smile; diminished gag reflex with mild dysphagia. Cognitive assessment revealed preserved higher mental functions (MMSE 29/30). No psychosis, mood disturbance, or cognitive impairment.

Initial laboratory investigations at an outside hospital revealed leukopenia (WBC 2.83×10⁹/L), elevated ESR (45 mm/h), positive ANA, and markedly elevated IgG (23.2 g/L, normal 7–16 g/L).

Brain MRI was normal. Nerve conduction studies demonstrated abnormal F-waves, prolonged H-reflexes, absent blink reflexes, and bilateral facial nerve involvement, consistent with acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy. CSF analysis showed cytoalbuminologic dissociation: protein 1314 mg/L, cells 1×10⁶/L.

Comprehensive infectious workup was negative: HIV antibody, HBsAg, anti-HCV, anti-EBV IgM, anti-CMV IgM, and CSF viral panel (HSV PCR, VZV PCR, enterovirus PCR) were all negative. Metabolic screening excluded diabetic neuropathy (fasting glucose 4.8 mmol/L, HbA1c 5.2%).

The patient received IVIG 25g daily for 5 days at the outside hospital with only partial improvement. Given incomplete response and systemic features, she was referred to rheumatology.

At the rheumatology department, further immunological assessment revealed: ANA 1:320 (cytoplasmic pattern), anti-dsDNA antibodies 128 IU/ml (normal <20), anti-ribosomal P protein antibodies 450 AU/ml (normal <20), complement C3 1.10 g/L (normal 1.15–1.50), complement C4 0.28 g/L (normal 0.35–0.45), and IgG 24.30 g/L. Complete blood count showed persistent leukopenia (2.9×10⁹/L) with lymphopenia (0.65×10⁹/L). ESR was 63 mm/h and CRP 2.35 mg/L.

Based only on the presentation and findings available, what is the most likely underlying diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Neuropsychiatric systemic lupus erythematosus (NPSLE) presenting as Guillain-Barré syndrome (acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy)

answer_key.diagnosis: Neuropsychiatric systemic lupus erythematosus (NPSLE) presenting as Guillain-Barré syndrome (acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy)
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune disease with anti-ribosomal P antibodies causing peripheral nerve demyelination
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate immunosuppressive therapy with moderate-dose corticosteroids (e.g., methylprednisolone 40 mg daily) and cyclophosphamide, plus hydroxychloroquine; consider B-cell targeted therapy (e.g., telitacicept) if available.

challenge_prompt:
A 46-year-old previously healthy male electrical technician presented with an insidious onset of high-grade continuous fever, chills, rigors, productive cough, headache, and arthralgia. He denied abdominal pain, urinary symptoms, cellulitis, seizures, or loss of appetite. After 1 week of unsuccessful outpatient treatment, he was admitted to a hospital.

On first admission, he was conscious (GCS 15/15), with blood pressure 100/60 mmHg, pulse 96/min, and had neck stiffness with positive Kernig’s and Brudzinski’s signs. Respiratory exam revealed bilateral rhonchi; cardiovascular and abdominal exams were unremarkable. Initial laboratory investigations: white cell count 22.54 × 10⁹/L with neutrophil predominance (18.62 × 10⁹/L), CRP 32.87 mg/L, ESR 124 mm/h, serum creatinine 0.84 mg/dL, sodium 153 mmol/L. Lumbar puncture showed CSF with granulocytes 250 cells/mm³, lymphocytes 12 cells/mm³, protein 132 mg/dL, glucose 54 mg/dL (random blood sugar 130 mg/dL). CT brain (noncontrast and contrast-enhanced) was unremarkable. Blood, urine, and CSF cultures yielded no growth. A presumptive diagnosis of acute pyogenic meningitis was made, and he received intravenous ceftriaxone, vancomycin, and meropenem. During the hospitalization, he developed limb weakness, hypertonia, and exaggerated tendon reflexes. He was discharged with residual neurological deficits and advised physiotherapy.

Two months later, he was readmitted with 2-week history of recurrent fever, generalized body aches, intermittent altered consciousness, involuntary movements of bilateral upper limbs, severe imbalance, and difficulty ambulating. Examination revealed marked hypertonia in all limbs, myoclonic jerks in bilateral upper limbs, exaggerated deep tendon reflexes in all four limbs, and positive jaw jerk. Cranial nerve exam was normal. Repeat investigations: white cell count 7.45 × 10⁹/L (neutrophils 60.0%, lymphocytes 25.1%), ESR 94 mm/h, CRP 7.0 mg/dL. CSF was clear, with 7 lymphocytes/mm³, no polymorphs, and protein 134 mg/dL. EEG was normal. MRI spine showed infective spondylitis involving thoracic vertebrae T2 through T6, with vertebral body marrow edema, preserved intervertebral disc spaces, paraspinal inflammatory changes, spinal canal narrowing at T5 causing cord compression with myelopathic signal changes and early syrinx formation. MRI brain demonstrated basal meningeal enhancement. Chest X-ray was unremarkable. Additional workup: HIV serology negative; no history of animal contact to suggest brucellosis; normal serum calcium (sarcoidosis unlikely); peripheral blood smear and full blood count unremarkable without lymphadenopathy; no clinical features or positive serology for autoimmune disease or vasculitis.

Based on the presentation and investigations, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Spinal tuberculosis (Pott's disease) with secondary meningoencephalitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Spinal tuberculosis (Pott's disease) with secondary meningoencephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Mycobacterium tuberculosis
answer_key.next_management_step: Start anti-tubercular therapy (fixed-dose combination HRZE) with adjunctive corticosteroids, and refer for neurosurgical/orthopedic evaluation for possible decompression if neurological deficits progress.

challenge_prompt:
A 20-year-old man presented with a 10-year history of epilepsy. Early psychomotor development was normal, with no perinatal complications or febrile seizures. Myoclonic jerks first appeared at age 10, predominantly during sleep and mainly involving the upper limbs, accompanied by sudden arousal and transient postural changes. Over subsequent years, the episodes gradually evolved into generalized tonic–clonic seizures that became increasingly frequent and refractory despite multiple antiseizure medications (ASMs). As seizures generalized, cognitive performance progressively declined, characterized by reduced speech output, increasing fatigue, and noticeable impairment in daily functioning. At this stage, the Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) score was 15/30. On neurological examination, he was alert and oriented, with mild dysarthria, slightly increased muscle tone in all limbs, and symmetrical deep tendon reflexes without pathological signs. Cranial nerves were intact: pupils equal and reactive, extraocular movements full without gaze palsy, facial expression symmetric, hearing normal, palatal and tongue movements midline. Coordination and sensory testing were unremarkable. Gait was normal, without ataxia, spasticity, or balance impairment. Systemic examination revealed no hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, or skeletal deformities. Twenty-four-hour video-EEG at age 19 recorded frequent myoclonic seizures associated with generalized polyspike-wave discharges and synchronous electromyographic bursts, confirming cortical myoclonus. Interictal recordings demonstrated diffuse background slowing with multifocal polyspike-wave discharges. Brain magnetic resonance imaging (3.0T, T1-, T2-, FLAIR-, and diffusion-weighted sequences) revealed no structural abnormalities. Cerebrospinal fluid analyses and autoimmune/paraneoplastic antibody panels were unremarkable. Metabolic testing revealed a transient elevation of serum lactate (>12 mmol/L) and mild hypoglycemia (2.76 mmol/L), whereas ammonia, thyroid hormones, homocysteine, and vitamin levels were within normal limits. Given the refractory epilepsy and unremarkable neuroimaging, enzymatic and genetic evaluations were pursued. Glucocerebrosidase activity was markedly reduced to 0.76 U/L (reference 1.19 to 22.23 U/L). Targeted sequencing identified two heterozygous GBA1 variants: c.907C>A (p.Leu303Ile, maternally inherited) and c.1505G>A (p.Arg502His, apparently de novo).

Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Neuronopathic Gaucher disease (GD type 3, chronic neuronopathic Gaucher disease)

answer_key.diagnosis: Neuronopathic Gaucher disease (GD type 3, chronic neuronopathic Gaucher disease)
answer_key.etiology: Autosomal recessive deficiency of glucocerebrosidase (GBA1 gene mutations: c.907C>A p.Leu303Ile and c.1505G>A p.Arg502His)
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate substrate reduction therapy (e.g., miglustat) if accessible; consider referral for genetic counseling and monitoring for systemic involvement; continue antiseizure medication and vagus nerve stimulation for seizure control.

Refractory to multiple ASMs despite polytherapy

challenge_prompt:
A 50-year-old male with no significant past medical history presented to the emergency department following an episode of altered mental status. He had no prior seizures, traumatic brain injury, or neurological conditions. Three days earlier, he began experiencing unusual sensations: a rising sensation accompanied by a conscious 'dream reenactment' and a sense of warmth starting in his pelvis and radiating upward to his head. These episodes were initially brief but progressively increased in frequency and duration. He had been diagnosed with vasovagal syncope at urgent care but presented due to persistent and worsening symptoms.

The episodes were characterized by staring spells with preserved awareness. He reported mild confusion after some, but not all, episodes. They typically lasted 30–45 seconds, occurring randomly the first two days, then every 90 minutes on the day of presentation. He also had two brief losses of consciousness (<5 minutes), one witnessed. During his ED visit, staff witnessed an episode of staring with preserved awareness, accompanied by sinus bradycardia of 40–50 beats per minute; no other vital sign changes, motor symptoms, or pupillary changes were observed.

On examination, he was alert, oriented, and able to converse. Neurological exam was otherwise unremarkable, with no focal deficits. Neurology was consulted. An electroencephalogram (EEG) was performed in the ED, revealing a 19-second, non-motor focal seizure originating from the left anterior temporal region. Brain MRI showed no acute structural abnormalities.

Based on the above, what is the most likely diagnosis? What is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Temporal lobe epilepsy (new-onset focal epilepsy with left anterior temporal lobe focus)

answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy (e.g., lacosamide or levetiracetam) and arrange outpatient neurology follow-up.

challenge_prompt: A 56-year-old woman with a history of arterial hypertension and a previous right carotid ischemic stroke with residual left hemiplegia presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of worsening general condition, worsening left-sided motor deficit, and language comprehension and expression difficulties. On examination, she is conscious but disoriented to time and place, with bradyphrenia, left hemiplegia with hypotonia and reduced reflexes, global aphasia (echolalia, reduced fluency, unable to follow commands), left central facial paresis, and cortical blindness. Vital signs: BP 125/78 mmHg, HR 108 bpm, O2 sat 98% on room air. Chest CT reveals right lower lobe pneumonia. Brain MRI with contrast shows a large infiltrative lesion affecting the frontotemporal lobes and right insula, extending into the contralateral hemisphere via the splenium of the corpus callosum. Lumbar puncture is performed; CSF analysis is positive for JC virus DNA by PCR. HIV serology returns positive by ELISA and confirmed by Western blot. Immunology panel: CD4+ T-lymphocyte count 64 cells/µL, HIV plasma viral load 600,000 copies/mL. Workup for other opportunistic infections, including CMV, toxoplasmosis, hepatitis B and C, syphilis, and tuberculosis (CSF and sputum), is negative. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next most appropriate management step?

challenge_prompt:
A 73-year-old man was referred for involuntary movements, lower extremity weakness, and generalized pain. He had a history of C3-C5 laminectomy, carpal tunnel release, hypothyroidism, arthritis, and cataracts. He reported that his legs would suddenly 'fly out' or give out, requiring concentration to reposition them. Pain occurred upon standing, improving with ambulation. An electrophysiologist noted dyskinesias and REM sleep behavior disorder-type symptoms.

On examination, there were choreiform movements, dystonic posturing of the right lower extremity and distal right upper extremity, and moderate-to-severe axial and appendicular ataxia. Notably, these abnormal movements worsened significantly when the patient closed his eyes. Strength was 4/5 to 4+/5 in all limbs proximally and distally. Sensation was severely diminished to all modalities, predominantly distally, with almost absent proprioception at fingers, wrists, toes, and ankles. Deep tendon reflexes were absent in the lower extremities. Gait with a cane showed unsteady station, dystonic right leg and hand, and impaired left foot dorsiflexion.

MRI of the lumbar spine revealed advanced multilevel degenerative changes, canal stenosis, and facet arthrosis with grade I anterolisthesis of L4 on L5.

Nerve conduction studies and electromyography (EMG) showed severe peripheral polyneuropathy with both segmental demyelination and axonal loss. Specific findings included minimally prolonged distal motor latencies of the left ulnar nerve, delayed right median sensory latencies, decreased peroneal and tibial motor conduction velocities, and delayed F-waves. Needle EMG was normal in all proximal and distal muscles tested in all four limbs.

Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed elevated protein (83 mg/dL) but was otherwise normal. Serum anti-SSA (Ro) antibody was positive.

The patient was initially diagnosed with chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP) associated with Sjögren’s disease and treated with intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) every four weeks and hydroxychloroquine. After fifteen months, he had worsened: he required a walker, had difficulty gripping it, exhibited diffuse areflexia, and his right hemibody was more affected. A short course of prednisone was given without clear benefit.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next most appropriate management step?
gold_diagnosis: Sensory ganglionopathy (sensory neuronopathy) due to Sjögren's disease

answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue ineffective therapies; consider immunosuppressive treatment (e.g., corticosteroids plus mycophenolate mofetil, or rituximab) and refer to rheumatology for systemic Sjögren's management.

challenge_prompt: A 32-year-old woman with a five-year history of Sjögren syndrome on prednisolone (10 mg/day) presented with one day of fever and altered consciousness. She had developed joint stiffness two months prior, followed by low-grade fever and malaise one month before admission. On examination, temperature 38.0°C, BP 126/83 mmHg, HR 108 bpm, O2 sat 100%. Lymphadenopathy was noted in cervical, axillary, and inguinal regions (nodes 1-2 cm, elastic, mobile). Neurological exam: GCS 11 (E3V3M5), pupils equal and reactive, extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus or ptosis, speech intact, bilateral limb weakness (MRC 4/5 distally, 3/5 proximally) with spasticity, brisk deep tendon reflexes, bilateral Babinski signs, unable to ambulate (modified Rankin Scale 5), no meningeal signs. Laboratory studies showed pancytopenia (WBC 2.7×10³/μL, hemoglobin 9.5 g/dL, platelets 12.5×10⁴/μL), elevated LDH 1818 U/L, CRP 9.35 mg/dL, ferritin 5629.6 ng/mL, sIL-2R 2689 U/mL, decreased C3 26.8 mg/dL, C4 12.7 mg/dL, CH50 14.9 U/mL. Antinuclear antibody was positive at 1:1280 speckled pattern. Anti-SS-A antibody was >1200 U/mL. Anti-Sm antibody was >600 U/mL. Anti-dsDNA and antiphospholipid antibodies were negative. Urinalysis normal. CSF analysis showed 12 mononuclear cells/mm³, protein 139 mg/dL, glucose 29 mg/dL (blood glucose 95 mg/dL), sIL-2R 174 U/mL, IL-6 257 pg/mL. IgG index 0.91, oligoclonal bands negative. Meningitis/encephalitis panel and cultures negative. MRI brain showed symmetric FLAIR hyperintensity and swelling from the pons to the midbrain, with DWI hyperintensity and ADC hypointensity, without contrast enhancement. CT of the chest/abdomen/pelvis revealed generalized lymphadenopathy. Empirical treatment with meropenem, acyclovir, and intravenous methylprednisolone pulse (1 g/day for three days) was started on day 1-2. Bone marrow aspiration on day 2 and cervical lymph node biopsy on day 4 showed reactive changes without evidence of malignancy. The patient’s altered consciousness persisted without improvement through day 7. On day 8, she developed a discoid rash and an oral ulcer. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the best next step in management?

challenge_prompt:
Case Presentation:
A 14-year-old male with a complex medical history including 17q12 duplication syndrome, focal epilepsy, autism spectrum disorder, and attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder presents to the emergency department with acute onset of severe abdominal pain for three days. The pain is located in the upper abdomen and periumbilical region, described as unlike any pain he has previously experienced, and is associated with nausea and decreased oral intake. Over-the-counter calcium carbonate provided no relief. The patient was initially evaluated at a local emergency department, where an abdominal ultrasound was unremarkable but serum lipase was markedly elevated at 1,572 U/L (normal: 10-140 U/L). Consequently, he was transferred to a tertiary pediatric center.

One month prior to presentation, the patient was seen by pediatric neurology for an increase in seizure frequency from 1-2 per month to more than two per month. His valproic acid (VPA) dose was increased from 500 mg twice daily to 500 mg every morning and 625 mg every evening. Despite this adjustment, he experienced five seizures over the subsequent 21 days, prompting consideration of adding zonisamide.

Upon arrival at the tertiary center, the patient reports achy, non-radiating pain in the midabdomen and right lower quadrant, worsened by ambulation. Physical examination reveals no costovertebral angle tenderness, guarding, or rebound tenderness. He denies fever, chills, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, trauma, or lethargy. He has no history of alcohol use, gallstones, or recent abdominal trauma.

Laboratory studies from the outside hospital showed: lipase 1,572 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 424 U/L, AST 46 U/L, ALT 17 U/L, total bilirubin 1.3 mg/dL. Complete blood count and basic metabolic panel were within normal limits; specifically, triglycerides were normal at 34 mg/dL. Computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis was unremarkable. Repeat laboratories at the tertiary center revealed persistent elevation of pancreatic enzymes with amylase 137 U/L and lipase 190 U/L, along with mildly elevated AST 33 U/L and stable total bilirubin 1.2 mg/dL. A respiratory viral panel was negative.

The patient’s home medications include only valproic acid for epilepsy.

Based on the above presentation, what is the most likely etiology of this patient's acute pancreatitis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?
gold_diagnosis: Valproic acid-induced pancreatitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Valproic acid-induced pancreatitis
answer_key.etiology: Adverse drug reaction to valproic acid
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue valproic acid and switch to an alternative antiepileptic agent (e.g., lacosamide)
required_findings: Serum lipase markedly elevated at 1,572 U/L (normal: 10-140 U/L)
Triglycerides normal at 34 mg/dL
Abdominal CT and ultrasound unremarkable
Patient on valproic acid monotherapy for epilepsy
Valproic acid dose increased one month prior to symptom onset
No history of gallstones, alcohol use, or recent trauma
Respiratory viral panel negative
Repeat labs show persistent elevation of pancreatic enzymes (amylase 137 U/L, lipase 190 U/L)
optional_findings: Complete blood count and basic metabolic panel normal
Pain midabdominal and right lower quadrant, no rebound or guarding
Seizure frequency increase prior to VPA dose adjustment

provenance.title: Valproic Acid-Induced Pancreatitis in a Pediatric Patient With 17q12 Duplication Syndrome

challenge_prompt:
A four-year-old, typically developing female child with no significant past medical history, presented with a new onset of seizure. She was taking a nap at school, and upon awakening, school staff reported that she was on all fours with her elbows down to the ground, with her neck in a hyperextended position. When trying to interact and call the patient, she was not responsive. School staff turned her over and noted the right upper extremity shaking that lasted approximately 1 minute. During this time, it was noted that her eyes were open, and the patient was unresponsive to others calling her name or other forms of stimulation. Subsequently, she had an episode of emesis. EMS was called, and she was transported to the ED. During her transport, the patient stared as if having a 'glossed-over look' on her face without much responsiveness or interaction with her parents. She was unresponsive for a long time and returned to her baseline after one and a half hours. Upon arrival at the ED, the patient gradually improved and became more interactive with her parents. She has never had any similar episodes in the past. The family denies noticing any prior history of staring without responsiveness. The family reports no significant illnesses or any head traumas recently, but she sustained head trauma in September 2022 when the patient fell 15 steps and struck her head. The CT head during that presentation did not reveal any intracranial abnormalities. The family reports she is reaching milestones within normal limits. No behavior and mood concerns were reported. No obstetric complications or pregnancy complications were noted. She was born at full-term (40 weeks of gestational age). Family history was negative for seizures or neurological disorders. The neurological exam was nonfocal. She was subsequently admitted for video electroencephalogram (VEEG), which revealed bilateral parieto-occipital and frontal spike and wave discharges, more prominent on the right than on the left.

Based on the presentation and findings above, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Self-limited epilepsy with autonomic seizures (SeLEAS), also known as Panayiotopoulos syndrome

answer_key.diagnosis: Self-limited epilepsy with autonomic seizures (SeLEAS), also known as Panayiotopoulos syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Unknown; considered a self-limited childhood epilepsy syndrome with possible genetic predisposition.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate antiseizure medication; levetiracetam is a common first-line agent.
required_findings: Four-year-old child
Seizure upon awakening from nap (sleep-related)
Emesis during the event (autonomic symptom)
Right upper extremity shaking (focal motor activity)
Prolonged unresponsiveness (1.5 hours)
Nonfocal neurological examination
Normal CT head from prior trauma
VEEG showing bilateral parieto-occipital and frontal spike and wave discharges, more prominent on the right than on the left
optional_findings: Unusual presentation on all fours with neck hyperextended
Negative family history for seizures/neurological disorders
Gradual improvement to baseline after 1.5 hours

provenance.title: Peculiar Clinical Manifestation in a Child With Self‐Limited Epilepsy With Autonomic Seizures (SeLEAS) Presenting in an All‐Four Position: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 21-year-old female presented with a six-month history of pain and difficulty in chewing. She had a history of epilepsy for three years and was on phenytoin 100 mg twice daily and phenobarbitone 50 mg twice daily for the past 18 months. She noticed gingival changes approximately six months after starting phenytoin. On clinical examination, generalized edematous gingival overgrowth was present, involving both anterior and posterior regions, extending to the occlusal surfaces of posterior teeth and from the incisal third to the middle third of anterior teeth. The gingiva appeared pinkish-red with marked bleeding on probing. Generalized pseudopockets, tooth mobility, and subgingival calculus deposits were also observed.

Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Phenytoin-induced gingival enlargement

answer_key.diagnosis: Phenytoin-induced gingival enlargement
answer_key.etiology: Long-term phenytoin therapy
answer_key.next_management_step: Multidisciplinary approach: consultation with the treating physician to consider substitution of phenytoin with an alternative anticonvulsant (e.g., carbamazepine or valproic acid) and non-surgical periodontal therapy (scaling, root planing, oral hygiene reinforcement, 0.12% chlorhexidine mouthwash)
required_findings: 21-year-old female
Epilepsy for 3 years
Phenytoin 100 mg twice daily for 18 months
Phenobarbitone 50 mg twice daily
Gingival changes noticed 6 months after starting phenytoin
Pain and difficulty chewing for 6 months
Generalized edematous gingival overgrowth
Pinkish-red gingiva with marked bleeding on probing, pseudopockets, tooth mobility, subgingival calculus
optional_findings: Enlargement extends to occlusal surfaces of posterior teeth
Enlargement extends from incisal third to middle third of anterior teeth
Involvement of both anterior and posterior regions

provenance.title: Multidisciplinary Management of Phenytoin-Induced Gingival Enlargement: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 5-year-old boy with diplegic cerebral palsy presented to the Emergency Department 3 hours after an accidental medication overdose at home. He had undergone bilateral lower limb soft tissue contracture release surgery earlier that day and was discharged on a prescribed oral syrup (concentration 10 mg/mL) intended for spasticity management, at a dose of 2.5 mg (0.25 mL) every 12 hours. However, due to misinterpretation of the dosing instructions, he was given 15 mL (150 mg) of the syrup by his caregivers. He had never taken this medication before.

About 30 minutes after the ingestion, he became drowsy and unresponsive and then had a generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home. On arrival in the ED, he was postictal with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 9 (E2 V3 M4). He was afebrile, drooling with frothy sputum, and had constricted pupils. Chest, cardiac, and abdominal examinations were unremarkable. He had bilateral lower limb casts. Vital signs: heart rate 50–60 bpm, blood pressure fluctuating (systolic 102–119 mmHg, diastolic 47–75 mmHg), respiratory rate 25 breaths/min with shallow spontaneous respirations, oxygen saturation 100% on face-mask oxygen at 5 L/min. Capillary blood glucose was 6.4 mmol/L.

He had a known diagnosis of cerebral palsy due to periventricular leukomalacia from hypoxic-ischemic brain injury, confirmed by brain MRI at age 2 showing bilateral symmetrical white matter signal changes. He had been receiving regular physiotherapy. Birth history: emergency cesarean at 38 weeks for placenta previa; no NICU admission; walking delayed (tip-toe gait starting at 18 months).

Shortly after arrival in the ED, he had a second generalized tonic-clonic seizure, which was aborted with intravenous diazepam. A routine urine toxicology screen was negative. Serum levels of the ingested medication were not available. Basic laboratory tests (complete blood count, electrolytes, renal and liver function) were within normal limits. Venous blood gas: pH 7.35, pCO2 4.8 kPa, pO2 10.3 kPa, HCO3 20.9 mmol/L. ECG showed sinus bradycardia. Chest X-ray was also performed to rule out cardiopulmonary complications.

The patient remained postictally drowsy but had adequate spontaneous ventilation. He was transferred to the PICU for further management.

What is the most likely etiology of this patient's presentation? What is the next most appropriate management step?

He had been prescribed oral baclofen syrup (10 mg/mL) at a dose of 2.5 mg (0.25 mL) every 12 hours.
gold_diagnosis: Baclofen toxicity (oral baclofen overdose)

answer_key.diagnosis: Baclofen toxicity (oral baclofen overdose)
answer_key.etiology: Accidental ingestion of a massive dose of baclofen (150 mg, approximately 60 times the prescribed dose of 2.5 mg) in a child with cerebral palsy, leading to typical toxidrome: CNS depression, seizures, bradycardia, miosis, and respiratory depression risk.
answer_key.next_management_step: Supportive care in the PICU with close monitoring of airway, breathing, and circulation; seizure management with benzodiazepines (e.g., diazepam); no specific antidote; consideration of activated charcoal is limited due to time since ingestion; mechanical ventilation standby.
required_findings: History of cerebral palsy and antispasticity medication use
Accidental overdose: prescribed 2.5 mg (0.25 mL) but administered 150 mg (15 mL) of the syrup (60-fold dose error)
Presentation 3 hours post-ingestion with decreased consciousness, seizures, miosis, drooling
Bradycardia (HR 50-60 bpm) and fluctuating blood pressure
Normal blood glucose (6.4 mmol/L) and normal basic labs, ruling out metabolic causes
Negative routine urine toxicology screen
Venous blood gas without significant acidosis (pH 7.35)
ECG showing sinus bradycardia, chest X-ray performed to rule out causative pathology
optional_findings: Second seizure in ED aborted with diazepam
Postictal state with Glasgow Coma Scale 9
Shallow spontaneous respirations but O2 saturation 100% on oxygen
History of periventricular leukomalacia on MRI

provenance.title: Oral Baclofen Toxicity in a Pediatric Patient Managed Without Invasive Intervention: A Case Report and Literature Review

mend_provenance.added: He had been prescribed oral baclofen syrup (10 mg/mL) at a dose of 2.5 mg (0.25 mL) every 12 hours.
missing_discriminators: specific medication name (baclofen)

challenge_prompt:
A 5-year-old male presented with a 6-month history of recurrent, intermittent epigastric pain without fever, vomiting, jaundice, or weight loss. Physical examination: tenderness in right upper quadrant, Murphy’s sign positive, liver palpable 0.5 cm below costal margin. Abdominal ultrasound revealed gallbladder wall thickening (0.5 cm) with disordered, hypoechoic intraluminal echoes and scattered hyperechoic/anechoic areas. Abdominal MRI showed an enlarged gallbladder with wall thickening, intraluminal signal abnormalities, and mild dilatation of bilateral hepatic ducts, common hepatic duct, and common bile duct. Laboratory: WBC normal, eosinophils normal, AFP and CEA normal, NSE slightly elevated 16.9 ng/ml (normal 0–16.3), ALT 63 U/L (7–30), GGT 76 U/L (5–19), amylase 157 U/L (0–96), EBV IgG positive. He underwent laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Intraoperative findings: normal liver, distended gallbladder with marked edema and wall thickening, no significant common bile duct dilation. Sectioning of the gallbladder revealed a grayish-white, gelatinous wall with minimal mucoid material, no bile, calculi, or parasites. Histopathology: villous-tubular glands with low-grade glandular intraepithelial neoplasia, mucinous material and fragmented glandular epithelium within muscularis propria. IHC: glandular epithelium diffusely positive for CK8/18, high Ki67-positive cells in mucinous pools. The tumor had >50% extracellular mucin, consistent with gallbladder mucinous adenocarcinoma. Genetic testing of tumor tissue detected a low-frequency (6.31%) somatic KMT2C missense mutation (c.2922A>T, p.L974F). Postoperatively, the child recovered well with no abdominal pain.

Ten months later, he developed abnormal behaviors and regression in cognitive and motor abilities. Symptoms included purposeless hyperactivity, disruptive behaviors, aggressive actions, inappropriate vocalizations, social withdrawal, loss of previously acquired play skills, inability to follow commands, memory decline, apathy, reduced speech, dysarthria, unsteady gait, frequent falls, and deterioration of fine motor skills. Physical exam showed inattentiveness, slow responses, sluggish movements, abdominal wall reflex ++, knee reflex +. Brain MRI demonstrated symmetrical, punctate signal abnormalities in the white matter of both cerebral hemispheres, described as a 'leopard skin' pattern. Magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS) showed a reduced N-acetylaspartate (NAA) peak and an elevated choline peak in the cerebral white matter. Laboratory analysis revealed arylsulfatase A activity markedly reduced at 10.13 nmol/17 h/mg protein, less than 10% of the mean healthy control value.

What is the most likely underlying diagnosis? What is the next best diagnostic step to confirm?
gold_diagnosis: Metachromatic leukodystrophy (MLD)

answer_key.diagnosis: Metachromatic leukodystrophy (MLD)
answer_key.etiology: Autosomal recessive disorder due to homozygous or compound heterozygous pathogenic mutations in the ARSA gene, leading to arylsulfatase A deficiency and accumulation of sulfatides.
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform genetic testing for ARSA mutations (whole exome sequencing or targeted ARSA gene sequencing) to confirm the diagnosis. Consider referral to neurology and genetic counseling.
required_findings: Arylsulfatase A activity 10.13 nmol/17 h/mg (<10% of normal)
Brain MRI showing symmetrical punctate white matter signal abnormalities ('leopard skin' pattern)
Magnetic resonance spectroscopy showing reduced NAA peak and elevated choline peak in cerebral white matter
Clinical history of neurodevelopmental regression with memory loss, behavioral changes, ataxia, dysarthria 10 months after cholecystectomy
Histologically confirmed gallbladder mucinous adenocarcinoma in a 5-year-old child
Tumor histology: invasive glandular epithelium with mucin pools, CK8/18+, Ki67+
Normal AFP and CEA levels
Mildly elevated NSE (16.9 ng/mL)
optional_findings: Elevated liver enzymes (ALT 63 U/L, GGT 76 U/L)
Tumor somatic KMT2C mutation (c.2922A>T, p.L974F) at low frequency (6.31%)
Mild dilatation of hepatic and common bile ducts on MRI
Grayish-white, gelatinous gallbladder wall without stones or parasites
No fever, vomiting, or jaundice during initial abdominal pain episode

provenance.title: Gallbladder mucinous carcinoma in a child with metachromatic leukodystrophy, case report and literature review

challenge_prompt:
A 38-year-old man presented to the emergency department with a one-week history of progressive vision loss predominantly affecting the right eye, accompanied by a severe frontal headache and photophobia. His right-sided blurred vision had been worsening over the week prior to presentation, and by the day before presentation, he reported complete vision loss of the right eye. Additionally, on the day of presentation, he also reported decreased color vision of the left eye.

His medical history was notable for hypertension, severe obstructive sleep apnea, and episodic migraine-like headaches with visual aura for the past year. He reported inconsistent use of continuous positive airway pressure therapy for his sleep apnea. His social history was notable for high-risk sexual behavior, including unprotected sexual encounters with both male and female partners. He reported a two-year history of self-administering intramuscular anabolic steroids (testosterone and nandrolone decanoate) with sterile needles for body-building purposes. He denied intravenous drug use.

Vital signs: blood pressure 149/92 mmHg; heart rate 89 beats per minute; temperature 36.7 °C (98.1 °F); respiratory rate 19 breaths per minute; oxygen saturation 98% on room air. On examination, visual acuity was 20/200 in the right eye and 20/40 in the left eye. The right pupil was dilated and minimally reactive to light, while the left pupil responded normally. Extraocular movements were intact bilaterally, and fundoscopic examination revealed normal-appearing optic discs without papilledema. The remainder of the neurological examination was unremarkable.

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain and orbits revealed T2 hyperintense signal abnormalities in the periphery of the optic nerves bilaterally, more pronounced on the right side, consistent with optic neuritis.

Initial laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (WBC 19.7 × 1000/mcL), hypertriglyceridemia (190 mg/dL), and a low high-density lipoprotein level (34 mg/dL). The myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (MOG) antibody IgG and aquaporin-4 (AQP4) receptor IgG were both negative. A lumbar puncture was performed, and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis revealed a lymphocytic pleocytosis (3–4 cells/mm3 with 82–90% lymphocytes) and elevated glucose (82 mg/dL) but was otherwise unremarkable, with negative bacterial, viral, and fungal studies and a negative multiple sclerosis panel.

Additional imaging included MRI of the cervical, thoracic, and lumbar spine, which did not reveal demyelinating lesions. Computed tomography (CT) of the chest showed no evidence of granulomatous disease. CT angiography of the head and neck demonstrated severe intracranial stenoses.

Based on the presentation and initial workup, what is the most likely underlying etiology for this patient's bilateral optic neuritis, and what is the next most appropriate diagnostic step?
gold_diagnosis: Acute HIV infection presenting with bilateral optic neuritis

answer_key.diagnosis: Acute HIV infection presenting with bilateral optic neuritis
answer_key.etiology: HIV-1 infection
answer_key.next_management_step: Obtain HIV-1/2 antigen/antibody combination assay and confirmatory HIV-1 antibody testing
required_findings: Bilateral T2 hyperintense signal abnormalities in the optic nerves on MRI
Negative myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (MOG) antibody IgG
Negative aquaporin-4 (AQP4) receptor IgG
High-risk sexual behavior including unprotected encounters with male and female partners
Self-administered intramuscular anabolic steroids (testosterone and nandrolone decanoate) for two years
Cerebrospinal fluid lymphocytic pleocytosis (3–4 cells/mm3 with 82–90% lymphocytes)
Severe intracranial stenoses on CT angiography of the head and neck
Rapidly progressive bilateral visual loss (right eye complete, left eye decreased color vision) with right afferent pupillary defect
optional_findings: Hypertriglyceridemia (190 mg/dL) and low HDL (34 mg/dL)
Negative MRI of cervical, thoracic, and lumbar spine for demyelinating lesions
Negative CT chest for granulomatous disease
History of episodic migraines with visual aura

provenance.title: Bilateral Optic Neuritis as the Initial Presentation of Acute HIV Infection in a Young Bodybuilder: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
The patient was a 28-year-old man with no comorbidities or history of continuous medication use, admitted after a gunshot wound to the head. Initial presentation showed obliteration of the left frontal bone and left orbit, multifocal bifrontal contusions, and suspected left anterior cerebral artery injury. He underwent bifrontal craniectomy, external ventricular drain placement, and tracheostomy. He was discharged after ventricular drain removal and decannulation.

Two months later, cranial CT revealed extensive fractures of facial bones, left frontal bone, and orbit, with left herniation and multiple retained ballistic fragments, primarily in the left frontal lobe. Neurologically, he responded to commands, verbalized simple phrases, and moved upper limbs. He was scheduled for bifrontal cranioplasty with a customized PEEK implant and orbital reconstruction.

On the day of surgery, cranioplasty was performed under general anesthesia with propofol, midazolam, fentanyl, and dexmedetomidine. A bicoronal incision and subperiosteal dissection were done, frontal sinus obstruction was identified, and reconstruction was completed without complications. Immediate postoperatively, due to delayed awakening, flumazenil and naloxone were administered. Three minutes later, he was extubated in the operating room, showing spontaneous eye opening, following commands, with good respiratory drive and normal tidal volume. Shortly after, in the PACU, he developed a generalized tonic-clonic seizure with ocular deviation and sudden decline in consciousness, lasting about two minutes. He was reintubated and given levetiracetam. An urgent cranial CT showed mild intracranial air and interval development of mild dilation of the lateral ventricles bilaterally, particularly right temporal horns, and mild dilation of third and fourth ventricles, suggestive of mild non-obstructive hydrocephalus. No other acute intracranial pathology was identified. He was admitted to the ICU for neurological monitoring.
gold_diagnosis: Post-cranioplasty pneumocephalus

answer_key.diagnosis: Post-cranioplasty pneumocephalus
answer_key.etiology: Introduction of air during cranioplasty surgery, likely due to a dural or bone defect, leading to intracranial air accumulation.
answer_key.next_management_step: ICU admission for neurological monitoring, antiepileptic therapy, serial cranial CTs to monitor resolution; consider surgical evacuation if tension pneumocephalus develops.
required_findings: Recent cranioplasty surgery
Postoperative generalized tonic-clonic seizure and decline in consciousness
Cranial CT showing intracranial air
Mild ventricular dilation suggestive of non-obstructive hydrocephalus
No other acute intracranial pathology on CT
Administration of flumazenil and naloxone postoperatively (potential confounder)
Reintubation required after seizure
Temporal association with surgery
optional_findings: Obstruction of nasofrontal duct identified during surgery

provenance.title: Pneumocephalus and Seizure Post Cranioplasty: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 53-year-old Japanese woman with a history of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) diagnosed at age 37, well-controlled on oral prednisolone, hydroxychloroquine, and belimumab, presented with acute onset of diplopia upon awakening, limited right eye movement, incomplete closure of the right eyelid, and drooling from the right corner of the mouth when drinking. Nausea developed on day 5 after onset.

Brain MRI on day 9 revealed a T2-hyperintense lesion along the right dorsal pons (Figure 1a–d). Neurological examination on day 25 showed the right eye in an adducted position at primary gaze with complete abduction palsy during voluntary and vestibulo-ocular reflex testing. The left eye was normal in primary position but exhibited paresis on adduction. No ptosis was noted, and left lateral and vertical gazes were intact. Convergence was preserved. Horizontal nystagmus was present in the left eye on left gaze but not in the right eye. Right peripheral facial nerve palsy was observed. There were no signs of optic neuritis, dizziness, dysarthria, dysphagia, hiccups, limb weakness, sensory disturbance, cerebellar ataxia, or pyramidal tract involvement.

Blood tests at admission (day 25) were positive for aquaporin-4 (AQP4) antibodies by ELISA (10.6 U/mL) and confirmed by a cell-based assay. Antinuclear antibodies were positive, but anti-double-stranded DNA antibodies were negative, and complement C3 and C4 were within normal limits. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed a normal cell count, normal protein and IgG levels, positive oligoclonal bands, and an elevated interleukin-6 level of 13.5 pg/mL. Spinal MRI revealed no abnormalities.

Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis and what would be the next appropriate management step?
gold_diagnosis: Aquaporin-4 antibody-positive neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD) presenting as isolated paramedian pontine reticular formation (PPRF) syndrome

answer_key.diagnosis: Aquaporin-4 antibody-positive neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD) presenting as isolated paramedian pontine reticular formation (PPRF) syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune astrocytopathy mediated by antibodies against aquaporin-4 water channels
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone and azathioprine, considering immunoadsorption plasmapheresis if response is inadequate
required_findings: Acute onset horizontal gaze palsy: complete right eye abduction palsy and left eye adduction paresis
Right peripheral facial nerve palsy
Intact convergence and vertical eye movements
MRI showing T2-hyperintense lesion in the right dorsal pons
Positive serum AQP4 antibodies (ELISA 10.6 U/mL, cell-based assay positive)
Cerebrospinal fluid positive oligoclonal bands
Absence of optic neuritis, spinal cord lesions, or area postrema symptoms
History of SLE but normal complement C3/C4 and negative anti-dsDNA, arguing against active neuropsychiatric SLE
optional_findings: Drooling from right mouth corner when drinking
Antinuclear antibody positivity with negative anti-dsDNA
Cerebrospinal fluid normal cell count, protein, and IgG
Horizontal nystagmus in left eye on left gaze only

provenance.title: Isolated Paramedian Pontine Reticular Formation (PPRF) syndrome in AQP4 antibody–positive neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder: a case report

challenge_prompt:
A 7-year-6-month-old girl in a first-grade special education classroom with in-class support was referred for a second multidisciplinary evaluation due to longstanding developmental concerns not explained by a prior evaluation that concluded no autism spectrum disorder (ASD).

Developmental history: global delays (walked at 18 months, first words at 2 years). Past evaluations included WPPSI-III at age 4 showing FSIQ 78 (verbal 77, performance 94), indicating below-average cognitive functioning with a verbal-performance discrepancy. At age 7, a community-based multidisciplinary evaluation (WISC-IV, SRS, DSM-5 criteria) concluded she did not meet ASD criteria. However, parents reported persistent attentional variability, sensory sensitivities, and social difficulties.

Second evaluation at the hospital-based developmental clinic: Neurologist observed short-duration eye contact, a small yet expressive range of facial expressions, and a discrepancy between limited reciprocity in verbal discourse and intact reciprocity during mutual play. Impulse control difficulties and delayed gratification affected reciprocal engagement. Parental report described sensory sensitivities, intense interests, repetitive behaviors, and rigid behavior, but these were not observed during the neurologist's examination.

Psychological evaluation revealed strong social motivation, emotional warmth, and reciprocal interaction, accompanied by impulsiveness and challenges with emotional regulation. ADOS-2 classification indicated a low level of autism-related symptoms. The Social Communication Questionnaire (SCQ) completed by parents yielded a score above the ASD cutoff. CARS2-ST raw score was 20.5, interpreted as “minimal-to-no symptoms of ASD.” Thus, there was a discrepancy between parent-reported concerns and direct clinical observations, and between quantitative tools.

Multidisciplinary team discussion reflected divergent impressions: the neurologist suspected ASD, whereas the psychologist emphasized adaptive social motivation and reciprocity inconsistent with ASD. Given the diagnostic ambiguity, the team recommended naturalistic peer-group observation.

The child participated in six weekly 50-minute structured peer-group sessions with age-matched peers, co-facilitated by two clinicians not involved in prior assessments. Observations across sessions: she generally arrived cheerful but often late, initially sought facilitator proximity but then willingly integrated into group activity. She frequently shared personal experiences and expressed emotions, showing clear social interest and communicative intent. Engagement was often self-focused, with reduced attentiveness to others. Reciprocity fluctuated and appeared closely related to her degree of emotional and attentional regulation: when more regulated, reciprocal engagement with peers increased noticeably. Nonverbal communication was consistently intact. She formed pleasant but relatively superficial connections, particularly with boys. No restricted or repetitive behaviors were observed, nor any sensory regulation difficulties. Attentional difficulties and hyperactivity were evident and interfered with sustaining engagement and deepening peer relationships. Adult support was required to maintain focus and manage frustration. Overall, the quality of her social interaction fell within normative expectations for a child with developmental and attentional vulnerabilities.

What is the most likely diagnosis?
gold_diagnosis: Below-average cognitive functioning (borderline intellectual functioning), Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), language disorder, and emotional immaturity; not autism spectrum disorder.

answer_key.diagnosis: Below-average cognitive functioning (borderline intellectual functioning), Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), language disorder, and emotional immaturity; not autism spectrum disorder.
aliases: ADHD with borderline intellectual functioning and emotional immaturity
Developmental delays with ADHD and language disorder
answer_key.etiology: Neurodevelopmental disorders, likely multifactorial.
answer_key.next_management_step: Targeted treatment for ADHD (behavioral and/or pharmacological), educational support for cognitive and language delays, and emotional regulation interventions; avoid ASD-specific services.
required_findings: Absence of observed restricted/repetitive behaviors or sensory regulation difficulties during clinical and peer observations.
Intact nonverbal communication and social reciprocity when the child was emotionally and attentionally regulated.
CARS2-ST raw score of 20.5, interpreted as minimal-to-no symptoms of ASD.
ADOS-2 classification showing a low level of autism-related symptoms.
SCQ score above ASD cutoff (parent report) contrasting with direct clinical observations.
Neurologist noted intact reciprocity during mutual play despite limited reciprocity in verbal discourse.
Psychological evaluation emphasized strong social motivation, emotional warmth, and reciprocal interaction.

Peer-group observation showed overall social interaction quality within normative expectations for a child with developmental/attentional vulnerabilities, with engagement deficits attributable to attention and impulse control.
optional_findings: Global developmental delays (walking at 18 months, first words at 2 years).
WPPSI-III FSIQ 78 (verbal 77, performance 94) indicating below-average IQ with verbal-performance gap.
First multidisciplinary evaluation (age 7) using WISC-IV and SRS concluded no ASD.
Parental report of rigid behavior, intense interests, repetitive behaviors not observed in clinic.
Short-duration eye contact and small expressive facial range noted by neurologist.
Fluctuating reciprocity level correlated with attentional/emotional regulation state.
Engagement self-focused with reduced attentiveness to others during peer observation.
Connections formed were pleasant but superficial, with activity level aligning more with boys’ interests.

provenance.title: Challenging Case Report: Reevaluating autism diagnosis in a 7-year-old girl

challenge_prompt:
A male infant was admitted on day of life one for 'recurrent seizure-like episodes after birth.' He was the second child (G2P1), delivered vaginally at 37 weeks +1 day of gestation, with a birth weight of 2.68 kg. Apgar scores were 9 (1 min, deducted for skin color) and 8 (5 min, deducted for skin color and response). The parents were healthy, non-consanguineous, and had no significant family history.

Shortly after birth, the infant exhibited limb rigidity, facial cyanosis, and seizures, which were temporarily alleviated by phenobarbital IV (30 mg/kg). However, seizures recurred, accompanied by persistent hypertonia, bilateral clubfoot deformity, and a simian crease on the right hand.

The infant required continuous non-invasive high-frequency oscillatory ventilation to treat respiratory failure and associated feeding difficulties. Complications included pulmonary infection (Ureaplasma urealyticum positive; chest X-ray demonstrated atelectasis and increased lung markings) and hypoalbuminemia (lowest albumin 22.1 g/L). EEG at 2 days of age revealed 2–3 Hz sharp-slow waves in the left frontoparietal and occipital regions, with scattered slow waves in the right frontal and midtemporal regions. Cranial ultrasound was remarkable for enhanced periventricular white matter signal.

Seizures remained refractory to additional antiepileptic drugs, including levetiracetam and midazolam infusion. Given the refractory epilepsy, whole-exome sequencing was performed. It revealed compound heterozygous variants in the BRAT1 gene: a paternally inherited synonymous variant c.1395G>C (p.Thr465Thr), which is predicted to affect mRNA splicing (SpliceAI score >0.8), is rare in the general population (frequency 0.000193), and has been reported in patients with epileptic encephalopathy; and a maternally inherited frameshift variant c.1297delC (p.Leu433Trpfs*), which is predicted to cause protein truncation and is absent from population databases (e.g., GnomAD).
gold_diagnosis: Lethal neonatal rigidity and multifocal seizure syndrome (RMFSL) due to biallelic BRAT1 mutations

answer_key.next_management_step: Supportive care, palliative care, and genetic counseling for the family

challenge_prompt:
Here is a clinical case. Based on the information below, what is the most likely diagnosis, its most probable etiology, and what should be the immediate next step in management?

A 36-year-old man with a history of alcoholism and no other comorbidities presented with a five-day illness of fever, retro-orbital pain, headache, myalgia, and generalized petechiae. He received symptomatic treatment at home and gradually improved. Ten days after the acute illness resolved, he developed sudden weakness in both lower limbs, paresthesias, and falling without head trauma. The next day, he developed urinary retention requiring catheterization.

On admission, he was awake, oriented, flushed, and hydrated. Muscle strength was grade V in the upper limbs, grade IV in plantar flexion of the feet, and grade III in abduction and adduction; the weakness was distal and symmetric. Deep tendon reflexes were brisk, with bilateral patellar hyperreflexia and upper limb hyperreflexia. Sensory examination revealed loss of touch and temperature sensation, along with paresthesias. Hoffmann sign was indifferent, Babinski sign was negative (plantar responses were flexor), and there was no dysmetria on finger-to-nose testing.

Lumbar puncture yielded cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) with 240 cells/mm³, 89% lymphocytes, glucose 57 mg/dL, and protein 52 mg/dL. Computed tomography (CT) of the brain and spine showed no spinal cord compression, expansile lesions, abnormal calcifications, or extra-axial collections; the brain parenchyma and ventricular system were normal. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the spine revealed a hyperintense signal on T2-weighted and STIR sequences in the anterior spinal cord at C6-C7, without gadolinium enhancement, and a linear hyperintense signal extending from T8 to T10. More than three spinal segments were involved.

Serologic testing for dengue virus showed reactive IgM and a positive NS1 antigen test. Tests for hepatitis B, hepatitis C, HIV, neurocryptococcosis, tuberculosis, and syphilis were all negative.

Based on the above, what is the most likely diagnosis, its most probable etiology, and what should be the immediate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Post-dengue longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (LETM)

answer_key.diagnosis: Post-dengue longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (LETM)
answer_key.etiology: Post-infectious immune-mediated demyelination triggered by dengue virus infection
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate high-dose intravenous corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 5 days) with neurological monitoring and supportive care, including bladder management and early rehabilitation.

CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis (240 cells/mm³, 89% lymphocytes) with normal glucose and mildly elevated protein
MRI shows T2/STIR hyperintensity involving multiple spinal segments (C6-C7 and T8-T10) without contrast enhancement
Serology positive for dengue IgM and NS1 antigen
No evidence of spinal cord compression on CT
Exclusion of other common infectious etiologies (HIV, syphilis, TB, hepatitis B/C, cryptococcus)
Temporal association (neurologic symptoms beginning 10 days after dengue-like illness resolution)
optional_findings: Distal symmetric weakness with foot plantar flexion grade IV, abduction/adduction grade III
Hoffmann sign indifferent and Babinski negative
Absence of gadolinium enhancement on MRI

challenge_prompt:
A 52-year-old woman with a history of hypertension presented to the emergency department after experiencing a generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home. She was postictal, with confusion and transient memory loss. Three days prior, she had started Aldactazide (spironolactone + hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg once daily) in addition to her current antihypertensive regimen of perindopril/amlodipine and captopril. Two weeks before, her serum sodium had been 131 mmol/L. She reported adherence to a low-salt diet.

On ICU admission, she was alert, hemodynamically stable (BP 121/71 mmHg, HR 53 bpm, RR 12/min, SpO2 97% on room air), with a GCS of 15 and no focal neurological deficits. Systemic examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory evaluation showed serum sodium 115 mmol/L, potassium 3.3 mmol/L (ionized K+ 2.7 mmol/L), chloride 76 mmol/L, and lactate 8.3 mmol/L. Renal function and serum osmolality were normal. Thyroid function and morning cortisol levels were normal. Urinalysis revealed low specific gravity (1.004) without proteinuria. CT and MRI of the brain demonstrated no acute pathology.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis and etiology? What is the next best management step?

challenge_prompt:
A 25-year-old woman has a history of epilepsy starting at age 3. Initially, she had frequent daily episodes of head and shoulder nodding and sudden falls with myoclonic jerks. Seizure frequency increased to 10-15 per day, often with loss of consciousness and urinary incontinence. Triggers included emotional stress (anger) and eye closure. Later, she developed generalized tonic-clonic seizures lasting 3-5 minutes, atonic attacks with backward falls and upward eye deviation, and generalized myoclonic seizures. Urinary incontinence accompanied all seizure types. She has comorbidities of hypothyroidism and unexplained gastrointestinal complaints. Family history: paternal side has progressive myoclonic epilepsy; siblings are healthy. Examination: limited verbal communication, responding only to simple questions, indicating cognitive impairment; psychomotor slowing but independent ambulation. Repeated psychological assessments confirmed mild intellectual disability. Cranial nerves, motor, reflexes, coordination intact. EEG: diffuse paroxysmal dysrhythmic activity with spike and polyspike-wave complexes at 2-4 Hz, maximal frontocentral, with generalization and marked photosensitivity. Brain MRI: mild lateral ventricular asymmetry, patchy periventricular and parietal white matter hyperintensities on T2/FLAIR, mild white matter reduction, consistent with gliotic changes likely from perinatal hypoxic-ischemic insult. She was initially treated as Lennox-Gastaut syndrome and underwent metabolic and genetic evaluation. Metabolic studies were normal. Skin biopsy was negative for Lafora bodies. She has been treated with multiple antiepileptic drugs (valproic acid, ethosuximide, topiramate, clonazepam, lamotrigine, brivaracetam, phenobarbital) with persistent drug-resistant seizures. She had a vagus nerve stimulator implanted in 2013 with only mild benefit, and it became inactive in 2018. Based on this information, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

Genetic testing revealed homozygous c.100G>A (p.Gly34Arg) mutation in the EPM2A gene, confirming the diagnosis of Lafora disease.
gold_diagnosis: Lafora disease

answer_key.diagnosis: Lafora disease
answer_key.etiology: Pathogenic mutation in EPM2B (NHLRC1) gene
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform muscle biopsy with histopathological examination (e.g., PAS stain) to detect Lafora bodies, followed by genetic testing for EPM2A/EPM2B mutations.

challenge_prompt: A 46-year-old woman with a 20-year history of presumed generalized epilepsy with tonic–clonic seizures and comorbid major depressive disorder with panic attacks is admitted to an epilepsy monitoring unit (EMU) for evaluation of medically refractory epilepsy. Her first seizure occurred at age 13, a generalized tonic–clonic seizure without aura. She has been maintained on valproic acid (500 mg and 1000 mg twice daily) and carbamazepine (200 mg twice daily) for over three decades, yet she continues to have generalized tonic–clonic seizures approximately every three months, often in the morning. Over the last two to three years, her seizure semiology changed; events became less stereotyped and no longer predominantly in the morning. One episode involved a sudden fall without typical tonic–clonic movements, possible hypotonic posturing, and prolonged postictal confusion with incomprehensible speech lasting up to four hours. She consistently denies any auras, déjà vu, jamais vu, or epigastric rising sensations. During hospitalization, neuropsychiatric evaluation reveals unprovoked episodes of intense fear accompanied by rising epigastric discomfort, diaphoresis, hyperventilation, irritability, and social withdrawal. During these episodes, she stops ongoing activities, becomes mute, flexes her head forward, and covers her face with her hands. She reports multiple such panic-like attacks weekly despite trials of SSRIs, benzodiazepines, and cognitive behavioral therapy. These episodes have never been associated with convulsions. Past antiseizure medication trials include clobazam, lacosamide, lamotrigine, and topiramate, all unsuccessful. While in the EMU, a typical episode is captured on video-EEG, demonstrating left mid-temporal epileptiform activity coinciding with symptom onset. Brain MRI reveals no structural temporal lobe abnormalities. Based on the above, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Temporal lobe epilepsy presenting with ictal fear

answer_key.diagnosis: Temporal lobe epilepsy presenting with ictal fear
answer_key.etiology: Non-lesional mesial temporal lobe epilepsy
answer_key.next_management_step: Transition from generalized epilepsy regimen to targeted focal seizure management (e.g., reduce valproic acid and increase carbamazepine dose)
