challenge_prompt:
A 56-year-old male with multiple comorbidities (severe ischemic heart disease, ischemic dilated cardiomyopathy, congestive heart failure NYHA III, poorly controlled insulin-dependent type 2 diabetes mellitus with neuropathy, hypertension, grade III obesity, generalized anxiety disorder, active tobacco use) was admitted for elective right lower limb femoro-popliteal bypass surgery for occlusive peripheral arterial disease. There was no history of upper respiratory tract infections or gastroenteritis in the 4 weeks preceding admission; screening for influenza and COVID-19 were negative. Surgery was performed under epidural anesthesia but complicated by intraoperative venous graft thrombosis requiring reconstruction, extending duration to 8 hours.

Postoperatively, he developed delirium despite preventive measures, with agitation leading to epidural catheter and drain displacement, necessitating continuous light intravenous sedation. Sedation was titrated to a RASS score of 0 for 48 hours. Mild muscular weakness was noted but attributed to pre-existing diabetic and vascular peripheral neuropathies.

On postoperative day 3, the patient had an apnea episode shortly after a RASS score of −1, followed by cardiac arrest (asystole). ACLS was initiated per guidelines, achieving ROSC after 10 minutes. Workup for the cause of arrest: ECG and cardiac enzymes showed no evidence of malignant arrhythmias or myocardial infarction; therapeutic anticoagulation for the graft ruled out pulmonary embolism. CT head showed no ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke but revealed findings consistent with probable aspiration pneumonia.

Twenty-four hours post-arrest, with sedation stopped, neurological exam using the FOUR scale yielded a score of 5; pupils and corneal reflexes were normal. The patient had flaccid quadriplegia with diminished deep tendon reflexes, no cranial nerve involvement, and was comatose. Continuous eight-channel EEG initiated shortly after arrest and over the following week showed no epileptiform activity. Mechanical ventilation maintained stable oxygenation and CO2 clearance. Hemodynamically, he exhibited persistent sinus tachycardia and arterial hypertension. Low-grade fever was present from ICU admission, modestly responsive to antipyretics.

Cerebral MRI with contrast, performed 72 hours post-arrest due to logistical delays, showed no pathological changes. Laboratory studies for metabolic causes of coma showed mild hypothyroidism (TSH elevated, free T4 normal), moderate hypernatremia, normal thiamine. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome was excluded; benzodiazepine challenge did not produce a Lazarus response, ruling out catatonia/akinetic mutism. Cerebrospinal MRI with contrast also showed no macroscopic abnormalities.

Lumbar puncture (performed after 24-hour anticoagulation hold) revealed albuminocytologic dissociation: CSF protein 63.2 mg/dL (reference 15–60 mg/dL), white cell count < 50 × 10^9/L. Tests for NMDA receptor antibodies, neuronal IgG antibodies, antiganglioside antibodies, and systemic vasculitis were negative. Blood and CSF cultures obtained after antibiotic suspension and coagulation correction were negative.

Electromyography (EMG) performed during the third week of ICU care demonstrated the following: sural sparing sensory phenomenon (normal sural sensory nerve action potential [SNAP] with reduced amplitude upper limb SNAPs, including an abnormal median SNAP); severe, acute, symmetric motor axonal polyneuropathy affecting both proximal and distal segments.

Based on the available data, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Guillain-Barré syndrome (hyperacute/fulminant form, likely acute sensory and motor axonal neuropathy [ASMAN] variant)

answer_key.diagnosis: Guillain-Barré syndrome (hyperacute/fulminant form, likely acute sensory and motor axonal neuropathy [ASMAN] variant)
aliases: acute sensory and motor axonal neuropathy
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune, potentially triggered by recent surgery and prolonged intraoperative inflammatory response
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) therapy (0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days) or consider plasma exchange if not contraindicated

challenge_prompt:
A 52-year-old male presented with unsteady walking and slurred speech in September 2020. He had noticed slurring of speech since early 2019, but it was the unsteadiness of gait that prompted referral. His medical history included pulmonary sarcoidosis, diagnosed in 2015 and previously managed with prednisolone 10/5 mg on alternate days; however, he had not been on any steroids or other immunosuppressive treatment for a whole year before the onset of his balance problems.

Brain MRI in November 2020 showed increased signal intensity in the cerebellar peduncles, more prominent on the right, with no contrast enhancement. The signal abnormalities had a striking asymmetry and somewhat nodular appearance. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis revealed a slightly elevated protein level of 0.9 g/L and positive oligoclonal bands. A whole-body PET scan was normal. The patient had no autonomic features.

Laboratory investigations revealed persistent CD4 lymphopenia, with an absolute CD4+ T-cell count of 0.18 × 10⁹/L (180 cells/µL). Investigations for other causes of immunodeficiency, including HIV serologies, were normal.

Given these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what next diagnostic step is most appropriate?

answer_key.etiology: JC virus reactivation in the setting of CD4 lymphopenia associated with untreated sarcoidosis
answer_key.next_management_step: Brain biopsy of the affected cerebellar peduncle to confirm PML

challenge_prompt:
A Caucasian boy born at term after uneventful pregnancy, with no family history of metabolic or neurological diseases, presented with delayed motor development and marked muscular hypotonia in the first year. He crawled at 1 year, stood with assistance by 2 years, then lost that ability over the following 2 years due to spastic tetraparesis. He never learned to speak. He had generalized seizures at 1 year 11 months and at 4 years. Ophthalmologic exam revealed pale optic discs; no cataracts. No porphyric attacks were noted.

Relevant investigations:
- Urine studies at age 2 years 11 months:
  - ALA: 240.8 µmol/g creatinine (normal <25)
  - PBG: 483.3 µmol/g creatinine (normal <8)
  - Uroporphyrin: 957.2 nmol/g creatinine (normal <38.3)
  - Heptacarboxyporphyrin: 28.6 nmol/g creatinine (normal <12)
  - Pentacarboxyporphyrin: 53.3 nmol/g creatinine (normal <7.2)
  - Coproporphyrin: 125.3 nmol/g creatinine (normal <182.8) – not elevated
  - Total porphyrins elevated to 1191 nmol/g creatinine (normal <250)
- HMBS (hydroxymethylbilane synthase) enzyme activity in erythrocytes: reduced to 16% of normal (3.1 nmol/l/s, normal 13.3–24.7 nmol/l/s).
- Liver enzymes normal, no clinical liver dysfunction.
- CSF (lumbar puncture at age 2y10m): cells, glucose, protein normal; lactate 2.6 mmol/l (normal). Previous CSF at 1y11m showed lactate 3.7 mmol/l (slightly elevated). CSF ALA 0.6 µmol/l, PBG 3.65 µmol/l. (No CSF reference values available; compared to serum references: ALA normal 0.36–0.41, PBG normal <0.12)
- Serum lactate: elevated in 3 of 7 samples (2.8, 2.8, 3.7 mmol/l); urine organic acids and serum carnitine normal.
- Nerve conduction study at 4y11m: motor nerve conduction velocity of ulnar nerve normal (52.0 m/s), no peripheral neuropathy.
- Brain MRI at 1y11m: T2 high signal changes in bilateral white matter with occipital accentuation, T2 high signal changes of bilateral dorsal columns extending C1-C5, and a right temporo-occipital periventricular cyst (5 mm).
- At 2y4m: supratentorial white matter changes and spinal findings persist; MR spectroscopy from temporo-parietal white matter showed increased myo-inositol.
- At 3y9m: marked increase in supratentorial T2 high signal white matter changes, increased number of white matter cysts, and increased spinal T2 high signal changes now more central; persistent increased myo-inositol. No gadolinium enhancement.

Given this patient's presentation and workup, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next most appropriate diagnostic step?

challenge_prompt: A 51-year-old man with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis attended a screening visit for a clinical trial. His MS had been treated with four cycles of alemtuzumab between 2003 and 2013, followed by an autologous haematopoietic stem-cell transplant 3 years previously following an episode of acute optic neuritis in the right eye. The right optic neuritis resulted in a central scotoma which prevented fixation, and his full-field visual evoked potential (FF-VEP) was unrecordable. He had no history of left-sided optic neuritis. challenge_prompt:
A 32-month-old female presented to the emergency department with abnormal movements lasting >10 minutes, preceded by a 3-day history of cough, low-grade fever (37.7°C), and increased work of breathing. She was unresponsive on arrival with O2 sat 89%, RR 29, T 37.8°C, BP 105/68, HR 112. Status epilepticus was controlled with IV diazepam.

Past history: Recurrent seizures since 3 months, multiple episodes of status epilepticus, repeated admissions for bronchiolitis and aspiration, global developmental delay, failure to thrive (weight and head circumference <3rd percentile, height 10th percentile), congenital microcephaly, dysmorphic facial features (bilateral ptosis, exophthalmos, low-set ears, widely spaced teeth, microretrognathia). Feeding difficulties and recurrent diarrhea. No consanguinity; first and only child; no family history of neurodevelopmental disorders. A metabolic disorder was previously suspected but undiagnosed due to limited genetic testing access.

Examination: Bilateral wheezing, mild respiratory distress. Neurologic: increased tone, spasticity, brisk reflexes (more in lower limbs), clonus, bilateral Babinski sign.

Investigations:
- Labs: Hb 9.2 g/dL, MCV 62.3 fL (microcytic anemia); CRP 8.10 mg/L; normal renal/liver function; mild electrolyte abnormalities. Later: low serum iron 30 µg/dL, normal LDH 299 U/L, non-fasting triglycerides 207 mg/dL. ABG: pH 7.47, pCO2 31 mmHg, HCO3 23.2 mmol/L, pO2 89 mmHg, normal anion gap and lactate – mild respiratory alkalosis with compensation. Endocrine/metabolic: thyroid, PTH, B12, folate, ferritin, haptoglobin normal. Urine organic acids, acylcarnitine, amino acids, pyruvic acid, ammonia not available.
- Chest X-ray: right lower lobe airspace opacities (pneumonia).
- Brain MRI (previously): thinning of corpus callosum, delayed myelination for age, hypoplasia of anterior horn of right lateral ventricle.
- Abdominal ultrasound: minimal right hydronephrosis, otherwise normal.
- EEG: diffuse slowing with bilateral multifocal epileptiform discharges, more prominent on right side.

The pediatric neurologist suspected a neurometabolic disorder and recommended genetic testing. The patient was discharged on anticonvulsants (levetiracetam and carbamazepine) with follow-up; genetic testing by whole-exome sequencing was planned but not yet performed.

Based on the presentation and available data, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
gold_diagnosis: Combined oxidative phosphorylation deficiency 35 (COXPD35)

answer_key.diagnosis: Combined oxidative phosphorylation deficiency 35 (COXPD35)

answer_key.next_management_step: Perform whole-exome sequencing (WES) and Sanger sequencing for confirmation; consider referral to a mitochondrial disease specialist.

challenge_prompt:
A 32-year-old Chinese Han female with a known history of aquaporin-4 (AQP4) antibody-positive neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD) presented with acute severe epigastric pain and vomiting. She had initially been diagnosed 22 months earlier following an episode of right limb numbness and hypoalgesia. At that time, spinal MRI showed two hyperintense T2 lesions in the spinal cord at C2/C4 and T2 vertebral levels, brain MRI was normal, and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis showed mild pleocytosis (WBC 25 × 10⁶/L, 80% mononuclear), slightly elevated protein (0.423 g/L), slightly low glucose (2.79 mmol/L), and negative Gram stain and cultures. Serum AQP4-IgG was positive at 1:100 while CSF was negative; other antibodies (MOG, MBP, GFAP) were negative, and oligoclonal bands were absent. She was treated with intravenous methylprednisolone followed by oral prednisolone and mycophenolate mofetil (1500 mg/day) with improvement.

Twenty-two months later, she was readmitted with recurrent right upper limb numbness and weakness. Spinal MRI showed progressive cervical cord lesions from C2 to C6 without thoracic lesions. Repeat serology revealed AQP4-IgG titer of 1:1000. She was diagnosed with NMOSD relapse and started on intravenous methylprednisolone 1000 mg/day.

On the fourth day of methylprednisolone therapy, she developed acute gastrointestinal symptoms: severe epigastric pain radiating to the back with intractable vomiting. Physical examination revealed diffuse abdominal distension and localized tenderness in the right hypochondriac region. Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated abundant gallstones in the gallbladder. Laboratory tests showed profound hyperamylasemia: serum amylase 2293.8 U/L, pancreatic amylase 1843.6 U/L.

What is the most likely diagnosis for the acute abdominal symptoms, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Acute pancreatitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Acute pancreatitis
answer_key.etiology: Gallstone pancreatitis, possibly related to AQP4 autoimmunity and/or high-dose corticosteroids
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue methylprednisolone and mycophenolate, initiate bowel rest (NPO), intravenous fluids, and pain management

provenance.title: Aquaporin-4 (AQP4) antibody-positive neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD) complicated with acute pancreatitis: A case report

challenge_prompt: A 56-year-old woman presents with worsening headaches over 2 years, acute bilateral visual loss and projectile vomiting one week ago. She had blurred vision for 3 years, for which glasses were prescribed. One year ago, a head CT scan for worsening headaches revealed a pituitary adenoma measuring 3 cm by 2 cm. She was referred to neurosurgery but did not attend due to fear. She now reports complete visual loss in the left eye and reduced vision in the right. On examination: conscious, vital signs stable (BP 121/80 mmHg, heart rate 80 bpm, temperature 36.7°C, respiratory rate 18 bpm). Confrontation visual field testing reveals bitemporal hemianopia. Extraocular movements are normal. Laboratory investigations: prolactin 2 ng/L (normal 4.79–23.3 pmol/L), free T4 7.1 pmol/L (12.0–22.0), free T3 <1.7 pmol/L (3.1–6.8), serum cortisol 669 nmol/L (140–690), ACTH 1.0 pg/mL (2.8–64.6), glycated hemoglobin 5.9% (<5.7%). A repeat CT head (skull base to vertex) shows a cystic mass forming a figure of eight with an enhancing rim, measuring 4.6 x 2.7 cm, located in the sella turcica, with no hemorrhage, and compression of the optic chiasm. Paranasal sinuses are clear. Humphrey visual field: no light perception left eye, right temporal hemianopia; fundoscopy normal. Optical coherence tomography shows thinning of the optic nerve in both eyes, with neuroretinal rim loss inferiorly, nasally, and superiorly.
gold_diagnosis: Pituitary apoplexy

answer_key.diagnosis: Pituitary apoplexy
answer_key.etiology: Hemorrhagic infarction of a pituitary adenoma
answer_key.next_management_step: Start high-dose corticosteroids (e.g., prednisolone 60 mg daily) and arrange urgent neurosurgical consultation for possible transsphenoidal resection.

free T4 7.1 pmol/L (low)

challenge_prompt:
A 27-year-old male with a history of epilepsy (on levetiracetam, poorly compliant) and myasthenia gravis (diagnosed by positive serum acetylcholine receptor antibodies and abnormal repetitive nerve stimulation, on pyridostigmine 60 mg once daily) was admitted to the emergency department with progressive dysphagia over 2 months (initially solids, now liquids), regurgitation of liquids from mouth and nose, progressive shortness of breath over 4 days (worsening over 1 week), and untreated fever for 2 days. He had no chills, sweats, cough, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, aspiration, chest pain, hemoptysis, or cold-induced dyspnea. He had several episodes of undocumented fever in recent weeks. No history of substance abuse. Past surgical and family histories are unremarkable.

On examination, he appeared ill. Vital signs: blood pressure 144/87 mmHg, pulse 124 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 96% on room air, random blood sugar 386 mg/dL. No pallor, jaundice, cyanosis, edema, or clubbing. Neurological exam: Glasgow Coma Scale 4/15 (E1 V0 M3), pupils bilaterally constricted and sluggishly reactive to light, normal muscle tone. Respiratory: bilaterally normal vesicular breath sounds. Cardiovascular: audible S1 and S2, no murmurs or gallops. Abdomen: soft, non-tender, palpable liver.

Laboratory studies: serum electrolytes, liver function tests, and coagulation profile normal. Complete blood count: mild normocytic anemia, no leukocytosis. Blood cultures negative, urinalysis normal.

CT brain non-contrast: normal ventricular size and configuration, maintained gray-white matter differentiation; no intracranial hemorrhage, infarction, mass, or midline shift. MRI brain with contrast: normal cerebral parenchyma, no abnormal enhancement, demyelinating disease, infarcts, or space-occupying lesions.

Diagnostic and therapeutic modalities are limited due to resource constraints; no EEG or chest imaging is available.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's acute deterioration, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Myasthenic crisis

answer_key.diagnosis: Myasthenic crisis
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune acetylcholine receptor antibody-mediated neuromuscular junction disorder with acute respiratory muscle and bulbar weakness, likely precipitated by infection and/or hyperglycemia
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate ventilatory support (non-invasive or invasive as needed); administer intravenous immune globulin (IVIG) or plasma exchange; avoid medications that worsen myasthenia (e.g., certain antibiotics, high-dose steroids unless necessary); start empiric antibiotics for possible infection; closely monitor respiratory function.

Altered mental status (GCS 4) with sluggishly reactive pupils
Normal CT and MRI brain (excluding structural CNS causes)
Normal serum electrolytes and liver function tests (excluding metabolic causes)
Tachycardia (pulse 124) and fever
Random blood glucose 386 mg/dL
optional_findings: No leukocytosis and negative blood cultures
Normal muscle tone despite altered consciousness

challenge_prompt:
A 79-year-old man was initially treated with 20 mg of prednisolone (PSL) at a previous hospital for recurrent arthritis and lower-extremity cellulitis (day 0). Simultaneously, melena was noted, and colonoscopy revealed sub-obstructive sigmoid colon cancer (clinical stage cT3N1aM0, cStage IIIB). Serum tumor markers (CEA, CA19-9) were not consistently elevated. An endoscopic colonic stent was placed for the obstruction. PSL was tapered and discontinued on day 16, but his arthritis and cellulitis recurred. He then developed impaired consciousness (initially somnolence) and worsening oxygenation. Common causes of impaired consciousness, including heart failure, infective encephalitis, stroke, pneumonia, metabolic abnormalities, and electrolyte disturbances, were systematically excluded. Head MRI showed old cerebral infarction, old cerebellar hemorrhage, chronic ischemic changes, and age-related cerebral atrophy, but no acute lesions explaining the current impaired consciousness. CSF examination: protein 45 mg/dL (normal 10–40), glucose 65 mg/dL (normal 50–75), cell count 1 cell/3 mm³, interleukin-6 5.6 pg/mL (normal 0–4). No bacterial or viral infection was detected. Onconeural antibody testing (PNS12 panel) was negative. No additional neurological features (cerebellar ataxia, peripheral neuropathy, myoclonus, ocular motor disturbance, gastrointestinal dysmotility) were present.

On day 33, PSL 40 mg was resumed, and his consciousness improved. The sigmoid colectomy was postponed because of the PSL dose. PSL was tapered to 20 mg by day 44, but impaired consciousness recurred. Concurrently, an increased inflammatory response led to imaging revealing an iliopsoas abscess. Blood cultures grew Klebsiella pneumoniae, and disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) developed. Broad-spectrum antibiotics and a PSL increase to 40 mg (later stabilized at 35 mg) were required; the abscess shrank and consciousness stabilized. The treating team now faces a trade-off: further PSL reduction risks recurrent encephalopathy, while continued high‑dose PSL risks worsening infection. Conservative management is deemed difficult.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Autoantibody-negative paraneoplastic neurological syndrome (paraneoplastic encephalopathy/encephalomyelitis) associated with sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma

answer_key.diagnosis: Autoantibody-negative paraneoplastic neurological syndrome (paraneoplastic encephalopathy/encephalomyelitis) associated with sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma
answer_key.etiology: Paraneoplastic immune-mediated cross-reaction against neural antigens triggered by sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, without detectable onconeural antibodies
answer_key.next_management_step: Surgical resection of the primary tumor (e.g., laparoscopic Hartmann’s procedure) to reduce tumor burden, allow steroid weaning, and control the neurological syndrome while minimizing infection risk
required_findings: Known advanced sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (cT3N1aM0, stage IIIB)
Impaired consciousness developing after steroid taper, temporally related to cancer diagnosis
Steroid responsiveness: improvement with PSL 40 mg, recurrence when tapered to 20 mg
Negative onconeural antibody panel (PNS12)
CSF: mild protein elevation (45 mg/dL), normal glucose, normal cell count, mildly elevated IL-6 (5.6 pg/mL) – inflammatory but non-infectious pattern
Exclusion of other causes (heart failure, stroke, infection, metabolic/electrolyte disturbances) by systematic workup
Brain MRI: no acute structural lesions, only chronic ischemic/atrophic changes
Development of iliopsoas abscess and Klebsiella bacteremia on high-dose steroids, illustrating the risk of prolonged immunosuppression
optional_findings: Recurrent arthritis and lower-extremity cellulitis prior to neurological symptoms
Endoscopic colonic stent placement for sub-obstruction
Serum tumor markers not consistently elevated
No additional neurological signs (ataxia, neuropathy, myoclonus, ocular motor disturbance, dysmotility)

challenge_prompt: A 4-year-old female with global developmental delay and refractory epilepsy presents for evaluation. She had delayed motor and language milestones. At 7 months of age, she began having unprovoked seizures, including infantile spasms, generalized tonic-clonic, and focal impaired awareness seizures. Prolonged video-EEG monitoring revealed hypsarrhythmia and a burst-suppression pattern during sleep. Brain MRI with a 3T scanner showed: T1-weighted imaging – bilateral mildly noticeable hyperintense halo in the substantia nigra; susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) – mild hypointensity in substantia nigra and globus pallidus; cortical–subcortical atrophy; thinning of the corpus callosum; and dentate nucleus hyperintensity on T2-weighted sequences. FDG-PET demonstrated cerebellar hypometabolism. Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what next diagnostic step is most appropriate?
gold_diagnosis: Beta-propeller protein-associated neurodegeneration (BPAN)

challenge_prompt: A 32-year-old male with a history of chronic kidney disease on maintenance hemodialysis thrice weekly and hepatitis B on treatment for 4 months presented to the emergency department after experiencing a generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home lasting approximately 30 seconds. There was no history of trauma or fall. The patient reported pain in the left groin and inability to move the left lower limb. On examination, the left lower limb was shortened, abducted, and externally rotated, with localized tenderness and restricted range of motion at the hip. Distal neurovascular status was intact. No prior history of seizures was noted. Laboratory investigations showed HBsAg reactive (Index Value 57.08), serum creatinine 6.8 mg/dL, urea 78 mg/dL, hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, total protein/albumin 5.3/2.9 g/dL, PT-INR 1.1. Pelvic radiograph demonstrated disruption of the left ilio-pectineal line and medial displacement of the femoral head into the pelvis. CT pelvis confirmed loss of continuity of the anterior column of the acetabulum with central protrusion of the femoral head. Echocardiogram and chest radiograph were normal. NCCT head, EEG, and MRI were ordered for seizure evaluation. After consultation with nephrology, hepatology, and neurology, the patient was optimized for surgical management.

answer_key.next_management_step: Open reduction and internal fixation using a 10-hole 3.5 mm quadrilateral surface plate via a modified Stoppa approach, with postoperative non-weight-bearing for 6 weeks

challenge_prompt:
A 71-year-old woman presented with a 12-year history of progressive tremor, initially affecting the head, jaw, and both upper limbs. In 2022, she first sought evaluation. She exhibited head and jaw tremor at rest and bilateral upper limb tremor when hands were on the knees; tremor amplitude 1–2 cm at rest, 3–4 cm on action, worse on the right. No bradykinesia, rigidity, or pill-rolling tremor. She had dizziness, unsteady gait, speech changes, low mood, and poor sleep. Brain MRI showed cerebral atrophy, ventricular enlargement, and widened sulci without midline shift. Neuropsychological testing: MMSE 16, MoCA 6, HAMA 25, HAMD 17, SSS 43. A trial of Arotinolol (a beta-blocker) and other symptomatic medications did not improve the tremor.

Two years later, she returned with worsening tremors, dizziness, fatigue, and new symptoms: gait ataxia (described as “walking on cotton”), occasional diplopia, hoarseness, choking on liquids, constipation, irritability, and sleep disturbance. Family history: her father and several siblings had tremor. Examination: dysarthria, hoarseness, festinating gait, increased muscle tone, normal strength, vertical nystagmus, bilateral finger-to-nose dysmetria, clumsy rapid alternating movements, and water drinking test grade 2. Rest and action tremor persisted, right > left.

Repeat brain MRI revealed: brainstem degeneration/ischemia; lacunar infarcts in bilateral parietal lobes and right semioval center; cerebellar atrophy; and bilateral hippocampal atrophy (reduced volume with enlarged choroidal fissures and temporal horns). Small round lesions with long T1/T2 signal were seen in both hippocampi.

What is the most likely diagnosis? What is the next best diagnostic step?

Genetic testing revealed an expanded CAG repeat in the PPP2R2B gene, consistent with spinocerebellar ataxia type 12.
gold_diagnosis: Spinocerebellar ataxia type 12 (SCA12)

answer_key.diagnosis: Spinocerebellar ataxia type 12 (SCA12)
answer_key.etiology: Autosomal dominant CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion in the PPP2R2B gene on chromosome 5q32
answer_key.next_management_step: Genetic testing for spinocerebellar ataxia type 12 – specifically Sanger sequencing or repeat-primed PCR to quantitate CAG repeats in the PPP2R2B gene, followed by genetic counseling and symptomatic management.

Family history of tremor in multiple relatives (father and siblings)
Bilateral hippocampal atrophy on MRI (reduced volume, enlarged choroidal fissures and temporal horns)

Cerebral atrophy with ventricular enlargement on MRI
No significant improvement with beta-blocker (Arotinolol)
optional_findings: Lacunar infarcts in bilateral parietal lobes and right semioval center

challenge_prompt: A 28-year-old woman with end-stage renal disease secondary to IgA nephropathy, managed with automated peritoneal dialysis for the past 15 months, presents to the emergency department with altered consciousness and disorientation. She has a history of secondary arterial hypertension (Grade II) and hypertensive cardiopathy. On admission, blood pressure is 150/90 mmHg. Shortly after admission, she experiences a generalized tonic-clonic seizure, with no prior history of epilepsy. She is transferred to the ICU. gold_diagnosis: Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES)

answer_key.diagnosis: Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES)
answer_key.etiology: Endothelial dysfunction and failure of cerebral autoregulation leading to vasogenic edema, triggered by hypertension and uremia in the setting of end-stage renal disease on peritoneal dialysis.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate intravenous antihypertensive therapy and anticonvulsants, and consider temporary hemodialysis for volume and uremic control.

challenge_prompt: A male infant was born with low APGAR scores, fetal distress, and jaundice. Shortly after birth, he had a left-sided focal seizure requiring NICU stabilization. Development was otherwise uncomplicated until 20 months of age, when he presented with a tonic-clonic seizure believed to be triggered by hypoglycemia due to poor feeding. At that time, blood glucose was approximately 30 mg/dL, insulin was undetectable at 3 mIU/L, free fatty acids were elevated at 1.14 mmol/L, and free carnitine levels were low. Imaging showed no acute findings, but bilateral adrenal calcifications were noted incidentally, attributed to neonatal hemorrhage. He was managed with a diet providing 30% of calories from fat, with feedings every 2-3 hours. He experienced persistent fatigability, prompting further evaluation at age 11. Metabolic testing for recurrent hypoglycemia revealed elevated acylcarnitine levels, raising suspicion for carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase (CACT) deficiency or carnitine palmitoyltransferase II (CPT II) deficiency. Mitochondrial studies on cultured skin fibroblasts from the patient and family members showed elevated C16 acylcarnitine in the patient, mother, and father, but not in an unaffected fraternal twin. CPT2 activity measured in the patient's fibroblasts was 0.48 nmol/min/mg protein, compared to a control mean of 0.94 ± 0.21 nmol/min/mg protein. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next best step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Carnitine palmitoyltransferase II deficiency

answer_key.diagnosis: Carnitine palmitoyltransferase II deficiency
answer_key.etiology: Impaired CPT2 enzyme activity due to compound CPT2 genetic variants (Val368Ile and Met647Val) resulting in mild-to-moderate CPT II deficiency
answer_key.next_management_step: Start carnitine supplementation (levocarnitine) and dietary management (low-fat, high-carbohydrate diet with medium-chain triglycerides, avoidance of fasting)

challenge_prompt:
A 5-year-old previously healthy girl presented with a 10-day history of abdominal pain and tremulousness in the lower limbs. The leg cramps were associated with pain, hyperhidrosis, and numbness, severely limiting her activities. Over the course of admission, she developed sleep disturbance, cognitive decline, behavioral changes, memory impairment, and decreased interest in her surroundings. There was no recent illness or medication use. Birth and family histories were unremarkable. On examination, she appeared depressed with reduced verbal output. There were no focal neurological deficits. EEG showed slow background activity and focal slowing. Brain MRI and nerve conduction study were normal. CT of chest, abdomen, and pelvis was negative for neoplasm. CSF analysis revealed no pleocytosis, normal protein, and normal glucose. Based on the clinical presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

challenge_prompt:
A male infant with developmental delay was evaluated for intractable epilepsy. From 7 months of age, he experienced episodes of excessive salivation and involuntary twitching of the right eyelid without loss of consciousness, occurring dozens of times per day. Video EEG showed an abnormal infant EEG; during monitoring, a focal-onset seizure arose from the right cerebral region. Interictal discharges (spikes, spike-and-waves, sharp-and-waves) were seen in the right frontal, anterior, middle, and inferior temporal lobes, and similar discharges in the left middle and posterior temporal lobes. He was diagnosed with focal epilepsy. Oxcarbazepine was started, but seizures persisted; sodium valproate and levetiracetam were added with poor response. At 11 months, seizures progressed to epileptic spasms: clusters of limb jerking (4–5 events per cluster, 5–6 clusters per day). Ictal EEG showed clustered generalized slow waves with low-amplitude fast activities, meeting criteria for infantile epileptic spasms syndrome. PET-CT revealed reduced cortical glucose metabolism in multiple areas of the right frontal lobe. Levetiracetam was discontinued, topiramate and high-dose methylprednisolone were initiated, and seizures ceased. Over 4 years of follow-up, no further seizures occurred. Brainstem auditory evoked potential demonstrated no identifiable waveforms in the right ear, leading to a diagnosis of right-sided hearing loss. At 5 years, growth and development remain markedly delayed: unstable walking, inability to stand on one foot, only a few reduplicated words, and little active communication. Physical exam reveals microcephaly, wide eye distance, epicanthus, external auditory canal malformation, and syndactyly of the third and fourth toes. Cranial MRI showed widening of the right temporal lobe gyrus with abnormal signals, and multiple small nodules along the ependyma of the bilateral lateral ventricles, consistent with gray matter heterotopia. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what test would confirm it?

Whole-exome sequencing revealed compound heterozygous pathogenic variants in the DDX11 gene.
gold_diagnosis: Warsaw breakage syndrome (WABS)

answer_key.diagnosis: Warsaw breakage syndrome (WABS)
answer_key.etiology: Biallelic pathogenic variants in the DDX11 gene (c.2120delT and c.1949-3C>T)
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform whole-exome sequencing or targeted DDX11 genetic testing to confirm the diagnosis; initiate appropriate supportive care and multidisciplinary management.

Bilateral periventricular gray matter heterotopia on MRI
Refractory epilepsy evolving from focal seizures to infantile epileptic spasms
Severe global developmental delay

challenge_prompt: A 15-year-old girl with ADHD presents with five days of continuous spinning vertigo, preceded by a single episode of emesis. She also reports right upper arm soreness, transient left-sided weakness, intermittent 'pushback' when lifting with right arm, episodic involuntary twitching of the right upper extremity after cold exposure, and transient numbness/paresthesias in the left upper extremity. Gait is unsteady. One year ago, she had an episode of headache, vomiting, and balance difficulties; head CT was normal, and since then she has had persistent baseline gait unsteadiness. Examination: afebrile, hemodynamically stable. Nystagmus observed. Subjective intermittent blurred vision and difficulty focusing, without diplopia or visual field deficits. No focal neurological deficits. Initial labs: CBC, CMP, ESR, CRP, glucose, urine toxicology, vitamin B12, respiratory viral panel, autoimmune panel all within normal limits. Lumbar puncture: CSF: mild leukocytosis (lymphocytic predominance), mildly decreased glucose, mildly elevated protein. CSF immunology: elevated IgG index, elevated IgG synthesis rate, presence of CSF-restricted oligoclonal bands. MOG antibody in CSF negative. CSF culture negative. Brain MRI with and without contrast: extensive supratentorial and infratentorial T2/FLAIR hyperintensities, most prominent in periventricular white matter oriented perpendicular to the ventricles, with corpus callosum involvement (Dawson's finger morphology). Many lesions are hypointense on T1. Multiple enhancing lesions: incomplete ring-enhancing 1.0 cm lesion in left centrum semiovale, 0.4 cm nodular enhancing focus in right centrum semiovale, incomplete ring-enhancing 0.6 cm lesion in right basal ganglia, and a ring-enhancing 0.7 cm lesion in the right middle cerebellar peduncle. No restricted diffusion. MRV: no dural venous sinus thrombosis. MRI orbits: right-greater-than-left optic nerve atrophy with increased T2 signal, no definite enhancement, interpreted as sequelae of prior optic neuritis. Cervical spine MRI: numerous non-enhancing short-segment T2 hyperintense lesions from C1-C6, with an enhancing lesion at C5-C6 (1.2 cm). Thoracic spine MRI: short-segment T2 hyperintense lesion at T4-T5 without enhancement. Lumbar spine MRI normal.
gold_diagnosis: Pediatric-onset multiple sclerosis (POMS)

answer_key.diagnosis: Pediatric-onset multiple sclerosis (POMS)
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune demyelinating disease of the central nervous system
answer_key.next_management_step: High-dose intravenous corticosteroids (e.g., methylprednisolone) for acute relapse and referral to a specialized multiple sclerosis center for long-term disease-modifying therapy.

CSF-restricted oligoclonal bands and elevated IgG index
MRI evidence of optic nerve atrophy without enhancement, consistent with prior optic neuritis

Multiple short-segment non-enhancing spinal cord lesions and an enhancing cervical cord lesion
Negative CSF MOG antibody
No diffusion restriction on brain MRI

CSF mild lymphocytic pleocytosis and mildly elevated protein
Intermittent right arm sensory/motor symptoms including cold-induced twitching

challenge_prompt: A 12-year-old boy was admitted after an afebrile convulsion characterized by eyes deviated to right, twitching and slanting of the mouth to the right, lip cyanosis, clenched fists, limbs stiffness, and loss of consciousness lasting several minutes. He was the first child of non-consanguineous, healthy parents, with normal intelligence and no remarkable family history. On admission, he appeared alert and oriented. Measurements and vital signs: body height 142 cm (3th–10th centile), body weight 32 kg (3th–10th centile), temperature 36.7°C, blood pressure 96/65 mmHg, heart rate 92/min, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 100% on room air. Neurological examination—including cranial nerve examination, meningeal irritation signs, and pathologic reflexes—was unremarkable. Serum electrolytes: serum potassium (K) 3.0 mmol/L (3.5–5.3 mmol/L), serum sodium (Na) 137.0 mmol/L (137.0–147.0 mmol/L), serum chlorine (CL) 94.0 mmol/L (99.0–110.0 mmol/L), serum magnesium (Mg) 0.77 mmol/L (0.75–1.02 mmol/L), serum calcium 2.30 mmol/L (2.41–2.52 mmol/L), serum phosphorus (P) 1.44 mmol/L (0.85–1.51 mmol/L). Blood ammonia, serum glucose, and blood lactic acid levels were normal. Renal function test: serum urea 5.33 mmol/L (2.60–7.50 mmol/L), creatinine (Cr) 40.00 µmol/L (31.00–132.00 µmol/L), blood urea nitrogen/creatinine ratio 33. Blood gas analysis: PH 7.45 (7.35–7.45), pO2 101.00 mmHg (83–108 mmHg), pCO2 44.00 mmHg (35–48 mmHg), HCO3 29.30 mmol/L (21–28 mmol/L), BE 5.70 mmol/L (−2–3 mmol/L). 24-h urine electrolyte test: urine potassium 32.67 mmol/24 h (25–100 mmol/24 h), sodium 91.00 mmol/24 h (130–260 mmol/24 h), chlorine 76.00 mmol/24 h (170–250 mmol/24 h), calcium 0.21 mmol/24 h (2.5–7.5 mmol/24 h). Hypertension-related tests showed activation of the renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system. Electrocardiogram (ECG) was normal. Brain magnetic resonance imaging showed no abnormality. Renal and adrenal computed tomography scans showed decreased volume of the left kidney and increased volume of the right kidney. The electroencephalogram (EEG) revealed generalized spike-and-wave discharges, predominantly in bilateral anterior regions. Review of the patient’s medical history revealed two episodes of syncope (specific manifestations not available) at approximately 4 and 5 years of age. During the second syncope episode, serum potassium level was 2.9 mmol/L, which improved after symptomatic treatment.
gold_diagnosis: Gitelman syndrome and epilepsy

answer_key.diagnosis: Gitelman syndrome and epilepsy
answer_key.etiology: Compound heterozygous mutations in SLC12A3 gene (c.961C>T, p.R321W and c.1288T>G, p.C430G)
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate oral potassium chloride supplementation and levetiracetam; order genetic testing for SLC12A3 mutations to confirm Gitelman syndrome.

challenge_prompt:
A 31-year-old man presented to the emergency department after a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately one to two minutes. Earlier that morning, he had brief staring behavior and then a headache. He had no prior history of epilepsy, immunodeficiency, or recent infection. He was born and raised in Nepal and had been living in Japan. On arrival, his Glasgow Coma Scale score was 12 (E4V3M5). Vital signs: blood pressure 170/100 mmHg, heart rate 112 beats/min, respiratory rate 24 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Electrocardiography showed normal sinus rhythm. Neurological examination revealed no focal motor deficits, sensory disturbance, dysarthria, or meningeal signs; pupils were 3 mm and reactive bilaterally.

Laboratory studies showed a C-reactive protein of 0.4 mg/dL (reference <0.3 mg/dL), white blood cell count 8,900/µL (3,300-8,600/µL), hemoglobin 18.3 g/dL (13.5-17.6 g/dL), and platelet count 329,000/µL (150,000-350,000/µL).

Non-contrast head computed tomography performed about two hours after symptom onset revealed a calcified mass lesion in the right frontal lobe. Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated ring enhancement of the lesion. On magnetic resonance imaging, the lesion was hypointense on T1-weighted images. T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences showed marked perilesional edema extending into the surrounding white matter with mild local mass effect. Gadolinium-enhanced T1 images showed a solitary cystic intracerebral lesion measuring approximately 15 × 10 mm with ring-like enhancement. No definite scolex was seen on imaging.

After two weeks, a right frontotemporal craniotomy was performed. Intraoperatively, the lesion appeared as a firm, well-circumscribed intraparenchymal nodule. Following excision, the specimen was incised, releasing a purulent-like fluid.

Histopathological examination showed an encapsulated cystic lesion with a distinct three-layered architecture. The inner layer consisted of a cystic cavity containing necrotic debris; no parasite-specific structures were identified. The middle layer was a fibrous capsule with capillary proliferation and inflammatory cell infiltration (predominantly lymphocytes and plasma cells). The outer layer was composed of adjacent brain parenchyma with mild reactive gliosis. No microorganisms were detected on periodic acid–Schiff, Grocott methenamine silver, or Ziehl–Neelsen staining. The surrounding brain tissue showed mild glial proliferation without significant nuclear atypia or mitotic activity; the Ki-67 labeling index was extremely low. Overall, the histological features were considered compatible with a three-layered structure suggestive of a parasitic cyst, but no definitive parasitic elements, such as a scolex, were identified.

Based on the above information, what is the most likely diagnosis? What is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Probable neurocysticercosis (NCC)

answer_key.diagnosis: Probable neurocysticercosis (NCC)
aliases: Cysticercosis of the central nervous system
answer_key.etiology: Larval stage of Taenia solium
answer_key.next_management_step: Observation without antiparasitic therapy; consider antiepileptic drugs for seizure control if needed.
required_findings: born and raised in Nepal
witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure
calcified mass lesion in the right frontal lobe
ring enhancement
solitary cystic intracerebral lesion measuring approximately 15 × 10 mm with ring-like enhancement
three-layered architecture
inner layer consisted of a cystic cavity containing necrotic debris
no microorganisms were detected on periodic acid–Schiff, Grocott methenamine silver, or Ziehl–Neelsen staining
optional_findings: blood pressure 170/100 mmHg, heart rate 112 beats/min

challenge_prompt:
An 82-year-old female with a history of obstructive sleep apnea, stroke, diabetes, hypertension, chronic kidney disease, obesity, and right bundle branch block was referred to hematology for persistent thrombocytosis. Her platelet count had been >700,000/µL (normal 140-366 ×10^9/L) for six months. She was asymptomatic, with an unremarkable review of systems. Physical examination revealed a small left axillary lymph node and mild edema. No other lymphadenopathy or organomegaly was appreciated. Complete blood count showed normocytic anemia (hemoglobin 11 g/dL, hematocrit 34.5%, MCV 86.6 fL, MCH 28.9 pg, MCHC 33.4 g/dL).

Serum protein electrophoresis and immunofixation identified an IgG kappa monoclonal protein. Free light chain assay showed kappa 163.5 mg/L (reference 3.3-19.4), lambda 22 mg/L (reference 5.7-26.3), and a kappa/lambda ratio of 7.43 (reference 0.26-1.65).

Bone marrow biopsy demonstrated hypercellular marrow with increased megakaryocytes and a clonal plasma cell population accounting for 20-30% of nucleated cells. Immunostaining showed kappa light chain restriction (kappa/lambda >10). Karyotype and fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) for plasma cell neoplasms were normal.

PET/CT scan showed fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG)-avid activity in the left axillary node. Core needle biopsy of the node revealed reactive follicular hyperplasia without evidence of malignancy.

Vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) level was 832 pg/mL (normal range 31-86 pg/mL).

The patient had no symptoms or signs of peripheral neuropathy on history or neurological examination.

Based on the information provided, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next diagnostic step?
gold_diagnosis: Atypical POEMS (Polyneuropathy, Organomegaly, Endocrinopathy, Monoclonal protein, Skin changes) syndrome

answer_key.diagnosis: Atypical POEMS (Polyneuropathy, Organomegaly, Endocrinopathy, Monoclonal protein, Skin changes) syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Underlying plasma cell disorder (IgG kappa) with markedly elevated vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) driving systemic manifestations
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform electromyography (EMG) and nerve conduction studies to evaluate for subclinical polyneuropathy, which is a mandatory diagnostic criterion for POEMS.
required_findings: Thrombocytosis (platelet count >700,000/µL for six months)
IgG kappa monoclonal protein
Serum free kappa light chain 163.5 mg/L with kappa/lambda ratio 7.43
Bone marrow plasma cells 20-30% with kappa light chain restriction
VEGF level 832 pg/mL (markedly elevated)
Left axillary lymphadenopathy
Presence of edema
Absence of clinical peripheral neuropathy
optional_findings: Reactive follicular hyperplasia on lymph node biopsy
History of stroke and multiple comorbidities
Absence of family history of hematologic malignancy

challenge_prompt:
Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

A 30-year-old woman was admitted to hospital in November 2024. She initially reported aching pain with swelling in the lower limbs. Days later, she developed progressive limb weakness that limited her arms elevation above shoulder level and impaired independent walking. During the week before admission, she experienced bilateral ptosis, difficulty chewing, dysphagia with choking on water, and exertional dyspnea. She underwent thymectomy for type B1 thymoma in May 2024. Upon admission, the patient was conscious and alert but exhibited dysarthria. Marked proximal weakness was present in both the upper and lower limbs. Both upper limbs were held in a flexed posture with the fingers adducted toward the palms, and extension was restricted by joint stiffness. Tenderness was noted in the gastrocnemius muscles. Laboratory testing revealed a markedly elevated creatine kinase level (1985 U/L) and positivity for anti-AChR antibody (>80 nmol/L, detected by radioimmunoassay (RIA)), anti-titin (1:100), anti-RyR (1:30), and ANA (1:320) antibodies. Myositis-specific antibodies were tested using a cell-based assay (CBA) and immunoblot, and the results were negative. Electromyography (EMG) demonstrated myopathic changes characterized by short-duration, low-amplitude, and polyphasic motor unit potentials. Repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS) revealed a significant decremental response at low-frequency stimulation. Muscle MRI displayed diffuse T2 hyperintensity involving multiple muscle groups of the right upper and lower limbs, consistent with diffuse muscle edema. Unfortunately, muscle biopsy was refused.
gold_diagnosis: Anti-acetylcholine receptor antibody-positive generalized myasthenia gravis with concurrent idiopathic inflammatory myopathy (IIM-MG overlap syndrome)

aliases: Myasthenia gravis with inflammatory myopathy overlap
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune; likely paraneoplastic associated with thymoma, involving anti-AChR antibodies and possibly other myositis-associated antibodies, though myositis-specific antibodies were negative
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate efgartigimod (or another fast-acting immunomodulatory therapy) along with high-dose corticosteroids, acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (pyridostigmine), and a steroid-sparing agent (mycophenolate mofetil)

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

A 23-year-old Indian male presented to the emergency department in the United Arab Emirates with two days of abnormal behaviour, confusion, and agitation. He had relocated from India for work and had been living in a labor camp with poor sleep (2–3 hours/night). Two days before admission, he became mute, gave incoherent answers, was restless, fearful, and unaware of surroundings. He played loud music without reason and insisted he did not want to return to the camp. He was moved to a hotel but slept only 2–3 hours. The next day, he hid behind a door, gestured as if crying for help, and sent incoherent text messages. He exhibited frequent blinking, lip trembling, and repetitive hand movements. He recalled little of these events. After a brief calm period, he became agitated, refused to board a flight, snatched his passport, and was combative. In the ED, he was pacing, mute with eye contact, right arm and leg rhythmic movements, blinking. After diazepam, he became calmer but remained incoherent.

The psychiatric assessment noted mutism and intermittent restlessness. Collateral history revealed a similar episode nine months prior in India with aggression, suspiciousness, disorganized speech lasting four days; treated with valproate, cariprazine, amisulpride, clonazepam, with full recovery. He discontinued medications afterwards.

He also had two generalized tonic–clonic seizures: the first nine months earlier (poorly recalled), the most recent three weeks prior after a febrile illness, characterized by whole-body jerking 2–3 min, eye-rolling, frothy secretions, and post-ictal confusion. He did not seek medical attention. No family history of psychiatric or neurological illness. He reported remote recreational drug use (marijuana, LSD, crystal methamphetamine) two years ago leading to a brief substance-induced psychotic admission, but denied any use since.

On presentation, vital signs were stable (BP 134/85 mmHg, HR 70 bpm, RR 18/min, T 36.9°C, SpO2 98%). He was alert but poorly responsive with mutism, staring, psychomotor retardation, and no focal neurological deficits. Mental status examination revealed constricted affect, soft hesitant speech, incoherent thought processes, persecutory delusions, and auditory hallucinations (voices warning him not to trust others). Insight and judgment were poor.

Routine laboratory tests: mild lymphocytosis (3.1 × 10³/μL), elevated creatine phosphokinase (263 U/L), normal electrolytes, liver, and renal function. As part of workup for altered mental status, a CT brain was performed, showing a 3 mm cortical calcification with perilesional edema in the inferior posterior left frontal lobe. EEG was normal.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Calcified parenchymal neurocysticercosis

answer_key.diagnosis: Calcified parenchymal neurocysticercosis
answer_key.etiology: Infection by larval stage of Taenia solium (pork tapeworm)
answer_key.next_management_step: Begin combination treatment with albendazole 400 mg twice daily for 2 weeks, dexamethasone 2 mg twice daily for 1 week, along with lamotrigine (antiepileptic) and risperidone (antipsychotic), and ensure multidisciplinary neurology–psychiatry follow-up.

Acute psychotic episode with mutism, agitation, incoherent thought, persecutory delusions, and auditory hallucinations
History of two generalized tonic-clonic seizures
CT brain shows a 3 mm cortical calcification with perilesional edema in the left inferior posterior frontal lobe
EEG is normal
Mild lymphocytosis (3.1 × 10³/μL) and elevated creatine phosphokinase (263 U/L)
No focal neurological deficits on examination
Prior episode nine months ago with full recovery on valproate, cariprazine, amisulpride, clonazepam

Recent febrile illness prior to last seizure
Sleep deprivation and stress from labor camp living
Right arm/leg rhythmic movements and blinking during ED assessment

Normal electrolytes, liver, and renal function

challenge_prompt:
A 63-year-old man presented with reduced vision and vivid, well-formed visual hallucinations of brightly coloured animals and snakes occurring multiple times daily. Each episode lasted several minutes and abated with eye closure. He retained insight that the hallucinations were not real and denied auditory hallucinations or other psychotic features. He initially concealed these symptoms from family and clinicians due to embarrassment.

Fifteen days earlier he had undergone bilateral Nd:YAG peripheral iridotomy for acute angle-closure glaucoma. On examination, visual acuity was 6/24 in the right eye and 6/18 in the left eye. There was corneal oedema, shallow anterior chambers, immature cataracts, a cup–disc ratio of 0.5 bilaterally, and a left macular dot-blot haemorrhage. Intraocular pressures were unrecordable at that time; anterior segment optical coherence tomography confirmed narrow/closed angles. MRI brain was recommended to exclude structural lesions but was declined.

Psychiatric evaluation showed intact orientation and cognition, with preserved insight. The hallucinations were exclusively visual, and there was no evidence of psychotic depression (no pervasive low mood at presentation), no brainstem signs or sleep–wake disturbance to suggest peduncular hallucinosis, and no cognitive impairment to suggest dementia. The temporal correlation with the recent acute visual loss was noted.

Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Charles Bonnet syndrome

answer_key.diagnosis: Charles Bonnet syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Sensory deprivation with cortical release phenomenon precipitated by acute visual loss due to angle-closure glaucoma
answer_key.next_management_step: Reassurance and psychoeducation about the benign nature of the hallucinations; consider low-dose clonazepam (0.25 mg at night) for associated anxiety if needed

Preserved insight that hallucinations are unreal
No auditory hallucinations or other psychotic features
Recent bilateral Nd:YAG peripheral iridotomy for acute angle-closure glaucoma
Reduced visual acuity (6/24 OD, 6/18 OS) and narrow/closed angles on OCT
Intact orientation and cognition on psychiatric assessment
Temporal correlation with acute visual loss
optional_findings: Corneal oedema and shallow anterior chambers
Absence of pervasive low mood at initial presentation

challenge_prompt:
A 34-year-old woman with a history of depression, cannabis use disorder, and suspected cyclic vomiting syndrome was transferred from a psychiatric facility to the emergency department for persistent tachycardia, agitation, and profound fatigue. Three weeks prior, she had been admitted for acute-onset paranoia, auditory hallucinations, and behavioral disturbances, including fear of contamination, present for several weeks and accompanied by a 15-pound weight loss. She was started on aripiprazole 10 mg daily at the psychiatric facility.

On arrival to the emergency department, she appeared confused and reported visual hallucinations. Vitals showed tachycardia (heart rate 100-120 bpm) with otherwise stable vital signs. She was tremulous, with a mild goiter on examination. Laboratory evaluation revealed thyrotoxicosis with TSH <0.008 μIU/mL (reference range 0.4-4.0), free T4 4.13 ng/dL (0.8-1.8), and total T3 4.98 ng/mL (0.8-2.0). A TSH 1 year prior had been normal. Mild transaminitis was noted. No personal or family history of autoimmune disease.

Endocrinology was consulted, and the diagnosis of Graves disease was made based on clinical features, diffuse goiter, tachycardia, tremor, weight loss, and elevated thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin (TSI) 3.0 IU/L (RR <0.55), thyrotropin receptor antibodies (TRAb) 4.05 IU/L (RR 0-0.9), and anti-thyroid peroxidase (TPO) antibody 300.2 IU/mL (RR <35). Thyroid ultrasound revealed a diffusely enlarged, heterogeneous, hypervascular gland. She was started on methimazole 20 mg daily and propranolol 25 mg twice daily.

Despite resolution of tachycardia, neuropsychiatric symptoms persisted, prompting replacement of aripiprazole with risperidone. She was discharged to psychiatry but readmitted the following day after being found slumped in her seat with drooling, unresponsiveness, and extremity tremors.

On readmission, neurological examination revealed nonrhythmic left-predominant tremors, bilateral upper extremity stiffness, and hyperreflexia. EEG showed mild diffuse encephalopathy; brain MRI was normal. Repeat thyroid function testing: free T4 2.27 ng/dL, total T3 2.71 ng/mL. CSF analysis: mild lymphocytic pleocytosis (9 WBCs/μL), glucose 67 mg/dL, protein 23 mg/dL. Oligoclonal bands: 13 in CSF, absent in serum. CSF GAD65 antibodies: 183 IU/mL (reference <5 IU/mL). Serum GAD65 antibodies: 1600 IU/mL (reference <5 IU/mL). Other autoimmune CSF antibodies (NMDA receptor, GABA-B receptor, paraneoplastic panel) returned negative.

Based only on the presentation above, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

answer_key.diagnosis: Stiff-person syndrome (GAD65 antibody-associated autoimmune encephalitis) with concurrent Graves disease

answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate high-dose corticosteroids (e.g., IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily) for suspected autoimmune encephalitis and consider therapeutic plasma exchange

challenge_prompt:
A 7-year-old boy with no significant medical history presented with a 2-month history of reduced visual acuity, right-sided ocular misalignment, headaches, and drowsiness, progressing to marked sleep disturbances, disinhibited behavior, psychomotor regression, and acute abnormal movements. Neurological examination revealed right hemiparesis with bilateral pyramidal signs, cerebellar ataxia, multiple cranial nerve involvement including visual impairment, right divergent strabismus with bilateral ophthalmoplegia. He had cataplexy and generalized chorea. Ophthalmologic examination: right eye visual acuity limited to light perception, left eye 5/10; sluggish pupillary light reflex and pale optic discs; macular OCT showed thinning of macular ganglion cell layer, papillary OCT showed thinning of temporal RNFL, indicating bi-temporal optic nerve atrophy. CASE score 9/27. Brain MRI: extensive diencephalic infiltrative lesion extending into brainstem, with bilateral anterior optic nerve and optic chiasm involvement, suggestive of MOGAD. EEG: mild generalized slowing. CSF analysis: unremarkable on biochemical and microbiological studies; negative Gram stain and culture. Isoelectric focusing revealed mild intrathecal synthesis of IgG oligoclonal bands, IgG index 0.86, no blood-brain barrier disruption. Serological testing for HIV, hepatitis B and C negative. AQP4-IgG was negative. Comprehensive autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel (including LGI1, CASPR2, AMPAR1, AMPAR2, GABABR) was negative. Paraneoplastic panel (Cv2, Ma2, Ri, Yo, Hu, recoverin, titin, SOX1, amphiphysin) negative; NSE mildly elevated 31.35 ng/ml (ref ≤17). Tumor markers CEA, CA125, CA19-9 negative. Chest-abdomen-pelvis CT negative for tumor. Based on the clinical presentation and workup to this point, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what specific diagnostic test should be performed next?

Both anti-MOG and anti-NMDAR antibodies were detected in serum.
