challenge_prompt: A 16-year-old boy with Crohn's disease, treated with adalimumab 40 mg subcutaneously every other week for three months, presented with a one-day history of acute fever, dizziness, drowsiness, blurred vision, frontal and nuchal pain, and diplopia. He had missed two scheduled doses due to a preceding upper respiratory tract infection. He was admitted for urgent evaluation of suspected central nervous system infection or inflammation. On examination: height 170 cm, weight 42 kg, BMI 14.53 kg/m²; mild somnolence and poor mental status; neck supple with slight pain on movement; ocular motility intact but diplopia at a certain visual level; cardiovascular, respiratory, and abdominal examinations normal; limb muscle strength and tone normal; no meningeal irritation signs; rest of neurological exam unremarkable. Initial blood tests revealed no significant abnormalities. Influenza A and B tests were negative. Serum IgG antibodies were positive for Epstein-Barr virus, herpes simplex virus type I, rubella virus, and Toxoplasma. Serum procalcitonin was normal. Fundoscopic examination demonstrated pronounced edema of the optic disc in both eyes. Cerebral and orbital MRI showed a minor accumulation of fluid within the optic nerve sheath bilaterally near the scleral attachment (<1 mm width), a partially empty sella turcica, and no other significant abnormalities. Initial lumbar puncture: opening pressure 295 mmH₂O; cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) was colorless; white blood cell count was increased with lymphocytic predominance; cultures were negative for bacterial, fungal, and tuberculous pathogens. Due to high suspicion, a second lumbar puncture was performed: opening pressure 330 mmH₂O. CSF next-generation sequencing (NGS) detected human herpesvirus 7 (HHV-7): sequence number 7766, relative abundance 99.94%, confidence 99%, genomic coverage 37.69%. No other pathogen was identified. Based on the presented information, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what next management step is most appropriate?
gold_diagnosis: Human herpesvirus 7 (HHV-7) viral encephalitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Human herpesvirus 7 (HHV-7) viral encephalitis
answer_key.etiology: HHV-7 infection in the setting of adalimumab-induced immunosuppression
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue adalimumab, initiate intravenous acyclovir (10 mg/kg every 8 hours for 2 weeks, then oral acyclovir for 1 week), and consider an alternative immunomodulatory agent (e.g., thalidomide) for Crohn's disease control.
required_findings: 16-year-old with Crohn's disease on adalimumab (immunosuppressed state)
Acute onset fever, drowsiness, diplopia, headache, and blurred vision
Bilateral optic disc edema on fundoscopy
CSF opening pressures 295 and 330 mmH₂O (marked intracranial hypertension)
CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis
Negative initial CSF cultures
CSF NGS detection of HHV-7 with 99.94% relative abundance and 99% confidence
Brain MRI without parenchymal lesions
optional_findings: Positive IgG for EBV, HSV-1, rubella, Toxoplasma (prior exposures)
Missed adalimumab doses and preceding URI
Mild fluid in optic nerve sheaths (<1 mm)
Rapid symptom resolution after specific therapy

provenance.title: Viral encephalitis related to adalimumab in a 16-year-old Crohn’s disease patient: case report and literature review

challenge_prompt:
A 14-year-old previously healthy female with no history of diabetes, obesity, or insulin resistance ingested approximately 50 grams of copper sulfate and immediately developed severe burning oral and gastric pain.

On admission, vitals: temperature 36.6°C, heart rate 147 bpm, respiratory rate 37/min, blood pressure 134/110 mmHg, oxygen saturation 87% on 15 L/min mask oxygen. She was drowsy; cardiopulmonary exam was unremarkable except for tachycardia. Severe metabolic acidosis with respiratory acidosis prompted intubation, mechanical ventilation, and sodium bicarbonate.

At 5 hours post-ingestion, in the ICU, blood heavy metal panel showed: copper 15,660 ng/mL, zinc 1,550 ng/mL; gastric juice copper 114,830 ng/mL, zinc 1,195.6 ng/mL. Lab studies revealed hemolytic anemia, acute hepatic and renal impairment, coagulopathy, methemoglobinemia, rhabdomyolysis, and gastrointestinal bleeding. Treatment included plasma exchange, calcium disodium edetate and dimercaptopropanesulfonic acid sodium chelation, methylene blue, and continuous renal replacement therapy.

At 22 hours, she developed increased muscle tone in the left upper limb and right lower limb. Lower extremity ultrasound and CT ruled out DVT and fractures. The findings were attributed to rhabdomyolysis and capillary leak syndrome, and improved somewhat with mannitol and albumin.

On day 7, the right lower limb muscle tone remained increased, with worsened limitation of active movement of the right ankle and toe joints, numbness on the dorsum of the right foot, and tremors in the left hand middle and ring fingers. MRI of the right lower leg showed diffuse swelling of muscle groups and fascia. Nerve conduction studies/electromyography reported “complete injury of the right common peroneal nerve and tibial nerve at the level distal to the popliteal fossa.” Neurology and orthopedics consultation suggested toxic nerve injury from copper. Fursultiamine, mecobalamin, and vitamins B1/B6 were added.

On day 16, she developed recurrent limb convulsions with increased tone and loss of consciousness, lasting tens of seconds to 3 minutes. Bedside random blood glucose was >34 mmol/L. Cranial CT showed no structural abnormalities. Ambulatory EEG revealed increased background slow activity, more prominent on the right side. The convulsions were thought to be related to severe electrolyte disturbance and toxic encephalopathy. No antiepileptic drugs were given, and with glycemic control and supportive care, convulsions ceased during that hospitalization.

Throughout the admission, she had persistent marked hyperglycemia, highest 40 mmol/L. Admission HbA1c was normal, but repeat half a month after poisoning was 7.7%. Fasting insulin was 24.39 pmol/L, C-peptide 3.97 ng/mL, and anti-glutamic acid decarboxylase (GAD) antibody was 145.54 IU/mL.

The patient remains on chelation and is in the ICU. What is the most likely diagnosis unifying these findings, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Acute copper sulfate poisoning with central nervous system impairment (toxic encephalopathy, seizures), peripheral nerve injury (right common peroneal and tibial neuropathy), and autoimmune diabetes mellitus secondary to copper-induced beta-cell damage.

answer_key.diagnosis: Acute copper sulfate poisoning with central nervous system impairment (toxic encephalopathy, seizures), peripheral nerve injury (right common peroneal and tibial neuropathy), and autoimmune diabetes mellitus secondary to copper-induced beta-cell damage.
aliases: Copper sulfate intoxication with neurotoxicity and autoimmune diabetes
answer_key.etiology: Massive copper sulfate ingestion leading to direct copper toxicity, oxidative stress, and immune-mediated beta-cell destruction via GAD antibody formation.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate intensive insulin therapy for hyperglycemia, consider discontinuation of copper chelation if glucose remains uncontrolled (as chelators may paradoxically worsen oxidative beta-cell injury), continue neurotrophic support and physiotherapy, and start antiepileptic medication if seizures recur.
required_findings: Ingestion of approximately 50 grams of copper sulfate.
Blood copper level 15,660 ng/mL.
Recurrent convulsions with EEG increased background slow activity and normal cranial CT.
Nerve conduction studies: complete injury of right common peroneal and tibial nerves.
Persistent hyperglycemia >34 mmol/L with normal admission HbA1c and repeat HbA1c 7.7%.
Anti-GAD antibody 145.54 IU/mL.
Insulin 24.39 pmol/L, C-peptide 3.97 ng/mL.
MRI diffuse leg muscle swelling, no DVT/fracture.
optional_findings: Increased muscle tone and movement limitation.
Limb edema initially attributed to capillary leak syndrome.

provenance.title: Neurological impairment and hyperglycemia following acute copper sulfate poisoning: a case report

challenge_prompt:
A 22-year-old woman with an 11-year history of severe aplastic anemia (unresponsive to cyclosporine, androgens, growth factors) was admitted for maternal haploidentical hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (Haplo-HSCT) on April 26, 2021, with an 8/12 HLA match. Conditioning: busulfan 0.8 mg/kg q6h days -7 to -6, fludarabine 30 mg/m² daily days -7 to -4, cyclophosphamide 40 mg/kg daily days -5 to -3, antithymocyte globulin 2.5 mg/kg daily days -5 to -2. GVHD prophylaxis: cyclosporine, mycophenolate mofetil, short-course methotrexate.

On May 6 (day -5), the patient developed sudden severe occipital headache, dizziness, visual disturbances, and nausea. Intravenous cyclosporine was stopped immediately. Methylprednisolone 40 mg IV led to symptom improvement. One hour later, a generalized tonic-clonic seizure occurred, terminated with 10 mg diazepam IV. MRI was not performed due to severe bone marrow suppression. On May 9 (day -2), she reported blurred vision and right upper limb weakness (grade IV). Brain CT revealed multiple small ischemic infarcts in bilateral occipital lobes, left basal ganglia, and bilateral parietal lobes. Given thrombocytopenia, anticoagulation was contraindicated; neuroprotective and anti-edema therapy (butylphthalide, mannitol 150 mL q12h) was started. Muscle strength declined to grade III, but visual disturbance improved.

After completing conditioning, on May 10, she had persistent blurred vision and right limb weakness. Platelet transfusion raised platelets to 87 × 10⁹/L. Following guidelines, enoxaparin 30 mg, citicoline sodium, edaravone were given. May 11-12: slight visual improvement; weakness unchanged. During this period, she received two infusions of donor hematopoietic stem cells. By May 13-19, vision returned to normal, fine motor coordination and right limb strength improved, paralleling hematopoietic recovery.

On May 21 (day +10), she reported lower lip numbness and occipital pain. Brain MRI showed multiple symmetric patchy long T1/T2 signal lesions in bilateral frontal cortex, parieto-occipital lobes, and basal ganglia corona radiata, hyperintense on FLAIR, focal DWI hyperintensity without low ADC, suggestive of posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES). Cyclosporine was switched to oral tacrolimus (low trough), and methylprednisolone was added.

On May 25 (day +14), she developed skin erythema on the face, neck, anterior chest, back, and palms (acute grade 2 GVHD, stage I); lower lip numbness had improved. GVHD risk testing indicated high probability of severe aGVHD. She was treated with methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg. After three days, the skin rash worsened with vesicles and grade I gastrointestinal aGVHD progressing to grade II. Steroid-refractory; treatment intensified with basiliximab 20 mg on days +1, +3, +8; ruxolitinib 5 mg twice daily increased to 10 mg twice daily; umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cells 1×10⁶/kg twice weekly for four doses; and later etanercept 25 mg subcutaneously for two days. During follow-up, mild chronic GVHD involving skin (Score 1), oral mucosa, and nails developed, controlled with tacrolimus, ruxolitinib, and low-dose corticosteroids. Neurological symptoms were alleviated and PRES remained controlled.

Three months after transplantation (August 17, 2021), she developed reduced temperature and pain sensation and sensory ataxia in the right upper and lower limbs. Head CT showed a slight low-density area in the left basal ganglia corona radiata. Brain MRI revealed right pontine acute/subacute infarction, multiple abnormal signals, a long T1 lesion in the occipital sulcus, multiple low signals on SWAN, and reduced perfusion in the left cerebral hemisphere on ASL. Cervical MRI showed abnormal anterior spinal cord signals at the C1–2 level, suspected inflammatory damage, along with C5/6 and C6/7 disc herniation. Blood and cerebrospinal fluid analyses excluded infectious, metabolic, cerebrovascular, neoplastic, and drug-related central nervous system complications. Autoimmune-mediated neurogenic injury was suspected.

MOG antibody testing was performed: negative by cell-based assay (CBA) and tissue-based assay (TBA); weakly positive (1:32 titer) by live cell immunofluorescence. A preliminary diagnosis of immune-mediated CNS injury was made. Intravenous immunoglobulin (0.4 g/kg for 5 days) and methylprednisolone (40 mg) led to rapid relief of hyperalgesia in the right limbs and MOG antibody seroconversion to negative.
gold_diagnosis: Central nervous system graft-versus-host disease (CNS-GVHD) with a secondary, transient MOG antibody humoral response (bystander phenomenon)

answer_key.diagnosis: Central nervous system graft-versus-host disease (CNS-GVHD) with a secondary, transient MOG antibody humoral response (bystander phenomenon)
answer_key.etiology: Donor-derived alloreactive T-cells and immune dysregulation targeting CNS antigens, leading to perivascular inflammation, microglial activation, endothelial injury, and BBB disruption, with secondary MOG autoantibody production as a bystander effect rather than primary MOG antibody-associated disease.
answer_key.next_management_step: Continue and potentially intensify immunosuppressive therapy (e.g., corticosteroids, rituximab) targeting CNS-GVHD, with close monitoring for neurological improvement and systemic GVHD control; consider repeat MOG testing to confirm transient nature of seropositivity.

provenance.title: Neurological graft-versus-host disease with MOG antibody positivity after allogeneic stem cell transplantation: a case report

challenge_prompt:
A previously healthy 6-year-old boy presented with one day of slurred speech, progressive gait instability, intermittent blurred vision, olfactory hallucinations, and horizontal nystagmus. He reported a severe bilateral headache that had peaked at 10/10 intensity earlier that day and resolved spontaneously. He denied numbness, tingling, head trauma, nausea, or vomiting. He was born full term, up to date on vaccinations. Family history: maternal migraines and spondylolisthesis, grandparent with systemic lupus erythematosus.

Initial vital signs were stable, afebrile, room air saturation normal. Physical exam: dysarthria, dysmetria, dysdiadochokinesia, unsteady gait. Neurologic exam otherwise unremarkable.

Laboratory studies: complete blood count and basic metabolic panel normal. Toxicology screen, non-contrast head CT, electrocardiogram, and chest radiograph were unremarkable. Respiratory viral panel negative. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 19 mm/hr (ref <15).

During the first two hospitalization days, the patient developed two febrile episodes (max 38.6°C and 38.3°C), each resolved with acetaminophen 15 mg/kg.

Lumbar puncture on day 2: CSF white blood cells 58/mm3 (ref <7), differential 66% lymphocytes, 30% monocytes. CSF bacterial culture, multiplex viral PCR panel (including herpes simplex virus, enterovirus, Listeria), and blood cultures all negative at 48 and 72 hours. Blood and throat cultures also negative. CT angiography head and neck showed normal intracranial and extracranial vasculature.

MRI brain with and without contrast: poorly demarcated T2 and FLAIR hyperintensities in the pons, right basal ganglia, right temporal lobe, bilateral temporal-occipital junctions, and right cingulate gyrus. No well-demarcated T2 lesions, T1 hypointensities, or typical periventricular/corpus callosum lesions. MRI orbits: increased T2 signal of the right optic nerve, concerning for optic neuritis. MRI spine (cervical, thoracic, lumbar) showed no evidence of myelitis. Ophthalmology: visual acuity 20/50 right eye, 20/20 left eye.

Serum and CSF antibody panels for myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein IgG/IgM, aquaporin-4 IgG, ANA, anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith, and anti-neuronal autoantibodies were sent; results remain pending.

Based on the available data, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibody-associated disease (MOGAD) rhombencephalitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibody-associated disease (MOGAD) rhombencephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune demyelination mediated by anti-myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (MOG) antibodies
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg/day for 4 days and intravenous immunoglobulin 1 g/kg/day for 2 days, while awaiting antibody results.
required_findings: Subacute brainstem/cerebellar syndrome (dysarthria, ataxia, nystagmus, dysmetria)
Optic neuritis (decreased visual acuity right eye, right optic nerve T2 signal increase)
Multifocal poorly demarcated T2/FLAIR hyperintensities involving pons, basal ganglia, temporal lobe, occipital junctions, cingulate gyrus
CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis (58 WBC, 66% lymphocytes)
Negative extensive infectious workup (cultures, PCR, viral panel)
Absence of typical MS features on MRI (no well-demarcated T2 lesions, no T1 hypointensities, no periventricular/corpus callosum lesions)
Pediatric age (6 years) with acute presentation
No spinal cord involvement on MRI
optional_findings: Family history of systemic lupus erythematosus in grandparent

provenance.title: Acute Pediatric Myelin Oligodendrocyte Glycoprotein (MOG) Antibody-Associated Rhombencephalitis With Multifocal Brain Involvement: A Case Report and Discussion

challenge_prompt:
A 50-year-old man was diagnosed with typical chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (CIDP) based on clinical presentation and electrophysiological studies. He was initially treated with intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIg) every 4 weeks, which effectively controlled his symptoms. Due to work commitments, he was switched to self-administered subcutaneous immunoglobulin (SCIg). However, his muscle weakness gradually worsened on SCIg. Oral prednisolone 10 mg daily was added, but no improvement was noted. Prednisolone was discontinued, and he was switched back to IVIg. IVIg again provided benefit, but he experienced recurrent treatment-related fluctuations, with muscle weakness returning 2–3 weeks after each infusion. At 13 months after diagnosis, he seeks a treatment that can be self-administered at home with weekly dosing to avoid these fluctuations.

What is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: typical CIDP

answer_key.diagnosis: typical CIDP
answer_key.etiology: autoimmune demyelinating polyneuropathy
answer_key.next_management_step: Switch to efgartigimod (FcRn inhibitor) therapy, administered subcutaneously weekly.
required_findings: Diagnosis of typical CIDP
Initial response to IVIg but development of treatment-related fluctuations 2–3 weeks after each infusion
Worsening of muscle weakness after switching to SCIg
No improvement with oral prednisolone 10 mg
Patient desires self-administered home therapy with weekly dosing
Age 50 years
Male sex
Disease duration 13 months at time of decision
optional_findings: Mild abnormal sensations in upper limbs persisted
No mention of other autoimmune conditions

provenance.title: Real-world outcomes after switching from standard therapy to efgartigimod in five patients with chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy: a case series study in Japan

challenge_prompt: An 18-year-old woman with no prior medical history presented with a 5-day history of fever, 1 day of altered mental status, and 2 days of right-sided weakness. Her family reported that she had undergone carotid massage by a traditional healer shortly before the onset of confusion. On arrival, she was unconscious (GCS 7), with mid-dilated pupils sluggishly reactive to light. Vital signs: BP 117/69 mmHg, pulse 81 bpm, oxygen saturation 85% on room air. Decreased air entry over the right lung. Right hemiplegia confirmed. Laboratory studies: leukopenia, lymphopenia, anemia, elevated C-reactive protein, deranged liver and renal function tests, and metabolic acidosis on arterial blood gas. Non-contrast CT brain showed diffuse cerebral edema with a large left middle cerebral artery infarction, 3 mm midline shift, left mild uncal herniation, and compression of the left lateral ventricle horns. CT chest revealed right lung consolidation consistent with pneumonia. The patient rapidly deteriorated, suffered a cardiac arrest, and was resuscitated. Given the CT findings of mass effect and impending herniation, urgent decompressive hemicraniectomy was recommended but the family declined. Mannitol was started and she was admitted to the ICU, where she developed hemodynamic instability and a second cardiac arrest. Broad-spectrum antibiotics (aztreonam and metronidazole) were initiated empirically. No vascular imaging could be performed due to rapid decline.
gold_diagnosis: Malignant left middle cerebral artery infarction with cerebral edema and uncal herniation

answer_key.diagnosis: Malignant left middle cerebral artery infarction with cerebral edema and uncal herniation
aliases: Large hemispheric infarction with mass effect
answer_key.etiology: Carotid artery dissection (presumed, temporally associated with traditional carotid massage)
answer_key.next_management_step: Urgent neurosurgical consultation for decompressive hemicraniectomy; if declined, maximal medical management with mannitol and intensive monitoring
required_findings: 18-year-old female
No prior medical conditions
Carotid massage by traditional healer shortly before symptom onset
Acute onset of right hemiplegia and depressed consciousness (GCS 7)
Non-contrast CT brain: large left MCA territory hypoattenuation with diffuse cerebral edema
3 mm midline shift and compression of left lateral ventricle
Left uncal herniation on CT
No vascular imaging performed due to rapid deterioration

provenance.title: Carotid artery dissection and brain herniation following traditional carotid massage: a case report

challenge_prompt: A 49-year-old man with a 12-year history of CIDP (fulfilling 2010 EFNS criteria), chronic progressive sensorimotor course, treated with immunoglobulins, mycophenolate mofetil, and methylprednisolone. After 9 years of stability, he developed left retrobulbar optic neuritis. Visual Evoked Potentials (VEPs) showed increased P100 latency on the left, and optical coherence tomography (OCT) showed retinal nerve fiber layer thinning. Brain MRI revealed multiple bilateral periventricular demyelinating lesions. CSF: protein 45.4 mg/dL, three unique oligoclonal bands. Extensive workup: p-ANCA, c-ANCA, ANA IgG panel, serum protein electrophoresis, vitamin B12, HIV, syphilis, hepatitis B/C negative. Antineuronal antibodies (contactin-1, neurofascin-155, neurofascin-186, CASPR-1) negative. TTR gene sequencing negative. AQP4 antibodies negative. Borrelia serology: positive IgM, negative IgG; treated for borreliosis without improvement. MOG antibodies detected at low titer (1:20). Over 2 years, serial brain and cervical MRI showed new demyelinating lesions in left frontal subcortical region, left cerebellum, left mesencephalon, and cervical spinal cord, fulfilling 2017 McDonald criteria for dissemination in time and space. Follow-up EMG demonstrated progressive deterioration in nerve conduction despite ongoing immunosuppression. Repeat CSF: persistent single oligoclonal band, MOG and Borrelia antibodies now negative. Neurological examination: bilateral steppage gait with foot drop, tetraparetic motor deficit with distal predominance. Upper limbs: weakness limited to ulnar grip. Lower limbs: proximal weakness (MRC 4/5), more severe distal weakness (MRC 3/5), especially foot dorsiflexion, mild left predominance. Deep tendon reflexes present, brisker on right. Babinski sign positive right, equivocal left. Generalized muscle atrophy, hammer toes, bilateral pes cavus. Fasciculations in thighs. Sensory: distal paresthesia in both lower extremities, hypoesthesia to tactile, vibratory, proprioceptive stimuli. Fine postural tremor in upper extremities. Given the progression despite standard immunosuppression, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next management step?
gold_diagnosis: Combined central and peripheral demyelination (CCPD)

answer_key.diagnosis: Combined central and peripheral demyelination (CCPD)
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune demyelinating process involving both central and peripheral nervous systems, likely driven by B-cell-mediated immunity
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate anti-CD20 B-cell depletion therapy (e.g., ocrelizumab or rituximab)
required_findings: CIDP diagnosis for 12 years with progressive sensorimotor course
New-onset left optic neuritis with VEP P100 latency increase and OCT RNFL thinning
Brain MRI with multiple bilateral periventricular demyelinating lesions
CSF oligoclonal bands (initially three unique bands, later persistent single band) and elevated protein (45.4 mg/dL)
Serial MRI revealing new demyelinating lesions in brain and cervical spinal cord fulfilling 2017 McDonald criteria
Progressive deterioration on nerve conduction studies despite immunosuppression (mycophenolate, corticosteroids)

Extensive negative workup excluding alternative etiologies: AQP4 negative, MOG low titer (1:20) then negative, Borrelia treated without improvement, negative antineuronal antibodies, negative genetic testing
Neurological exam with mixed central (brisk reflexes, Babinski sign, postural tremor) and peripheral (distal weakness, atrophy, fasciculations, sensory loss) involvement
optional_findings: History of IVIG and mycophenolate therapy for CIDP
Borrelia IgM positive, IgG negative, treated without effect
Bilateral pes cavus and hammer toes
Fine postural tremor in upper extremities
Distal weakness more severe than proximal, foot drop, steppage gait
Visual involvement progressed to bilateral optic neuritis with right-eye predominance

provenance.title: B-Cell Depletion as Evidence for Shared Neuroimmune Pathways in Combined Central and Peripheral Demyelination: A Case Report and Literature Review

challenge_prompt:
A male in his 60s was referred to the emergency department from an outpatient podiatry visit for lethargy, confusion, and slowed responses. The patient was a limited historian at baseline and generally not oriented to date/time. He had a recent mechanical fall without a head strike. Past medical history was notable for parkinsonism (paliperidone-induced), seizure disorder (inactive for 10 years), type 2 diabetes, sleep apnea, and schizoaffective disorder (bipolar type). Outpatient medications at time of admission were notable for amantadine (100 mg by mouth three times daily), benztropine, divalproex ER, levetiracetam, paliperidone (monthly injection), risperidone (as needed), and once-weekly semaglutide. In the emergency department his vitals were stable. Labs were normal with the exception of blood reported on urinalysis, which resolved without intervention. Ammonia was normal, and liver function tests were within normal limits. Both valproic acid and levetiracetam levels were normal (97.13 mcg/mL and <2 mcg/mL, respectively). Initial imaging showed no acute cardiopulmonary process, and computerized tomography (CT) of the head and lungs showed no evidence of acute intracranial bleed or pulmonary embolism, respectively. The patient was admitted for altered mental status. Late on hospital day 2, the patient experienced a choking episode and was made NPO, resulting in the temporary hold of all home medications, including amantadine. On night 3 of admission, the patient became restless and anxious and was yelling out, and 2.5 mg of olanzapine was administered intramuscularly. He was transferred to the ICU for acute mental status changes concerning for neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS). Both neurology and psychiatry were consulted for concern of possible post-ictal state versus pharmacologic effects (olanzapine) or withdrawal (amantadine). Neither specialist had a high suspicion for NMS, citing lack of fever or significant rigidity upon exam. The only symptoms that were consistent with NMS were tachycardia and confusion. Neurology expressed low suspicion of seizure given that the patient was seizure free for 10 years, and previous seizure was due to alcohol withdrawal or lithium toxicity, which the patient was not using or taking at the time of admission.

Based on the information available, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology for this patient's acute deterioration, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?
gold_diagnosis: Amantadine withdrawal syndrome

answer_key.diagnosis: Amantadine withdrawal syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Abrupt cessation of chronic amantadine therapy
answer_key.next_management_step: Resume amantadine, preferably via nasogastric tube if oral route not possible
required_findings: Chronic amantadine 100 mg three times daily
Abrupt discontinuation of all oral medications, including amantadine, after NPO order
Acute onset of restlessness, anxiety, yelling within 1-2 days after hold
Absence of fever and significant rigidity on exam
Normal valproic acid and levetiracetam levels
Normal ammonia and liver function tests
Normal head CT
History of parkinsonism requiring dopaminergic therapy
optional_findings: Improvement within 24-48 hours after restarting amantadine
Persistent fevers despite antibiotics, resolving with withdrawal treatment
No other metabolic or infectious cause identified

provenance.title: Altered Mental Status Due to Amantadine Withdrawal: A Case Report
provenance.journal: Reports - Clinical Practice and Surgical Cases

challenge_prompt: A 39-year-old previously healthy man was referred with suspected acute optic neuritis of the right eye. He reported several days of retro-orbital pressure and pain with right eye movements, most pronounced on upgaze, followed by progressively worsening blurred vision in the right eye over the preceding two days. No history of previous visual, sensory, or motor symptoms. Family history unremarkable. Examination: visual loss in right eye (high-contrast visual acuity 0.6), mild red desaturation, relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) on the right. Visual evoked potentials showed conduction block after stimulation of the right eye. Brain MRI with orbital protocol: T2-hyperintense lesion in the right optic nerve without contrast enhancement, no other orbital pathology; multiple FLAIR hyperintense lesions in white matter, including corpus callosum and midbrain, none enhancing. CSF: positive oligoclonal bands. Diagnosed with relapsing-remitting MS per McDonald criteria. Antibody testing for MOG-IgG and aquaporin 4-IgG not performed. Treated with intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 8 days, followed by oral taper; visual acuity improved to 0.7 at discharge. Two months later, started on ocrelizumab 600 mg every 6 months. At that time, visual acuity 0.5. Over 5.5 years, right eye visual acuity gradually declined to 0.3, with fluctuating course, no acute ON symptoms or MRI activity. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) performed 5.5 years after initial ON revealed: right eye peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer (pRNFL) 47 μm (baseline 101 μm), macular ganglion cell–inner plexiform layer (mGCIPL) 46.3 μm (baseline 75.3 μm), inner nuclear layer (INL) thickness 57.6 μm (baseline 39.6 μm). Retrospective review of serial OCT scans showed the first appearance at 6 months of multiple lacunar, hyporeflective areas in the INL, which progressively increased in number and size toward the fovea over time. Left eye remained stable (visual acuity 1.0, minimal OCT changes). Based only on the presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?
gold_diagnosis: Microcystic macular edema (MMO)

answer_key.diagnosis: Microcystic macular edema (MMO)
answer_key.etiology: Retrograde trans-synaptic neurodegeneration following optic neuritis in relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis
answer_key.next_management_step: Serial optical coherence tomography (OCT) monitoring to track progression of microcysts and retinal layer changes; consider adherence to disease-modifying therapy.
required_findings: multiple lacunar, hyporeflective areas in the INL
right eye pRNFL 47 μm (baseline 101 μm)
mGCIPL 46.3 μm (baseline 75.3 μm)
INL thickness 57.6 μm (baseline 39.6 μm)
progressive visual decline over 5.5 years
history of acute optic neuritis in right eye
diagnosed with relapsing-remitting MS
no new ON episodes or MRI activity
optional_findings: fluctuating visual function around 0.5, with gradual decline to 0.3
visual evoked potentials showed conduction block
T2-hyperintense lesion in right optic nerve on MRI
stable left eye with visual acuity 1.0
first appearance at 6 months of lacunar hyporeflective areas
retrospective serial OCT showed progressive increase in cysts

provenance.title: Case Report: Progressive visual decline after optic neuritis associated with microcystic macular oedema

challenge_prompt:
A 25-year-old woman with schizophrenia, multiple psychiatric inpatient hospitalizations, and previous suicide attempts was admitted for inpatient psychiatric care for severe psychosis and catatonia. On presentation, she demonstrated severe psychosis, hallucinations, and dysphoria. She also exhibited classic catatonic features, including psychomotor slowing, mutism, negativism, staring, and stupor, with marked functional impairment and limited verbal engagement. The pharmacologic regimen at admission included clozapine 75 mg daily, benztropine 0.5 mg twice daily, metformin 500 mg daily, duloxetine 60 mg daily, fludrocortisone 0.2 mg nightly, and atenolol 12.5 mg daily.

An initial trial of benzodiazepine therapy with clonazepam (0.5 mg each morning and 1 mg nightly) resulted in no meaningful clinical improvement and was associated with worsening sedation, increased isolation, and prolonged time spent in bed. Due to the lack of response and poor tolerability, clonazepam was reduced and discontinued. The patient declined ECT and did not meet criteria for involuntary ECT under state regulations; therefore, alternative pharmacologic strategies were pursued.

Based on this information, what is the most appropriate next pharmacologic step?
gold_diagnosis: Catatonia (benzodiazepine-refractory, in the setting of schizophrenia)

answer_key.etiology: Associated with GABAergic dysfunction; underlying schizophrenia
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate a zolpidem challenge (e.g., 10 mg oral dose), monitoring for response and sedation.

challenge_prompt: A 68-year-old man with a history of essential hypertension (on diltiazem 300 mg, irbesartan 300 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg daily) presents with sudden onset of altered consciousness. He was found vacant and unresponsive, and his daughter (a healthcare worker) initiated chest compressions after not palpating a carotid pulse. Compressions were stopped after about a minute when he regained responsiveness, though he remained disoriented. In the ED, he is alert but disoriented (GCS 14). Vitals: HR 50 bpm, BP 102/41 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.4°C. Physical exam is notable for mild bradycardia, low-normal blood pressure with a wide pulse pressure (61 mmHg), and no focal neurological deficits; cardiovascular, respiratory, and abdominal exams are otherwise unremarkable. He denies any pain. Pupil and limb assessment for pulse deficits or interarm blood pressure difference is not performed. CT head without contrast shows no acute intracranial abnormality. Chest X-ray reveals a widened mediastinum. ECG shows sinus rhythm, large QRS voltages, and repolarization abnormalities in the anterolateral and inferior leads. Venous blood gas: lactate 2.5 mmol/L (normal 0.5-1.6). Blood glucose, electrolytes, and inflammatory markers are normal. High-sensitivity troponin T drawn 9 hours after symptom onset is 25 ng/L (normal <14). The patient's consciousness fully returns to baseline within 1 hour of onset. A working diagnosis of a neurological event (e.g., non-convulsive seizure, TIA) is made, and plans are made for brain MRI and EEG, but these are not urgently available. He is admitted for observation. Based on the available information, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next most appropriate step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Acute type A aortic dissection (Stanford type A aortic dissection)

answer_key.diagnosis: Acute type A aortic dissection (Stanford type A aortic dissection)
answer_key.etiology: Spontaneous dissection in a hypertensive patient; likely exacerbated by chest compressions but primary event is dissection causing cerebral malperfusion.
answer_key.next_management_step: Urgent CT angiogram of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis to evaluate for aortic dissection (or transesophageal echocardiography if CT not immediately available). Immediate cardiothoracic surgery consultation.

Wide pulse pressure of 61 mmHg

Absence of chest/back/abdominal pain
Sudden onset of altered consciousness with full recovery within 1 hour
Negative head CT
Mildly elevated lactate (2.5 mmol/L)
Elevated high-sensitivity troponin T (25 ng/L, reference <14)

challenge_prompt:
A 68-year-old male presented with a 3-year history of progressive dizziness, exacerbated by positional changes (e.g., turning in bed, looking up) and accompanied by gait instability, lower limb weakness, and autonomic symptoms (diaphoresis and palpitations). A prior diagnosis of benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) and canalith repositioning maneuvers provided only minimal relief. Past medical history: reflux esophagitis, gastric ulcer, anxiety disorder. Vascular risk factors: 40-pack-year smoking history (quit 3 years ago). Comprehensive neurological examination was unremarkable, with no focal deficits, cranial nerve abnormalities, or long-tract signs.

Imaging: Computed Tomography Angiography (CTA) revealed the left vertebral artery (VA) arising directly from the aortic arch, its origin situated between the left common carotid and subclavian arteries. Magnetic Resonance Angiography (MRA) (July 29, 2025) demonstrated left middle cerebral artery (MCA) hypoplasia with duplicate trunks and distal tortuosity; the hypoplasia was defined by a visibly diminished caliber of the left MCA main trunk, estimated to be less than 50% of the diameter of the contralateral right MCA on axial MRA images, without focal stenosis or signs of acquired disease. The left VA was also noted to be non-dominant on MRA. Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA) confirmed a duplicate left MCA with a hypoplastic trunk and distal tortuosity. DSA also identified a right fetal-type posterior cerebral artery (PCA) and a patent left posterior communicating artery (Pcom). The anterior communicating artery (ACom) was poorly visualized, suggesting it is either hypoplastic or associated with slow flow, rendering it functionally insignificant, though patency could not be definitively ruled out. Laboratory investigations (inflammatory markers, thrombophilia screen) were unremarkable. Transthoracic echocardiogram and ECG showed no cardioembolic source.

What is the most likely unifying diagnosis explaining this patient’s chronic positional symptoms, and what is the most appropriate initial management?
gold_diagnosis: Coexisting left middle cerebral artery hypoplasia, aortic arch origin of the left vertebral artery, and incomplete circle of Willis (fetal-type right PCA, patent left Pcom, functionally absent ACom) causing hemodynamic cerebral hypoperfusion and positional symptoms.

answer_key.diagnosis: Coexisting left middle cerebral artery hypoplasia, aortic arch origin of the left vertebral artery, and incomplete circle of Willis (fetal-type right PCA, patent left Pcom, functionally absent ACom) causing hemodynamic cerebral hypoperfusion and positional symptoms.
aliases: Complex congenital cerebrovascular anomaly syndrome with hemodynamic insufficiency
answer_key.etiology: Congenital developmental anomalies of the cerebral and aortic arch vessels, likely from disrupted embryogenesis during the 7th–8th gestational week, with the incomplete circle of Willis exacerbating hemodynamic vulnerability.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 100 mg daily), high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40 mg daily), and consider microcirculatory improvement agents; arrange annual neurovascular imaging (MRA) for surveillance and risk factor modification.
required_findings: 3-year history of progressive dizziness, worsened by positional changes (turning in bed, looking up), with gait instability, lower limb weakness, diaphoresis, and palpitations.
Minimal relief with canalith repositioning maneuvers for previously diagnosed BPPV.
CTA: left vertebral artery arising directly from aortic arch (between left common carotid and subclavian arteries).
MRA: left MCA hypoplasia (caliber <50% of right MCA), with duplicate trunks and distal tortuosity; no focal stenosis.
MRA: left vertebral artery is non-dominant.
DSA: duplicate left MCA with hypoplastic trunk and distal tortuosity.
DSA: right fetal-type PCA and patent left Pcom.
DSA: anterior communicating artery poorly visualized (suggesting hypoplasia or slow flow, functionally insignificant).
optional_findings: Normal neurological examination (no focal deficits, cranial nerve abnormalities, or long-tract signs).
Normal inflammatory markers and thrombophilia screen.
Normal transthoracic echocardiogram and ECG (no cardioembolic source).
Reflux esophagitis and gastric ulcer history.
Positional triggers specifically including turning in bed and looking up.

challenge_prompt:
A 35-year-old man with a history of chronic alcohol dependence and epilepsy presented to the emergency department following an episode of generalized tonic-clonic status epilepticus. He had been consuming alcohol heavily in the days prior. He was known to be prescribed regular oral thiamine.

Despite benzodiazepines, he had recurrent seizures, so he was intubated and transferred to the ICU. During ICU stay, he was sedated and treated with antiepileptic drugs. His oral medications, including thiamine, were withheld and not replaced parenterally.

After four days, sedation was weaned and he was extubated. Thereafter, he exhibited persistent confusion, disorientation, impaired attention, and fluctuating alertness. Neurological examination revealed horizontal nystagmus. No other focal deficits were noted.

What is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next management step?
gold_diagnosis: Wernicke's encephalopathy

answer_key.diagnosis: Wernicke's encephalopathy
answer_key.etiology: Thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency precipitated by interruption of oral thiamine supplementation in a chronic alcohol user
answer_key.next_management_step: Start high-dose intravenous thiamine therapy immediately (e.g., 500 mg IV three times daily)

Prescribed regular oral thiamine
Epilepsy and status epilepticus requiring ICU admission
Oral thiamine withheld during ICU stay and not replaced parenterally
Post-extubation persistent confusion, disorientation, impaired attention, fluctuating alertness
Horizontal nystagmus on neurological exam
optional_findings: Heavy alcohol consumption prior to admission
No focal neurological deficits other than confusion and nystagmus

challenge_prompt:
A 17-year-old male with known, uncorrected Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF) and a history of secondary polycythaemia presented to the emergency department with recurrent headaches, dizziness, and exertional shortness of breath. His chronic medications included furosemide, spironolactone, metoprolol, and sodium valproate for a seizure disorder. He had been receiving intermittent therapeutic phlebotomy for symptomatic erythrocytosis. Definitive surgical correction had not been performed because of limited access to specialized cardiac surgical services.

On arrival, his vital signs were blood pressure 88–90/60–68 mmHg, pulse 90–96 beats/min, respiratory rate 22 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 85% on room air. A therapeutic phlebotomy was performed, and he was discharged. Five days later, he returned with recurrence of the same symptoms and was kept in the emergency unit for evaluation and planned symptomatic phlebotomy pending laboratory results. While awaiting investigations, he suddenly developed right-sided weakness and facial deviation to the left, without loss of consciousness, convulsions, fever, or bleeding tendencies.

On examination, he was alert and oriented (GCS 15/15). There was right-sided supranuclear facial palsy and expressive aphasia, with preserved comprehension but impaired fluency, naming, reading, and writing. Muscle strength was 0/5 in the right upper limb and 2/5 in the right lower limb, with increased tone and brisk deep tendon reflexes on the right side (patellar reflex +3), compared to +2 on the left. The right plantar response was extensor, and visual fields were grossly intact.

Laboratory investigations revealed a hematocrit of 62.3%, red blood cell count of 7.79 × 10^6/μL, mean corpuscular volume of 80 fL, and red cell distribution width of 26.5%. Iron studies, renal function, and electrolytes were within normal limits. Lipid profile was unremarkable except for low HDL cholesterol (34 mg/dL). Non-contrast brain CT showed no acute hemorrhage but subtle hypodensity in the left basal ganglia and insular region. Subsequent MRI of the brain demonstrated acute ischemic changes involving the left corpus striatum, insular region, and adjacent temporal and parietal cortices, showing hyperintensity on diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) and corresponding low signal on the apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) map. MR venography excluded cerebral venous thrombosis. Arterial vascular imaging (MRA) was not performed. An echocardiogram performed during admission demonstrated the typical TOF anatomy without any new abnormalities, intracardiac thrombus, or changes in shunt physiology.

Based only on the information above, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?
gold_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke due to hyperviscosity from secondary polycythemia in unrepaired Tetralogy of Fallot

answer_key.diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke due to hyperviscosity from secondary polycythemia in unrepaired Tetralogy of Fallot
aliases: Polycythemic stroke in cyanotic heart disease
answer_key.etiology: Severe secondary erythrocytosis (hematocrit 62.3%) from chronic hypoxemia in uncorrected TOF, leading to hyperviscosity and in situ cerebral thrombosis; recent therapeutic phlebotomy may have contributed by causing volume depletion and microcytosis (MCV 80 fL) with increased thrombotic risk.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 81 mg daily) and atorvastatin 40 mg daily, administer subcutaneous unfractionated heparin 5000 IU twice daily, provide hydration and oxygen, hold further phlebotomy, and plan for definitive surgical repair of TOF.
required_findings: Known uncorrected Tetralogy of Fallot with secondary polycythemia
Hematocrit 62.3%, red blood cell count 7.79 × 10^6/μL
Mean corpuscular volume 80 fL, red cell distribution width 26.5%
Acute onset right-sided weakness (right upper limb 0/5, right lower limb 2/5) with right supranuclear facial palsy and expressive aphasia
Non-contrast brain CT: no acute hemorrhage, subtle hypodensity left basal ganglia and insula
MRI brain: acute infarction left corpus striatum, insular, temporal, parietal cortices with DWI hyperintensity and ADC low signal
MR venography: no cerebral venous thrombosis
Echocardiogram: typical TOF anatomy, no new abnormalities, no intracardiac thrombus
optional_findings: Iron studies, renal function, electrolytes within normal limits
Lipid profile: low HDL cholesterol 34 mg/dL
Chronic medications: furosemide, spironolactone, metoprolol, sodium valproate
Absence of fever, convulsions, bleeding tendencies

provenance.title: Hyperviscosity‐Related Ischemic Stroke in an Adolescent With Unrepaired Tetralogy of Fallot: A Case Report From a Resource‐Limited Setting

challenge_prompt: An otherwise healthy male child was noted to have a cherry-red macular spot on routine eye examination at age 6 years. Developmental milestones were normal, and initial neurological examination was unremarkable. At age 12, he developed progressive ataxia (SARA score 5/40), hyperreflexia, saccadic eye movements, and horizontal nystagmus. At age 14, he began to experience myoclonic seizures of the lower limbs, triggered by sensory stimuli, leading to frequent falls and loss of ambulation. At age 15, he had a single bilateral tonic-clonic seizure. Interictal EEG showed paroxysms of irregular, bilateral synchronous spike-waves. Cognitive function remained intact. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next diagnostic step?
gold_diagnosis: Sialidosis type I (late-onset normosomatic sialidosis, also known as cherry-red spot myoclonus syndrome)

answer_key.diagnosis: Sialidosis type I (late-onset normosomatic sialidosis, also known as cherry-red spot myoclonus syndrome)
answer_key.etiology: Biallelic pathogenic variants in the NEU1 gene resulting in deficiency of α-N-acetylneuraminidase (sialidase)
answer_key.next_management_step: Confirm diagnosis by demonstrating reduced neuraminidase activity in cultured skin fibroblasts and/or by identifying pathogenic NEU1 variants through genetic testing.

challenge_prompt:
A 32-year-old woman with no significant past medical history developed mild COVID-19 symptoms on February 1, 2024, including a low-grade fever, cough, and anosmia. Her infection was confirmed by a nasopharyngeal reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) test for SARS-CoV-2. After 10 days of conservative management, her respiratory symptoms resolved completely. However, on Day 24 post-symptom onset (14 days after the resolution of respiratory symptoms), she began experiencing daily episodes of intractable nausea, non-bilious vomiting, and persistent hiccups. These symptoms occurred in the absence of any other neurological complaints, such as headache, dizziness, limb weakness, sensory disturbances, or visual changes. After 7 days of persistent vomiting led to dehydration, she was admitted to the hospital for further evaluation.

On admission, intermittent hiccups and vertical nystagmus were observed, with no other abnormalities in physical or neurological examinations. Cranial nerve function, muscle strength, sensation, and coordination were all normal. Initial laboratory tests (including blood biochemistry) and abdominal ultrasound showed no abnormalities. Standard antiemetic treatment was minimally effective. Given the persistent and unexplained symptoms following COVID-19 infection, a neurogenic etiology was considered.

Brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed an isolated T2-weighted fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) hyperintense lesion in the dorsal medulla, localized to the area postrema at the floor of the fourth ventricle. Axial imaging showed a characteristic inverted “V” shape without gadolinium enhancement. Full-spine MRI was normal. Brainstem visual evoked potentials indicated prolonged bilateral P100 latencies. Lumbar puncture cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis showed mildly elevated protein (56 mg/dL), a lymphocyte count of 5/µL, and no oligoclonal bands. Notably, CSF metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS) detected two SARS-CoV-2 sequences with a relative abundance of 0.24%. Serum and cerebrospinal fluid NMOSD-related demyelination tests, including AQP4-IgG, MOG-IgG, and GFAP-IgG (cell-based assay), and antinuclear antibodies, were negative, with no other infectious or autoimmune markers detected.

Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Monophasic post-infectious brainstem demyelinating syndrome (isolated area postrema syndrome)

answer_key.diagnosis: Monophasic post-infectious brainstem demyelinating syndrome (isolated area postrema syndrome)
aliases: Isolated area postrema syndrome, post-COVID brainstem syndrome, seronegative post-infectious brainstem demyelination
answer_key.etiology: Post-COVID-19 immune dysregulation
answer_key.next_management_step: Start high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone (1 g/day for 5 days) followed by oral prednisone taper, and consider adjunctive intravenous immunoglobulin.

Characteristic inverted “V” shape without gadolinium enhancement on MRI

CSF metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS) detected two SARS-CoV-2 sequences with a relative abundance of 0.24%
Serum and CSF AQP4-IgG, MOG-IgG, GFAP-IgG (cell-based assay) were negative
No other infectious or autoimmune markers detected
No other neurological complaints (headache, dizziness, limb weakness, sensory disturbances, visual changes)

Standard antiemetic treatment was minimally effective
Normal abdominal ultrasound and initial laboratory tests
Mild COVID-19 symptoms resolved after 10 days

challenge_prompt:
A 57-year-old male with a history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus (irregular insulin and metformin, no known complications) and chronic alcohol use presented to the emergency department with new-onset generalized tonic–clonic seizures. On the day of admission, he had five consecutive seizures with upward eye rolling, frothing at the mouth, and urinary incontinence. Postictally, he regained consciousness within 10–15 minutes but remained drowsy for about 30 minutes. He had been on antitubercular therapy for pulmonary tuberculosis. There was no history of electrolyte imbalances, prior hyponatremia correction, liver transplantation, or malnutrition.

On examination, he was alert (GCS 15), hemodynamically stable (BP 100/62 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, RR 20/min, T 36.5°C, SpO2 96% on room air). BMI was 28. Neurological exam: cranial nerves intact, no focal deficits; power 4+/5 in all limbs, normal sensation, no cerebellar signs. Deep tendon reflexes were brisk in all extremities, and plantar responses were extensor bilaterally. Fundoscopy showed no papilledema or diabetic retinopathy.

Laboratory results on admission: blood glucose 390 mg/dL, HbA1c 15.2%, serum albumin 3.3 g/dL, corrected serum sodium 135 mEq/L. A non-contrast CT of the brain performed on admission showed no significant abnormality.

The patient was treated with intravenous insulin, intravenous levetiracetam, metformin, and continued antitubercular therapy. Seizure activity persisted during the hospital stay.

On the 11th day of hospitalization, the patient developed new focal neurological deficits, initially left-sided weakness that progressed to quadriparesis. MRI of the brain (1.5T) was performed. FLAIR and T2-weighted images showed hyperintensities in the central pons, appearing triangular and extending to the middle cerebellar peduncles. There was no diffusion restriction on DWI/ADC images. After gadolinium administration, there was no enhancement of the pontine abnormality. The basal ganglia, thalami, and both cerebral hemispheres appeared normal, without extrapontine abnormalities.

Based on the clinical presentation and imaging findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next appropriate step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Osmotic demyelination syndrome with central pontine myelinolysis secondary to hyperosmolar hyperglycemia

answer_key.diagnosis: Osmotic demyelination syndrome with central pontine myelinolysis secondary to hyperosmolar hyperglycemia
answer_key.etiology: Hyperosmolar hyperglycemia leading to osmotic stress and oligodendrocyte injury, possibly exacerbated by chronic alcohol use.
answer_key.next_management_step: Supportive care with gradual correction of hyperglycemia, avoiding rapid osmotic shifts, continuous neurological and metabolic monitoring, and rehabilitation.
required_findings: Blood glucose 390 mg/dL and HbA1c 15.2%
Corrected serum sodium 135 mEq/L
Brain MRI: T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in the central pons, triangular and extending to the middle cerebellar peduncles
No diffusion restriction on DWI/ADC images
No enhancement after gadolinium administration
Basal ganglia, thalami, and cerebral hemispheres normal
Progressive quadriparesis developed on day 11 after initial admission for seizures
optional_findings: Brisk deep tendon reflexes and extensor plantar responses
No prior hyponatremia correction or electrolyte imbalances
History of pulmonary tuberculosis and current antitubercular therapy
Presentation with multiple generalized tonic-clonic seizures

challenge_prompt:
An 82-year-old woman with a 10-year history of Parkinson's disease and severe autonomic dysfunction presented with an altered level of consciousness and a severe headache. Her home blood pressure had peaked at 210/115 mmHg. At presentation, her blood pressure was 119/79 mmHg, and her Glasgow Coma Scale was 3 (E1, V1, M1). She was intubated. Medications included levodopa/carbidopa 100/25 mg three times daily and pramipexole 0.5 mg three times daily; no recent dosage changes or sympathomimetics were reported. Brain magnetic resonance imaging revealed T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in the bilateral parieto-occipital regions with focal diffusion restriction. Inpatient blood pressure monitoring showed fluctuations from 190/110 mmHg to 105/65 mmHg.

Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES)

answer_key.diagnosis: Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES)
answer_key.etiology: Blood pressure fluctuations due to autonomic dysfunction from advanced Parkinson's disease.
answer_key.next_management_step: Aggressive blood pressure control targeting normotension and avoiding rapid fluctuations, seizure prophylaxis if indicated, and supportive care including airway management and neuroimaging to monitor edema.

challenge_prompt: A 20-year-old male with a known diagnosis of Niemann-Pick disease type B (genetically confirmed: SMPD1 c.1177T>G, p.Trp393Gly) and history of intellectual disability, short stature, dystrophy, and hepatosplenomegaly was admitted for aggressive behaviour. Medications: lorazepam, risperidone, biperiden (unchanged). Admission labs: thrombocytopenia 116 × 10⁹/L, CK 196 U/L, AST 57 U/L, ALT 72 U/L. Due to escalating aggression, zuclopenthixol was started and titrated to 12 mg/day over three days. On day 2 of zuclopenthixol, he became excessively drowsy, lethargic, with reduced responsiveness. No fever, muscle rigidity, or focal neurological signs. Laboratory investigation revealed CK 22,000 U/L, and AST/ALT elevation; platelet count decreased slightly. There was no history of trauma, prolonged physical restraint, intramuscular injections, dehydration, prolonged immobilization, or sustained extreme agitation. Zuclopenthixol was discontinued. CK and transaminases returned to normal within three days. The patient’s clinical status improved progressively, although psychomotor slowing persisted for two more days. No leukocytosis, no renal impairment, no autonomic instability. What is the most likely explanation for the acute deterioration?
gold_diagnosis: Zuclopenthixol-induced inhibition of acid sphingomyelinase (ASM) activity (FIASMA effect) causing rhabdomyolysis and metabolic decompensation resembling Niemann-Pick disease type A

answer_key.diagnosis: Zuclopenthixol-induced inhibition of acid sphingomyelinase (ASM) activity (FIASMA effect) causing rhabdomyolysis and metabolic decompensation resembling Niemann-Pick disease type A
answer_key.etiology: Zuclopenthixol, a thioxanthene antipsychotic, acts as a functional inhibitor of acid sphingomyelinase (FIASMA), reducing residual enzymatic activity below a critical threshold in a patient with Niemann-Pick disease type B.
answer_key.next_management_step: Immediate discontinuation of zuclopenthixol and supportive care (intravenous fluids, monitoring for renal failure); avoid other FIASMA medications; after recovery, evaluate for enzyme replacement therapy with olipudase alfa.

challenge_prompt:
A 45-year-old African American female with a past medical history of migraines and remote childhood seizures presented after experiencing a 5-day headache and fever. She was found unresponsive on the floor with a facial "bruise." Her lactate was 17.5 mmol/L (0.7–2.0). EEG captured a focal seizure with left head turn and eye deviation; she progressed to status epilepticus and was intubated and started on levetiracetam 1 g twice daily and lacosamide 100 mg twice daily. Lumbar puncture showed unremarkable CSF (total nucleated cells 3 per mm³, protein 72 mg/dL, glucose 145 mg/dL), and infectious studies including HSV were negative. An E. coli urinary tract infection was treated. MRI brain with and without contrast revealed gyriform leptomeningeal enhancement overlying the right temporal and occipital lobes with minimal involvement of the right posterior parietal lobe, a small acute infarct in the right occipital lobe with T2 FLAIR hyperintensity, right temporal and occipital parenchymal calcifications, prominent bilateral optic nerve sheaths, and a partially empty sella. CT venography of the head demonstrated leptomeningeal angiomatosis and gyriform calcifications involving the right posterior cerebral hemisphere without evidence of venous thrombus. After extubation, the patient reported that the facial "bruise" was actually a birthmark she has had since birth.

Based on the clinical presentation and imaging findings, what is the most likely diagnosis? In addition to antiepileptic therapy, what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Sturge-Weber syndrome

answer_key.diagnosis: Sturge-Weber syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Somatic mosaic mutation in GNAQ gene leading to abnormal vascular development and endothelial cell proliferation
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate low-dose aspirin (81 mg daily) for secondary stroke prevention

T2 FLAIR hyperintensity in right occipital lobe

challenge_prompt:
A 53-year-old male with no history of medication use or alcohol abuse. He had acute hepatitis B 30 years prior and achieved spontaneous HBsAg seroconversion 8 years ago; his HBsAg remains negative. He also has an 18-year history of Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome. His father had hepatitis B. Two weeks ago he developed persistent dull upper abdominal pain and frequent diarrhea. At a local hospital, labs showed: ALT 6,153 U/L (RR 5–40), AST 8,255 U/L (RR 9–48), total bilirubin 110.2 μmol/L (RR 2–20), PT 23.3 s (RR 11–14.6), INR 4.25, WBC 12.8 ×10⁹/L (RR 4–10). He received cefoperazone-sulbactam, hepatoprotective agents, and one session of therapeutic plasma exchange. During this time, he had a supraventricular tachycardia episode treated with amiodarone and cardioversion. Four days before transfer he developed recurrent fever (max 38.5°C), delirium, and palpitations. Chest CT showed bilateral pneumonia and right pleural effusion. ALT, AST, and WBC normalized, but total bilirubin rose to 219.9 μmol/L. He was transferred to your center.

On admission: temperature 37.6°C, heart rate 175 bpm, BP 140/80 mmHg. Physical exam: jaundice, palmar erythema, spider angiomas, subcutaneous ecchymoses, bilaterally diminished breath sounds, enlarged thyroid gland with vascular bruit. Labs: TSH 0.006 μIU/mL (RR 0.55–4.78), free T4 84.52 pmol/L (RR 11.5–22.7), free T3 11.7 pmol/L (RR 3.5–6.5), thyroid peroxidase antibody 529.6 U/mL (RR 0–60), thyrotropin receptor antibody 14.21 U/L (RR <12), BNP 2,550 pg/mL (RR <100). ECG: supraventricular tachycardia and atrial fibrillation with heart rate >250 bpm. Abdominal ultrasound: coarse heterogeneous liver echotexture, peritoneal effusion. Cardiac ultrasound: atrial enlargement, mitral and tricuspid regurgitation, LVEF 59%.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's liver failure, and what is the next best management step?
gold_diagnosis: Hyperthyroidism-induced liver failure due to Graves' disease

answer_key.diagnosis: Hyperthyroidism-induced liver failure due to Graves' disease
answer_key.etiology: Graves' disease (autoimmune hyperthyroidism)
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate anti-thyroid medication (e.g., methimazole) and manage complications (rate control, heart failure, and supportive liver care)

Elevated free T4 84.52 pmol/L

challenge_prompt:
A 63-year-old man with asthma and GERD, previously cognitively intact and fully independent, presented with subacute onset of confusion, insomnia, dream enactment, involuntary movements, and rapidly progressive cognitive and behavioral decline over weeks. Family reported frequent nocturnal falls, consistent with parasomnia. On retrospective collateral history, they noted episodic facial twitching and abnormal left upper limb movements, brief and intermittent, early in the illness. During initial admission, he was in a side room for suspected infective encephalitis, with limited continuous observation. He was under psychiatric care for agitation and intermittent aggressive behavior, treated with antipsychotics and as-needed sedatives, which may have masked the typical semiology of his abnormal movements. Examination revealed subtle cortical signs, including ideomotor apraxia, use of body parts as tools, and executive dysfunction. He could not identify the date or perform reverse sequencing tasks, but remained oriented to place and person. He had persistent, severe hyponatraemia with sodium 115 mmol/L. Further evaluation with endocrinology demonstrated findings consistent with SIADH. All other routine blood tests (CBC, renal, liver, thyroid, B12, folate, inflammatory markers, HIV) were normal. Autoimmune screening was negative. CSF analysis showed no evidence of infection, malignancy, or inflammation (normal cell count, protein, glucose). EEG demonstrated only nonspecific slow-wave activity, no epileptiform discharges. Whole-body PET and CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis showed no malignancy. Brain MRI revealed bilateral medial temporal lobe hyperintensities, more pronounced on the right, with involvement of the insular cortex. No diffusion restriction, haemorrhage, or mass effect. A comprehensive neuronal antibody panel was sent; initial results for common paraneoplastic antibodies (anti-Hu, Yo, Ri, Ma2, CV2, amphiphysin) and cell-surface antibodies (NMDA, AMPA, GABA-B) were negative, but results for certain other antibodies are pending.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next step in management?

Subsequently, LGI1 antibody testing was positive.
gold_diagnosis: Autoimmune limbic encephalitis likely due to LGI1 antibodies

answer_key.diagnosis: Autoimmune limbic encephalitis likely due to LGI1 antibodies
answer_key.etiology: Autoantibodies targeting leucine-rich glioma-inactivated 1 (LGI1) protein, a component of the voltage-gated potassium channel complex
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate immunotherapy with high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone, and consider IVIg or other therapies as needed, while awaiting LGI1 antibody result
required_findings: Subacute onset of cognitive decline, psychiatric symptoms, and involuntary movements
Persistent severe hyponatraemia (Na 115 mmol/L) consistent with SIADH
Brain MRI shows bilateral medial temporal lobe T2/FLAIR hyperintensities
CSF analysis normal (no pleocytosis, normal protein/glucose)
EEG nonspecific slow-wave activity, no epileptiform discharges
Negative infectious and initial paraneoplastic antibody workup
Episodic facial twitching and abnormal left upper limb movements (consistent with FBDS)
Whole-body imaging negative for malignancy
optional_findings: Previous cognitively intact and fully independent
Dream enactment and frequent nocturnal falls (parasomnia)
Ideomotor apraxia and executive dysfunction on exam
Use of antipsychotics and sedatives may have masked FBDS

provenance.title: Autoimmune Limbic Encephalitis With Leucine-Rich Glioma-Inactivated 1 (LGI1) Antibodies Presenting as Rapidly Progressive Cognitive Decline and Psychiatric Symptoms: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A male gynecologist in his seventies was reported for sexual assault by an adult patient in October 2021. During a routine gynecological examination for menstrual cycle problems, he engaged in inappropriate touching and progressed to stimulating the patient's intimate parts with bare hands. He also asked questions about her sexual habits unrelated to the visit. The patient noted that the doctor seemed oblivious to the inappropriateness of his actions. He ceased professional activity shortly after.

He had no prior history of malpractice or legal issues. He was described as cheerful, joyful, and passionate about reading and languages. He had a stable marriage of several decades with three adult children. Both his wife and daughter, who are physicians, reported that over the past couple of years (since 2019-2020), he had gradually developed emotional lability, increased irritability, and progressive mood deflection leading to depressive symptoms. They initially attributed this to retirement. Subsequently, he developed language and memory deficits and motor slowing. These cognitive changes worsened after the legal allegation in 2021.

He had a history of medically controlled hypertension, mild diabetes, and recently developed benign prostatic enlargement under hormonal treatment. No prior neurological or psychiatric problems were reported.

A formal neurological consultation was sought in October 2023 due to marked worsening. On examination, he had disorganized speech and ideational apraxia. He demonstrated no insight into his condition. Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) score was 15/30 (13.3/30 after correction for age and education). The neurological examination was otherwise negative. The neurologist suspected an underlying dementia process and recommended a brain MRI.

Brain MRI performed in October 2023 revealed two lesions in the left frontal lobe. The smaller lesion measured 21 x 22 x 25 mm and was situated in the antero-inferior region of the frontal operculum. This lesion was associated with vasogenic edema of the surrounding parenchyma, right displacement of median structures with transfalcine herniation, and partial obliteration of the ipsilateral ventricle. The second lesion measured 32 x 34 x 35 mm, was located at the anterior vertex of the frontal lobe, and was accompanied by signs of mass effect. No other lesions or radiological signs of overt neurodegenerative or significant microvascular disease were described.

Based on the presentation and imaging findings, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

He was eventually diagnosed with multiple meningiomas in the anterior portion of the left cerebral hemisphere.
gold_diagnosis: Multiple (two) left frontal lobe meningiomas

answer_key.diagnosis: Multiple (two) left frontal lobe meningiomas
answer_key.etiology: Neoplastic: benign meningiomas originating from arachnoid cap cells
answer_key.next_management_step: Neurosurgical referral for surgical resection
required_findings: Gradual onset of behavioral and personality changes over ~2 years (irritability, mood deflection, depressive symptoms)
Progressive cognitive decline with language/memory deficits and motor slowing
Neurological exam: disorganized speech, ideational apraxia, no insight into deficits
MMSE score 15/30 (13.3/30 after correction for age and education)
MRI: two left frontal lesions; one 21x22x25 mm in antero-inferior frontal operculum with vasogenic edema, transfalcine herniation, ventricular obliteration
Second lesion 32x34x35 mm at anterior vertex of frontal lobe with mass effect
No other lesions or radiological signs of neurodegenerative or microvascular disease
optional_findings: Episode of inappropriate sexual behavior with lack of awareness
Long history of professional integrity before symptom onset
Family report of premorbid cheerful personality
Acute stressor of legal allegation worsening cognitive symptoms

provenance.title: Sexual assault by a gynecologist with two frontal lobe meningiomas: implications and challenges for forensic psychiatric assessment: a case report

mend_provenance.added: He was eventually diagnosed with multiple meningiomas in the anterior portion of the left cerebral hemisphere.
missing_discriminators: Pathology or radiological diagnosis confirming the lesions are meningiomas

challenge_prompt:
A 69-year-old female presents with acute onset back pain, lower limb numbness and weakness, and urinary and fecal incontinence. Neurological examination reveals 4/5 strength in bilateral lower extremities, sensory loss in the saddle area (saddle anesthesia), and bilaterally reduced patellar and ankle reflexes. Lumbar spine MRI shows mild swelling of the conus medullaris at the T12-L1 level with focal punctate enhancement. Brain MRI is unremarkable. CSF analysis reveals elevated protein (1.14 g/L) with normal white cell count (5 × 10^6/L) and normal opening pressure (95 mmH2O). Metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS) of CSF is negative for infectious pathogens. Initial cancer screening with CT chest, breast ultrasound, and breast MRI is unremarkable. The patient declines whole-body PET/CT due to financial constraints. What is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what is the next diagnostic or treatment step?

Anti-amphiphysin antibodies were detected in serum and CSF.
gold_diagnosis: Paraneoplastic autoimmune conus medullaris-cauda equina syndrome (suspected anti-amphiphysin antibody-associated neurological syndrome, probable PNS)

answer_key.diagnosis: Paraneoplastic autoimmune conus medullaris-cauda equina syndrome (suspected anti-amphiphysin antibody-associated neurological syndrome, probable PNS)

answer_key.next_management_step: Send serum and CSF anti-amphiphysin antibody testing (along with broad paraneoplastic antibody panel) and perform whole-body PET/CT for occult malignancy screening.

challenge_prompt:
A 36-year-old male presents with 3 days of gradually worsening diffuse headache, vomiting, and a subjective feeling of fever. He denies altered mental status, neck stiffness, photophobia, or focal neurological deficits. He has a history of multiple unsafe sexual exposures 1–2 years ago, and type 2 diabetes for 5 years on metformin, linagliptin, and glimepiride. He consumes alcohol only socially; no tobacco, recreational drugs.

On examination: alert and oriented, BP 140/90 mmHg, HR 62/min, RR 21/min, temperature 97.6°F, SpO2 98% on room air. Neurologic exam (higher mental function, cranial nerves, motor, sensory, cerebellar) is normal; meningeal signs absent. Ophthalmologic exam normal. Other systems normal.

Laboratory: CBC – WBC 13,500/μL (81% neutrophils), hemoglobin 14.7 g/dL, platelets 175,000/μL. Random blood glucose 302 mg/dL, HbA1c 8.4%. Serum VDRL reactive at 1:8 dilution; TPHA reactive. HIV, HBsAg, anti-HCV nonreactive. Cranial CT/MRI not performed.

A lumbar puncture was done. CSF analysis: total leukocyte count normal; protein normal; LDH mildly elevated; glucose elevated (reflecting high serum glucose). Further specific CSF serologic tests are pending.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next best step in management?

CSF VDRL was reactive and CSF TPHA was reactive.
gold_diagnosis: Neurosyphilis (early acute syphilitic meningitis)

answer_key.etiology: Treponema pallidum
answer_key.next_management_step: Obtain CSF VDRL and TPHA (or FTA-ABS) to confirm neurosyphilis

challenge_prompt:
A 34-year-old man with no prior neurological history presented with a 3-day history of progressive left-sided weakness and numbness. Two days before neurological symptom onset, he developed burning discomfort on the left neck radiating to the shoulder and upper arm, accompanied by a pruritic vesicular rash in a C3-C4 distribution. He was diagnosed with herpes zoster and started on valacyclovir. Despite antiviral therapy, neurological symptoms progressed.

Vital signs were normal, he was afebrile. Cranial nerve exam normal. A pruritic vesicular rash with erythematous papules was present on the left lower neck to mid-back. Neurological exam showed left hemiparesis (MRC grade 4/5 in both upper and lower limbs), reduced light touch and pinprick sensation over left hemibody, mild dysmetria on finger-to-nose testing, and an ataxic gait with leftward veering.

Non-contrast CT brain demonstrated a 3.5 x 3.0 x 2.6 cm hypodense lesion in the right anterior frontal lobe white matter without hemorrhage, significant mass effect, or midline shift. Brain MRI showed a ring-enhancing lesion in the right frontal lobe with surrounding vasogenic edema on T2/FLAIR sequences; enhancement was heterogeneous with focally thicker margins, and no significant mass effect. Spinal MRI revealed an enhancing lesion at the C2-C3 level.

Laboratory investigations: normal total white blood cell count and differential; C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate not elevated. HIV testing negative. CSF analysis: protein 0.49 g/L (mildly elevated; reference range 0.18-0.45), normal white cell count and glucose, and oligoclonal bands present. Aquaporin-4 antibody negative. CSF VZV PCR negative; serum VZV serology negative.

Based on the above, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Tumefactive multiple sclerosis (TMS)

answer_key.diagnosis: Tumefactive multiple sclerosis (TMS)
answer_key.etiology: Demyelinating disease (multiple sclerosis variant)
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate high-dose intravenous corticosteroids (e.g., methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3-5 days) with neurology follow-up; consider disease-modifying therapy if future relapses or MRI activity.

Concurrent enhancing cervical spinal cord lesion at C2-C3
CSF oligoclonal bands present
Normal peripheral white blood cell count and differential, no fever
CSF white cell count and glucose within normal limits
Negative infectious workup (CSF VZV PCR, serum VZV serology, HIV, CRP/ESR)
Negative aquaporin-4 antibody
optional_findings: Absence of family history of multiple sclerosis or autoimmune disease
No visual symptoms, cognitive changes, or meningeal signs
Lesion located in anterior frontal lobe white matter
