challenge_prompt:
A cohort of nine adult patients developed a neurological syndrome while receiving immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) therapy for various malignancies. The patients were aged 59–79 (mean 67.9), predominantly male (66.7%), with non-small cell lung cancer being the most common primary (3/9). All were on PD-1 inhibitors: six as monotherapy, three combined with CTLA-4 inhibitors; none on CTLA-4 monotherapy. Four had received prior chemotherapy. The neurological syndrome began a median of 6 weeks (range 2–24) after starting ICI, typically within the first 4 cycles (median 2 cycles, range 1–7). Four patients had concurrent immune-related adverse events, including encephalitis (2), hypophysitis (2), pneumonitis (1), hepatitis (1), hypothyroidism (1), and myasthenia gravis–myositis syndrome (1).

Clinically, all patients presented with symmetric limb muscle weakness (9/9), with lower limb predominance in seven and all four limbs in two. Sensory disturbances (numbness, paresthesia, or sensory ataxia) occurred in eight patients. Deep tendon reflexes were reduced or absent in all. One patient (the one with concurrent myasthenia gravis–myositis) also had ocular weakness, bulbar dysfunction, and respiratory involvement; no other patient had cranial neuropathy. The median time from symptom onset to nadir was 14 days (range 7–90); in 8 of 9 patients nadir was reached within 8 weeks.

Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) was examined in 8 patients. All had elevated protein (median 110 mg/dL, range 46–594). Mild pleocytosis (<50 cells/µL) was present in 5; the other 3 had normal cell counts (<5). CSF glucose was normal in all (median 57 mg/dL, range 51–105).

Electrodiagnostic studies were performed in all nine patients at a mean of 5.9 weeks after symptom onset. According to Hadden's criteria, four had primary demyelinating neuropathy, one axonal, and four equivocal. In the demyelinating group, common findings were prolonged distal motor latencies, slow conduction velocities, and prolonged or absent F-wave latencies. Sensory nerve action potentials were diminished in 3 of 4 demyelinating cases, with a sural-sparing pattern in two. Needle EMG showed spontaneous activity and myogenic patterns in four patients, with normal creatine kinase in three of them.

Based on this pattern, what is the most likely diagnosis and what immediate management step(s) should be taken?
gold_diagnosis: Immune-related polyradiculoneuropathy (irPRN) / Guillain-Barré-like syndrome secondary to immune checkpoint inhibitors

answer_key.diagnosis: Immune-related polyradiculoneuropathy (irPRN) / Guillain-Barré-like syndrome secondary to immune checkpoint inhibitors
answer_key.etiology: Immune checkpoint inhibitor (PD-1 inhibitor, alone or with CTLA-4 inhibitor) triggering immune-mediated demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy
answer_key.next_management_step: Immediately discontinue the immune checkpoint inhibitor. Administer high-dose corticosteroids (e.g., methylprednisolone 1 g/day IV for 5 days or oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) with a tapering regimen, and consider concurrent intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) 2 g/kg divided over 2-5 days. Monitor for respiratory compromise.
required_findings: ICI therapy with PD-1 inhibitors (alone or with CTLA-4 inhibitor) initiated weeks to months prior
Symmetric limb muscle weakness with lower limb predominance
Reduced or absent deep tendon reflexes
Sensory disturbances (numbness, paresthesia, or sensory ataxia)
Elevated CSF protein (all tested patients, median 110 mg/dL)
Mild pleocytosis in some (<50 cells/µL), normal in others; normal glucose
Nerve conduction studies showing demyelinating features: prolonged distal motor latencies, slow conduction velocities, prolonged/absent F-waves
Rapid progression to nadir within 8 weeks (median 14 days)
optional_findings: Sural-sparing pattern on sensory nerve action potentials
Myogenic patterns on needle EMG with normal creatine kinase
Concurrent other immune-related adverse events (encephalitis, hypophysitis, etc.)
Monophasic course with improvement after immunotherapy
Absence of preceding infection or alternative cause
Axonal variant in one patient (primary axonal neuropathy by Hadden criteria)
Equivocal electrodiagnostic patterns in some patients with radicular involvement
Patient with overlapping myasthenia gravis–myositis syndrome

provenance.title: Immune‐Related Polyradiculoneuropathy Associated With Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors: A Comprehensive Case Series

challenge_prompt:
We present a 14-year-old male with a complex neurodevelopmental history. He was conceived via in vitro fertilization, born prematurely at 32 weeks after a high-risk pregnancy complicated by preeclampsia at 23 weeks and gestational diabetes mellitus at 26 weeks (insulin‐treated). Fetal nonstress testing became pathological, prompting emergency cesarean section. Birth weight was 2040 g, length 49 cm, head circumference 32 cm (appropriate for gestational age). He had mild respiratory distress initially, required endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation for 48 hours, and had a prolonged NICU stay with hypotonia, poor feeding, and intermittent apnea.

At 8 months he developed clusters of infantile spasms; EEG showed hypsarrhythmia. He was diagnosed with West syndrome and treated with vigabatrin with excellent control, later transitioned to valproic acid. Between 1 and 3 years he had three episodes of complex focal seizures, two of which progressed to status epilepticus. Seizures were controlled on a triple regimen of valproic acid, levetiracetam, and topiramate. He has been seizure‐free since age 3 (now 11 years seizure‐free).

Brain MRI revealed a thin corpus callosum and diffuse cerebral atrophy. Video EEG at 3 years showed diffuse encephalopathic activity. An initial diagnosis of early infantile epileptic encephalopathy, hypothetically secondary to perinatal asphyxia, was considered.

Over time, he developed progressive microcephaly (head circumference <3rd percentile) and a spastic quadriplegic pattern, more pronounced on the right side, with progressive muscle wasting and joint contractures. He has severe intellectual disability (developmental quotient ~20), absent expressive speech, and oropharyngeal dysphagia requiring dietary modifications. Neurologic examination: muscle strength Grade 1 (MRC scale) on the right, Grade 4 on the left; central hypotonia; increased muscle tone, especially on the right (spasticity); reduced motor coordination; hyperreflexia; pain sensation unaffected. No dysmorphic features; growth parameters (weight, height, head circumference) below the 3rd centile.

Extensive metabolic workup (serum amino acids, urine organic acids, plasma lactate/pyruvate, ammonia, acylcarnitine profile) was normal. No relevant family history. Whole exome sequencing has been sent for genetic analysis.

Whole exome sequencing revealed a heterozygous pathogenic SPAST variant (NM_014946.4: c.1507C>T; p.Arg503Trp).
gold_diagnosis: Hereditary spastic paraplegia type 4 (SPG4) due to a heterozygous pathogenic SPAST variant (c.1507C>T, p.Arg503Trp)

answer_key.diagnosis: Hereditary spastic paraplegia type 4 (SPG4) due to a heterozygous pathogenic SPAST variant (c.1507C>T, p.Arg503Trp)
answer_key.etiology: Missense mutation in the SPAST gene encoding the microtubule-severing protein spastin, likely de novo given negative family history.
answer_key.next_management_step: Confirm the diagnosis with whole exome sequencing or targeted SPAST sequencing, provide genetic counseling, and continue multidisciplinary supportive care (neurorehabilitation, seizure management, feeding support).
required_findings: Spastic quadriplegia (right > left) with hyperreflexia and increased tone
Severe intellectual disability (DQ ~20) and global developmental delay
History of West syndrome (infantile spasms, hypsarrhythmia) and refractory epilepsy
Brain MRI showing thin corpus callosum and diffuse cerebral atrophy
Normal extensive metabolic workup
Secondary/acquired microcephaly (<3rd percentile)
No family history of similar neurological disorder
Absence of dysmorphic features
optional_findings: IVF conception and prematurity (32 weeks) with prenatal complications (preeclampsia, GDM)
NICU stay with hypotonia, poor feeding, apnea
Seizure freedom since age 3 on valproate and topiramate
Muscle strength asymmetry (Grade 1 right, Grade 4 left)
Progressive muscle wasting and joint contractures
Decrease in head circumference below 3rd percentile over time
Diffuse encephalopathic activity on video EEG

provenance.title: Spastic Quadriplegia Resulting From a Pathogenic Variant in the SPAST Gene: A First Report

mend_provenance.added: Whole exome sequencing revealed a heterozygous pathogenic SPAST variant (NM_014946.4: c.1507C>T; p.Arg503Trp).

challenge_prompt: A 37-year-old man with a history of medically refractory left action and postural tremor underwent right ventralis intermedius (VIM) nucleus deep brain stimulation (DBS) in 2001 (Medtronic). He achieved good clinical improvement and was followed regularly. Over two decades, he had three battery revisions, the most recent in 2020, when a rechargeable model (Activa RC, Medtronic) was implanted based on patient preference. Six months after this last battery change, he noticed gradual worsening of his tremor. Unipolar interrogation of the DBS system showed high impedance (>2,000 Ω) in contacts 1 and 2, and normal impedance in contacts 0 and 3 (925 Ω and 662 Ω, respectively). The patient also reported a shock-like sensation in his neck and a need for frequent battery recharging. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely underlying problem, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Fatigue fracture of the DBS extension cable

answer_key.diagnosis: Fatigue fracture of the DBS extension cable
aliases: Fatigue fracture of DBS extension cable
answer_key.etiology: Mechanical fatigue fracture of the extension cable due to repetitive stress from neck movements, possibly exacerbated by connector placement, leading to a breach in the wire and insulation.
answer_key.next_management_step: Obtain plain radiographs (X‑ray) of the DBS system (skull, neck, chest) to evaluate hardware integrity for fracture or breach; if confirmed, surgical replacement of the extension cable.
required_findings: History of VIM DBS with recent battery change
Gradual worsening of tremor six months after last battery change
High impedance (>2,000 Ω) in contacts 1 and 2 on unipolar interrogation
Normal impedance in contacts 0 and 3 (925 Ω and 662 Ω)
Shock‑like sensation in the neck
Need for frequent battery recharging
optional_findings: Long duration of DBS therapy (>20 years)

provenance.title: Fatigue fracture of a DBS extension cable: a pictorial review
provenance.journal: Annals of The Royal College of Surgeons of England

challenge_prompt: A 46-year-old woman with a history of total thyroidectomy and parathyroidectomy years ago, maintained on levothyroxine, was brought to the emergency department with a six-month history of progressive deterioration. She had discontinued levothyroxine for an unknown period. She developed progressive functional decline, lethargy, social withdrawal, poor intake, irritability, sound sensitivity, and auditory hallucinations with behavioral responses to unseen stimuli. She developed urinary and fecal incontinence and complete neglect of personal hygiene. On examination, she was confused with a disorganized thought process, clothing soiled with feces, non-pitting edema involving all four extremities (more pronounced in the lower limbs), and rigidity of both upper and lower limbs. Respiratory, cardiovascular, and gastrointestinal examinations were unremarkable. Laboratory investigations revealed blood glucose 2.69 mmol/L, potassium 3.07 mmol/L, and phosphate 0.69 mmol/L. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis was normal. Brain CT showed symmetrical calcification of the bilateral basal ganglia, thalami, right corona radiata, and bifrontal subcortical white matter. Brain MRI demonstrated bilateral basal ganglia and subcortical calcifications with diffuse mild to moderate brain atrophy, raising the possibility of Fahr syndrome. Additional endocrine workup showed TSH 199 mIU/L, free T4 4.8 pmol/L, and morning cortisol 66.8 nmol/L. Based on these results, levothyroxine and hydrocortisone replacement therapy were initiated. On the second day of admission, psychiatric evaluation revealed an agitated, disoriented patient who stared blankly, responded inappropriately, interacted with unseen stimuli, and expressed fear of having cancer. She was started on intramuscular haloperidol 5 mg at bedtime, later reduced to 2.5 mg, but this was discontinued after two days due to QTc prolongation of 568 ms on ECG. During the second week of hospitalization, the patient developed prominent catatonic features including immobility, persistent forward neck flexion, mutism, poor eye contact, severe plantarflexion contractures, and cogwheel rigidity of the upper limbs. The Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale score was 5. A lorazepam challenge test (1 mg orally) led to partial improvement in speech, interaction, and upper limb movement. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next best management step?
gold_diagnosis: Myxedema madness (severe hypothyroidism presenting with psychosis and catatonia)

answer_key.diagnosis: Myxedema madness (severe hypothyroidism presenting with psychosis and catatonia)
answer_key.etiology: Severe hypothyroidism due to non-adherence to levothyroxine replacement after total thyroidectomy
answer_key.next_management_step: Continue levothyroxine therapy, optimize dose as needed; continue lorazepam for catatonia; initiate aripiprazole (atypical antipsychotic with low QTc prolongation risk) for psychosis and catatonia, avoiding haloperidol due to QTc prolongation.
required_findings: History of total thyroidectomy and parathyroidectomy
Discontinued levothyroxine therapy
Progressive lethargy, social withdrawal, irritability, auditory hallucinations
Non-pitting edema of all extremities
Rigidity of both upper and lower limbs
TSH 199 mIU/L
Free T4 4.8 pmol/L
Catatonic features (immobility, mutism, etc.) and partial response to lorazepam challenge
optional_findings: Hypoglycemia (2.69 mmol/L), hypokalemia (3.07 mmol/L), hypophosphatemia (0.69 mmol/L)
Brain CT/MRI: bilateral basal ganglia and subcortical calcifications, brain atrophy
Late age of first psychiatric symptoms (46 years)

provenance.title: A Rare Presentation of Severe Hypothyroidism With Catatonia and Psychosis: Myxedema Madness

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

A 38-year-old male patient with no significant past medical history presented to the emergency department with sudden loss of consciousness. This was followed by altered mental status, confusion, and a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure occurring whilst he was awaiting uroflowmetry testing for lower urinary tract symptoms attributed to a UTI. His companion reported that he had been instructed to maintain a full bladder for the procedure and had consequently ingested approximately 8 liters of water over a three-hour period immediately preceding the onset of symptoms. There was no antecedent history of psychiatric illness, substance abuse, malignancy, recent head trauma, fever, or recent travel. He was not taking any regular medications.

On presentation, the patient was confused and unable to give a coherent history. Vital signs: temperature 36.8°C, heart rate 92 beats per minute, blood pressure 130/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 22 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Glasgow Coma Scale score was 12/15 (E3V4M5). Neurological examination revealed disorientation to time and place with sluggish bilateral pupillary responses, but no focal neurological deficits. There were no signs of meningism. The remainder of the systemic examination was unremarkable.

Urgent laboratory investigations revealed serum sodium 120 mEq/L, serum osmolality 252 mOsm/kg, urine sodium 47 mEq/L, urine osmolality 240 mOsm/kg. Thyroid-stimulating hormone was within normal limits. Morning serum cortisol levels were within normal limits. Renal function, blood glucose, and hematological parameters were unremarkable. A non-contrast computed tomography scan of the brain demonstrated no evidence of cerebral edema, intracranial hemorrhage, or space-occupying lesion.
gold_diagnosis: Acute water intoxication leading to dilutional hyponatremia

answer_key.diagnosis: Acute water intoxication leading to dilutional hyponatremia
aliases: Dilutional hyponatremia secondary to acute excessive water intake
answer_key.etiology: Excessive oral water intake (approximately 8 liters over 3 hours) prior to uroflowmetry, exceeding renal free water clearance capacity, causing acute dilutional hyponatremia.
answer_key.next_management_step: Urgent administration of 3% hypertonic saline (e.g., 150 mL over 20 minutes, repeated as needed based on response and sodium checks), strict fluid restriction (less than 800-1000 mL/day), frequent serum sodium monitoring to avoid overcorrection (target rise ≤6-8 mEq/L in first 24 hours), and adjunctive loop diuretic (e.g., furosemide) to promote free water excretion. Initiate seizure prophylaxis if clinically indicated.
required_findings: History of drinking approximately 8 liters of water over 3 hours immediately before symptom onset
Serum sodium 120 mEq/L
Serum osmolality 252 mOsm/kg
Urine osmolality 240 mOsm/kg
Urine sodium 47 mEq/L
No history of psychiatric illness, malignancy, or offending medications
Normal thyroid-stimulating hormone and morning serum cortisol
CT brain without cerebral edema, hemorrhage, or mass lesion
optional_findings: No history of substance abuse or recent head trauma

provenance.title: Acute Water Intoxication Leading to Dilutional Hyponatremia in a Patient With Urinary Tract Infection: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A previously healthy 16-month-old boy, born full-term via normal vaginal delivery, with normal growth and development, presented with high-grade fever, vomiting, and diarrhea. He was initially managed at another facility with oral rehydration and antipyretics. On the fifth day of illness, blood cultures grew extensively drug-resistant Salmonella Typhi, and he was started on meropenem 40 mg/kg/dose every 8 hours. Within hours, he developed altered sensorium and seizures, prompting high-dose corticosteroid therapy (dexamethasone 3 mg/kg loading dose, then 1 mg/kg every 6 hours for 3 days). Basic laboratory tests including complete blood count, renal function, and electrolytes were within normal limits. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis and brain MRI were unremarkable. The patient was diagnosed with Salmonella-associated encephalopathy. Following the MRI, he developed autonomic dysfunction with heart rate fluctuations and bradypnea with episodes of labored breathing during sleep, necessitating transfer to the Pediatric Intensive Care Unit. During his PICU stay, he developed new-onset flaccid limb weakness with loss of deep tendon reflexes. This weakness progressed over several days to axial hypotonia with marked head lag and inability to sit independently. A repeat lumbar puncture revealed no abnormalities. Electrodiagnostic evaluation, though technically challenging due to irritability, demonstrated markedly reduced compound muscle action potential amplitudes in the bilateral median nerves, inelicitable motor responses in the lower extremity nerves (peroneal and tibial), absent bilateral sural sensory nerve action potentials, and absent left median and ulnar sensory responses, consistent with a predominantly axonal polyneuropathy.

Based on the clinical presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis? What is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Guillain-Barré syndrome (acute motor axonal neuropathy variant)

answer_key.diagnosis: Guillain-Barré syndrome (acute motor axonal neuropathy variant)
aliases: acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculopathy (with axonal features)
answer_key.etiology: Post-infectious immune-mediated polyneuropathy triggered by extensively drug-resistant Salmonella Typhi infection
answer_key.next_management_step: Administer intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) 2 g/kg total dose divided over 2 days

developed new-onset flaccid limb weakness with loss of deep tendon reflexes
progression to axial hypotonia with marked head lag and inability to sit independently

electrodiagnostic evaluation demonstrated markedly reduced CMAP amplitudes in bilateral median nerves, inelicitable motor responses in lower extremity nerves, absent bilateral sural SNAPs, absent left median and ulnar sensory responses
cerebrospinal fluid analysis and brain MRI were unremarkable
autonomic dysfunction with heart rate fluctuations and bradypnea
high-dose corticosteroid therapy for altered sensorium and seizures
16-month-old previously healthy child

symptomatic management with oral rehydration and antipyretics initially
meropenem 40 mg/kg/dose every 8 hours started on day 5
recent travel history (if available in article - but not in prompt; exclude)
repeat lumbar puncture normal (albuminocytologic dissociation absent)

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

A 68-year-old right-handed male patient with a past medical history of hypothyroidism and hypertension presented with increased anxiety, depression, auditory hallucinations, nightmares, and suicidal thoughts. He reported that his symptoms began 2 years ago, and he had no prior psychiatric history. His social history was notable for alcohol dependence, abstaining since 1989 and no documented family history for neurodegenerative diseases.

The patient was hospitalized and started on antipsychotics (olanzapine, quetiapine). Over time, his condition progressed, and he began experiencing involuntary movements (jerky limb movements, poor coordination, unsteadiness, multiple falls, orofacial dyskinesia, and foot drop). Antipsychotics were discontinued as they exacerbated these involuntary movements. On neurological examination, findings were largely unremarkable, aside from unsteadiness on the heel-to-toe test, past-pointing in the left arm, and mild dysdiadochokinesia.

A brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed cerebral and cerebellar hemispheric atrophy. A subsequent DaTSCAN was conducted. The patient was pretreated with a thyroid blocking agent approximately an hour before the intravenous administration of 185 MBq of [123I] Ioflupane. Three hours after the administration, SPECT-CT imaging was performed using a dual-head SPECT gamma camera model Symbia Intevo (SIEMENS Healthineers) with integrated CT (16 slices/rotation). Striatal binding ratios were calculated using DaTQuant software by GE Healthcare, to semiquantify striatum to background (occipital region) ratios of SPECT counts within the predefined volume of interests.

DaTSCAN revealed borderline reduced putaminal binding ratios bilaterally (more prominent on the left) and left putamen-to-caudate asymmetry. These findings were suggestive of a dopamine transporter deficit at the presynaptic level, so degenerative parkinsonism (Idiopathic Parkinson’s disease/Parkinsonian syndromes [IPD/PS]) was suspected. However, due to persistent nightmares and involuntary movements, Huntington's disease was also strongly considered.
gold_diagnosis: Huntington's disease

answer_key.diagnosis: Huntington's disease
answer_key.etiology: CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion in the HTT gene (autosomal dominant inheritance)
answer_key.next_management_step: Genetic testing for CAG repeat expansion in HTT gene

challenge_prompt:
A 49-year-old female with a past medical history of Crohn disease, hypertension, and insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus presented to the emergency department with four days of intermittent severe headaches. She described each episode as a sudden “boom” of maximal intensity at onset (thunderclap headache). She was taking venlafaxine for mood disorder and estradiol for vasomotor menopausal symptoms. Review of her medical record revealed she had completed a three-day burst of methylprednisolone 1 gram daily immediately before symptom onset, followed by a prednisone taper for pyoderma gangrenosum of a lower extremity. Initial blood pressure was 179/77 mm Hg; other vital signs were normal. Physical examination revealed no focal neurological deficits; the lower extremity wound appeared non-infected. CT brain without contrast showed no acute intracranial hemorrhage and was grossly normal. CT angiography (CTA) of the head and neck revealed a possible 2-mm aneurysm of the right posterior cerebral artery without evidence of rupture, but was otherwise normal. After consultation, cerebral digital subtraction angiography (DSA) was performed and demonstrated no actual aneurysm. Lumbar puncture showed normal opening pressure and unremarkable cerebrospinal fluid analysis. Her headache improved with blood pressure control, and she was discharged.

Two days later, she returned with confusion. Her family reported she was slow to respond and could no longer perform activities of daily living. She endorsed return of her headache. She was alert and oriented but had delayed verbal responses. Neurological examination was non-focal. Repeat CT brain without contrast revealed acute infarctions in the right cerebellum and left parietal lobe. Repeat CTA head and neck demonstrated extensive vasoconstriction of the intracranial vasculature with diminished flow through the cerebral arteries compared to the prior study. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic or therapeutic step?
gold_diagnosis: Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome

answer_key.diagnosis: Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Triggered cerebral arterial vasoconstriction, possibly by vasoactive medications (venlafaxine, estradiol) and recent high-dose glucocorticoid exposure
answer_key.next_management_step: Cerebral digital subtraction angiography (DSA) with consideration for intra-arterial verapamil infusion

Recent completion of methylprednisolone burst 1g daily for 3 days
Use of vasoactive medications (venlafaxine and estradiol)
Acute hypertension (179/77 mm Hg)
Initial CT and CTA normal except possible aneurysm; DSA showed no aneurysm
Normal lumbar puncture (opening pressure and CSF analysis)
Return visit with confusion, acute infarcts in right cerebellum and left parietal lobe on CT
Repeat CTA showing extensive intracranial vasoconstriction with diminished flow
optional_findings: Vasomotor menopausal symptoms treated with estradiol
Pyoderma gangrenosum of the lower extremity
Slow verbal responses without focal neurological deficits on second presentation

challenge_prompt: An 8-year-old, right-handed boy with a history of seizures since age 5 years and 9 months presented with drug-resistant focal epilepsy. His seizures manifested as bi-hand-clapping movements with repeated blinking, followed by asymmetric tonicity in the upper limbs and right deviation, accompanied by impaired awareness. He sometimes experienced an aura of numbness in his head and body before seizures. The seizures became very frequent, up to more than ten per day, both during wakefulness and sleep. Initial brain MRI revealed abnormal signals in the left inferior frontal gyrus, specifically in the opercular part (T2 Flair 3mm slice). PET demonstrated hypometabolism in the same region. VEEG monitoring detected focal seizures originating from the left frontal region, with interictal spikes and spike-complexes in the left inferior frontal area. Trio whole-exome sequencing identified no explanatory pathogenic variants. His IQ on the Wechsler Intelligence Scale was 119. He was diagnosed with drug-resistant epilepsy after failing multiple antiepileptic drugs (oxcarbazepine, levetiracetam, lacosamide, clonazepam, perampanel), with a longest seizure-free period of 4 months. Given the lesion's location in the classical Broca’s area (BA44/45), a multidisciplinary team recommended resection or thermal coagulation of the epileptogenic focus but expressed concern about potential language deficits. Wada test and functional MRI were refused due to invasiveness and poor tolerance to MRI noise. Therefore, the patient underwent language mapping with a 128-channel optically pumped magnetometer (OPM-MEG) system, using a children-size helmet and a magnetic shielding system. A resting-state examination was performed to detect epileptiform discharges, and a verb-association paradigm was used for language task mapping. The data were analyzed with dipole fitting for interictal spikes and a DICS beamformer for beta event-related desynchronization during the language task. The results showed: (1) Dipole localization of epileptiform spikes primarily in the left middle frontal gyrus, inferior frontal gyrus, and insular cortex; (2) Language task beta desynchronization revealed left-hemisphere dominance with high-signal areas in the left orbitofrontal cortex, insula, and superior temporal gyrus; (3) The overlap between spike dipoles and language areas was minimal; (4) The language distribution significantly differed from the classical Broca’s area. Based on these findings, what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Drug-resistant focal epilepsy due to a structural lesion in the left opercular inferior frontal gyrus (likely focal cortical dysplasia or low-grade tumor).

answer_key.diagnosis: Drug-resistant focal epilepsy due to a structural lesion in the left opercular inferior frontal gyrus (likely focal cortical dysplasia or low-grade tumor).
answer_key.etiology: Unknown (structural lesion, possibly focal cortical dysplasia)
answer_key.next_management_step: Proceed with MRI-guided laser interstitial thermal therapy (MRgLITT) or surgical resection of the epileptogenic focus, as functional mapping indicates no significant overlap with language areas.

PET: hypometabolism in opercular left IFG
VEEG: focal seizures left frontal, spikes in left inferior frontal region
OPM-MEG dipole localization: spikes in left middle frontal gyrus, inferior frontal gyrus, insula
OPM-MEG language beamformer: left-hemisphere dominance, activation in orbitofrontal cortex, insula, STG
Minimal overlap between spike foci and language areas
Language distribution differs from classical Broca's area
Patient has drug-resistant epilepsy failing multiple AEDs
optional_findings: Seizure semiology: bi-hand-clapping, blinking, asymmetric tonicity
Aura: numbness in head and body
128-channel OPM-MEG with children-size helmet and magnetic shielding

challenge_prompt:
An 80-year-old male with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, prior stroke, and polycythemia vera presented with multiple falls over one week due to progressive right lower extremity weakness and gait instability. He denied pain but reported right hip discomfort, balance instability, and dizziness with positional changes. His diabetes was suboptimally controlled (hemoglobin A1c 7.6%). Medications included empagliflozin, glipizide, and sitagliptin; insulin therapy was initiated during hospitalization. Neurologic examination revealed proximal right lower extremity weakness with right thigh muscle atrophy, diminished right Achilles reflex, reduced bilateral patellar reflexes, and impaired balance and ambulation. CT head and MRI brain were unremarkable. MRI of the lumbar spine and pelvis showed no disc herniation, stenosis, or other structural abnormality. Orthostatic vital signs were normal. Nerve conduction studies and electromyography demonstrated positive sharp waves in distal right lower extremity muscles with preserved conduction in proximal muscle groups, suggestive of peripheral neuropathy with possible right peroneal involvement. A therapeutic trial of carbidopa/levodopa provided no improvement.

What is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what is the next most appropriate management step?
gold_diagnosis: Atypical diabetic amyotrophy (diabetic lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy)

answer_key.diagnosis: Atypical diabetic amyotrophy (diabetic lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy)
answer_key.etiology: Diabetic microvasculitis causing ischemic nerve injury
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate supportive care with physical therapy, optimize glycemic control, and implement fall prevention strategies

challenge_prompt:
A 22-year-old healthy primigravida was admitted at 36+0 weeks of gestation with premature spontaneous rupture of membranes. Prenatal ultrasound had shown isolated intrauterine growth restriction (fetal abdominal circumference <10th percentile) with normal umbilical artery Doppler. She had no significant past medical history or preexisting hypertension. At admission, she was normotensive (115/75 mmHg). Laboratory screening revealed mild isolated thrombocytopenia (83 × 10^9/L; reference 130–400 × 10^9/L), elevated lactate dehydrogenase (710 U/L; reference 227–450 U/L), and normal serum creatinine, uric acid, and liver transaminases (AST/ALT). Bedside 24-hour proteinuria was negative.

She underwent emergency cesarean section two days later for failed induction of labor, under spinal anesthesia without complications. A male neonate weighing 2100 g (<5th percentile) was delivered.

On postpartum day 1, she developed a rapid-onset neurological syndrome: profound lethargy, disorientation, persistent diffuse headache, blurred vision, and intermittent scotoma. Blood pressure rose to 170/100 mmHg. While arranging imaging, she had a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting about 2 minutes. Intravenous magnesium sulfate was administered per Zuspan regimen, terminating the seizure. Immediate brain MRI showed multiple bilateral cortical and subcortical lesions, predominantly in frontal and parietal regions, T1-hypointense and markedly T2/FLAIR-hyperintense, suggestive of posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES). Laboratory tests immediately after the seizure showed hemoglobin 13.7 g/dL, platelet count 94 × 10^9/L (slight improvement), and liver transaminases still within normal limits.

Based on the information available up to this point, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?
gold_diagnosis: Postpartum HELLP syndrome with posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES)

answer_key.diagnosis: Postpartum HELLP syndrome with posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES)
aliases: Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Endothelial dysfunction and microangiopathy associated with preeclampsia/HELLP syndrome, leading to blood-brain barrier disruption and PRES
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate aggressive blood pressure control with intravenous labetalol or hydralazine, continue magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis, monitor complete blood count, liver enzymes, and LDH closely, and consider early administration of corticosteroids (e.g., dexamethasone) given high suspicion for HELLP syndrome.

Admission isolated thrombocytopenia (83 × 10^9/L) and elevated LDH (710 U/L)
Normal liver transaminases and negative proteinuria at admission
Postpartum day 1 sudden hypertension (170/100 mmHg)
Neurological symptoms: lethargy, disorientation, headache, visual disturbances
Generalized tonic-clonic seizure
Brain MRI: bilateral cortical/subcortical T2/FLAIR hyperintensities predominantly frontal/parietal, T1 hypointense, suggestive of PRES
Post-seizure labs: hemoglobin stable, platelet count slightly improved to 94, liver enzymes still normal
optional_findings: Neonate small for gestational age (<5th percentile)

challenge_prompt:
A 75-year-old male patient presented with residual cognitive and motor dysfunction persisting for more than 3 months after recovery of consciousness from acute carbon monoxide (CO) poisoning. According to his caregiver, the patient experienced acute CO exposure during indoor charcoal heating in January 2025 and was treated at a local hospital with comprehensive management, including hyperbaric oxygen therapy and supportive/neurotrophic treatment; consciousness was restored after approximately 6 days. However, neurocognitive and functional deficits persisted and gradually progressed thereafter, manifested as an inability to answer daily questions normally and complete simple calculations. Concurrently, he had limb weakness and impaired voluntary urination and defecation (urinary and fecal incontinence). He also developed sleep disturbance, including difficulty initiating sleep and frequent nocturnal awakenings. These symptoms collectively led to a marked decline in functional independence, rendering him unable to perform activities of daily living independently.

On admission, he was awake but fatigued and poorly cooperative, with marked impairment in memory, orientation, and calculation. Pupils were equal and reactive, and cranial nerve screening was grossly unremarkable; motor strength was approximately 4/5 in all limbs with normal tone and reflexes, and Babinski signs were negative bilaterally; no meningeal signs were noted. Relevant past interventions included a brief trial of memantine and donepezil, as well as structured rehabilitation at a rehabilitation facility, with limited clinical improvement reported. Past medical history was notable for hypertension and coronary artery disease. He denied known drug/food allergies, smoking, alcohol abuse, or illicit drug use. Family history was unremarkable.

Cranial magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed diffuse cerebral white matter hyperintensities on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences, consistent with demyelination. No acute infarction, hemorrhage, or mass was identified.
gold_diagnosis: Delayed Encephalopathy after Acute Carbon Monoxide Poisoning (DEACMP)

answer_key.diagnosis: Delayed Encephalopathy after Acute Carbon Monoxide Poisoning (DEACMP)
answer_key.etiology: Delayed neurological sequelae of carbon monoxide poisoning, likely involving cerebral hypoxia, oxidative stress, and white matter demyelination.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate a trial of acupuncture therapy as an adjunctive neurorehabilitation intervention, given persistence of deficits despite standard care.

recovery of consciousness after approximately 6 days
more than 3 months before progressive cognitive and motor decline

urinary and fecal incontinence
diffuse cerebral white matter demyelination on MRI
failed trial of memantine and donepezil
limited response to structured rehabilitation

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

A seven-year-old boy with a known thalassemia trait and mild autism presented with a one-day history of sudden-onset bilateral painful fluctuating vision. This was associated with headache and fever; however, there was no history of nausea or vomiting. Upon examination, visual acuity (VA) using Lea symbols was 6/12 in the right eye (RE) and 6/30 in the left eye (LE). Intraocular pressures were 11 mmHg bilaterally. Optic nerve function tests, including light reflex and colour vision, were within normal limits, and the patient was orthophoric. Formal perimetry was attempted but was unreliable due to limited patient cooperation. Anterior segment examination was unremarkable, and dilated fundoscopy revealed no optic disc swelling in either eye. A systemic neurological examination was also unremarkable. An urgent non-contrast computed tomography (CT) of the brain showed no acute intracranial pathology or space-occupying lesions. Over the following days, the patient’s vision continued to fluctuate. Given the inconclusive clinical findings, urgent magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain and orbits was performed. The MRI revealed features of an inflammatory demyelinating disease involving the optic nerves. Hematological and biochemical investigations were within normal limits and did not suggest an active infection. A lumbar puncture was performed; cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis was negative for bacterial and viral cultures. Serological testing for AQP4-IgG and MOG-IgG was negative.
gold_diagnosis: Bilateral optic neuritis due to acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM)

answer_key.diagnosis: Bilateral optic neuritis due to acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM)
answer_key.etiology: Immune-mediated demyelination, presumably parainfectious; serum negative for AQP4-IgG and MOG-IgG
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone (e.g., 30 mg/kg/day) and consider empiric antimicrobial coverage until infection is definitively excluded

Normal funduscopic examination without optic disc swelling
MRI brain/orbits showing inflammatory demyelinating disease involving optic nerves
Negative cerebrospinal fluid bacterial and viral cultures
Serum AQP4-IgG negative
Serum MOG-IgG negative
Headache and fever at presentation
Normal brain CT
optional_findings: Normal optic nerve function tests (light reflex, color vision)
Hematological and biochemical investigations within normal limits
Unreliable formal perimetry due to limited cooperation

challenge_prompt:
A 28-year-old man with a three-year history of schizophrenia (previously stable on risperidone 2 mg/day, but self-discontinued one month ago) presented with one week of recurrent auditory hallucinations, persecutory delusions, and acute deterioration to complete mutism, immobility, refusal of food and water, and inability to perform self-care. He had been lost to contact for two weeks prior to admission. On examination, temperature 38.6°C, heart rate 112 bpm, blood pressure 122/76 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min. Chest auscultation revealed bilateral lower lobe moist rales. Neurological exam showed flaccid limbs (limited cooperation); marked hypotonia was noted. Pupils were equal and reactive. Babinski reflexes were negative; meningeal irritation signs absent. Mental status examination: conscious but mute, blank stare, flat affect, waxy flexibility (passive compliance to limb manipulation). Laboratory tests: white blood cells 14.1×10^9/L, neutrophils 12.16×10^9/L, high-sensitivity C-reactive protein 16.1 mg/L. Blood biochemistry, thyroid function, and infectious disease screening were unremarkable. Serum risperidone level was undetectable (0 ng/mL). Chest CT showed small infectious lesions in both lower lungs. Brain MRI and EEG were normal.

The patient was admitted with diagnoses of schizophrenia and pulmonary infection. He received ceftriaxone 2.0 g/day, ambroxol, fluid support, and risperidone (titrated from 1 mg to 3 mg/day). Benzodiazepines were withheld due to the pulmonary infection. Modified electroconvulsive therapy was recommended but refused by the family. A nasogastric tube was inserted because attempts at oral hydration caused choking and coughing. By hospital day 6, he remained mute and immobile. Physical examination now clearly revealed generalized hypotonia (flaccid limbs), hyporeflexia, and hypoesthesia in both lower limbs. Pathological reflexes remained absent. Follow-up chest CT showed significant resolution of the infectious foci; WBC had normalized (5.2×10^9/L) and CRP was 2.1 mg/L. The risperidone was increased to 3 mg/day.

Over the following days, the patient’s psychotic symptoms gradually improved. By day 20, he began to speak; hallucinations and delusions had largely remitted. He could sit at the bedside but was unable to stand. He whispered to his mother, “Ask the doctor if my legs will get better?” Swallowing remained slow, with easy choking. Upper limb movement was normal, but lower limbs were weak and required support for movement. Dysphonia (low voice) was noted. The neurological deficits—flaccid weakness, hyporeflexia, sensory loss, dysphagia, and dysphonia—persisted despite the resolution of psychiatric symptoms.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what diagnostic test should be performed next?
gold_diagnosis: Guillain-Barré syndrome (acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy)

answer_key.diagnosis: Guillain-Barré syndrome (acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy)
answer_key.etiology: Immune-mediated acute inflammatory peripheral neuropathy, most likely post-infectious (pulmonary infection).
answer_key.next_management_step: Lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid analysis to confirm albuminocytologic dissociation.

Flaccid limb weakness/hypotonia
Hyporeflexia
Hypoesthesia in lower limbs
Dysphagia with choking and dysphonia (bulbar involvement)
Antecedent pulmonary infection (fever, bilateral rales, chest CT lesions)
Normal brain MRI and EEG (excludes central nervous system etiology)
Undetectable serum risperidone level (rules out drug-induced effects)
optional_findings: Negative Babinski reflexes and lack of meningeal signs
Rapid psychiatric improvement after resuming antipsychotic
Normalization of inflammatory markers by day 6
Requirement of nasogastric tube for dysphagia
Persistent weakness despite resolving psychotic symptoms

provenance.title: Case Report: When catatonia-like symptoms are not catatonia: Guillain–Barré syndrome in a patient with schizophrenia

challenge_prompt: A 10-day-old male neonate, born at 40+1 weeks via cesarean section for placental abruption, had an uneventful early neonatal course and was discharged at 3 days of life. At 10 days, he presented with poor feeding, irritability, and fever (38.6°C). On admission, he was lethargic but responsive, with normal tone and reflexes. Initial laboratory tests: leukocyte count 6.5×10⁹/L, C-reactive protein <2.9 mg/L. Lumbar puncture yielded clear cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) with 2 cells/µL, protein 75 mg/dL, glucose 54 mg/dL. CSF Gram stain and bacterial culture were negative. PCR testing of CSF for herpes simplex virus (HSV), varicella-zoster virus (VZV), cytomegalovirus (CMV), and enterovirus was negative. Brain MRI (1.5T) with diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) and apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) maps demonstrated multiple small punctate lesions in the supratentorial deep white matter bilaterally, characterized by diffusion restriction on ADC maps, spontaneous hyperintensity on T1-weighted images, and no significant contrast enhancement; the basal ganglia, brainstem, and cerebellum were spared. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what diagnostic or management step should be taken next?
gold_diagnosis: Human parechovirus (HPeV) central nervous system infection/encephalitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Human parechovirus (HPeV) central nervous system infection/encephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Human parechovirus, most likely genotype A3
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform CSF PCR for human parechovirus RNA and initiate supportive care (monitor for seizures, consider repeat MRI, plan neurodevelopmental follow-up).

challenge_prompt:
A 28-year-old male with no prior psychiatric history, substance use, medical comorbidities, or family history of psychotic disorders presented with a six-month course of profound mutism and severe self-neglect, including refusal to eat or engage in personal hygiene. The symptoms began after he consumed 3.4 g of psilocybin (first-time use, out of curiosity) six months earlier. Shortly after ingestion, he became silent and reflective, later went missing, and was found 24 hours later in a disorganized state in a park. After returning home, he remained nonverbal, slept approximately 16 hours per day, was intermittently aggressive with family, and required complete assistance with feeding and grooming. The family did not seek medical care until six months after symptom onset, when they called emergency services due to persistent behavioral disturbance and aggression. En route to the hospital, he was aggressively agitated and received midazolam 15 mg intramuscularly. In the inpatient unit, he was stuporous, nearly mute, and intermittently agitated, and he was uncooperative with the safety evaluation. For agitation, he was given ziprasidone 10 mg and lorazepam 2 mg intramuscularly. Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain and comprehensive laboratory studies were unremarkable.

Based on the information available at this point, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Catatonia induced by psilocybin (substance-induced catatonia)

answer_key.diagnosis: Catatonia induced by psilocybin (substance-induced catatonia)
answer_key.etiology: First-time psilocybin ingestion leading to a prolonged catatonic syndrome
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform a lorazepam challenge (e.g., 1-2 mg IM/IV/PO) and monitor for resolution of catatonic signs within 30 minutes; if positive, continue benzodiazepine therapy.

challenge_prompt:
A 15-year-old boy with a history of juvenile dermatomyositis (diagnosed at age 6) presented with an 8-day history of knee pain and intermittent fever unresponsive to anti-infection therapy. He was initially diagnosed in August 2014 after a three-year history of facial rash and one month of lower limb weakness: laboratory tests showed elevated muscle enzymes and myoglobin, electromyography demonstrated myopathic changes, and a right gastrocnemius muscle biopsy revealed partial degeneration of striated muscle fibers, loss of striation, and interstitial fibrosis. He was treated over the following years with prednisone, methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil, methylprednisolone, hydroxychloroquine, intravenous immunoglobulin, high-dose glucocorticoid pulses, cyclosporine, thalidomide, alendronate, tacrolimus, tofacitinib, and later a combination of oral hydroxychloroquine, prednisone acetate, thalidomide, and methotrexate. During this time he developed progressive subcutaneous calcification and skin ulceration.

On current admission (March 2024), his temperature was 36°C, pulse 85/min, respiratory rate 18/min, blood pressure 119/75 mmHg. There was no superficial lymphadenopathy. Erythema, swelling, and ulceration with calcifications were present over bilateral knee joints and the left elbow joint, with a milky-white exudate mixed with yellowish discharge during joint movement. Cardiopulmonary auscultation was normal. Abdominal palpation revealed hepatomegaly (+2 cm below the costal margin) without splenomegaly. Neurological examination and muscle strength/tone were normal; Childhood Myositis Assessment Scale (CMAS) score was 42/52.

Laboratory results:
- White blood cell count 36.46 × 10⁹/L, platelet count 76 × 10⁹/L, hemoglobin 123 g/L.
- Peripheral blood smear: 14% blasts.
- C-reactive protein 53 mg/L, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 108 mm/h.
- Ferritin 3240.0 ng/mL.
- Lactate dehydrogenase 1028 U/L (3.5 × upper limit of normal).
- Procalcitonin 0.24 ng/mL.
- Creatine kinase <20 U/L, myoglobin <21.00 ng/mL.
- Antibodies to extractable nuclear antigen (ENA) and antinuclear antibody (ANA) were negative.

Bone marrow aspiration: 7.5% abnormal promyelocytes.
Immunophenotyping: approximately 5.4% abnormal myeloblasts.
Fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH): ETO/AML1 fusion gene associated with the t(8;21)(q22;q22) translocation in 92.3% of cells.

What is the most likely diagnosis for his current presentation?
gold_diagnosis: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML-M2) with t(8;21)(q22;q22) translocation

answer_key.diagnosis: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML-M2) with t(8;21)(q22;q22) translocation
answer_key.etiology: Therapy-related myeloid neoplasm secondary to prolonged immunosuppressive therapy for juvenile dermatomyositis (most probable); alternatively, an independent de novo AML with a coincidental autoimmune disorder.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate AML-directed chemotherapy (e.g., daunorubicin, cytarabine, homoharringtonine) with antimicrobial prophylaxis and supportive care; prepare for allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation as consolidation.

provenance.title: Case Report: Juvenile dermatomyositis complicated by acute myeloid leukemia: achievement of sustained remission through hematopoietic stem cell transplantation

challenge_prompt: A 58‐year‐old female with a history of juvenile idiopathic arthritis, celiac disease, and hypothyroidism on levothyroxine presented with one month of progressively worsening neurological symptoms: dizziness, imbalance, left facial droop, word‐finding difficulties, and right‐sided headache. Two days before admission, she rapidly deteriorated to severe somnolence and mutism. Initial laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (17.5 × 10³/μL), elevated ESR (94 mm/hr), elevated CRP (4.54 mg/dL), mildly elevated TSH (6.40 µIU/mL), and normal free T4 (1.64 ng/dL). Noncontrast head CT and CTA of the head and neck were negative for acute pathology; urine toxicology was unremarkable. Brain MRI with and without contrast demonstrated mild, diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement; axial FLAIR images showed increased signal within parietal sulci. Lumbar puncture revealed lymphocytic pleocytosis (WBC 414 cells/μL, 74% lymphocytes), elevated protein (162 mg/dL), normal glucose (50 mg/dL), and negative Gram stain, fungal culture, and CSF culture. Empiric acyclovir and ceftriaxone were started. HSV PCR from CSF returned negative. Continuous EEG showed slow, disorganized background rhythms. Additional workup included the following, all negative: CSF Lyme, CSF meningoencephalitis panel, CSF VDRL, CSF AFB smear/culture, CSF oligoclonal bands, serum HSV‑1/2, serum WNV IgM, serum VZV DNA PCR, Lyme IgM/IgG, HIV 1/2 antibody screen, syphilis screen, HCV RNA, CSF ACE, paraneoplastic panel, ENC2 panel, PAC1, myelin basic protein, serum EEE serology, serum Powassan panel, serum 14‑3‑3, serum RPR, serum Treponema antibody, ANA, SS‑A antibody, SS‑B antibody, MAG antibody, islet cell antigen 512 antibody, and insulin antibody. Serum WNV IgG was positive (past infection). Serum anti‑GAD65 antibody was 135 U/mL. Serum thyroglobulin antibody was 57.4 IU/mL (positive), and serum thyroid peroxidase antibody was 84 IU/mL (positive).
gold_diagnosis: Steroid‐responsive encephalopathy associated with autoimmune thyroiditis (SREAT) / Hashimoto encephalopathy

answer_key.diagnosis: Steroid‐responsive encephalopathy associated with autoimmune thyroiditis (SREAT) / Hashimoto encephalopathy
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune encephalopathy associated with elevated antithyroid antibodies (anti‑TG and anti‑TPO), likely immune‑mediated
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate high‑dose intravenous corticosteroids (e.g., methylprednisolone 1 g daily) and/or intravenous immunoglobulin, followed by a steroid taper.

Negative extensive infectious workup including HSV PCR, meningoencephalitis panel, and cultures
Serum thyroglobulin antibody positive (57.4 IU/mL)
Serum thyroid peroxidase antibody positive (84 IU/mL)
Continuous EEG with slow, disorganized background rhythms

Elevated ESR 94 mm/hr and CRP 4.54 mg/dL

Axial FLAIR hyperintensity in parietal sulci

challenge_prompt:
A previously healthy 70-year-old Japanese woman presented with pain radiating from the neck to the left arm. She was initially diagnosed with cervical spondylosis and treated conservatively at an orthopedic clinic. Two weeks later, she developed clusters of vesicular eruptions extending from the neck to the left arm, leading to a diagnosis of herpes zoster. She was treated with oral valacyclovir 1000 mg three times daily for 7 days and acetaminophen 500 mg every 6 hours as needed. Despite this, her pain persisted, so tramadol 50 mg twice daily and gabapentin 300 mg three times daily were added, along with vitamin B12 1000 µg daily and vitamin B6 50 mg daily.

Two weeks after the rash onset (four weeks after the initial visit), she reported reduced muscle strength in her left arm. Neurological examination revealed decreased strength in left shoulder flexors and elbow flexors and extensors, predominantly in muscles innervated by the C5–C7 roots. Manual muscle testing showed: shoulder flexion right 5, left 2+; elbow flexion right 5, left 4+; shoulder extension, elbow extension, and all other upper limb muscles were 5/5 bilaterally. Sensation was mildly decreased in the left C5–C7 dermatomes. Nerve conduction studies of both arms revealed decreased compound muscle action potential amplitudes in the left musculocutaneous and axillary nerves, with relatively preserved sensory nerve action potentials, indicating predominant motor involvement of the C5–C7 segments. Cervical spine magnetic resonance imaging showed no evidence of spinal cord compression, myelopathy, or structural abnormalities.

The patient was hemodynamically stable, without cranial nerve involvement or signs of infection. She had strong family support and easy access to medical follow-up, so she was managed on an outpatient basis with regular neurological reassessments.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what next step in management is most appropriate?
gold_diagnosis: Segmental zoster paresis (herpes zoster motor paresis involving left C5–C7 myotomes)

answer_key.diagnosis: Segmental zoster paresis (herpes zoster motor paresis involving left C5–C7 myotomes)
answer_key.etiology: Varicella zoster virus reactivation within dorsal root ganglia, with spread to anterior horn cells/motor roots
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate early physical therapy and occupational therapy for the upper limb, continue outpatient pain management with gabapentinoids and analgesics, and arrange close follow-up with repeated neurological examinations to monitor for improvement or progression; consider inpatient admission if weakness worsens.
required_findings: Radicular pain from neck to left arm initially attributed to cervical spondylosis
Clusters of vesicular eruptions in a dermatomal pattern on the neck and left arm, diagnosed as herpes zoster
Motor weakness in left shoulder flexors (2+/5) and elbow flexors (4+/5), in C5–C7 distribution, documented by manual muscle testing
Mildly decreased sensation in left C5–C7 dermatomes

Nerve conduction studies showing decreased compound muscle action potential amplitudes in left musculocutaneous and axillary nerves with preserved sensory nerve action potentials, indicating motor axonal involvement
Cervical spine MRI without spinal cord compression, myelopathy, or structural abnormalities
Onset of segmental weakness approximately 2 weeks after appearance of the herpetic rash
Temporal sequence of radicular pain, dermatomal vesicular rash, then segmental motor weakness
optional_findings: Prior treatment with valacyclovir for herpes zoster
Subsequent use of tramadol and gabapentin for neuropathic pain
Hemodynamic stability and absence of cranial nerve involvement
Strong family support enabling outpatient follow-up
Supplementary vitamins B6 and B12 administered
Bilateral nerve conduction studies performed for comparison

provenance.title: Upper limb paresis due to herpes zoster successfully managed in an outpatient setting: a case report

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

A 9-year-old boy was hit by a car in 2014, sustaining intracranial bleeding, seizures, and multiple physical and orthopedic complications. He was comatose for three months and hospitalized for four months. During hospitalization, he progressed from coma to a vegetative state (unresponsive wakefulness syndrome), then to a minimally conscious state. Seizures were noted early and antiepileptic treatment was initiated. After prolonged critical illness, he underwent rehabilitation but showed poor engagement. He then entered a prolonged confusional phase marked by inattention, disorientation, agitation, impulsivity, and poor participation in rehabilitation. Basic function gradually improved, but persistent cognitive and behavioral impairment limited rehabilitation and school reintegration.

Approximately one year after the accident, he was referred to child and adolescent psychiatry for assessment of functional impairment. At the initial consultation, he remained in a confusional state characterized by disorientation, impaired attention, fluctuation in symptoms, and behavioral dysregulation. His global score on the Rancho Los Amigos Levels of Cognitive Functioning Scale (RLA-LOCF) was below Level IV, indicating a confused and agitated state. He displayed non-purposeful and aggressive behavior and was unable to cooperate with treatment because of cognitive symptoms and behavioral problems. The clinical team considered that his severe externalizing behavior would limit the effectiveness of applied behavior analysis alone, and pharmacotherapy was planned.

On specialist neuropsychiatric assessment, the presentation was dominated by severe cognitive-behavioral dysregulation without focal psychotic symptoms. He was distractible, disoriented, impulsive, poorly redirectable, and at times aggressively disorganized. There was no sustained evidence of a primary mood disorder, formal thought disorder, or persistent hallucinations.

Electroencephalography (EEG) showed occasional frontal slowing without epileptiform discharges, which did not suggest an active epileptiform process as the main driver of the presentation.

Initial pharmacotherapy with risperidone (up to 0.25 mg three times daily) produced no meaningful improvement. A trial of methylphenidate 10 mg was also ineffective. Both were discontinued. Later, olanzapine 5–10 mg was stopped because of excessive sedation, and aripiprazole 7.5 mg was trialed but adherence was poor, with worsening behavior and refusal to take the medication.

In March 2022, at age 17, he was brought to the emergency department in an acutely agitated and aggressive state, requiring intramuscular haloperidol for immediate behavioral control. At this point, he continued to demonstrate severe behavioral dysregulation, impulsivity, and aggression, and repeated oral medication trials had failed due to inadequate response, poor tolerability, or difficulty maintaining consistent oral treatment.
gold_diagnosis: Prolonged post-traumatic confusional state (PTCS) with severe behavioral dysregulation and dysexecutive syndrome following severe traumatic brain injury

answer_key.diagnosis: Prolonged post-traumatic confusional state (PTCS) with severe behavioral dysregulation and dysexecutive syndrome following severe traumatic brain injury
answer_key.etiology: Severe pediatric traumatic brain injury with intracranial bleeding, seizures, prolonged coma, vegetative state, and minimally conscious state
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate monthly long-acting injectable paliperidone palmitate (starting at 100 mg, increased to 150 mg) with adjunct low-dose risperidone 2 mg at night, given poor adherence and refractory agitation.
required_findings: History of severe TBI with intracranial bleeding, seizures, prolonged coma, and progression through vegetative and minimally conscious states
Persistent confusional state after emergence from minimally conscious state, characterized by disorientation, inattention, agitation, impulsivity, and behavioral dysregulation
RLA-LOCF score below Level IV, indicating a confused and agitated state
Non-purposeful aggressive behavior and inability to cooperate with rehabilitation due to cognitive-behavioral problems
Absence of sustained primary mood disorder, formal thought disorder, or persistent hallucinations
EEG showing occasional frontal slowing without epileptiform discharges, ruling out seizure-driven behavior
Failure of multiple oral psychotropic trials (risperidone, methylphenidate, olanzapine, aripiprazole) due to inadequate response, poor tolerability, or poor adherence
Acute agitation episode requiring intramuscular haloperidol, with ongoing severe behavioral dysregulation and inability to maintain consistent oral treatment
optional_findings: One-year interval from injury to psychiatric referral
Repeated school aggression and risky behaviors at home
No benefit from traditional herbal/mineral remedies
RLA-LOCF assessment repeated in 2020 still at Level IV

provenance.title: Case report: Effective treatment of posttraumatic aggression in a child affected by TBI treated with long-acting injectable paliperidone: a case study and literature review

challenge_prompt:
A 61-year-old man presented with a several-year history of progressive neurologic decline. Initial symptoms began in late 2023 with gait instability and frequent falls, followed by worsening dysarthria, involuntary movements, and cognitive impairment characterized by memory loss, personality changes, and diminished executive function. Over approximately one year, he experienced unintentional weight loss of nearly 100 pounds. His course was complicated by chronic non-healing wounds and progressive functional decline requiring repeated hospitalizations. There was no family history of neurodegenerative disease.

Neurologic examination revealed marked gait instability, dysarthric speech, and abnormal involuntary movements. Cognitive testing demonstrated global impairment without a focal cortical pattern.

Initial neuroimaging with CT and MRI of the brain showed no acute intracranial pathology or structural abnormalities. Genetic testing for Huntington’s disease was negative. Routine laboratory evaluation revealed persistent normocytic anemia.

The patient had previously worked at a chemical and metal processing facility but had left the job; his symptoms continued to progress after cessation of that occupational exposure. Further investigation identified retained BB fragments in the right hand and right foot from remote injuries earlier in life. The BB from the hand was removed, and point-of-care testing was negative for lead. The fragment in the foot was fully embedded within bone and not amenable to removal. A retained abdominal wall wire fragment was also identified and evaluated by general surgery; its contribution to exposure had not been established, and conservative management with surveillance was recommended. The patient lived in a mid-20th-century home.

What is the most likely diagnosis and etiology, and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

Subsequent laboratory testing revealed a blood lead level of 45 μg/dL, confirming chronic lead toxicity.
gold_diagnosis: Chronic lead toxicity

answer_key.diagnosis: Chronic lead toxicity
answer_key.etiology: Occupational exposure to lead at a chemical and metal processing facility, with possible ongoing endogenous exposure from a retained lead-containing abdominal wall wire fragment.
answer_key.next_management_step: Measurement of blood lead level and erythrocyte protoporphyrin (or a heavy metal panel) to confirm lead toxicity; if elevated, initiation of chelation therapy with succimer and consideration of surgical removal of the retained foreign body.
required_findings: Progressive neurologic decline with gait instability and frequent falls
Dysarthria and involuntary movements
Cognitive impairment with memory loss, personality changes, and diminished executive function
Persistent normocytic anemia
Previous work at a chemical and metal processing facility
Retained BB fragments and an abdominal wall wire fragment (one BB tested negative for lead)
Brain CT and MRI without acute intracranial pathology or structural abnormalities
Negative genetic testing for Huntington’s disease
optional_findings: Unintentional weight loss of nearly 100 pounds
No family history of neurodegenerative disease
Global cognitive impairment without focal cortical pattern
Foot fragment not amenable to removal
Point‑of‑care test on hand BB negative for lead
Symptom progression after leaving the occupational exposure

challenge_prompt:
A 40-year-old male with a history of ADHD, anxiety, depression, erectile dysfunction, gastric bypass, cholecystectomy, and GERD presented to the hospital with a 4-day history of anxiety, confusion, restlessness, memory gaps, and severely decreased sleep (only 3 hours over 4 days). About 2 weeks prior, he had begun taking a new over-the-counter supplement for memory and focus. One week later, he self-discontinued his lisdexamfetamine 40 mg daily (prescribed for ADHD) due to abdominal pain. He also reduced his trazodone from 150 mg to 75 mg nightly at an uncertain time. He regularly consumed 2 caffeinated energy drinks daily and smoked marijuana 2-3 times per week for many years; there was no other substance use. He had never experienced similar episodes.

On arrival, he was hypertensive (154/95 mmHg), heart rate 70-102 bpm, with bilateral tremor and conjunctival injection. He was disoriented to time, with tangential, pressured speech. After initial agitation treated with lorazepam and olanzapine, his mental status gradually improved.

Laboratory workup: CK 778 IU/L, potassium 3.2 mmol/L, AST 48 IU/L, hemoglobin 12.7 g/dL. Urine drug screen was positive for amphetamines and marijuana. Complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, thyroid-stimulating hormone, Tylenol and salicylate levels, urinalysis, hepatitis panel, CT and MRI of the brain, chest X-ray, and video EEG were all normal. The patient denied any recent amphetamine use beyond his prescribed lisdexamfetamine, which he had stopped 1.5 weeks earlier; his partner corroborated this. (Trazodone is known to cause false-positive amphetamine screens.)

The patient reported the supplement contained the following: vitamin B6, L-tyrosine, L-theanine, oat straw extract, phosphatidylserine, cat's claw bark extract, L-alpha glycerylphosphorylcholine (alpha-GPC), Bacopa leaves extract, toothed clubmoss extract providing huperzine A (1%, 400 mcg), L-leucine, and pterostilbene. He had never taken it before.

The supplement was discontinued upon admission. Within 24 hours, his confusion and agitation resolved completely; lab abnormalities normalized (CK stabilized to 508 IU/L with IV fluids). By the next day, on psychiatric interview, he reported feeling “spacey” but all other symptoms had cleared. He described preceding symptoms of lightheadedness, jitteriness, clumsiness, increased energy, and muscle cramping during the episode.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Supplement-induced delirium (delirium due to dietary supplement ingestion)

answer_key.diagnosis: Supplement-induced delirium (delirium due to dietary supplement ingestion)
answer_key.etiology: The patient's multi-ingredient supplement (containing huperzine A, Bacopa, cat's claw, and other substances with cholinergic and stimulant properties) likely triggered an episode of delirium. Potential mechanisms include direct psychoactive effects, drug-herb interactions (e.g., CYP3A4 inhibition increasing trazodone levels), and additive stimulant activity.
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue the supplement definitively, provide supportive care with monitoring, and manage agitation with benzodiazepines or antipsychotics as needed. Once delirium resolves, restart his home psychiatric medications (lisdexamfetamine 40 mg daily and trazodone 75 mg nightly) as previously prescribed. Avoid re-challenge with the supplement, and counsel the patient on the risks of unregulated dietary supplements.
required_findings: Acute onset of confusion, agitation, disorientation, memory gaps, and decreased sleep for 4 days
Symptoms started approximately 2 weeks after beginning a new over-the-counter supplement
Rapid and complete resolution of delirium within 24 hours of discontinuing the supplement
Supplement ingredients include huperzine A (acetylcholinesterase inhibitor), Bacopa (cholinergic effects), cat's claw (CYP3A4 inhibitor), and other stimulant-like compounds
No prior history of manic or similar episodes; symptoms resolved faster than typical manic duration
Urine drug screen positive for amphetamines, but patient and collateral deny recent use; trazodone can cause false-positive amphetamine result
Normal brain imaging (CT/MRI) and EEG, excluding structural or primary neurological cause
Mild laboratory abnormalities (elevated CK, low potassium, mild AST) that normalized with supportive care
optional_findings: Physical exam: bilateral tremor, conjunctival injection, disoriented to time, tangential pressured speech
Regular long-term caffeine (2 energy drinks daily) and cannabis use without prior similar episodes
Self-discontinuation of lisdexamfetamine 1.5 weeks prior and dose reduction of trazodone
Mild rhabdomyolysis (CK 778 IU/L) likely from psychomotor agitation
Past surgical history of gastric bypass and cholecystectomy
History of ADHD, anxiety, depression, erectile dysfunction, GERD
Psychiatric interview after resolution revealed no psychotic or manic residual symptoms

provenance.title: A Case of Manic-Like Symptoms and Confusion After Supplement Ingestion

challenge_prompt:
A 34-year-old woman with a history of focal epilepsy since childhood, complicated by status epilepticus and chronic hyponatremia attributed to her anti-epileptic medications, presented at 25 weeks' gestation with recurrent seizures. Over the preceding weeks she had multiple seizures; on the day of presentation she had five seizures. In the emergency department she had a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure, then multiple further seizures with nystagmus, each lasting 90 seconds to 2 minutes, with return to baseline between seizures. Her mental status worsened as seizures persisted.

Her long-term anti-epileptic drug history included treatment since childhood with carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, fosphenytoin, tiagabine, zonisamide, phenobarbital, lacosamide, perampanel, and brivaracetam. At admission she was on levetiracetam and oxcarbazepine.

On physical exam during a rapid response, she did not open eyes to verbal stimuli, could not follow commands, had dysconjugate gaze, absent corneal and cough reflexes, and 0/5 strength in all extremities. Vital signs: BP 112/61 mmHg, HR 123 bpm, temperature 37.2 °C.

Admission laboratory results: sodium 154 mmol/L (reference 136–145), calcium 5.30 mg/dL (1.32 mmol/L; reference 8.50–10.50 mg/dL), normal renal function, normal glucose, and no hypomagnesemia. Preeclampsia laboratories were normal. Non-contrast head CT showed no acute intracranial pathology. Brain MRI with contrast revealed no acute findings except a new punctate enhancement in the left medial orbital gyrus of indeterminate significance and inflammatory changes in the paranasal sinuses. Lumbar puncture was negative for infection. Blood and urine cultures were negative; sputum culture grew moderate Haemophilus influenzae.

Despite intravenous magnesium, lorazepam, levetiracetam, calcium gluconate (3 g IV), and eventual intubation with propofol sedation, seizures continued. Continuous EEG showed persistent status epilepticus. Additional anti-epileptics were added (fosphenytoin, phenobarbital, ketamine, midazolam, lacosamide, perampanel, brivaracetam, topiramate) without seizure control. Serum phenytoin and phenobarbital levels were therapeutic (19.60 mg/L and 20.10 mg/L, respectively). Emergency cesarean section was performed for non-reassuring fetal tracings. Postoperatively the patient was admitted to the neuro-ICU and later developed Clostridium difficile colitis with persistent watery diarrhea.

What is the most likely underlying etiology of the refractory seizures and hypocalcemia? What is the next best step in management?

Serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D level was undetectable (<4 ng/mL).
gold_diagnosis: Anti-epileptic drug (enzyme-inducing) induced vitamin D deficiency leading to severe hypocalcemia and refractory status epilepticus

answer_key.diagnosis: Anti-epileptic drug (enzyme-inducing) induced vitamin D deficiency leading to severe hypocalcemia and refractory status epilepticus
answer_key.etiology: Long-term use of enzyme-inducing antiepileptic drugs (e.g., carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, fosphenytoin, phenobarbital) increases hepatic CYP450-mediated catabolism of vitamin D to inactive metabolites, depleting 25-hydroxyvitamin D and 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D, thereby impairing intestinal calcium absorption and causing severe hypocalcemia.
answer_key.next_management_step: Check serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D and parathyroid hormone levels to confirm vitamin D deficiency; then initiate high-dose vitamin D3 (e.g., 7000 units daily) or calcitriol and continue intravenous calcium repletion; consider switching to non-enzyme-inducing antiepileptic agents.
required_findings: Serum calcium 5.30 mg/dL (severe hypocalcemia)
No hypomagnesemia
Normal renal function
Long-term history of enzyme-inducing anti-epileptic drugs (carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, fosphenytoin, phenobarbital, etc.)
Therapeutic serum phenytoin (19.60 mg/L) and phenobarbital (20.10 mg/L) levels despite ongoing seizures
Non-contrast head CT and brain MRI with contrast showing no acute intracranial pathology
Lumbar puncture negative for infection
Normal preeclampsia laboratories
optional_findings: Hypernatremia (sodium 154 mmol/L) that normalized within 48 hours
Witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizures with nystagmus and dysconjugate gaze
New punctate enhancement in left medial orbital gyrus of indeterminate significance on MRI
Chronic hyponatremia attributed to anti-epileptic medications
Sputum culture positive for Haemophilus influenzae
Clostridium difficile colitis with persistent watery diarrhea
Later genetic finding of a variant associated with aggressive seizures

provenance.title: Refractory seizures as a manifestation of anti-epileptic drug induced hypocalcemia and vitamin D deficiency

mend_provenance.added: Serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D level was undetectable (<4 ng/mL).
missing_discriminators: Serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D level confirming deficiency

challenge_prompt:
A 5-month-old girl presented with unstable head control and inability to roll over. Gesell's developmental schedules indicated moderate developmental delay. Physical examination revealed wide-set eyes, micrognathia, microcephaly, bilateral single transverse palmar creases, generalized hypotonia, and poor responsiveness. At 9 months, she developed unprovoked seizures characterized by eye staring, limb rigidity, and unresponsiveness, occurring in clusters of 1 to 3 per day. Metabolic screening and routine biochemical tests were unremarkable. Brain MRI demonstrated congenital absence of the corpus callosum. EEG showed frequent generalized and multifocal spike waves and polyspike and slow-wave discharges.

Based on this information, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next diagnostic test to confirm it?
gold_diagnosis: Mowat-Wilson syndrome

answer_key.diagnosis: Mowat-Wilson syndrome
answer_key.etiology: ZEB2 gene mutation
answer_key.next_management_step: Genetic testing for ZEB2 (sequencing and deletion/duplication analysis)

Micrognathia
Microcephaly
Bilateral single transverse palmar creases
Moderate developmental delay
Generalized hypotonia
Brain MRI demonstrated congenital absence of the corpus callosum
EEG showed frequent generalized and multifocal spike waves and polyspike and slow-wave discharges
optional_findings: Unstable head control and inability to roll over at 5 months
Unprovoked seizures at 9 months with eye staring and limb rigidity
