challenge_prompt:
A 38-year-old male presented to the emergency department after head trauma from a sudden episode of decreased consciousness while driving. He reported recurrent attacks of headache, tongue heaviness, and mouth deviation over the preceding month. Initial noncontrast brain CT revealed a lesion involving the left anterior ethmoidal air cells, irregularities of the middle cerebral arteries, and old basal ganglia infarcts. Laboratory investigations, including tumor markers and cytoplasmic antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (C-ANCA), were unremarkable. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest and abdomen showed no evidence of distant malignancy. Subsequent MRI with contrast demonstrated an aggressively enhancing lesion centered in the left anterior ethmoidal air cells, extending into the frontal sinus, with destruction of the left medial orbital wall, involvement of the cribriform plate, minimal intracranial extension into the anterior cranial fossa, and postseptal extraconal orbital extension displacing the globe laterally. MRI also showed signs of vasculitis and acute-on-chronic ischemic insults. A biopsy via functional endoscopic sinus surgery was performed. Histopathology revealed a spindle cell mesenchymal neoplasm; immunohistochemistry was positive for CD99 and BCL2. No additional immunohistochemical or molecular studies were available. Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

Molecular analysis detected SS18 rearrangement, confirming the diagnosis of synovial sarcoma.

challenge_prompt:
A 19-year-old female presents with a 3-month history of persistent cough, 6 weeks of progressive dyspnea, and right-sided chest pain for 1 month. No fever, night sweats, autoimmune or genetic disorders; no smoking history; no relevant occupational exposure. Empirical antibiotics and painkillers gave no response. Laboratory studies show hypochromia with anisocytosis, elevated ESR, mildly elevated CRP. Sputum GeneXpert negative. Chest X-ray: inhomogeneous opacity in right upper lung zone. Contrast-enhanced CT chest: large heterogeneous mass in the right upper lobe with internal septations and solid peripheral components, invading T3-T4 vertebrae, with extension into spinal canal causing spinal cord compression; displacement of right pulmonary hilum and main bronchus; a similarly appearing mass in the medial basal segment of the left lung. MRI: large cystic mass with solid nodular peripheral enhancement, spinal canal invasion, thoracic cord compression at T3, marrow signal abnormality of involved vertebra. Bone and soft tissue windows show vertebral erosion. Core needle biopsy of the posterior mediastinal mass: gray-white to dark brown tissue fragments, histopathology shows sheets of spindle and epithelioid cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, eccentric nuclei (rhabdoid morphology), high mitotic activity and necrosis. Immunohistochemistry: tumor cells show focal positivity for Myogenin and MyoD1, diffuse positivity for Desmin; negative for SOX10, CD68, SALL4. What is the most likely diagnosis? What is the next best step in management?

challenge_prompt:
A 6-year-old female was born at 39 weeks via elective cesarean section to a 28-year-old mother and 30-year-old father, nonconsanguineous, with unremarkable pregnancy. Birth weight 3100 g, Apgar scores 8 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes. At 2 days of life, she had episodes of fixed gaze, perioral cyanosis, and transient generalized rigidity, consistent with neonatal seizures; approximately four episodes occurred in 24 hours. Neonatal EEG revealed a burst-suppression pattern. She was treated with intravenous phenobarbital, with seizure control, and discharged on oral phenobarbital. She remained seizure-free for several months; phenobarbital was tapered off at 6 months. At 8 months, she had a three-minute bilateral tonic-clonic febrile seizure during an upper respiratory infection. Between 8 months and 2 years, no afebrile seizures occurred. At 2 years and 3 months, spontaneous generalized tonic-clonic seizures emerged, initially 1-2 per month, later increasing in frequency and clustering, with multiple daily seizures and episodes of status epilepticus by 3 years. Treatment included valproate, topiramate, clobazam, and a ketogenic diet initiated at 4 years, which partially reduced seizure frequency. Currently she is on valproic acid and clobazam, with significant reduction; her last seizure was 4 months ago. Developmental milestones were delayed: head control at 6 months, independent sitting at 12 months, walking unassisted after 3 years. At 6 years, she speaks only a limited number of isolated words and cannot formulate sentences. She meets DSM-5 criteria for autism spectrum disorder (ASD), classified as Level 2 (requiring substantial support), with persistent deficits in social communication, reduced eye contact, limited symbolic play, and repetitive behaviors, not solely attributable to intellectual disability. Neuropsychological testing at age 5 revealed moderate intellectual disability (IQ approximately 50). Physical examination showed slightly enlarged auricular helices, ocular hypertelorism with epicanthal folds, a broad and low-set fontanelle, maxillary hypoplasia, a thin lower lip, short neck, fusiform fingers with absent palmar creases. Neurological exam noted strabismus, mild hypertonia in the lower limbs, diffuse hyperreflexia, preserved strength, no cerebellar signs. Ophthalmological evaluation confirmed strabismus and dyschromatopsia. Karyotype was 46,XX [20]. Cranial CT, urinary tract ultrasound, audiometry and brainstem auditory evoked potentials, and metabolic screening were normal. Laboratory tests including hepatic enzymes, ammonia, lactate, thyroid function, urea, and creatinine were normal; lipid profile showed hypercholesterolemia and hypertriglyceridemia. Brain MRI at 5 years revealed subtle abnormalities in the cerebral white matter and enlarged perivascular spaces, predominantly in bilateral frontoparietal regions. No major structural malformations or gross volumetric asymmetries. EEG at 2 years (during non-REM sleep) showed background of medium-amplitude irregular slow waves in theta-delta range, with reduced sleep elements in the first recording but more organized sleep architecture in the second. Frequent epileptiform discharges were seen over parietal and occipital regions bilaterally, characterized by medium-amplitude sharp waves, synchronous and asynchronous, sometimes in brief trains; the second recording also showed occasional right frontal sharp waves with propagation, and phase reversals at P4, O2, P3, O1, F8. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next most appropriate diagnostic step?

Genetic testing revealed a de novo heterozygous missense mutation in KCNQ3 (c.740C>T, p.Thr247Met).
gold_diagnosis: KCNQ3-related developmental and epileptic encephalopathy (KCNQ3 mutation)

answer_key.next_management_step: Genetic testing via whole-exome sequencing or an epilepsy gene panel to confirm the diagnosis

challenge_prompt:
A 78-year-old woman was admitted to the acupuncture department due to a one-month history of involuntary, dance-like movements of her right-sided limbs. Higher cortical functions, including memory, calculation, understanding, and orientation, were impaired. Muscle strength was normal (5/5) in all limbs. Bilateral Babinski and Chaddock signs were negative. She had a long-standing history of type 2 diabetes mellitus but had not been taking any medications and reported no other relevant medical history. Admission random blood glucose was 18.6 mmol/L, and glycosylated hemoglobin was 16.4%. Urine ketones were negative, and blood gas analysis showed no acid–base imbalance. Head CT revealed a high-density shadow in the right caudate and lentiform nuclei (CT value ~50 HU), raising suspicion for metabolic lesions, though cerebral hemorrhage could not be excluded. Brain MRI showed patchy short T1 signals and long T2 signals within the right caudate nucleus and putamen; FLAIR and DWI images demonstrated hypointensity, consistent with metabolic lesions. There was no evidence to support Parkinson's syndrome or familial chorea disease.

Based only on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?
gold_diagnosis: Hemichorea associated with non-ketotic hyperglycemia (diabetic striatopathy)

answer_key.diagnosis: Hemichorea associated with non-ketotic hyperglycemia (diabetic striatopathy)
answer_key.etiology: Severe non-ketotic hyperglycemia causing reversible basal ganglia dysfunction, possibly related to osmotic or metabolic insult, leading to unilateral choreiform movements.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate aggressive glycemic control with insulin therapy.

challenge_prompt:
A 23-year-old woman with a history of bipolar disorder was admitted to the inpatient psychiatric unit for acute psychosis characterized by paranoia, auditory hallucinations, and disorganized thought processes. At baseline, she is intermittently noncompliant with prescribed medications; when off medication, she typically develops command auditory hallucinations but is not somnolent or withdrawn. She had several prior psychiatric admissions and had been prescribed valproate as a mood stabilizer. She had no significant medical comorbidities, no history of substance use, and no other chronic illnesses.

On admission, risperidone was initiated at 1 mg twice daily and titrated over several days to 3 mg twice daily due to persistent psychotic symptoms. She continued valproate for mood stabilization. Subsequent laboratory testing revealed a serum valproate level of 125 µg/mL (therapeutic range: 50-100 µg/mL). As the risperidone dose was escalated, the patient developed progressive somnolence, coarse tremors, groaning, and refusal to eat, ultimately becoming bedridden. She required one-to-one observation due to an inability to perform activities of daily living; nursing staff reported that she required assistance with feeding and hygiene. Vital signs remained stable.

Laboratory evaluations, including complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, thyroid function tests, serum ammonia level, urinalysis, electroencephalography, and head CT, were unremarkable aside from the supratherapeutic valproate level. A lorazepam challenge produced no clinical improvement. There was no rigidity, hyperthermia, or autonomic instability.
gold_diagnosis: Medication-induced catatonia-like syndrome due to risperidone-valproate interaction with supratherapeutic valproate level

answer_key.diagnosis: Medication-induced catatonia-like syndrome due to risperidone-valproate interaction with supratherapeutic valproate level
aliases: Drug-induced catatonia-like presentation from combined risperidone and valproate
answer_key.etiology: Pharmacokinetic interaction: valproate inhibits glucuronidation and CYP2C9, increasing risperidone exposure and CNS depression, especially at supratherapeutic valproate levels.
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue risperidone and switch to an alternative antipsychotic with fewer pharmacokinetic interactions (e.g., olanzapine).

Lorazepam challenge produced no improvement

Vital signs stable; no autonomic instability, rigidity, or hyperthermia
Normal CBC, CMP, thyroid, ammonia, urinalysis, EEG, head CT

Baseline function: when off medications, not somnolent or withdrawn

challenge_prompt:
A 45-year-old para 2 woman, heavy smoker, with a family history of a sister diagnosed with breast cancer at age 37, presented with bloating. Pelvic ultrasonography showed bilateral complex adnexal masses. Pelvic MRI revealed bilateral suspicious ovarian masses measuring 12 × 8 cm on the right and 12 × 6 cm on the left. CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed no evidence of distant metastases or peritoneal carcinomatosis. CA-125 level was 3997 U/mL. A diagnostic laparoscopy confirmed the presence of a peritoneal nodule in the right iliac fossa and was consistent with high-grade serous epithelial ovarian cancer. The patient underwent primary debulking surgery including hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, pelvic peritonectomy, ileo-cecal resection en bloc, infragastric omentectomy, and para-aortic and pelvic lymphadenectomy. Complete cytoreduction was achieved. Histopathological examination of the surgical specimen revealed high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma infiltrating the meso-colon, meso-appendix, and uterine serosa; two of 70 para-aortic lymph nodes were positive, corresponding to FIGO stage IIIA.

On postoperative day 6, the patient developed headache and focal neurological deficits including paresthesia, weakness, and clonic movements of the left limbs. Brain MRI with gadolinium showed two ring-enhancing lesions: a 17-mm lesion in the right fronto-parietal lobe and a 6-mm lesion in the right parietal lobe, both associated with significant surrounding vasogenic edema.

Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis for the brain lesions, and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic or therapeutic step?

aliases: Cerebral metastases from epithelial ovarian cancer
answer_key.etiology: Hematogenous dissemination of high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (with underlying germline BRCA1 mutation as a predisposing factor)
answer_key.next_management_step: Stereotactic biopsy for histologic confirmation, followed by stereotactic radiosurgery/radiotherapy and systemic chemotherapy with carboplatin, paclitaxel, and bevacizumab, then maintenance PARP inhibitor (olaparib).

challenge_prompt:
We present a female infant born at 39 weeks to a non-consanguineous couple. Pregnancy and delivery were unremarkable. Apgar scores were 9 at 1 and 5 minutes. Birth weight was 3500 g (93rd percentile), with microcephaly noted at birth. Family history was negative for epilepsy or neurological disorders. At 1 month of age, she developed tonic seizures with cyanosis and stereotyped abnormal movements, leading to initiation of sodium valproate. At 3 months, clusters of epileptic spasms appeared multiple times per day, characterized by brief bilateral flexor contractions, especially during awakening and sleep transitions. Video-EEG captured an epileptic spasm and showed fragmented hypsarrhythmia during sleep. Despite treatment with vigabatrin and corticosteroids for infantile spasms, her course was unfavorable with subsequent generalized tonic-clonic seizures, prompting addition of topiramate. Neurological examination at 5 months revealed severe developmental impairment: axial hypotonia, absent head control, poor visual tracking, absence of social smiling, convergent strabismus. Manual stereotypies (repetitive hand movements) were observed. Deep tendon reflexes were preserved and no focal deficits were noted. Feeding difficulties were present. Routine laboratory investigations were normal. Metabolic workup including serum lactate, ammonia, plasma amino acids, and urinary organic acids showed no abnormalities. Brain MRI revealed bilateral and symmetrical hyperintensity of the basal ganglia (particularly thalamus and putamen) and fronto-temporo-parietal cortico-subcortical atrophy. The initial diagnostic hypothesis was a mitochondrial encephalopathy (Leigh syndrome) given the MRI findings, but metabolic testing did not support this.

Based on the presentation and workup findings, what is the most likely underlying etiology or genetic diagnosis, and what is the appropriate next diagnostic step?

Genetic testing revealed a de novo heterozygous pathogenic variant in the CDKL5 gene (c.1196C>T, p.Thr399Met).
gold_diagnosis: CDKL5 deficiency disorder (CDKL5-related developmental and epileptic encephalopathy with infantile spasms phenotype)

challenge_prompt:
A 41-year-old female with a history of polysubstance use (nicotine, buprenorphine/naloxone, methamphetamine, amphetamine/dextroamphetamine), alcohol use disorder, bipolar disorder, ADHD, iron-deficiency anemia, and prior thoracic epidural abscess was found unresponsive in her bathroom after ingesting six pregabalin tablets of unknown strength with approximately 7–8 shots of vodka. She had used intranasal buprenorphine/naloxone two days prior. On arrival, she was febrile (101°F) and reported profound quadriparesis and neck pain. Neurological exam demonstrated near-complete distal paralysis of all four extremities, preserved cranial nerves, intact sensation above the neck, diminished deep tendon reflexes, and inability to lift arms against gravity.

Urine toxicology: positive for amphetamine, methamphetamine, buprenorphine; negative for methadone, cannabis, opiates, oxycodone, PCP, benzodiazepines, tricyclic antidepressants. Initial labs: CPK 16,093 U/L, AST 349 U/L, ALT 158 U/L, Na 129 mmol/L (mild hyponatremia, normalized during hospitalization). CSF: glucose 69 mg/dL, protein 50 mg/dL, 3 WBCs, negative oligoclonal bands. EEG showed no seizure activity. Transthoracic echocardiogram normal. CT head and CT cervical/thoracic/lumbar spine without acute abnormalities. Chest X-ray normal. MRI brain with and without contrast normal. MRI cervical spine without contrast: longitudinal T2-hyperintense lesion extending from C2 to T3 with mild cord expansion and extensive posterior paraspinal muscle edema involving the multifidus and semispinalis muscles. Infectious/autoimmune workup: HIV negative, hepatitis serologies negative, ANA negative.

What is the most likely diagnosis/etiology, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Cervical myelopathy secondary to deep neck muscle rhabdomyolysis (venous hypertensive myelopathy) due to polysubstance abuse

answer_key.diagnosis: Cervical myelopathy secondary to deep neck muscle rhabdomyolysis (venous hypertensive myelopathy) due to polysubstance abuse
aliases: Venous hypertensive myelopathy from deep neck muscle rhabdomyolysis
Cervical myelopathy associated with paraspinal rhabdomyolysis
answer_key.etiology: Polysubstance abuse (pregabalin, methamphetamine, buprenorphine, alcohol) leading to severe rhabdomyolysis and compartment syndrome of deep cervical muscles, causing venous outflow obstruction and spinal cord edema.
answer_key.next_management_step: Aggressive intravenous hydration to prevent renal injury, supportive care (monitor renal function, electrolytes), and consider corticosteroids; monitor for compartment syndrome and neurological status.

MRI cervical spine without contrast: longitudinal T2-hyperintense lesion C2–T3 with mild cord expansion and extensive posterior paraspinal muscle edema involving multifidus and semispinalis

History of polysubstance use and found unresponsive after ingesting pregabalin and alcohol

challenge_prompt:
A 38-year-old right-handed woman with a high school education and 10 years of marriage presented with complaints of involuntary rhythmic movements of her head and body, especially noticeable when semi-standing. She also reported sudden finger jerks when bringing them together or separating them, a sensation of internal shock in her head, and sudden jerks during sleep. Over the past year, she had believed she had Parkinson's disease.

Family history: Her uncle and aunt were initially diagnosed with Parkinson's disease in their 30s-40s, later reclassified as Huntington's disease. She described normal childhood development, eldest of nine children. The patient and family reported that she meets diagnostic criteria for an anxiety disorder.

Psychiatric history: Multiple significant losses—her father and later her 17-year-old brother. During the first 6 years of marriage, childlessness worsened her anxiety. After her first child, she experienced postpartum depression with anhedonia. In her second pregnancy, she developed severe obsessive-compulsive symptoms; at 6 months pregnant, a CT angiography for suspected pulmonary embolism was negative, but she was told she had a panic attack. Reading about CT risks to the fetus worsened her anxiety, and after the birth, she had persistent obsessive thoughts about the baby’s health. She was assessed to have obsessive thoughts, anxiety, and dependent personality traits, with marital difficulties and pathological grief.

On physical and neurological examination, all findings were normal except for a postural tremor, which appeared voluntarily exaggerated. She demonstrated occasional chorea-like movements, mainly in the left thumb, but these increased with anxiety and stress, decreased with distraction, and lacked motor perseveration. No choreiform movements were elicited. She was highly suggestible; after learning that a relative had died from Huntington’s disease, she searched online and became convinced she might develop chorea. Comprehensive history confirmed no use of psychiatric medications, illicit drugs, or other substances.

The evaluating clinicians noted that she did not seem to be seeking attention or tangible gain, and her distress appeared genuine. There was no evidence of secondary gain, making malingering unlikely.

What is the most likely diagnosis? What is the next appropriate step in management?

Genetic testing for Huntington's disease was negative.
gold_diagnosis: Illness Anxiety Disorder (IAD, formerly hypochondriasis)

answer_key.diagnosis: Illness Anxiety Disorder (IAD, formerly hypochondriasis)
aliases: Somatic symptom disorder with predominant health anxiety
answer_key.etiology: Psychiatric disorder characterized by excessive preoccupation with having or developing a serious illness, triggered by vicarious experiences (relative's HD diagnosis) and cognitive distortions, in the absence of objective neurological disease.
answer_key.next_management_step: Psychoeducation about illness anxiety disorder, reassurance, avoid unnecessary neurological investigations, and initiate treatment with an SSRI and possibly a beta-blocker; arrange psychiatric follow-up.
required_findings: Postural tremor, which appeared voluntarily exaggerated.
Chorea-like movements that increased with anxiety and stress, decreased with distraction, and lacked motor perseveration.
All physical and neurological examinations normal except for the postural tremor.
Family history of Huntington's disease only in an uncle and aunt (not first-degree relatives).
Prior belief of Parkinson's disease after relative's diagnosis changed to Huntington's.
History of anxiety, obsessive-compulsive symptoms, postpartum depression, and pathological grief.
No history of psychiatric medication, illicit drug use, or substance use.
No evidence of secondary gain or attention-seeking; distress appeared genuine.
optional_findings: Highly suggestible; searched online about Huntington's disease.
CT angiography for suspected PE was negative, with panic attack diagnosed.
Sensation of internal shock in head and sudden jerks during sleep.
Sudden jerks in fingers when bringing them together or separating them.

provenance.title: Illness Anxiety Disorder Manifesting as Huntington's Disease: A Rare Case Report

mend_provenance.added: Genetic testing for Huntington's disease was negative.
missing_discriminators: Negative genetic test for Huntington's disease (or other organic causes)

challenge_prompt: A 32-year-old previously healthy male from Sweden developed bilateral lower limb numbness and weakness 30 minutes after surfacing from a recreational dive at Unawatuna, Galle, Sri Lanka. He had completed 18 self-reported diving sessions over the previous week, including two dives the day before the incident. The patient did not have adequate surface intervals between dives. On the day of the incident, he completed a 26 m dive lasting 25 minutes, diving with an instructor and using a wrist-mounted diving computer. Notably, he had used different dive computers from various centers during the week. His ascent to surface was guided by the diving computer. At the diving center, oxygen was administered via face mask, with only slight symptomatic improvement without resolution of neurological deficits. He was transferred to the hospital, arriving approximately 8 hours after the diving session. Upon admission, he was conscious and rational with stable vital signs and normal oxygen saturation. He denied pain but reported bilateral lower limb weakness and numbness. Neurological examination revealed increased tone bilaterally in the lower limbs, exaggerated knee and ankle reflexes, mild lower limb weakness, and a sensory level at T10 with impaired pain and touch sensations. There was no spinal tenderness. Bowel and bladder functions were fully preserved with no urinary retention or incontinence. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next appropriate step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Type II Decompression Sickness (DCS)

answer_key.diagnosis: Type II Decompression Sickness (DCS)
answer_key.etiology: Nitrogen gas bubble formation from rapid ascent after repetitive diving with inadequate surface intervals and loss of cumulative dive history due to use of different dive computers, leading to spinal cord involvement.
answer_key.next_management_step: Arrange immediate transfer for hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBOT) using US Navy Treatment Table 6 or equivalent recompression protocol.
required_findings: History of repetitive diving (18 sessions in one week) with inadequate surface intervals
Use of different dive computers resulting in loss of cumulative dive history
Onset of bilateral lower limb numbness and weakness 30 minutes after surfacing from a 26 m dive
Neurological examination: increased tone bilaterally in lower limbs
Neurological examination: exaggerated knee and ankle reflexes
Sensory level at T10 with impaired pain and touch sensations
Normal oxygen saturation and no respiratory distress
Slight improvement with oxygen but no resolution of major neurological deficits

provenance.title: Managing Decompression Sickness in a Low‐Resource Setting, a Clarion Call to Improve Systems: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 60-year-old female with a history of high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (initially diagnosed stage IVB in 2016) presents with new-onset headaches. She underwent cytoreductive surgery and adjuvant chemotherapy (paclitaxel/carboplatin) in 2016, followed by treatment for retroperitoneal lymph node recurrence in 2017 (liposomal doxorubicin/oxaliplatin). In October 2018, she developed headaches. Cranial MRI revealed a right parieto-occipital mass with perilesional edema, suggestive of either a metastatic lesion or a primary glioblastoma. What is the most likely diagnosis?

Biopsy of the brain mass revealed high-grade serous carcinoma consistent with ovarian origin.
gold_diagnosis: Ovarian cancer brain metastasis

answer_key.next_management_step: Neurosurgical resection for histopathological confirmation and treatment (craniotomy) or stereotactic biopsy if resection not feasible

challenge_prompt:
An octogenarian Japanese-American woman presented with approximately 10 years of progressive cognitive and functional decline. Over the past 5 years, her verbal output had become predominantly monosyllabic, and she exhibited severe reduction in spontaneous interaction, chronic urinary incontinence, executive dysfunction, impaired mobility, dysphagia, and marked dependence in all activities of daily living. Baseline examination revealed marked hypofunction, flat affect, reduced spontaneous verbal output, prolonged somnolence, impaired executive behavior, dependent ambulation, dysphagia, urinary incontinence, and severely reduced social reciprocity and emotional expressiveness. During a single supervised session, she was administered 5 grams of psilocybin-containing mushrooms (Enigma strain). The acute phase was characterized by clinically suspected hyperthermia, profuse sweating, profound somnolence, and a prolonged deep sleep-like state. Approximately 19 hours after administration, she spontaneously initiated an autobiographical conversation lasting several hours. Over the following weeks, she experienced restoration of urinary continence, improved mobility, enhanced emotional reciprocity, increased spontaneous communication, and improved contextual social interaction. At one month post-administration, she remained continent and functionally improved compared with her baseline functional state.

The diagnosis of advanced Alzheimer’s disease had been established clinically approximately 10 years prior to intervention.
gold_diagnosis: Advanced Alzheimer's disease

answer_key.diagnosis: Advanced Alzheimer's disease
answer_key.etiology: Alzheimer's disease pathology (accumulation of amyloid-beta plaques and tau neurofibrillary tangles, clinically diagnosed)
answer_key.next_management_step: Continue supportive care and monitor for disease progression; ensure caregiver support and safety; no specific disease-modifying therapy is currently indicated outside of research settings.

Predominantly monosyllabic verbal output over the past 5 years
Chronic urinary incontinence for at least 5 years
Executive dysfunction
Impaired mobility and dependent ambulation
Dysphagia
Marked dependence in activities of daily living
Octogenarian age
optional_findings: Severely reduced social reciprocity and emotional expressiveness
Clinically suspected hyperthermia, profuse sweating, deep sleep-like state after ingestion
Spontaneous autobiographical conversation 19 hours after administration
Restoration of continence and improved mobility/communication following ingestion

missing_discriminators: Explicit statement of Alzheimer's disease diagnosis or biomarker evidence

challenge_prompt:
A 69-year-old woman with a history of total thyroidectomy for benign nodules in 1992, grade III hypertension, dyslipidemia, and obesity presented with a two-month history of intermittent headache and tinnitus. She denied head trauma. In March 2025, she developed conjunctival hyperemia and progressive bilateral exophthalmos, more pronounced in the right eye. Neurologic and ophthalmologic evaluations at that time did not reveal a definitive etiology. Endocrinology workup in April 2025 showed normal TSH and free T4, and negative thyroid autoantibodies (anti-TPO and TRAb). Cardiology evaluation confirmed grade III hypertension, dyslipidemia, and obesity; carotid Doppler ultrasonography revealed no significant abnormalities. In early May 2025, a neurological re-evaluation documented a left sixth cranial nerve palsy with abduction deficit. Brain MRI demonstrated a small meningioma of the right greater wing of the sphenoid, punctate demyelinating lesions consistent with a microangiopathic process, and normal-appearing orbits, internal and external carotid arteries, optic nerves, tracts, and chiasm. Time-of-flight (TOF) sequences showed no abnormalities of major arteriovenous structures. In late May 2025, she presented to the ophthalmology emergency unit with worsening ocular pain and diplopia. On examination: best-corrected visual acuity was 0.9 right eye and 1.0 left eye; intraocular pressure was 25 mmHg right and 26 mmHg left; ocular motility was full in the right eye but showed marked abduction deficit in the left eye. Anterior segment examination revealed bilateral episcleral venous congestion, diffuse conjunctival chemosis, and marked bilateral proptosis with orbital fat prolapse. Fundus examination and optic nerve optical coherence tomography excluded optic neuropathy. Topical brinzolamide-timolol was initiated for elevated intraocular pressure. Based on the available data, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next appropriate diagnostic step?

Diagnostic digital subtraction angiography (DSA) revealed a bilateral, low-flow, indirect (Barrow type D) carotid‑cavernous fistula.
gold_diagnosis: Bilateral indirect carotid-cavernous fistula (Barrow type D)

answer_key.diagnosis: Bilateral indirect carotid-cavernous fistula (Barrow type D)
answer_key.etiology: Low-flow arteriovenous connections from dural branches of both internal and external carotid arteries into the cavernous sinus
answer_key.next_management_step: Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) to confirm fistula and delineate angioarchitecture

Normal carotid Doppler ultrasonography
Progressive bilateral exophthalmos without pain or trauma history
History of headache and tinnitus

Normal posterior segment and optic nerve OCT
Visual acuity 0.9 right eye, 1.0 left eye
History of thyroidectomy for benign nodules
Worsening ocular pain and diplopia in late May

challenge_prompt:
An 86-year-old Asian woman with a 2-year history of Alzheimer's disease (baseline MMSE score 23) presented with acute right-sided weakness upon waking, accompanied by drowsiness, drooling, and impaired swallowing. On examination, vital signs were stable; GCS was E4V3M6. She had right facial weakness, absent gag reflex, right hemiparesis, wet voice, increased drooling. Oral motor examination showed symmetrical but impaired motor function of lips, tongue, soft palate, mandible, and dentition. Delayed and infrequent voluntary swallow initiation was noted. Voluntary cough, throat clearing, and gag reflex were absent; only reflexive coughing was observed. Swallowing was effortful with occasional choking on saliva. MMSE score was 2, indicating severe cognitive impairment. Brunnstrom stage was III in the right limbs. Laboratory tests were normal. Emergent brain MRI revealed acute infarctions in the left frontal and parietal watershed zones and the left putamen. The patient was diagnosed with acute ischemic stroke causing right hemiplegia, post-stroke cognitive impairment, and post-stroke dysphagia. NIHSS score was 21. A nasogastric tube was placed. Conventional rehabilitation (30-min sessions, 3 times per week) including swallowing therapy (thermal-tactile stimulation, oral motor exercises) and speech therapy was initiated.

After three months of standardized therapy, motor function improved: Brunnstrom stage V in the right limbs, GCS E4V5M6. However, swallowing function remained poor, with a Functional Oral Intake Scale (FOIS) score of 1. The patient still had severe cognitive impairment. A videofluoroscopic swallowing study (VFSS) demonstrated prolonged oral transit time and severely impaired bolus formation and control across all tested volumes and consistencies. No pharyngeal swallow was triggered; therefore, all pharyngeal-phase parameters were scored as maximally impaired, yielding a Functional Dysphagia Scale (FDS) score of 95 (oral-phase predominant).

Based on the information provided, what is the most likely etiology of this patient's persistent dysphagia, and what is the most appropriate next step in management to address it?
gold_diagnosis: Post-stroke oral-phase dysphagia secondary to cognitive impairment

answer_key.diagnosis: Post-stroke oral-phase dysphagia secondary to cognitive impairment
aliases: Oral-phase dysphagia due to cognitive dysfunction
answer_key.etiology: Left frontal/parietal/putamen infarcts causing severe cognitive impairment disrupting voluntary oral phase control
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) targeting the bilateral dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) for cognitive enhancement, using a protocol of high-frequency (10 Hz) stimulation at 50% of resting motor threshold, 3000 pulses per session.

acute right-sided weakness upon waking, accompanied by drowsiness, drooling, and impaired swallowing
MRI revealed acute infarctions in the left frontal and parietal watershed zones and the left putamen
After three months ... swallowing function remained poor, with a Functional Oral Intake Scale (FOIS) score of 1
VFSS demonstrated prolonged oral transit time and severely impaired bolus formation and control ... No pharyngeal swallow was triggered
MMSE score was 2, indicating severe cognitive impairment
Delayed and infrequent voluntary swallow initiation ... voluntary cough, throat clearing, and gag reflex were absent
Conventional rehabilitation ... was initiated ... swallowing function remained poor
optional_findings: effortful swallowing with occasional choking on saliva
symmetrical but impaired motor function of lips, tongue, soft palate

provenance.title: Case Report: Improvement in Swallowing Function Following DLPFC-Targeted Repetitive Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation in a Post-Stroke Patient with Cognitive Impairment

challenge_prompt:
An 18-year-old male was admitted with intermittent episodic limb convulsions accompanied by severe abdominal pain persisting for over 3 years, with recurrence of severe abdominal pain for 5 days before admission. The patient's symptoms initially manifested more than 3 years ago as episodic limb convulsions without identifiable triggers, accompanied by loss of consciousness, intense epigastric abdominal pain, profuse diaphoresis; each episode lasted approximately 5 minutes and resolved spontaneously, followed by postictal weakness without hemiparesis, anxiety, chest pain, or other neurological deficits. He was diagnosed with epilepsy at a local hospital and received oral sodium valproate, along with analgesic and gastroprotective agents (diclofenac sodium, omeprazole, sucralfate, resorcinol), achieving only modest symptomatic relief. Over the following years, recurrent seizures and severe abdominal pain continued. The abdominal pain occurred in two patterns: (1) temporally associated with seizures; (2) isolated episodes of sudden severe epigastric pain without accompanying epileptic features. Episode duration was 5–30 minutes for both patterns, with the isolated pain pattern being predominant. Over the past year, the frequency of both seizures and severe abdominal pain increased, with abdominal pain becoming particularly frequent. Multiple antiepileptic regimens were tried (levetiracetam 1.0 g every 12 hours, lacosamide 100 mg twice daily, phenytoin sodium 100 mg twice daily, clonazepam 0.25 mg twice daily) together with analgesics; seizure frequency decreased, but severe abdominal pain persisted during episodes without significant improvement.

Five days prior to admission, recurrent severe upper abdominal pain of unclear origin developed. During this hospitalization, episodes were predominantly isolated abdominal pain, with only one episode occurring in temporal association with a seizure. Pain intensity reached 9 on a numeric rating scale, episodes were accompanied by profuse diaphoresis and significant functional impairment requiring bed rest. Antispasmodic and analgesic therapy with resorcinol proved ineffective; intramuscular pethidine hydrochloride resulted in partial pain relief. Continuous intravenous midazolam plus oral levetiracetam and lacosamide led to a reduction in seizure frequency, but both seizures and severe abdominal pain continued to recur intermittently during hospitalization.

Past medical history: hepatic insufficiency of unknown cause not systematically evaluated or treated; a thyroid nodule was identified 1 year ago but not further investigated. No history of toxic exposure, parasitic infection, or relevant environmental factors. Both parents in good health, no known family history of hereditary or genetic disorders.

Neurological examination: body temperature 36.6°C, heart rate 75 bpm, respiratory rate 19/min, blood pressure 101/80 mmHg, oxygen saturation 94%. The patient was in poor general condition with altered mental status; bowel and bladder function normal. Higher cortical functions and cranial nerves intact. Sensation intact bilaterally, muscle strength grade 5 in all four limbs with normal tone. Deep tendon reflexes normoactive, coordination tests normal. Bilateral Kernig's sign positive, accompanied by nuchal rigidity. Abdominal examination: flat, symmetrical, no guarding, no tenderness, no rebound tenderness, no palpable masses; Murphy's sign and McBurney's point tenderness negative.

Auxiliary investigations:
- Complete blood count: normal.
- Biochemical analysis: hepatic dysfunction (alanine aminotransferase 324 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 158 U/L), hyperglycemia, impaired coagulation parameters.
- Thyroid function and electrolytes: normal.
- Serologies for HIV, Treponema pallidum, hepatitis C virus, hepatitis B virus antigen/antibody profiles: negative.
- Tumor markers: within normal limits.
- Chest and abdominal CT (non-contrast and contrast-enhanced): hepatic steatosis and multiple small pulmonary nodules; no evidence of underlying malignancy.
- Gastrointestinal endoscopy with histopathological evaluation: chronic colitis, without features of ulcerative colitis, Crohn’s disease, ulcerations, or gastrointestinal neoplasms.
- Brain MRI: no significant abnormalities.
- 48-hour video electroencephalography (EEG) monitoring: captured two typical episodes of isolated abdominal pain without motor manifestations; no ictal epileptiform discharges or evolution were recorded; overall findings unremarkable.

Given the clinical course, autoimmune encephalitis was suspected. Serum and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) were sent for autoimmune encephalitis-related antibody testing and further analysis. CSF results: white blood cell count 4/μL, protein level 36 mg/dL, negative oligoclonal bands. Antibody panel results are pending.

Based on the presentation, what is the most likely specific diagnosis, and what diagnostic test should be ordered next to confirm it?

Serum and CSF tested positive for anti-DPPX antibodies.
gold_diagnosis: Anti-dipeptidyl-peptidase-like protein 6 (DPPX) antibody-associated autoimmune encephalitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Anti-dipeptidyl-peptidase-like protein 6 (DPPX) antibody-associated autoimmune encephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Autoantibodies against DPPX (a regulatory subunit of Kv4.2 potassium channels), leading to neuronal hyperexcitability
answer_key.next_management_step: Order serum anti-DPPX antibody testing (with consideration of CSF testing if serum is negative or equivocal). Once confirmed, initiate immunotherapy with high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone and intravenous immunoglobulin.
required_findings: Recurrent episodes of severe epigastric abdominal pain occurring both in temporal association with seizures and as isolated episodes without epileptic features
48-hour video EEG captured isolated abdominal pain episodes with no ictal epileptiform discharges
Normal brain MRI
CSF analysis showing WBC 4/μL, protein 36 mg/dL, and negative oligoclonal bands
Gastrointestinal endoscopy with histopathology revealing only chronic colitis, without evidence of inflammatory bowel disease or malignancy
Positive bilateral Kernig's sign and nuchal rigidity on neurological examination
History of recurrent limb convulsions with multiple antiepileptic drugs, only partial seizure control
Hepatic dysfunction (ALT 324 U/L, AST 158 U/L) and hyperglycemia
optional_findings: Hepatic steatosis and multiple small pulmonary nodules on CT
Negative infectious disease serologies and tumor markers
Poor general condition and altered mental status
Absence of abdominal tenderness or peritoneal signs despite severe pain
Partial pain relief with pethidine but not with antispasmodics
No weight loss or diarrhea reported

provenance.title: Severe Abdominal Pain as a Prominent Clinical Manifestation of Anti‐DPPX Autoimmune Encephalitis: A Case Report and Systematic Review

mend_provenance.added: Serum and CSF tested positive for anti-DPPX antibodies.
missing_discriminators: Anti-DPPX antibody serology result

challenge_prompt:
A 61-year-old male with a history of gastric sleeve surgery, major depressive disorder, and PTSD presented with persistent neurological symptoms 4 months after an episode of serotonin syndrome. Four months prior, he was treated for a UTI with linezolid while continuing fluoxetine 40 mg and buspirone 30 mg. Within 48 hours, he developed acute confusion, diaphoresis, tremor, hyperreflexia, clonus, agitation, hypertonia, and hyperthermia, meeting Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria. All serotonergic agents were discontinued, and the acute autonomic instability resolved within days, but he did not return to his neurological baseline.

Over the following months, he experienced a range of episodic symptoms: right-sided facial droop, gait disturbance, episodes resembling seizures, brain zaps, internal tremors, severe anxiety, panic attacks, intrusive thoughts, depression, tinnitus, and complex parasomnias (sleepwalking). The facial droop was documented on examination; it fluctuated in severity across visits, was not always present, and did not follow a fixed upper or lower motor neuron pattern. Notably, the asymmetry diminished when the patient was distracted and intensified during emotional distress and close observation.

Investigations:
- Brain MRI: no acute intracranial pathology, restricted diffusion, or structural lesions.
- Lumbar puncture was not performed; the treating team deemed it unnecessary given the clinical trajectory and absence of progressive encephalopathic features.
- Porphyria screen: urine analysis showed elevated coproporphyrin I, heptacarboxyl, pentacarboxyl, and uroporphyrins. Genetic testing for acute intermittent porphyria was negative.
- Liver function tests: mildly elevated transaminases; albumin low-normal. Renal function (BUN, creatinine) and inflammatory markers (CRP, CBC) normal.
- Pharmacogenomic panel: SLC6A4 homozygous short promoter polymorphism (reduced serotonin transporter expression), MTHFR heterozygous C677T, ADRA2A homozygous C allele.
- A trial of hemin infusions yielded only subjective, inconsistent improvement.

The patient was started on vilazodone 5-10 mg with caution but developed severe depression (PHQ-9 25, GAD-7 21), electric shock sensations, and parasomnias.
gold_diagnosis: functional neurological disorder with psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (FND with PNES)

answer_key.diagnosis: functional neurological disorder with psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (FND with PNES)
answer_key.etiology: triggered by acute physiological stress from serotonin syndrome in a patient with pre-existing psychiatric comorbidities and altered post-bariatric pharmacokinetics
answer_key.next_management_step: refer to a multidisciplinary FND clinic for integrated physical, occupational, and speech therapy; cautious pharmacological management of psychiatric symptoms
required_findings: fluctuating right-sided facial droop that diminishes with distraction and intensifies with emotional distress, inconsistent with a fixed neurological lesion
normal brain MRI without structural or acute pathology
elevated urinary porphyrins with negative genetic testing for acute porphyria
subjective and inconsistent response to hemin infusions
absence of progressive encephalopathy or focal neurological deficits on examination
severe psychiatric symptoms (PHQ-9 25, GAD-7 21) and multiple somatic complaints
history of acute serotonin syndrome as potential physical trigger
development of parasomnias and electric shock sensations with vilazodone rechallenge, suggesting functional overlay
optional_findings: history of gastric sleeve surgery altering drug pharmacokinetics
mildly elevated liver transaminases and low-normal albumin
symptoms began after acute medical event
gait disturbance without fixed neurological pattern
episodes resembling seizures without EEG evidence

challenge_prompt: A 49-year-old right-handed man with no history of trauma presented to the emergency room with left pronator drift, left hemisensory neglect, and left homonymous hemianopia. MRI of the brain revealed small, punctate infarcts in the right middle cerebral artery (MCA) distribution. MRA showed minimal stenosis of the distal right cervical internal carotid artery (ICA), suggestive of dissection. He was started on warfarin and discharged. Over the following 3 months, he experienced several transient ischemic attacks and progressive weakness of the left hand. Repeat brain MRI demonstrated new restricted diffusion in the right MCA territory. Repeat vascular imaging demonstrated new vessel wall irregularity involving the cavernous and supraclinoid segments of the right ICA, with an approximately 50% stenosis of the high cervical segment confirmed on digital subtraction angiography. Given the progression despite therapeutic anticoagulation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next management step?
gold_diagnosis: Secondary intracranial extension of spontaneous cervical internal carotid artery dissection

answer_key.diagnosis: Secondary intracranial extension of spontaneous cervical internal carotid artery dissection

Failed medical management of spontaneous cervical artery dissection with intracranial progression

answer_key.next_management_step: Endovascular stenting of the right ICA dissection using overlapping stents under dual antiplatelet therapy

challenge_prompt:
A 47-year-old Caucasian female presented with a 6-day history of persistent throbbing frontal headache, photophobia exacerbated by bright lights and loud noises, low-grade fever (37.6°C), a single episode of nausea and vomiting on the day of admission, dizziness for 1 day described as feeling of losing balance, intermittent ringing sensation in both ears, and fatigue for the past 2 days. She denied sick contacts, recent travel, confusion, memory loss, hearing or vision loss, personality changes, tremors, or seizures. She reported no rash prior to admission. Past medical history: hypertension.

On admission, vital signs: blood pressure 161/79 mmHg, otherwise normal. Physical exam: alert but in mild distress due to pain and photophobia. Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal, round, reactive to light; extraocular movements intact; no nystagmus. Tympanic membranes normal. Painful vesicular rash observed at the right T4–T6 dermatome region near the right breast. Cranial nerves II–XII intact. Rinne test: air conduction > bone conduction; Weber test: equal lateralization. Motor and sensory exam normal (no deficits to light touch, pain, position sense, vibration; muscle strength 5/5 all four extremities; no tremors). Reflexes: biceps, knee tendon, and Babinski sign normal. Nuchal rigidity present; Kernig and Brudzinski signs negative.

Initial laboratory work-up unremarkable. Lumbar puncture performed. CSF: colorless, clear; WBC 648/μL (92% lymphocytes); RBC 47/μL; protein 396 mg/dL; glucose 41 mg/dL; sodium 151 mmol/L. Concurrent serum glucose: 56 mg/dL. CSF bacterial antigens (group B streptococcus, Haemophilus influenzae, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis) negative. HIV screening negative. CSF coccidioides antibody negative. CSF HSV PCR negative. CSF West Nile virus IgM and IgG negative.

Based on the above, what is the most likely diagnosis? What is the next best diagnostic step?

CSF VZV PCR was positive.
gold_diagnosis: Varicella-zoster virus (VZV) meningitis

answer_key.etiology: Varicella-zoster virus reactivation with meningeal involvement
answer_key.next_management_step: Send CSF for VZV PCR

challenge_prompt: A 45-year-old right-handed male insurance maker with 12 years of education and unremarkable medical history presented with somnolence and disorientation with regard to time and location, without lateralizing signs at the neurological examination. Two weeks after admittance he improved in orientation. MRI was performed one day after admission and revealed an infarction in the left paramedian thalamus (dorsomedial nucleus). Based on the presentation and imaging findings, what is the most likely diagnosis? What is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Left paramedian thalamic ischemic stroke (lacunar infarct)

answer_key.etiology: Small vessel occlusion, likely due to lipohyalinosis or microatheroma; other causes to be excluded given young age and no risk factors.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate antiplatelet therapy (e.g., aspirin) and evaluate stroke etiology with vascular risk factor assessment, echocardiography, cardiac rhythm monitoring, and imaging of cervical/cerebral arteries.

challenge_prompt:
A 51-year-old man with genetically confirmed early Huntington's disease (HD) presented with stereotyped paroxysmal episodes. His mother and two brothers had HD. He was diagnosed after developing involuntary movements and difficulty with complex tasks, with positive HTT genetic testing. Shortly after diagnosis, he informed his employer and took early retirement on medical grounds, which he found highly stressful. A few months later, he began having episodes of repetitive, purposeless complex movements and unresponsiveness. The episodes occurred about once weekly, lasted approximately 5 minutes, and had no obvious triggers or prodrome. He would stare at an object for 30–60 seconds, then exhibit repetitive, purposeless complex hand and upper limb movements while unresponsive to verbal stimuli. The movements then waned, and he became disoriented to place and person with emotional lability and fatigue for about 10 minutes, before full recovery. His wife videotaped an episode. He had no prior psychiatric history or trauma. Between episodes, neurological examination showed signs consistent with HD without unexpected focal deficits. Routine and sleep-deprived electroencephalograms (EEGs) and brain MRI were normal.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next management step?
gold_diagnosis: Probable psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (functional/dissociative seizures)

answer_key.diagnosis: Probable psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (functional/dissociative seizures)
answer_key.etiology: Functional neurological symptom disorder (conversion) triggered by the major stress of an early Huntington's disease diagnosis in a patient with underlying neuropsychiatric vulnerability
answer_key.next_management_step: Explain the non-epileptic nature of the attacks to the patient and carers, avoid antiepileptic drugs, and refer for psychiatric and psychological support (e.g., psychotherapy, pharmacological treatment of mood/anxiety)

Highly stressful life event (early retirement) shortly before symptom onset
Episodes of repetitive, purposeless complex movements with unresponsiveness lasting ~5 minutes
Episodes begin with staring at an object, then complex limb movements, followed by disorientation and emotional lability
No triggers or prodrome
Normal routine and sleep-deprived EEGs
Normal brain MRI
Neurological examination between episodes consistent with HD but no focal signs
optional_findings: Family history of HD in mother and two brothers
Absence of prior mental illness or trauma
Onset a few months after HD diagnosis
Video recording of an episode available
No epileptiform activity on interictal EEG

provenance.title: Psychogenic non-epileptic seizures in early Huntington's disease

challenge_prompt:
A 71-year-old woman, nonsmoker, presented with 3 months of progressive slowness of thought, excessive daytime sleepiness, generalized weakness, and sialorrhoea. One month after onset, she developed severe constipation, and 15 days before admission, urinary retention. Symptoms acutely worsened after a colonoscopy. Neurological examination revealed hypomimia, bradyphrenia, severe bradykinesia, micrographia, bilateral limb rigidity (left > right), mild rest tremor, and reduced arm swing while walking, consistent with an extrapyramidal syndrome. No vertical gaze palsy or recurrent falls. MMSE was normal. After admission, she developed intestinal pseudo-obstruction refractory to laxatives, acute urinary retention requiring bladder catheterization, and orthostatic hypotension (blood pressure 125/80 mmHg supine, 95/70 mmHg after 3 minutes standing). 

Chest X-ray showed mediastinal widening; contrast-enhanced CT chest revealed a lobulated mediastinal mass suggestive of thymoma/thymic carcinoma versus germ cell tumor or lymphoma. Abdomen CT and total body PET scan showed no systemic dissemination. Brain MRI with contrast did not reveal neoplastic involvement or abnormal signal in the putamen, middle cerebellar peduncles, pons, or cerebellum. CSF analysis was normal. Routine blood tests, autoimmune screening (including antinuclear antibodies), peripheral blood smear, and oncomarkers were negative. Thyroid studies showed mild hypothyroidism with highly positive anti-thyroid antibodies, but EEG was normal and clinical presentation did not meet criteria for Hashimoto's encephalopathy. 

Given the subacute presentation and temporal association, paraneoplastic antibody testing was performed. Serum and CSF tested positive for anti-Hu antibodies by indirect immunofluorescence on monkey cerebellum commercial sections (Euroimmun®) and confirmed by immunoblotting (Ravo Diagnostika®). Positive immunofluorescence was also observed on human myenteric plexus commercial sections. Testing for anti-Ri, Yo, Ma2, CV2/CRMP5, LGI1, CaspR2, NMDAR, and GAD antibodies was negative in both serum and CSF. 

Transthoracic needle biopsy of the mediastinal mass confirmed thymoma. Repetitive motor nerve stimulation and anti-neuromuscular junction antibodies (AChR, MuSK, voltage-gated calcium channel) were negative. 

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next appropriate management step?
gold_diagnosis: Paraneoplastic dysautonomia associated with anti-Hu antibodies and thymoma (with co-existing extrapyramidal syndrome of uncertain relation)

answer_key.diagnosis: Paraneoplastic dysautonomia associated with anti-Hu antibodies and thymoma (with co-existing extrapyramidal syndrome of uncertain relation)
aliases: Paraneoplastic autonomic failure due to thymoma
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune reaction targeting Hu antigens, triggered by thymoma, leading to autonomic dysfunction
answer_key.next_management_step: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) therapy and surgical resection of the thymoma
required_findings: Serum and CSF tested positive for anti-Hu antibodies
Transthoracic needle biopsy of the mediastinal mass confirmed thymoma
Intestinal pseudo-obstruction refractory to laxatives
Acute urinary retention requiring bladder catheterization
Orthostatic hypotension (blood pressure 125/80 mmHg supine, 95/70 mmHg after 3 minutes standing)
Testing for anti-Ri, Yo, Ma2, CV2/CRMP5, LGI1, CaspR2, NMDAR, and GAD antibodies was negative in both serum and CSF
Brain MRI with contrast did not reveal neoplastic involvement or abnormal signal in the putamen, middle cerebellar peduncles, pons, or cerebellum
Repetitive motor nerve stimulation and anti-neuromuscular junction antibodies (AChR, MuSK, voltage-gated calcium channel) were negative
optional_findings: Progressive slowness of thought and motor impairment over 3 months
No vertical gaze palsy or recurrent falls
Mild hypothyroidism with highly positive anti-thyroid antibodies
Negative autoimmune screening (including ANA) and oncomarkers
Normal chest X-ray except for mediastinal widening
Abdomen CT and PET scan showed no systemic dissemination

challenge_prompt:
A 55-year-old woman with a history of chronic paranoid–hallucinatory schizophrenia (onset age 30, with delusions of sin, auditory and visual hallucinations, thought broadcasting, negative symptoms, cognitive impairment, and poor response to multiple antipsychotics and valproate augmentation) and intermittent alcohol and benzodiazepine abuse presents for evaluation of long‑standing neurological issues. At age 20, she first experienced easy fatigability and transient numbness of the left arm and foot. She twice developed transient right foot dorsi‑flexor paresis following mechanical pressure to the legs. At ages 32 and 35, she had transient left brachial plexus palsies after minor pressure. Her most recent neurological examination at age 55 showed: discrete right foot dorsi‑flexor paresis (MRC grade 4), absent Achilles tendon reflexes bilaterally, mild pallhypesthesia worse on the right (5/8 right vs 6/8 left), and an ataxic, unsteady gait. She reports that her older brother had transient numbness of a hand and her father had shoulder weakness, but neither underwent diagnostic testing. Laboratory workup including serum, CSF, and brain MRI showed no evidence of an immunological cause. Electrophysiological studies revealed a sensorimotor, demyelinating polyneuropathy, accentuated in the legs. Neurosonography demonstrated bilateral enlargement of the median and ulnar nerves at typical entrapment sites. Brain MRI showed generalized atrophy. Magnetic resonance spectroscopy showed relatively high glutamate concentrations in the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) and pregenual anterior cingulate cortex (pACC), and relatively low myo‑inositol in the pACC. Neuropsychological testing revealed considerable deficits in working memory and mild psychomotor slowing. EEG showed rare intermittent slowing.

Based only on the neurological presentation and available data, what is the most likely diagnosis for her recurrent transient focal neuropathies, and what genetic test would confirm the underlying etiology?
gold_diagnosis: Hereditary neuropathy with liability to pressure palsies (HNPP)

answer_key.diagnosis: Hereditary neuropathy with liability to pressure palsies (HNPP)
answer_key.etiology: Heterozygous deletion of the peripheral myelin protein 22 (PMP22) gene on chromosome 17p12
answer_key.next_management_step: Genetic testing for PMP22 deletion (e.g., MLPA or chromosomal microarray)

transient right foot dorsi-flexor paresis following mechanical pressure
transient left brachial plexus palsies after minor pressure
absent Achilles tendon reflexes bilaterally
sensorimotor, demyelinating polyneuropathy, accentuated in the legs
bilateral enlargement of the median and ulnar nerves at typical entrapment sites
older brother had transient numbness of a hand and her father had shoulder weakness

mild pallhypesthesia worse on the right (5/8 right vs 6/8 left)

challenge_prompt:
A 34-year-old male presents with a 4-year history of progressive involuntary movements. He reports difficulty with fine movements of his upper limbs and slowness of movement (bradykinesia), predominantly affecting the right side. He also describes involuntary movements of his mouth, including opening and closing, and intermittent snorting. He has gradually developed lower limb weakness. Over the past two years, similar symptoms have appeared in his left limbs, and he now experiences difficulty swallowing and coughing when drinking water. Laboratory investigations show a red blood cell count of 5.25 × 10^12/L. A peripheral blood smear reveals some red blood cells of variable size with spiked cell membranes consistent with acanthocytes. Brain MRI demonstrates lateral ventricle enlargement and atrophy of the putamen and caudate nuclei. The patient has not received any pharmacological treatment.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
gold_diagnosis: Chorea-acanthocytosis (neuroacanthocytosis)

challenge_prompt:
A 37-year-old man with a history of schizoid personality disorder and previous brief psychotic episodes was admitted to the psychiatric department for a rapid onset of psychomotor agitation associated with delusional ideation, confusion, aggressive behavior, and mood elevation with dysphoria. These symptoms apparently started 5 days before, rapidly worsening during the 12 h before admission. The patient was on stable treatment with aripiprazole 10 mg daily, and 2 weeks before the described episode the dose of aripiprazole was reduced to 7.5 mg daily. Combined antipsychotic and benzodiazepine treatment was immediately started with poor effect, making physical restraint necessary.

During the first days the patient developed physical alterations such as high blood pressure, tachycardia, elevation of body temperature (BT) and significant elevation of creatine phosphokinase (CPK). Levels of ammonia, antinuclear antibodies (ANA), extractable nuclear antigens (ENA), anti-DNA autoantibodies, C3, C4, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and procalcitonin did not reveal any alteration. C-Reactive Protein (CRP) was elevated, reaching 50 mg/L. Blood culture and urine culture were negative.

On day 3, a brain CT was normal. MRI of the brain revealed an ovoid area of hyperintensity on T2-weighted and FLAIR images in the central part of the splenium of the corpus callosum. On diffusion weighted images the lesion showed high intensity of the signal with a low ADC value in comparison to the other components of the corpus callosum. After intravenous injection of paramagnetic contrast agent, no enhancement was detected.

Based on the presentation above, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?
gold_diagnosis: Mild encephalitis with reversible splenial lesion (MERS) associated with delirious mania

answer_key.diagnosis: Mild encephalitis with reversible splenial lesion (MERS) associated with delirious mania
answer_key.etiology: Unknown; possibly triggered by infection or adverse drug reaction, but no specific cause identified.
answer_key.next_management_step: Transfer to ICU for deep sedation (e.g., propofol/midazolam) and management of autonomic instability, given severity and risk of organ failure.

High blood pressure, tachycardia, fever, and significant CPK elevation.
CRP 50 mg/L; normal ammonia, ANA, ENA, anti-DNA, C3, C4, ESR, procalcitonin.
Negative blood and urine cultures.
Normal brain CT.
MRI: ovoid hyperintense lesion in central splenium on T2/FLAIR.
MRI: lesion with high DWI signal and low ADC (restricted diffusion).
MRI: no contrast enhancement.
optional_findings: History of schizoid personality disorder and brief psychotic episodes.
Poor response to combined antipsychotics and benzodiazepines.
Rapid symptom worsening over 12 hours before admission.

challenge_prompt:
A 73-year-old right-handed man presented with dizziness and was noted to have auditory-verbal hallucinations and paranoid delusions. He reported hearing imperative and commentating voices of two witches, which he believed controlled him and caused his dizziness. Physical examination revealed a right-dominated hand tremor. The patient had a history of a similar episode at age 66, when he experienced paranoid delusions, auditory-verbal and tactile hallucinations, and assaulted his daughter-in-law (whom he misjudged as a witch). That episode remitted after treatment with olanzapine (15 mg/d); risperidone was not tolerated due to extrapyramidal side effects. Antipsychotics were discontinued after discharge, and he remained without psychiatric follow-up or medication until the current presentation. He had no history of mental disorders before age 66. He was born in 1940, immigrated from the former Soviet Union to Germany at age 56, has 10 years of education, and worked as a truck driver and later as a school caretaker until retirement at 65.

Comprehensive evaluation included:
- Structural brain MRI: moderate subcortical arteriosclerotic encephalopathy.
- Cerebrospinal fluid analysis: moderately elevated tau protein; NIA-AA biomarker profile A− (amyloid negative), T+ (tau positive), N+ (neurodegeneration positive).
- 18F-FDG-brain-PET: reduced metabolism in the mesial temporal regions.
- Neurocognitive testing (CERAD battery): impairments in short-term verbal memory, figurative memory, attention, and psychomotor speed, but the patient remained independent in activities of daily living.

A diagnosis of a mild neurocognitive disorder was suggested in addition to the primary psychiatric condition. Treatment with olanzapine (up to 20 mg/d) led to remission of the delusions and dizziness, but auditory-verbal hallucinations persisted partially. After 12 weeks without full remission, the patient was re-admitted for consideration of an add-on treatment.

What is the most likely primary diagnosis, and what should be the next management step?
gold_diagnosis: Very-late-onset schizophrenia-like psychosis (VLOSLP)

answer_key.diagnosis: Very-late-onset schizophrenia-like psychosis (VLOSLP)
answer_key.etiology: Likely multifactorial: neurovascular (subcortical arteriosclerotic encephalopathy) and neurodegenerative (Alzheimer's disease pathologic change indicated by A− T+ N+ CSF profile) contributions, possibly with accelerated brain aging.
answer_key.next_management_step: Add-on inhibitory theta-burst stimulation (TBS) over the left temporoparietal junction (TP3 area) to augment antipsychotic pharmacotherapy.
required_findings: onset of psychosis after age 60 (first episode at 66, recurrence at 73)
no premorbid psychiatric history
prominent auditory-verbal hallucinations (imperative, commentating voices) and paranoid delusions
structural brain MRI showing moderate subcortical arteriosclerotic encephalopathy
CSF analysis with moderately elevated tau and NIA-AA profile A− T+ N+
18F-FDG-PET showing mesial temporal hypometabolism
neurocognitive deficits (impaired memory, attention, psychomotor speed) with preserved independence in daily functioning
partial response of psychotic symptoms to olanzapine (delusions and dizziness remitted, AVH persisted)
optional_findings: risperidone not tolerated due to EPS

provenance.title: Theta-Burst Stimulation for Auditory-Verbal Hallucination in Very-Late-Onset Schizophrenia-Like Psychosis—A Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging Case Study

challenge_prompt:
A 39-year-old female with no prior psychiatric history presented with a 21-month history of progressive neuropsychiatric symptoms. Before onset, she had worked as a business controller. She developed severe catatonia, delusions, cognitive deficits, one generalized epileptic seizure, and states of altered consciousness. She also developed amnesia for the entire acute phase and was unable to recall any dreams.

On examination, she exhibited catatonic features and fluctuating level of consciousness.

Initial investigations included:
- Electroencephalography (EEG) showing diffuse slowing; independent component analysis identified three components with EEG slowing.
- Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis revealed pleocytosis initially.
- [18F]-fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography (FDG-PET) demonstrated left hemispheric hypometabolism.
- Magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS) revealed a hypoglutamatergic state in the left prefrontal cortex.
- Brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed enlargement of the lateral ventricles, predominantly in the posterior horns, and slight striatal and insular atrophy; there was no contrast enhancement or diffusion restriction.
- Extensive workup for infectious etiologies, including herpes simplex virus, was negative.
- Gynecological screening for teratoma was negative.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next best diagnostic step to confirm the etiology?
gold_diagnosis: Anti-N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor (anti-NMDA-R) encephalitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Anti-N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor (anti-NMDA-R) encephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Autoantibodies against the GluN1 subunit of the NMDA receptor
answer_key.next_management_step: Test for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies (IgG anti-GluN1) in serum and cerebrospinal fluid.

Generalized epileptic seizure
Altered consciousness

CSF pleocytosis
FDG-PET left hemispheric hypometabolism
MRS hypoglutamatergic state in left prefrontal cortex
Negative infectious workup and negative gynecological teratoma screening

challenge_prompt:
A 55-year-old man with a history of severe open traumatic brain injury (TBI) sustained 14 years ago during a combat attack, resulting in deep penetrating injury along the midsagittal plane involving frontal, parietal, primary motor, premotor, somatosensory areas, anterior cingulate, corpus callosum, and surrounding white matter. He emerged from a prolonged coma and underwent intensive rehabilitation, but despite continuing daily physical therapy, he experienced a plateau in motor and cognitive recovery. He also has ongoing post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) with night terrors and hypervigilance.

In an effort to overcome the plateau, he was enrolled in a study combining physical therapy (PT) with translingual neurostimulation (TLNS) using the Portable Neuromodulation Stimulator (PoNS®), which delivers sequenced non-invasive electrical stimulation to the tongue via the trigeminal and facial nerves. A 1-year PT-only baseline period was established, followed by a 14-week PT + TLNS treatment phase.

Brain vital signs were assessed using a portable 32-channel EEG system, measuring event-related potentials (ERPs) in response to an auditory stimulus sequence targeting three cognitive domains: N100 (auditory sensation), P300 (basic attention), and N400 (cognitive processing). Data were analyzed using a non-parametric percentile-bootstrapping approach comparing treatment time points to pooled baseline data, with p-values adjusted for multiple comparisons.

Results showed no significant ERP changes during the PT-only baseline. However, by the second treatment assessment (T5), significant increases from baseline were observed in:
- N100 amplitude: Central  p = 0.0196 (adjusted padj = 0.0297); Right Temporal p = 0.0162 (padj = 0.0297)
- P300 amplitude: Central p = 0.0042 (padj = 0.0252); Right Temporal p = 0.0086 (padj = 0.0258)
- N400 amplitude: Right Temporal p = 0.0062 (padj = 0.0374)
No significant changes were observed in ERP latencies or in the Left Temporal electrode group.

Additionally, the patient reported a considerable reduction in PTSD symptoms during and after the treatment, specifically a decrease in night terror frequency and intensity.

What is the most likely mechanism underlying the cognitive improvements observed during PT + TLNS therapy?
gold_diagnosis: Enhanced neuroplasticity induced by combined physical therapy and translingual neurostimulation

answer_key.diagnosis: Enhanced neuroplasticity induced by combined physical therapy and translingual neurostimulation
answer_key.etiology: Translingual neurostimulation activates trigeminal and facial nerves, leading to bottom-up modulation of brainstem and cerebellar pathways, which augments global neuroplasticity; when paired with physical therapy, this enhances cognitive recovery.
answer_key.next_management_step: Continue combined PT + TLNS with ongoing monitoring of brain vital signs, and add quantitative PTSD measures.
required_findings: history of severe open TBI with deep penetrating injury along midsagittal plane
plateau in motor and cognitive recovery despite intensive PT
addition of TLNS to PT using PoNS® device
significant increases in N100 amplitude during treatment
significant increases in P300 amplitude during treatment
significant increases in N400 amplitude during treatment
no significant changes in ERP latencies
reduction in PTSD symptoms
optional_findings: injury affected primary motor, premotor, somatosensory, anterior cingulate, corpus callosum
PTSD with night terrors and hypervigilance
right temporal and central electrode group changes
reported considerable reduction in PTSD symptoms

provenance.title: Brain Vital Signs Detect Cognitive Improvements During Combined Physical Therapy and Neuromodulation in Rehabilitation From Severe Traumatic Brain Injury: A Case Report

challenge_prompt: A 50-year-old male with well-controlled hypertension presented with a 2-week history of cough, fever, and fatigue. Nasopharyngeal swab RT-PCR was positive for SARS-CoV-2, and a CT chest showed bilateral ground-glass opacities consistent with COVID-19 pneumonia. Admission laboratory tests revealed elevated D-dimer and C-reactive protein (CRP). He was started on prophylactic-dose low molecular weight heparin (LMWH). One week post-admission, he became drowsy, developed a severe headache, right-sided hemiplegia, and right-sided hemihypesthesia. A non-contrast CT head demonstrated an intracerebral hemorrhage in the left parietal-occipital region with intraventricular extension. CT angiogram of intracranial vessels was normal. Coagulation tests (INR, APTT, platelet count, fibrinogen) were normal. What is the most likely etiology of his intracerebral hemorrhage, and what is the next best step in management?
gold_diagnosis: COVID-19-associated intracerebral hemorrhage

answer_key.diagnosis: COVID-19-associated intracerebral hemorrhage
aliases: Spontaneous ICH in the setting of COVID-19
answer_key.etiology: Multifactorial: hypertension, severe COVID-19 infection causing endothelial dysfunction and a hypercoagulable state
answer_key.next_management_step: Neurosurgical consultation for possible hematoma evacuation, aggressive blood pressure control, and consideration of anticoagulation reversal.

challenge_prompt: A 68-year-old woman with a history of blepharospasm, oromandibular and segmental dystonia underwent bilateral globus pallidus internus (GPi) deep brain stimulation (DBS). In the acute recovery period, she reported "forgetting how to type on the computer," despite prior proficiency and no dystonia in her arms. She could only "hunt and peck" for keys. This isolated typing dysfunction persisted over 2 years of follow-up. Neurological exam in the DBS ON state showed no motor weakness, apraxia, aphasia, or visuospatial neglect. Visuospatial testing (Trail Making B, cube copy, clock drawing) was normal. She performed three 1-minute online typing tests: (1) DBS ON – 0.6 words per minute (WPM); (2) 10 minutes after DBS OFF – 2.6 WPM; (3) 20 minutes after DBS OFF – 3.8 WPM. She felt typing improved when DBS was off but not to pre-DBS baseline. Testing was limited by the return of her dystonia symptoms. DBS settings at the time: left GPi 1-C+3.5V, 150µs, 120Hz; right GPi 9-C+2.5V, 150µs, 120Hz. Post-operative lead location analysis using Lead-DBS and tractography estimated volume of tissue activation involving the right angular gyrus, left calcarine fissure, left cuneus, bilateral supplementary motor areas, and superior parietal gyri, with probable involvement of the superior longitudinal fasciculus. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely cause of her typing dysfunction, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: GPi DBS-induced dystypia (keyboard typing dysfunction)

answer_key.diagnosis: GPi DBS-induced dystypia (keyboard typing dysfunction)
answer_key.etiology: Stimulation-induced side effect of bilateral GPi DBS, likely involving modulation of the superior longitudinal fasciculus and connected cortical regions.
answer_key.next_management_step: Consider DBS reprogramming to balance dystonia control and typing function, or obtain formal neuropsychological testing to characterize the deficit.

Normal visuospatial testing (Trail Making B, cube copy, clock drawing).
Typing speed improves from 0.6 WPM ON to 2.6 WPM at 10 min OFF and 3.8 WPM at 20 min OFF.
Other motor tasks unaffected.
Return of dystonia symptoms when DBS off limits testing duration.
Persistent typing dysfunction over 2 years with unchanged DBS settings.
optional_findings: DTI tractography shows VTA involvement of right angular gyrus, left calcarine fissure, left cuneus, bilateral SMA, superior parietal gyri.
Patient had prior proficient typing occupation.
Absence of visuospatial neglect or aphasia on formal testing.
Improvement but not resolution off DBS.
