challenge_prompt:
A 38-year-old man with no significant past medical history presented after a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. Recovery revealed a 10-day history of generalized headache. He denied weakness, numbness, speech or swallowing difficulties. On arrival, blood pressure was 170/101 mm Hg, otherwise hemodynamically stable. Neurological examination showed no cranial nerve or cerebellar deficits, no focal deficits, and normal symmetrical reflexes. General physical exam was unremarkable.

CT brain was normal. He was admitted to neurology intensive care. MRI brain revealed a right cerebellar small acute ischemic infarct. MR angiography of the brain and neck showed filling defects causing focal narrowing at the origins of both internal carotid arteries. Routine EEG showed no epileptiform activity.

He was started on aspirin, atorvastatin, and carbamazepine. A stroke-in-young workup was initiated. Laboratory studies revealed thrombocytopenia (50,000/μL). Transthoracic echocardiography showed a pedunculated, lobular left atrial mass measuring approximately 1 × 1.5 cm, freely mobile across the mitral valve. Transesophageal echocardiography confirmed the mass; the leading differential was atrial myxoma. Cardiothoracic surgery was consulted and planned for mass resection.

Thrombophilia and connective tissue disease tests returned. Anti-cardiolipin IgG was positive in high titer at >120 U/mL. Anti-β2-glycoprotein I IgG was 90 U/mL. Lupus anticoagulant studies: LA1 screen 109.30 sec, aPTT LA 153.8 sec, hexagonal phase neutralizing timing 25 sec, LA1 mixing time 88 sec, LA2 confirmation timing 38.46 sec (LA1/LA2 ratio 2.84). The dRVVT screen and aPTT were prolonged; mixing studies remained prolonged, consistent with an inhibitor. Hexagonal phase and dRVVT ratio were elevated, consistent with phospholipid dependence, indicating a strong lupus anticoagulant. Anti-nuclear antibody was negative, and ESR and CRP were within normal limits.

Based on the available data, what is the most likely unifying diagnosis, and what is the next best step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Primary antiphospholipid syndrome

answer_key.diagnosis: Primary antiphospholipid syndrome
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune disorder characterized by persistent antiphospholipid antibodies leading to arterial and/or venous thrombosis.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate anticoagulation with warfarin (bridged with heparin) and plan repeat antiphospholipid antibody testing in 12 weeks to confirm persistence.
required_findings: Young patient (38 years) with ischemic stroke (MRI showing cerebellar infarct)
Thrombocytopenia (50,000/μL)
Left atrial mobile mass on echocardiography
Prolonged aPTT and dRVVT with mixing study showing inhibitor, phospholipid dependence
Elevated anti-cardiolipin IgG (>120 U/mL)
Elevated anti-β2-glycoprotein I IgG (90 U/mL)
Negative ANA, normal ESR and CRP
optional_findings: MRA showing bilateral internal carotid artery narrowing

provenance.title: Atypical Presentation of Anti-Phospholipid Antibody Syndrome with Seizure and Atrial Mass

challenge_prompt:
A 61-year-old woman with 9 years of education and a history of working as a shop assistant presented with recurrent depressive episodes starting in 2011, followed by cognitive decline in 2012. Neuropsychological testing over 5 years showed severe, globally impaired deficits (memory, visuoconstruction, executive function, language, attention, psychomotor speed) that remained stable until a decline in 2020. She met general criteria for dementia. Since 2015, she has experienced acoustic, optic, and tactile hallucinations and delusional parasitosis causing skin scratching. Brain imaging: IBZM-SPECT (2015), amyloid PET (2016), DaT scan (2015), and FDG-PET (2019) were all normal. MRI brain was normal; MRI spine showed mild cervical degeneration and thoracic spinal canal stenosis without cord compression or edema. She underwent thoracic laminectomy in 2016, but limb weakness continued to progress.

Neurologic examination: pseudobulbar palsy, distal muscle weakness more in legs than arms, slightly increased muscle tone, hyperreflexia with bilateral Babinski signs, no fasciculations. Speech and language: initially mildly slurred, then progressive dysarthria, apraxia of speech, impaired comprehension of syntactically complex sentences, spared single-word comprehension and object knowledge, and dysphagia with 15-kg weight loss. Behavioral assessment: apathy and lack of empathy. Electromyography: no active denervation (fibrillations, positive sharp waves), but mild chronic denervation (increased motor unit potential amplitudes, reduced recruitment). Nerve conduction studies: pure axonal motor neuropathy of right peroneal and tibial nerves, reduced sensory amplitude of right sural nerve, no demyelination. Transcranial magnetic stimulation: prolonged central conduction time to the left leg. Tongue ultrasound: no fasciculations.

CSF analysis: no evidence of inflammation/paraneoplastic process; beta-amyloid, tau, phospho-tau within normal limits; neurofilament heavy chain markedly elevated at 1137 pg/mL.

Targeted next-generation sequencing panel for neurodegenerative disorders revealed a heterozygous variant in FIG4: c.2467C>T (p.Gln823Ter/p.Q823*). This variant is listed as pathogenic for autosomal recessive Charcot-Marie-Tooth type 4J in HGMD, and as likely pathogenic/pathogenic/VUS in ClinVar. It is present in heterozygous state in 1/121394 alleles in ExAC and 7/282818 alleles in gnomAD. No family history of neurological disease was reported, though information is incomplete.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Frontotemporal dementia (non-fluent variant primary progressive aphasia) with probable primary lateral sclerosis, associated with a heterozygous FIG4 variant of unknown significance (c.2467C>T, p.Q823*)

answer_key.diagnosis: Frontotemporal dementia (non-fluent variant primary progressive aphasia) with probable primary lateral sclerosis, associated with a heterozygous FIG4 variant of unknown significance (c.2467C>T, p.Q823*)
answer_key.etiology: Likely related to heterozygous loss-of-function variant in FIG4 gene (c.2467C>T, p.Q823*), though the variant is of uncertain significance and its pathogenicity in the heterozygous state is not confirmed.
answer_key.next_management_step: Multidisciplinary care (neurology, psychiatry, speech-language pathology, physical therapy, nutritional support); genetic counseling regarding the FIG4 variant with discussion of uncertain significance and potential implications for family members; periodic reassessment of clinical status and variant interpretation.
required_findings: severe, globally impaired deficits across cognitive domains stable for 5 years
apraxia of speech, impaired comprehension of syntactically complex sentences, spared single-word comprehension and object knowledge
pseudobulbar palsy, distal muscle weakness, hyperreflexia with bilateral Babinski signs, no fasciculations
EMG: no active denervation, mild chronic denervation
CSF neurofilament heavy chain 1137 pg/mL (elevated) with normal beta-amyloid, tau, phospho-tau
normal amyloid PET, DaT scan, FDG-PET, and MRI brain
heterozygous FIG4 variant c.2467C>T (p.Q823*)
progressive upper motor neuron signs without sensory loss or active denervation over >3 years
optional_findings: apathy and lack of empathy on behavioral assessment
axonal motor neuropathy on NCS with reduced sural amplitude
prolonged central conduction time to leg on TMS
weight loss of 15 kg due to dysphagia
thoracic laminectomy did not halt limb weakness progression
variant present in population databases at low frequency

provenance.title: Case Report: Association of a Variant of Unknown Significance in the FIG4 Gene With Frontotemporal Dementia and Slowly Progressing Motoneuron Disease: A Case Report Depicting Common Challenges in Clinical and Genetic Diagnostics of Rare Neuropsychiatric and Neurologic Disorders

challenge_prompt:
A 52-year-old patient with a 14-year history of Parkinson's disease (PD) underwent bilateral subthalamic nucleus (STN) deep brain stimulation (DBS) for treatment of motor fluctuations (severe rigidity and bradykinesia off, disabling dyskinesias on) despite optimized medications. The patient had longstanding baseline anxiety but no history of mood disorder or psychiatric treatment. During awake surgery, microelectrode mapping defined STN borders and sensorimotor territory. The left STN lead was implanted first. Intraoperative test stimulation (130 Hz, 90 μs) on the left using contacts 2−/4+ elicited paresthesia at 4.0 mA, and tongue contractions at 6 mA with 4−/2+. For the right STN, test stimulation elicited left face and chest paresthesia at an unacceptably low threshold of 2.0 mA, suggesting proximity to medial lemniscal fibers. Therefore, the right lead was repositioned 2 mm anteriorly, after which transient paresthesia occurred at 5.0 mA. Shortly after programming the second (right) side, the patient became hypomanic and severely anxious, requiring an urgent clinic visit. DBS settings were adjusted by reducing amplitude bilaterally and switching the right lead to a more dorsal contact. However, the patient developed severe depression, anxiety, and frequent crying. Four months after initial implantation, she attempted suicide with a pain medication overdose and recovered without medical treatment. DBS was turned off bilaterally, and she was briefly hospitalized for psychiatric care; her mood significantly improved. The patient returned for formal assessment of stimulation effects on mood. With DBS off, she was euthymic. She was blinded to stimulation state for subsequent evaluations. At 2.5 hours after morning medications, the right DBS lead alone was activated to the settings used at the time of the suicide attempt (left lead remained off). Within 2 minutes of stimulation onset, she reported feeling 'a profound sadness, hopelessness, despair, and loss of trust… I don’t think I can make it.' At 3 minutes she started crying, and her spouse noted a noticeable change. At 4 minutes the negative mood peaked. DBS was then turned off without informing the patient; immediately she said 'I feel hopeful. The room just became brighter,' and within 6 minutes her mood returned to baseline. When the left lead was activated alone, no mood changes occurred. Motor outcomes at 16 weeks post-implantation (with stimulation ON at settings limited by mood side effects) showed: left body (contralateral to right STN) UPDRS III subscore improved from 13 (off medication) to 9 (31% improvement). Total UPDRS III improved from 48 to 31 (35% improvement). Based only on this presentation, what is the most likely etiology of the patient’s neuropsychiatric symptoms and suboptimal motor improvement, and what next step in management is most appropriate?

Post-operative 7T MRI and computational modeling confirmed that the right DBS lead contacts were located in the associative and limbic territories of the STN.
gold_diagnosis: Stimulation of associative and limbic subthalamic nucleus territories due to anterior placement of the right DBS lead

answer_key.diagnosis: Stimulation of associative and limbic subthalamic nucleus territories due to anterior placement of the right DBS lead
answer_key.etiology: Anterior placement of the right STN DBS lead causing activation of associative and limbic pathways instead of the sensorimotor territory
answer_key.next_management_step: Surgical revision of the right DBS lead to the dorsolateral sensorimotor portion of the STN
required_findings: Right STN lead repositioned 2 mm anteriorly intraoperatively due to low paresthesia threshold (2.0 mA)
Onset of hypomania, severe anxiety, depression, and crying shortly after right lead programming
Suicide attempt 4 months after initial implantation
Mood symptoms completely reversed when DBS turned off bilaterally
During formal testing, right STN stimulation rapidly induced profound sadness and crying within 2 minutes, peaking at 4 minutes, and resolved immediately upon cessation
Left STN stimulation alone did not reproduce mood symptoms
Motor improvement at 16 weeks was only 31% on the left body (right STN) despite stimulation
Baseline history of anxiety but no prior mood disorder or psychiatric treatment
optional_findings: Left STN test stimulation paresthesia at 4.0 mA and tongue contractions at 6 mA
Intraoperative microelectrode mapping defined sensorimotor territory of STN
Transient paresthesia at 5.0 mA after right lead repositioned anteriorly
DBS programming adjustments attempted before formal assessment did not resolve mood symptoms
UPDRS III total score improved from 48 to 31 (35% improvement)

challenge_prompt:
A 43-year-old female was admitted for a lung infection. During hospitalization, she experienced recurrent seizures. Electroencephalogram (EEG) showed frequent abnormal epileptiform discharges in the right temporal region and a small amount in the left. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain revealed slightly increased T2 and T2 fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) signal in the bilateral hippocampi, suggestive of hippocampal neuronal damage. Serum creatine kinase, l-lactate dehydrogenase, and alpha-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase levels were all within normal limits. Further history revealed that the patient had experienced mild non-coordinated movements of the jaw and upper limbs for the past seven months. Family history was notable for consanguineous marriage of the parents and a brother who also developed similar non-coordinated jaw and upper limb movements at age 37. No other family members had similar symptoms.

As part of the diagnostic workup, whole-exome sequencing was performed on the proband. After rigorous filtering, six homozygous variants remained. These are listed below with their associated gene and known clinical associations based on the literature:

1. VPS13A: nonsense mutation NM_033305.2:c.8282C>G (p.S2761X)
2. NDUFAF7: missense mutation NM_144736.5:c.581T>A – associated with pathologic myopia
3. DSP: frameshift insertion NM_004415.4:c.1_2insC – associated with cardiomyopathy
4. GDPD4: missense mutation – associated with obesity
5. RPUSD1: missense mutation – associated with methylome changes at birth and adolescence
6. LMF2: missense mutation – limited studies suggest a role in digestion of dietary lipids

Which of these mutations is most likely to be the cause of the patient's neurological condition, and what is the corresponding diagnosis?
gold_diagnosis: Chorea-acanthocytosis (ChAc) due to homozygous nonsense mutation in VPS13A

answer_key.diagnosis: Chorea-acanthocytosis (ChAc) due to homozygous nonsense mutation in VPS13A
answer_key.etiology: Autosomal recessive mutation in VPS13A, leading to chorea-acanthocytosis
answer_key.next_management_step: Refer to neurology for management of seizures and movement disorder; genetic counseling for family; consider peripheral blood smear for acanthocytes to support diagnosis.

Choreiform movements of jaw and upper limbs
Consanguinity and brother similarly affected
Normal serum muscle enzymes (creatine kinase, LDH, alpha-HBDH)
Homozygous nonsense VPS13A mutation (c.8282C>G, p.S2761X)
NDUFAF7 mutation associated with pathologic myopia
DSP mutation associated with cardiomyopathy
Remaining gene variants (GDPD4, RPUSD1, LMF2) not linked to neurological disease
optional_findings: EEG showed left temporal discharges as well
MRI hippocampal hyperintensity suggestive of neuronal damage
Movement disorder duration of seven months

challenge_prompt:
A 56-year-old female inpatient with a 1-year history of difficult-to-treat late-onset depression presented with a severe major depressive episode with psychotic symptoms (ICD-10 F32.3). She was anhedonic, indecisive, with feelings of hopelessness, worthlessness, excessive guilt, and diminished concentration. MMSE 28/30, DemTect 15/18. She had psychomotor slowing, loss of interest, delusions, hallucinations. MADRS 46, CGI-S 6. No suicidal ideation. Comorbid nicotine dependence and psoriasis. Previous treatments: mirtazapine 45 mg, venlafaxine 375 mg, vortioxetine 10 mg, aripiprazole 20 mg, S-ketamine IV, and 11 bilateral ECTs with maintenance ECT failed to improve symptoms.

Due to massive anhedonia, progressive hypomimia, general slowness, delusions, and hallucinations, degenerative parkinsonism was suspected. No postural instability or falls. Brain MRI was unremarkable. [123I]FP-CIT-SPECT was performed. Four days prior, bupropion 150 mg/day was started and increased to 300 mg/day on the day of the scan. The tracer was injected ~1 h after medication and scanning done ~3 h later. SPECT showed pathological reduction of striatal DAT binding bilaterally with low contrast between striatal and cortical binding. Specific binding ratios (occipital reference) were: left caudate 1.77 (Z-score -4.2), right caudate 1.81 (Z-score -3.91), left putamen 1.68 (Z-score -3.65), right putamen 1.54 (Z-score -4.08).

A consultant neurologist found general slowness of movements without true bradykinesia, bilateral reduction of arm pendular movement on walking, no rigidity but active resistance. The ongoing bupropion therapy was noted; serum bupropion was 43 ng/mL and hydroxybupropion 2,332 ng/mL (within therapeutic range).

Based on the available data, what is the most likely explanation for the abnormal [123I]FP-CIT-SPECT findings, and what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
gold_diagnosis: Bupropion-induced false positive [123I]FP-CIT-SPECT (drug interference with dopamine transporter imaging)

answer_key.diagnosis: Bupropion-induced false positive [123I]FP-CIT-SPECT (drug interference with dopamine transporter imaging)
aliases: Drug-induced reduction of striatal DAT binding
answer_key.etiology: Bupropion, a norepinephrine-dopamine reuptake inhibitor, competitively binds to dopamine transporters, reducing [123I]FP-CIT binding and mimicking the pattern of degenerative parkinsonism.
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue bupropion for at least 4 half-lives (8 days) and repeat [123I]FP-CIT-SPECT to reassess dopamine transporter binding.
required_findings: Recent initiation of bupropion: 150 mg/day started 4 days prior, increased to 300 mg/day on the day of SPECT

Striatal DAT binding severely reduced bilaterally: right putamen 1.54 (Z-score -4.08), left putamen 1.68 (Z-score -3.65)
Low contrast between striatal and cortical binding on SPECT
Neurological exam: slowness without true bradykinesia, no rigidity, active resistance
Absence of postural instability or falls
Unremarkable brain MRI
Severe depression with psychotic features and psychomotor slowing as presenting symptoms
optional_findings: Treatment resistance to multiple antidepressants, antipsychotics, S-ketamine, and ECT

challenge_prompt:
An 8-year-old girl with focal drug-resistant epilepsy (about 10 seizures per day) is admitted for presurgical evaluation. Seizures consist of brief (up to ~10 s) periods of unresponsiveness and a blank expression, followed at times by arousal and forced head version to the left. Routine EEG shows frequent seizures over the right fronto-temporal electrodes, mostly during sleep. Early MRIs show only subtle asymmetry in temporal cortex sulci. Overnight MEG recording with simultaneous EEG captures one ictal event; electrographic changes precede clinical onset by ~6 s. Ictal MEG source imaging localizes to the right anterior temporal cortex and is concordant with source localization of interictal MEG spikes as well as subtle blurring at the gray-white matter junction on a 3T T1/FLAIR MRI. Interictal FDG-PET reveals right anterior temporal hypometabolism, and interictal SPECT shows right anterior temporal hypoperfusion.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what would be the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Focal cortical dysplasia (FCD) type IIa

answer_key.diagnosis: Focal cortical dysplasia (FCD) type IIa
answer_key.etiology: Focal cortical dysplasia type IIa
answer_key.next_management_step: Tailored right temporal resection (anterior temporal neocortex and amygdala to mid-posterior temporal neocortex, sparing hippocampus) targeting the MEG-defined seizure onset zone

challenge_prompt:
A 53-year-old woman with a history of mitral valve prolapse and hypercholesterolemia (treated with pravastatin 20 mg/d) presented with treatment-resistant depression. She had a first major depressive episode at age 48, successfully treated with paroxetine 20 mg/d. Her current symptoms began 1 year prior with suicidal ideation, abulia, anhedonia, emotional blunting, sleep disorder, followed by anorexia and rapid weight loss of 26 pounds, leading to hospitalization 8 months later. On admission, Montgomery-Asberg Depression Scale score was 18/60. Cerebral MRI and blood work were normal except for hypercholesterolemia. Atypical clinical features suggestive of a frontal syndrome were observed: psychomotor agitation, ideomotor apraxia, disinhibition, verbal aggression, verbal repetitiveness, unusual urge to smoke and eat, and ideomotor perseverations. Specifically, she devoted a significant amount of time to sorting, storing, and counting her cigarettes. Obsessive-compulsive disorder was ruled out because no obsessions were noted and repetitive behaviors were not aimed at relieving anxiety. These behaviors were recognized as punding. Treatment with venlafaxine 150 mg/d was started but later switched to sertraline 100 mg/d due to poor blood pressure tolerance. Extrapyramidal symptoms emerged after introduction of loxapine 50 mg/d. Neuropsychological evaluations showed deficits in memory and executive functioning. After 6 months of antidepressant treatment, depressive symptoms remitted, but frontal symptoms (especially punding) persisted. Repeated neuropsychological testing showed memory improvements but continued altered executive functioning. A lumbar puncture ruled out Alzheimer's disease. Brain perfusion SPECT showed hypoperfusion predominating in the right frontal and parietooccipital lobes, and slight hypoperfusion in the subthalamic nucleus including the posterior area of the right striatum.

Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis? What is the next best step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Frontotemporal dementia

answer_key.diagnosis: Frontotemporal dementia
answer_key.etiology: Sporadic neurodegenerative disorder; no strong genetic or familial indications in this case.
answer_key.next_management_step: Referral to a Memory clinic for multidisciplinary care including neurology monitoring, non-pharmaceutical interventions (physiotherapy, psychotherapy, family support), and psychotropic medication adjustment as needed.

Neuropsychological testing: executive dysfunction persisting despite memory improvement after depression remission
Brain perfusion SPECT: hypoperfusion in right frontal and parietooccipital lobes

challenge_prompt:
A 56-year-old woman with a history of chronic schizophrenia, primary angle-closure glaucoma (left eye), chronic bronchitis, coronary heart disease, and renal calculi was brought to the emergency department with altered mental status and mild respiratory distress. According to her husband, she had stopped taking antipsychotics without medical guidance three months prior and had since had near-total absence of motion and speech, refusing both medications and food. She had been hospitalized one month earlier and received antipsychotics and nutritional support, but her condition did not improve. The day before admission, she was noted to be breathing with her mouth open and producing a whirring sound. She had no history of alcohol, tobacco, or substance abuse. Compliance with antipsychotics had been poor and her ability to communicate had been impaired in recent years, limiting the history.

On examination, she weighed 45 kg, temperature 36.4°C, blood pressure 129/84 mmHg, pulse 105/min. She was bed-bound, awake with a blank, blunted facial expression, and refused to answer questions or follow most commands. Bilateral pupils were equal; the left pupil was fixed with no light reflex (known glaucoma), the right pupil was reactive. Notably, left horizontal gaze palsy was observed when her eyes were open, though she kept them closed most of the time. Muscle tone was increased and tendon reflexes were hyperreflexic in the extremities. The remainder of the neurologic examination was unremarkable or could not be completed due to poor cooperation.

Initial laboratory results: white blood cell count 13.03 × 10³/μL (88.8% neutrophils), C-reactive protein 21.2 mg/L, procalcitonin 0.31 ng/mL, myoglobin 81 μg/L, ischemia-modified albumin 57.6 μ/mL, plasma lactate 2.24 mmol/L. Electrolytes, thyroid hormones, liver enzymes, and renal function were unremarkable. Chest computed tomography showed diffuse infiltration in the right middle lobe and inferior lobes of both lungs. Electrocardiogram and noncontrast brain CT were normal. A diagnosis of aspiration pneumonia was made by the respiratory consultant.

She was admitted to the psychiatry department and started on nasogastric feeding without antipsychotics (to rule out malignant syndrome). After three days of oral and parenteral nutrition, she seemed to improve: she removed the nasogastric tube, ate a little porridge, could follow simple commands, and the whirring sound diminished. However, on hospital day 5, she suddenly developed difficulty breathing and became confused. Arterial blood gas revealed type I respiratory failure (pCO₂ 41.6 mmHg, pO₂ 53.6 mmHg), with oxygen saturation fluctuating between 63% and 83%, improving after airway suction. The respiratory consultant attributed this to massive airway secretions from mouth breathing.

On hospital day 6, she was transferred to the neurology intensive care unit. Lumbar puncture was performed; cerebrospinal fluid analysis was normal except for a slightly elevated glucose (5.51 mmol/L). Brain MRI was ordered.

Based on the history, examination, and initial investigations, what is the most likely diagnosis?
gold_diagnosis: Wernicke's encephalopathy (non-alcoholic)

answer_key.diagnosis: Wernicke's encephalopathy (non-alcoholic)
answer_key.etiology: Thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency secondary to prolonged food refusal in a patient with schizophrenia
answer_key.next_management_step: Administer high-dose thiamine (e.g., 100–200 mg IM or IV) and obtain brain MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging
required_findings: History of food refusal for three months
Altered mental status and confusion
Left horizontal gaze palsy on examination
Normal noncontrast brain CT
Normal cerebrospinal fluid (except for glucose 5.51 mmol/L)
Plasma lactate 2.24 mmol/L (mildly elevated)
No history of alcohol abuse
Weight 45 kg (low body weight)
optional_findings: Increased muscle tone and tendon hyperreflexia
Type I respiratory failure on day 5

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

Jason is a 16-year-old boy with no medical or psychiatric history except repeated ear infections as a child. The first symptoms appeared at the age of 14 with a slight psychomotor slowdown and a lack of motivation during the summer. Six months later, the psychomotor slowdown worsened, and several neuropsychiatric symptoms appeared: abnormal movements of the upper limbs, fingers, and head and obsessive compulsive disorder with the impossibility of swallowing his saliva. Within a few months, the patient developed a catatonic syndrome with catalepsy, posturing, progressive mutism, negativism, and a refusal to eat, which caused a loss in body weight of 10 kg.

Jason was first hospitalized in a neuropediatric department where he underwent exhaustive biological evaluations, brain MRI, several EEG recordings, video EEG, and lumbar puncture, which included a search for brain-targeted antibodies. All exams were normal, with the exception of moderate elevations in anti-streptolysin O (ASLO) and streptodornase antibodies. In this department, he was treated with a combination of benzodiazepine (lorazepam 12 mg/d) and amoxicillin (3 g/d for 3 weeks) due to a suspicion of PANDAS (Pediatric Auto-immune Neuropsychiatric Disorders Associated with Streptoccocus Infections), and this treatment led to a slight and very short improvement.
gold_diagnosis: Probable seronegative autoimmune encephalitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Probable seronegative autoimmune encephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune-mediated neuroinflammation with unidentified autoantibody
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform brain 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography (FDG-PET) to evaluate for metabolic abnormalities characteristic of autoimmune encephalitis.

Progression with abnormal movements of upper limbs, fingers, head, and OCD with inability to swallow saliva
Development of catatonic syndrome (catalepsy, posturing, progressive mutism, negativism, refusal to eat, 10 kg weight loss)
Normal brain MRI
Normal EEG recordings including video EEG
Normal lumbar puncture with negative search for brain-targeted antibodies
Moderate elevations in anti-streptolysin O (ASLO) and streptodornase antibodies
Only transient minimal response to benzodiazepine 12 mg/day and amoxicillin 3 g/day for 3 weeks
optional_findings: History of repeated ear infections in childhood
No other medical or psychiatric history
Catatonic features included catalepsy, posturing, mutism, negativism
Abnormal movements described as involving upper limbs, fingers, head
OCD manifested as inability to swallow saliva

challenge_prompt:
An 83-year-old woman with a history of hypertension and constipation, living alone on public income support, presented with a 1-year history of gradual loss of motivation, difficulty with housework, and mild memory complaints. About 6 months prior to presentation, she developed the delusional belief that her family doctor drew her blood to harm her. Despite her daughter's reassurance, the delusion persisted. One month later, the delusion abruptly changed: she became convinced that the same doctor had fallen in love with her and proposed marriage. She planned to accept and refused to move in with her daughter, stating she would soon live with the doctor. She had no insight into the delusions but acknowledged her cognitive difficulties.

On admission, no apparent depressive or manic symptoms were observed. Neurological examination revealed mild bradykinesia, mild rigidity in the left upper and lower limbs, and chronic constipation. Cognitive testing showed: Mini-Mental State Examination 20/30, Japanese version Alzheimer's Disease Assessment Scale-cognitive subscale 10/70, digit span index 5, and Mayo Fluctuation Questionnaire score 5/8, indicating mild recent memory impairment, attention deficit, and cognitive fluctuation. Neuropsychiatric Inventory yielded a total of 20 points, with delusion subscore 12/12 and apathy subscore 8/12. Blood tests including vitamins, thyroid function, and infection markers were normal. Brain MRI showed mild diffuse cortical atrophy and mild bilateral hippocampal atrophy, consistent with age. Perfusion SPECT revealed mild hypoperfusion in the bilateral parietal lobes. 123I-MIBG myocardial scintigraphy demonstrated low uptake (heart-to-mediastinum ratio: early 1.72, delayed 1.34).

Based on the above presentation and findings, what is the most likely diagnosis and what is the next most appropriate management step?
gold_diagnosis: De Clerambault's syndrome (secondary erotomania) due to probable dementia with Lewy bodies

answer_key.diagnosis: De Clerambault's syndrome (secondary erotomania) due to probable dementia with Lewy bodies
answer_key.etiology: Dementia with Lewy bodies
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate a cholinesterase inhibitor such as donepezil for cognitive symptoms and cautiously start a low-dose atypical antipsychotic (e.g., blonanserin) for delusions, with close monitoring for neuroleptic sensitivity.
required_findings: Erotomanic delusion with sudden onset, higher-status love object (family doctor), conviction of being loved, and persistence despite contrary evidence.
Prior persecutory delusion involving the same object (family doctor).
Cognitive impairment evidenced by MMSE 20/30 and ADAS-cog 10/70.
Cognitive fluctuation indicated by Mayo Fluctuation Questionnaire score 5/8.
Mild parkinsonism (bradykinesia and rigidity on exam).
Low uptake on 123I-MIBG myocardial scintigraphy (early 1.72, delayed 1.34).
Mild hypoperfusion in bilateral parietal lobes on perfusion SPECT.
Normal blood tests ruling out metabolic or infectious causes.
optional_findings: Age-appropriate mild atrophy on brain MRI.
Absence of depressive or manic symptoms.
Relatively preserved activities of daily living.
No family history of neurodegenerative or psychiatric disorders.

provenance.title: Case Report: De Clerambault's Syndrome in Dementia With Lewy Bodies

challenge_prompt:
A 57-year-old woman from a consanguineous family was admitted with sudden onset of lightheadedness, vertigo, nausea, and vomiting. MRI of the brain showed T2 hyperintensities in the bilateral white matter, thalamus, brain stem, and cerebellum. Her Mini-Mental State Examination score was 27, indicating normal cognition. She had no other motor or cognitive impairments during hospitalization. Medical history revealed she had suffered sudden hearing and vision loss. CT of the head showed tapering of both optic nerves. On eye examination, eye movements were normal but visual acuity was significantly reduced (left 0.3, right 0.1). Pure-tone audiometry demonstrated severe hearing loss. The patient also had chronic kidney disease; a renal biopsy performed 10 years earlier had revealed focal segmental glomerulosclerosis. Her body mass index was 21.7 kg/m², blood pressure 121/77 mmHg, and blood lipid and glucose levels were normal. Cerebrovascular CT was unremarkable. Family history: her parents were consanguineous; her parents and brother had normal vision, hearing, kidney function, and brain MRI showed no lesions.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what next diagnostic step would be most appropriate?
gold_diagnosis: PLD3-related leukoencephalopathy syndrome (homozygous PLD3 mutation causing novel leukoencephalopathy with hearing loss, vision loss, and focal segmental glomerulosclerosis)

answer_key.diagnosis: PLD3-related leukoencephalopathy syndrome (homozygous PLD3 mutation causing novel leukoencephalopathy with hearing loss, vision loss, and focal segmental glomerulosclerosis)
answer_key.etiology: Homozygous nonsense mutation in PLD3 (c.186C>G, p.Y62X)
answer_key.next_management_step: Genetic testing via whole exome sequencing to identify causative mutation

History of sudden hearing and vision loss
CT head: tapering of both optic nerves
Eye exam: reduced visual acuity (left 0.3, right 0.1)
Pure-tone audiometry: severe hearing loss

challenge_prompt: A 28-year-old male was admitted to the respiratory department with repeated coughing and yellow phlegm in 2018. Family reported an 8-year history of ketamine abuse (about five to seven times monthly), with the last use the day before admission. For the past 5 years, he had experienced progressive weakness of the lower limbs, numbness, sensory disorder, decreased muscle strength, difficulty climbing stairs, and recurrent urinary frequency, urgency, and pain. These symptoms persisted despite conservative treatments and were worsened 2 years ago by repeated convulsions, aching in the hepatic region, and jaundice. At that time, he was diagnosed with autoimmune hepatitis, chronic cholecystitis, and cholangitis. A craniocerebral CT scan at that time revealed a widened and deepened cerebral sulcus, the cerebral palsy became flattened, the ventricles expanded, the cerebellum was thickened, and brain volume decreased. Electromyography showed a central motor and sensory conduction disorder, and the motor nerve conduction velocity of bilateral tibial nerves was slower. His liver function normalized after dexamethasone treatment (15 mg treatment, 5 mg maintenance for more than 3 months). A planned cholecystectomy 1 year later was cancelled due to abnormal liver function and development of renal failure. A CT revealed a constricted bladder with dilatation of bilateral upper urinary tract, moderate hydronephrosis, and medullary sponge kidney with multiple renal papillary calcifications. Urine output decreased gradually until anuria, and he underwent hemodialysis twice per week. About 2 months before this admission, after a sudden fall, he became unable to walk and was confined to a wheelchair; he could not speak but made slurred sounds, though he was conscious 2 weeks before admission. He had no history of cerebral disease or alcohol abuse. On admission, he was somnolent and jaundiced. Physical exam: muscle strength of lower limbs 0/5, upper limbs 3/5. Laboratory findings: leukocytosis, anemia (Hb 65 g/L), fecal occult blood positive (3+). Liver function tests: serum bilirubin 283.3 mol/L, alkaline phosphatase 1,804 IU/L, alanine transaminase 19 IU/L, yGT 762 IU/L, albumin 28.2 g/dL. Renal function: urea 26.97 mmol/L, creatinine 375 μmol. Myocardial failure: NT-pro BNP >35,000 ng/L. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) was tested three times: routine tests, culture, and syphilis test were all negative. Chest and abdominal CT showed pneumonia and bilateral kidney abnormalities.
gold_diagnosis: Severe encephalatrophy (brain atrophy) due to chronic ketamine abuse

answer_key.diagnosis: Severe encephalatrophy (brain atrophy) due to chronic ketamine abuse
answer_key.etiology: Long-term ketamine abuse
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinuation of ketamine and supportive care, with neurological rehabilitation if possible

challenge_prompt:
A 47-year-old man presents with headache and vertigo. He has a history of hyperlipidemia untreated for more than 10 years. He has no hypertension, diabetes, or smoking history. CT angiography shows a thrombosed right vertebral artery (VA) V4 segment with luminal irregularities, raising suspicion for focal dissection, atherosclerosis, or both. Intravenous heparin is initiated. Routine brain MRI reveals an acute occipital lobe infarction and a slight increase in pre-contrast T1 signal within the right VA V4 segment. Laboratory values: LDL 123 mg/dL, HbA1c 5.7%. Further neurovascular imaging is performed: MRA reveals two stenotic lesions in the right VA. Pre- and post-contrast MR vessel wall imaging (VWI) shows eccentric wall thickening, presence of hyperintensity on pre-contrast VWI, and strong contrast enhancement on post-contrast VWI at these lesions.

Based on the above findings, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the best next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Intracranial atherosclerotic disease (symptomatic ICAD)

answer_key.diagnosis: Intracranial atherosclerotic disease (symptomatic ICAD)
answer_key.etiology: Atherosclerosis
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate high-intensity statin (e.g., atorvastatin 80 mg daily) and dual antiplatelet therapy (e.g., aspirin 325 mg and clopidogrel 75 mg daily).

challenge_prompt:
A 23-year-old male patient was referred because of a second episode of catatonia. At age 16, he had an episode of major depressive disorder with delusions and catatonia. Laboratory workup, including complete blood count, electrolytes, renal function, liver panel, cerebrospinal fluid analysis (focusing on infection and paraneoplastic disorders), and brain CT and MRI were normal. Treatment with lorazepam (maximum 24 mg/24h) was ineffective and caused paradoxical worsening. He then received a course of electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) twice weekly (bifrontotemporal electrode placement; initial seizure threshold 25.2 mC; electrical dose 50.4–151.1 mC; pulse width 0.5 ms; motor seizure duration 15–96 s; EEG seizure duration 22–257 s). Prompt recovery occurred after four sessions, but relapse followed 8 days after discontinuation. Complete remission was achieved after 21 additional sessions. Aripiprazole 30 mg was started as maintenance but was discontinued six months later due to extrapyramidal side effects. Subsequently, the patient reported difficulty playing piano, describing an inability to shift gaze between the keyboard and sheet music. Neurological examination revealed inability to look up and particularly down, consistent with vertical supranuclear gaze palsy (VSGP). Splenomegaly, subtle dystonia, and parkinsonism were also noted. He now presents with a second episode of catatonia, exhibiting clouded consciousness, mutism, grimacing, stereotypy, mannerisms, negativism, excitement, and perseveration. Laboratory studies are unremarkable except for low HDL cholesterol of 0.74 mmol/L (reference >1.00 mmol/L). Brain MRI is normal.

Genetic testing revealed compound heterozygous mutations in the NPC1 gene (c.3662A>G and c.3019C>T), confirming Niemann-Pick disease type C.
gold_diagnosis: Niemann-Pick Disease Type C

answer_key.diagnosis: Niemann-Pick Disease Type C
answer_key.etiology: Autosomal recessive lysosomal storage disorder caused by mutations in NPC1 or NPC2, leading to impaired intracellular cholesterol trafficking and lipid accumulation.
answer_key.next_management_step: Genetic testing for NPC1 and NPC2 mutations (or oxysterol assay). Initiation of miglustat therapy if confirmed.
required_findings: Vertical supranuclear gaze palsy (VSGP)
Splenomegaly
Subtle dystonia and parkinsonism
Recurrent catatonia
Treatment-resistant catatonia with paradoxical worsening to lorazepam
Normal brain MRI
optional_findings: History of major depressive disorder with psychosis
Lack of family history of psychiatric disorders
Rapid recovery with ECT but subsequent relapse off treatment

provenance.title: Case Report: “Niemann-Pick Disease Type C in a Catatonic Patient Treated With Electroconvulsive Therapy”

mend_provenance.added: Genetic testing revealed compound heterozygous mutations in the NPC1 gene (c.3662A>G and c.3019C>T), confirming Niemann-Pick disease type C.
missing_discriminators: Genetic or biochemical confirmation of NP-C (e.g., NPC1/NPC2 mutation analysis or filipin staining)

challenge_prompt:
Here is a clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

A 61-year-old right-handed man presented with right upper and lower limb paresis, facial paresis, and speech disturbance. On admission, Glasgow Coma Scale was: eye opening 3, verbal 5, motor 6. Head computed tomography showed a high-density area in the left thalamus consistent with hemorrhage. Acute rehabilitation was initiated on day 2. At 4 weeks post-onset, physical examination revealed right upper and lower limb paresis (Brunnstrom stages: arm 3, hand 3–4, foot 3–4), severe hypoesthesia of deep sensations (sense of passive movement and position sense of thumb) and superficial sensations, and the patient complained of pain in the right upper limb (affected side). At 107 days post-onset, Brunnstrom stages were arm 3, hand 4, leg 4, and he continued to have severe hypoesthesia in deep and superficial sensations. He reported increasing numbness and pain in the right upper limb.

Question: What is the most likely diagnosis or etiology of this patient's pain, and what is the next appropriate management step?
gold_diagnosis: Central post-stroke pain (thalamic pain syndrome)

answer_key.diagnosis: Central post-stroke pain (thalamic pain syndrome)
answer_key.etiology: Left thalamic hemorrhage (intraparenchymal hemorrhage) causing damage to the somatosensory pathways
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate pharmacological treatment for neuropathic pain (e.g., amitriptyline, gabapentin, or pregabalin) and refer to a pain specialist for multidisciplinary management, including physical therapy and cognitive-behavioral therapy if needed.
required_findings: Head CT showed high-density area in left thalamus consistent with hemorrhage
Right upper and lower limb paresis at 4 weeks (Brunnstrom stages: arm 3, hand 3-4, foot 3-4) and at 107 days (arm 3, hand 4, leg 4)
Severe hypoesthesia of deep sensations (sense of passive movement and position sense of thumb) at 4 weeks and 107 days
Severe hypoesthesia of superficial sensations at 4 weeks and 107 days
Pain in the right upper limb (affected side) reported at 4 weeks and increasing numbness and pain at 107 days

challenge_prompt:
The patient is a 17-year-old female. Her childhood history includes speech therapy since age 3, multiple dyslalia and sensory integration difficulties at age 6, normal IQ, and normal school performance, but social adjustment problems with peers.

Mood symptoms first appeared 2 years before the index admission. Three months later, following a suicide attempt, sertraline 50 mg/day was started without effect. She was admitted to the Adolescent Psychiatry Ward the next month due to suicidal tendencies (thoughts, ruminations, visions of being dead) and self-injurious behavior (skin scratching). During that admission, she developed psychotic symptoms: feelings of persecution, and auditory, visual, and cenesthetic hallucinations. A diagnosis of paranoid schizophrenia was established (ICD-10). Sequential treatment with lorazepam + olanzapine, olanzapine + perazine, aripiprazole, and perazine + opipramole failed. Improvement was achieved with clozapine 200 mg/day, and she was discharged on clozapine combined with aripiprazole, though full remission was not attained.

At the end of 2019, she was readmitted due to worsening. Increasing the clozapine dose was limited by side effects, and adding risperidone and lorazepam did not improve her state. She was then qualified for electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) after pre-ECT workup (neurological consultation, brain MRI, EEG, internal medicine consultation, carotid Doppler ultrasound) found no contraindications. Lorazepam withdrawal was started.

Four days before the planned first ECT, she had a generalized epileptic seizure with tongue biting and urination. ECT was postponed, and a slower lorazepam withdrawal was recommended. An EEG showed quite numerous delta waves in the frontal regions and several short, generalized discharges of sharp and slow waves in the right hemisphere. She remained on clozapine.

Ten days later, she experienced a brief blackout with anterograde amnesia and the next day fell and did not respond despite being conscious. A few hours later, she had another seizure. Initially, rapport was poor; 15 minutes later she could swallow medications, but convulsions started about a minute later. Diazepam 5 mg IV followed by 10 mg IV stopped the convulsions, but she did not regain consciousness and was transferred to the neurological ward. On arrival, she was sleepy, unresponsive, with equal round pupils poorly reactive to light and accommodation, no meningeal or pyramidal signs, and decreased muscle tone. Valproic acid was started IV then PO. EEG showed generalized delta waves and diffuse slowing without seizure activity.

Brain contrast MRI was normal. Lumbar puncture yielded normal CSF; borreliosis, neuroborreliosis, HSV CNS infection, and Wilson's disease were excluded, and CSF cultures were negative. Laboratory tests showed normal liver, pancreas, and kidney function; ammonia, lactate, and thyroid hormone levels were normal. Anti-TPO, anti-TSH, and anti-ATG antibodies were absent. Screening for rheumatic diseases (ESR, lupus anticoagulant, rheumatoid factors, ANA, ANCA) was negative. Antistreptolysin O was negative. CSF oligoclonal bands and anti-neuronal antibodies were absent.

During hospitalization, she had an episode interpreted as psychogenic: lying with eyes closed, deep breathing, unresponsive, with irregular left upper limb lifting movements; it lasted 10 minutes and stopped with saline. Subsequent video-EEG captured no further epileptic or psychogenic events. Autoimmune encephalitis was considered but the course was atypical; the EEG abnormalities and seizures were attributed to clozapine and benzodiazepine withdrawal.

She was transferred back to psychiatry. Clozapine dose was reduced and zuclopenthixol 5 mg/day added, with no improvement. Multiple delusions, hallucinations, seizure-like events, posturing, and food restriction persisted. EEG showed persistent bilateral frontal slow waves but no focal sharp waves. No full seizures occurred.

Her condition continued to deteriorate: she became alert but hypokinetic, mute, and required a feeding tube. A full picture of catatonia developed. A multidisciplinary team of specialists (psychiatry, ECT, child and adolescent psychiatry, neurology) met to discuss further management.

What is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Paranoid schizophrenia with new-onset seizures likely due to clozapine and lorazepam withdrawal, with catatonia.

answer_key.diagnosis: Paranoid schizophrenia with new-onset seizures likely due to clozapine and lorazepam withdrawal, with catatonia.
answer_key.etiology: Seizures are most probably secondary to clozapine (known up to 3% seizure risk) and benzodiazepine withdrawal; extensive organic workup was negative. Catatonia is a complication of schizophrenia.
answer_key.next_management_step: Proceed with electroconvulsive therapy after tapering and discontinuing valproate and lorazepam over 2 weeks, maintaining clozapine at 125 mg/day, with EEG monitoring every 7 days and emergency benzodiazepines if seizures occur.
required_findings: ICD-10 diagnosis of paranoid schizophrenia.
Failure of multiple antipsychotic trials (sertraline, olanzapine, perazine, aripiprazole, opipramole, risperidone) and only partial response to clozapine 200 mg/day.
Qualification for ECT before seizure, with normal pre-ECT brain MRI, EEG, and internal workup.
Generalized epileptic seizure during lorazepam withdrawal, with tongue biting and urination.
EEG after seizure: frontal delta waves and right hemisphere short discharges of sharp and slow waves.
Extensive neurological and laboratory workup negative for organic causes (normal MRI, CSF, autoantibodies, infections, and metabolic panels).
Progressive deterioration to mutism, hypokinesia, feeding tube dependence (catatonia) despite clozapine reduction and zuclopenthixol.
Multidisciplinary team consultation concluding that seizures are likely clozapine/withdrawal-related and not an absolute contraindication to ECT.
optional_findings: Childhood speech therapy, dyslalia, sensory integration issues.
Initial mood symptoms and suicide attempt 2 years earlier.
Specific psychotic symptoms: delusions of persecution, auditory/visual/cenesthetic hallucinations.
Seizure-like events later interpreted as possible psychogenic non-epileptic seizures.
Negative autoimmune and infectious disease panels.
EEG diffuse slowing without epileptiform activity during some recordings.

provenance.title: ECT in an Adolescent With Schizophrenia and Seizures: Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 61-year-old man with 20 years of formal education presents for evaluation of longstanding cognitive difficulties. He reports that his problems with attention and memory 'go way back, from early on.' As a child, he was often in trouble in elementary school and avoided tasks that required sustained mental effort, such as reading assigned books. He first sought help for inattention at age 45. Currently, he endorses at least five symptoms of inattention, including difficulty sustaining attention, being easily distracted, disorganization, forgetfulness in daily activities, and avoidance of tasks requiring prolonged mental focus. His symptoms have been persistent throughout his life. He has a history of depressive episodes treated with an antidepressant. He previously worked in a professional job and later transitioned to a management position. Neurological exam is unremarkable.

Over the past 5 years, he has undergone neuropsychological assessments approximately annually. Scores are reported as Z-scores normalized for age, sex, and education (Z = 0 is mean performance of healthy older adults; negative values indicate worse performance). Key findings include:
- Executive functions: Performance on the Rey-Osterrieth Complex Figure copy was impaired on multiple assessments. Backward digit span was impaired at age 65 but normalized at the following visit.
- Memory: On the California Verbal Learning Test, immediate and delayed recall scores were consistently within normal limits, but recognition hits were generally low.
- Language: Semantic fluency declined by 1.98 SD over 5 years, but remained within normal limits. Confrontation naming (Boston Naming Test) was intact.
- Other domains: Visuospatial function, phonemic fluency, speeded attention, and task switching were generally within normal limits.
Notably, there was marked within-subject variability: some abnormally low scores at one visit normalized at subsequent visits. Depressive symptoms were present at most assessments (ranging from mild to severe) but did not parallel cognitive fluctuations. An amyloid PET scan was negative for amyloid deposition. MRI brain showed no significant white matter disease.
gold_diagnosis: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), predominantly inattentive presentation

answer_key.diagnosis: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), predominantly inattentive presentation
answer_key.etiology: Neurodevelopmental disorder with genetic and environmental contributions
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate a trial of methylphenidate and consider cognitive-behavioral therapy for ADHD
required_findings: history of inattention since early childhood

challenge_prompt: A 20-year-old man suffered a sharp subtotal amputation of his right dominant arm at the axilla. Emergency reconstruction of the axillary artery was performed within 6 hours, but he developed compartment syndrome on day two requiring fasciotomy. Six days after injury, the disrupted nerves (infraclavicular brachial plexus, distance to hand ~110 cm) were microsurgically reconstructed using sural and median cutaneous antebrachii nerve grafts. Twenty-two months later, he had full motor recovery of elbow and wrist, but the hand remained paralyzed with no active movement and a claw deformity. Clinical neurophysiology showed voluntary contraction of reinnervated upper arm muscles but no voluntary action potentials in interosseus or abductor pollicis muscles. To assess sensory recovery, electrotactile stimulation was applied to the fingertips of both hands. Sensation thresholds were determined. For the affected hand, thresholds were increased on average by a factor of 3 compared to the intact side. Discomfort thresholds were also elevated. A mislocalization test was performed: each fingertip was stimulated, and the patient reported where he felt the sensation (five-alternative forced choice). Results for the affected hand: stimulation at D1 (thumb) was perceived as D4 (ring finger) in 47% of trials, with 0% correct localization. Stimulation at D2 (index) was perceived as D1 in 40% of trials, with 13% correct. Stimulation at D4 (ring) was perceived as D2 in 40% of trials, with 27% correct. Stimulation at D3 (middle) was perceived correctly in 47% of trials. Stimulation at D5 (little) was perceived correctly in 47%. On the intact hand, performance was 97±6% correct. fMRI tactile mapping during piezo-electric stimulation of the affected fingertips revealed: significant activation in contralateral primary somatosensory cortex (BA 3b) for all fingers except D3, where no activation was found. The map was disordered: D1 stimulation activated an area consistent with the expected D4 location; D2 activation was most medial, near the usual palm representation; D4 activation was at the expected D1 location; D5 activation was at or slightly lateral to the expected D5 location. The mediolateral succession was D2, then D5 and D1 (nearly co-located), then D4. Based on the above findings, what is the most likely explanation for the altered sensory perception and cortical maps, and what would be the next most appropriate step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Peripheral nerve regeneration with misrouting of sensory fibers to incorrect fingertip receptors, leading to central cortical reorganization.

answer_key.diagnosis: Peripheral nerve regeneration with misrouting of sensory fibers to incorrect fingertip receptors, leading to central cortical reorganization.
aliases: Sensory fiber misrouting after nerve graft
answer_key.etiology: Regenerating axons from the median nerve graft inaccurately innervated sensory receptors (e.g., D1 fibers innervating D4 territory) combined with central adaptation.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate sensory re-education therapy and schedule follow-up neurological and psychophysical assessments to monitor further plasticity.
required_findings: Subtotal arm amputation at axilla with infraclavicular brachial plexus injury, repaired with nerve grafts.
22 months post-injury: hand paralysis with claw deformity, no voluntary action potentials in hand intrinsic muscles.
Electrotactile sensation thresholds in affected hand increased on average 3-fold vs intact side.
Mislocalization: D1 stimulation perceived as D4 (47%), D2 as D1 (40%), D4 as D2 (40%), D3 and D5 with 47% correct but still impaired.
fMRI: D1 activation at expected D4 location, D4 at D1, D2 activation most medial (palm area).
D5 activation at expected location or slightly lateral.
No fMRI activation for D3.
Intact hand performance 97% correct on mislocalization test.
optional_findings: Distance from injury to hand approximately 110 cm.
Sural and median cutaneous antebrachii nerve grafts used for bridging.

challenge_prompt:
A 32-year-old man presents with a long history of visual perceptual disturbances. He reports seeing 'golden glitter' and 'glowing translucent patches' across his total visual field, as well as trails following moving objects. Bright, loud colors provoke more severe episodes of visual distortions lasting hours. The symptoms began gradually in the months after he repeatedly used hallucinogenic drugs at age 18. Due to the symptoms, he withdrew socially and became insecure. At age 19, he sought medical help; cranial MRI was unremarkable except for a left cerebellar arachnoid cyst, and a clinical EEG was interpreted as normal. Over the years, he developed depressive episodes with sleep difficulties, treated with citalopram and quetiapine, which improved mood but not the visual hallucinations. At about age 26, he experienced an acute crisis with anxiety, irritability, increased perceptual disturbances, and suicidal ideation, leading to psychiatric admission. He also reported one day of sound distortions classified as akoasms. This was interpreted as an acute schizophreniform psychotic episode, and quetiapine dose was increased with some improvement. Subsequent course involved alternating depression and suspected schizophrenic psychosis. He was tried on multiple neuroleptics (clozapine, haloperidol, amisulpride, ziprasidone, risperidone, quetiapine, aripiprazole, perazine, promethazine) but never had lasting relief from visual hallucinations. Neuroleptics were eventually discontinued due to akathisia and extrapyramidal motor symptoms. The patient sought care at a neuromodulation center. A 32-channel EEG was performed, revealing diffuse slowing of brain oscillations across broad cortical areas with high power in the delta range over occipital electrodes. The resting state EEG showed no clear alpha-peak with eyes open and no alpha blockage during eyes closed. Delta activity did not exhibit any notable lateralization. 

Based on the information provided, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what would be the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Hallucinogen Persisting Perception Disorder (HPPD), Type 2 (permanent form with fluctuating intensity)

answer_key.diagnosis: Hallucinogen Persisting Perception Disorder (HPPD), Type 2 (permanent form with fluctuating intensity)
answer_key.etiology: Prior hallucinogen use (most likely LSD or similar) leading to persistent perceptual changes, possibly via damage to inhibitory 5-HT2A interneurons causing cortical disinhibition and aberrant occipital delta oscillations.
answer_key.next_management_step: Consider a trial of non-invasive brain stimulation, specifically cathodal transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) targeting the occipital region (e.g., cathode over Oz), based on the EEG finding of occipital delta hypersynchrony.
required_findings: Chronic visual hallucinations (golden glitter, glowing patches, trails) onset months after hallucinogen use at age 18
Good insight and no sustained response to multiple antipsychotic trials
Normal early clinical EEG and unremarkable MRI except left cerebellar arachnoid cyst
Later 32-channel EEG showing diffuse slowing with high delta power over occipital electrodes
EEG: no clear alpha-peak with eyes open and no alpha blockage during eyes closed
History of acute psychiatric crisis with akoasms (sound distortions) interpreted as schizophreniform, but visual symptoms persisted
No lasting improvement from quetiapine or other neuroleptics for visual hallucinations
Symptoms cause significant distress and functional impairment
optional_findings: Visual analog scale ratings for hallucinations
Age at onset and duration >10 years

provenance.title: Pathological Delta Oscillations in Hallucinogen Persisting Perception Disorder: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 40-year-old, right-handed woman with a history of chronic migraine with aura, dyslipidemia, obesity, nonalcoholic fatty liver disease, endometriosis, gastroesophageal reflux disease, generalized anxiety disorder (GAD), and trichotillomania presented to the emergency department after acute dysarthria and bilateral distal arm numbness, which resolved before arrival. Examination: awake, alert, oriented; pupils equal and reactive; visual fields full; cranial nerves II–XII intact, no oculomotor deficits, no nystagmus; motor 5/5, sensation intact, speech normal, finger-to-nose normal, gait normal; NIHSS 0. CT angiography showed multifocal stenosis in M2/M3 branches of the right middle cerebral artery. Brain MRI showed a small area of restricted diffusion in the right mid insular cortex consistent with ischemia. She was not a candidate for tPA, so she received aspirin, clopidogrel load, and heparin infusion. After 3 days, repeat CTA showed recanalization of the stenotic segments. Workup revealed a large patent foramen ovale (transthoracic echo with bubble study); hypercoagulable panel and leg ultrasound were negative. She was discharged on aspirin 325 mg and rosuvastatin 40 mg. Two weeks later she underwent PFO closure.

Within days of discharge, she developed gradual onset of daily spinning vertigo, unsteadiness, and lightheadedness. The spinning vertigo resolved over days, but the non-spinning vertigo and unsteadiness persisted. Symptoms were exacerbated by standing upright, bending over quickly, moving visual stimuli, complex patterns, and tasks requiring precise visual focus (driving, watching TV, computer). Concurrently, she experienced low mood, loss of interest, sleep and appetite disturbance, prominent fatigue, and feelings of guilt and worthlessness; no hopelessness, helplessness, or suicidal ideation. She had no vertigo–migraine linkage. At six months post-stroke, she reported ongoing vestibular symptoms triggered by riding in cars, fluorescent lights, busy traffic, escalators, and patterned floors. Vestibular and oculomotor examination (smooth pursuit, saccades, convergence, VOR cancellation) were normal; no spontaneous or gaze-evoked nystagmus. Dix–Hallpike and roll tests negative. Audiometry: normal hearing, acoustic reflexes, Eustachian tube function. Psychiatric assessment: PHQ‑9 13 (moderate depression), GAD‑7 7 (mild anxiety). Mini International Neuropsychiatric Interview confirmed first major depressive episode and pre-existing GAD; no mania, psychosis, panic, PTSD, eating disorder, or illness anxiety. Laboratory studies (CBC, CMP, TSH, B12) and repeat head CT showed no new abnormalities.

Based on this presentation, what are the most likely diagnoses, and what is the next appropriate management step?
gold_diagnosis: Persistent postural–perceptual dizziness (PPPD) and post‑stroke depression (major depressive episode)

answer_key.diagnosis: Persistent postural–perceptual dizziness (PPPD) and post‑stroke depression (major depressive episode)
answer_key.etiology: Right insular stroke
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (e.g., sertraline) and refer for vestibular rehabilitation.
required_findings: Daily non‑spinning vertigo, unsteadiness, lightheadedness persisting >3 months.
Exacerbation by upright posture, active movements, and complex/moving visual stimuli.
Normal vestibular/oculomotor exam: no nystagmus, smooth pursuit/saccades/convergence normal, Dix–Hallpike and roll tests negative.
Onset days after a right insular stroke (MRI‑confirmed restricted diffusion).
Simultaneous onset of low mood, anhedonia, sleep/appetite disturbance, fatigue, guilt/worthlessness.
First lifetime major depressive episode meeting criteria, no prior depression history.
Pre‑existing generalized anxiety disorder.
Right mid insular cortex restricted diffusion on MRI.
optional_findings: Negative hypercoagulable workup and leg ultrasound.
Transient initial stroke symptoms (dysarthria, bilateral arm numbness).

provenance.title: Case Report: Right Insular Stroke Causing Simultaneous Onset of a Functional Vestibular Disorder and Psychiatric Disorder—Persistent Postural–Perceptual Dizziness and Post–stroke Depression

challenge_prompt: A 29-year-old man with schizoaffective disorder, antisocial personality disorder, and substance use disorder (cannabis) was hospitalized involuntarily for acute manic psychosis with grandiosity, psychomotor agitation, and hetero-aggressive threats. He had grade 1 obesity (99 kg, 180 cm, BMI 30.56), was an active smoker, and had no other significant medical history. His regular medications included valproate semisodium 2500 mg/day and paliperidone ER 150 mg/28 days, but recent non-compliance led to relapse. On admission, serum valproate was 23 mg/L (therapeutic range 41–100 mg/L). Valproate was re-prescribed at the previous doses along with diazepam (20 mg/day) and levomepromazine (300 mg/day). Despite a serum valproate level of 91 mg/L, the patient's psychiatric symptoms worsened. A decision was made to switch from valproate to lithium, using an overlap-and-taper strategy. Pre-therapeutic assessment was unremarkable. Lithium was started at 400 mg/day. After the third dose, the patient developed acute confusion with fluctuating temporal disorientation (day 3). Examination revealed psychomotor retardation, decreased alertness, and global cognitive impairment (MMSE 15/30, FAB 3/18); general and neurological exams showed no focal signs. Lithium was discontinued on day 3. Workup: complete blood count, electrolytes, liver, kidney, thyroid, coagulation, and vitamin studies were normal. Brain CT (day 5) was normal. EEG (day 5) showed no seizure activity but demonstrated a pattern of encephalopathy with fluctuating, slow background (theta/alpha and delta waves) predominant posteriorly, reactive to verbal stimulation and eye opening. Serum lithium level 24 hours after the third dose was undetectable (<0.05 mmol/L). Serum valproate level was 130 mg/L. Serum ammonia was 182 μmol/L (normal 16–60). Blood toxicology screen was positive for cannabis (THC-COOH 16.9 ng/L) but negative for other substances.
gold_diagnosis: Valproic acid-induced hyperammonemic encephalopathy

answer_key.diagnosis: Valproic acid-induced hyperammonemic encephalopathy
answer_key.etiology: Adverse drug reaction to valproate semisodium, likely exacerbated by initiation of lithium causing elevated valproate levels and hyperammonemia.
answer_key.next_management_step: Reduce valproate semisodium dosage and monitor serum ammonia and clinical status.
required_findings: acute confusion with fluctuating temporal disorientation
MMSE 15/30 and FAB 3/18
EEG showing encephalopathy with theta/alpha and delta waves
serum valproate level 130 mg/L
serum ammonia 182 μmol/L (elevated >16–60)
normal brain CT
undetectable serum lithium (<0.05 mmol/L)
normal laboratories (CBC, electrolytes, liver, kidney, thyroid, coagulation, vitamins)

provenance.title: Case Report: A Case of Valproic Acid-Induced Hyperammonemic Encephalopathy Associated With the Initiation of Lithium: A Re-duplicable Finding

challenge_prompt: A 64-year-old male with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and a prior cerebral infarction 12 years ago resulting in mild residual right-sided weakness presented with 4 hours of vomiting and limb shaking followed by 3 hours of unconsciousness. On examination, he was comatose (Glasgow Coma Scale 4), tetraplegic, and exhibited paroxysmal limb shaking (spontaneous episodes lasting 30 seconds every 20 minutes), bilateral extensor posturing, unequal pupils (left 2 mm, right 4 mm) that were sluggish to light, bilateral positive Babinski signs, profuse sweating, fever (38.0°C), tachycardia (102/min), tachypnea (22/min), and hypertension (180/87 mmHg). Non-contrast brain computed tomography (CT) revealed only chronic infarcts in the temporal and occipital lobes. Chest CT and routine blood tests were normal. Electroencephalogram showed no epileptic discharges. What is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_key.next_management_step: Emergent vascular imaging (CT angiography or MR angiography) to confirm large vessel occlusion, followed by mechanical thrombectomy if indicated

challenge_prompt:
A 24-year-old woman with a history of non-autoimmune hypothyroidism on hormone replacement presented to the psychiatry emergency department with acute onset of bizarre behavior, delusions of pregnancy, and visceral hallucinations. She repeatedly stated she was pregnant and about to give birth. There was no prodrome, no personal or family psychiatric history, and no substance use. On examination, she was restless, fearful, and covered her eyes and ears, refusing to answer most questions. Physical and neurological exams were normal. Basic laboratory tests and brain CT were normal. She was admitted to psychiatry and started on haloperidol and olanzapine. Despite two weeks of antipsychotic treatment, she showed no improvement, with frequent agitation, aggression, and persistent delusions. She also reported short-term memory deficits, difficult to confirm due to psychosis. On day 15, an EEG showed artifacts but no slowing or epileptiform discharges. Brain MRI under anesthesia was normal. Screening for systemic autoantibodies, vitamins, and infections including viral serologies was negative. On day 29, she developed catatonic symptoms: mutism, mannerisms, and echolalia, which responded to intravenous lorazepam 6 mg daily but psychotic symptoms persisted. Due to treatment-resistant psychosis and catatonia, autoimmune encephalitis was suspected. Serum testing for NMDAR IgG antibodies using a fixed cell-based assay returned positive with a titer of 1:2560 (reference <1:10). A lumbar puncture was performed for CSF analysis including NMDAR antibodies; results are pending. She was started on prednisone 60 mg daily. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most important next step in management?

CSF analysis for NMDAR IgG antibodies returned positive.
gold_diagnosis: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis

answer_key.diagnosis: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis
answer_key.etiology: Paraneoplastic syndrome associated with ovarian mature teratoma
answer_key.next_management_step: Perform imaging (CT or MRI) of the abdomen and pelvis to search for an ovarian teratoma; confirm CSF NMDAR antibodies; consider tumor removal and continue immunotherapy.

Serum NMDAR IgG antibodies positive with a titer of 1:2560
