challenge_prompt:
A 28-year-old woman presented to a specialized outpatient clinic with a wish to get vaccinated against SARS-CoV-2 but severe anxiety, panic, and fainting related to blood, injuries, and injections since childhood. She reported an inability to remember a time without intense fear of needles. At age 2, during a tonsillectomy, she forcibly removed an intravenous catheter while panicking. Her first fainting episode occurred in primary school after falling down stairs; at age 11, she fainted during a vaccination, reinforcing her fear. She also fainted during anticipation of blood donation in adolescence, sustaining a cerebral contusion. Her fear negatively impacted hobbies and her attitude toward pregnancy and childbirth.

Fainting episodes were typically <30 seconds, with loss of consciousness, no motor symptoms, and instant reorientation, occurring at most once monthly. She also experienced very infrequent episodes of loss of consciousness with motor features, triggered exclusively by sustained exposure to blood-related phobic stimuli or unexpected pain (e.g., injections, swallowing hot food). Typical epileptic triggers such as alcohol consumption or sleep deprivation had never provoked an event. During one school fainting spell, observers reported myoclonic jerks. A childhood neurological hospital stay yielded no epileptic origin for these recurrent events.

Medical history included hypothyroidism diagnosed at age 23 (treated with L-thyroxine 75 µg daily), migraine, pollinosis, and atopic eczema. At age 12, she briefly saw a psychologist for blood/injury/injection-related fears, but no long-term treatment followed. Family history noted circulation problems and needle fear in siblings.

On psychometric assessment before treatment, the Multidimensional Blood/Injury Phobia Inventory short version (MBPI-K) gave the following subscale scores (each out of 20): needle fears = 20, fainting = 11.25, blood/injury fears and disgust = 15, hospital fears = 15 (sum = 61.25). The Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-D) showed low generalized anxiety but panic symptoms limited to BII situations. The Liebowitz Social Anxiety Scale indicated low social fear and avoidance, and the Relationship Scales Questionnaire revealed a secure attachment style with high desire for independence. No other psychiatric comorbidities were identified. A DSM-5 diagnosis of blood-injection-injury phobia was confirmed.

During the eighth session of a graduated exposure protocol, while undergoing a second in vivo blood draw from a superficial forearm vein, she reported heightened anxiety and presyncope symptoms. After needle insertion, she described extreme anxiety and then a panic attack; the needle was immediately removed. The panic attack evolved into a seizure. She lost consciousness for approximately 2 minutes. Her eyes remained closed about 80%, without detectable bulbar deviation. She assumed a tonic opisthotonic posture. Her lips and perioral area were briefly and mildly cyanotic. Both hands demonstrated an incomplete Trousseau sign of latent tetany, and her arms made slow, asynchronous waxing movements. No convulsions were visible. Urinary incontinence occurred, but she did not bite her tongue or defecate. Postictally, she uttered repetitive phrases (“oh no”), reorientated immediately, and confusion lasted less than 3 minutes. She had amnesia for the event. A neurological examination performed directly afterward was normal.

A same-day electroencephalogram (EEG) showed normal alpha activity with intermittent unspecific arrhythmic activity; no interictal epileptiform discharges, focal slowing, or other focal abnormalities were detected. Cranial magnetic resonance imaging (1.5 T) revealed no pathological findings. A repeat EEG with hyperventilation three weeks later was also normal.

Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis for the witnessed seizure, and what is the most appropriate next management step?
gold_diagnosis: Psychogenic non-epileptic seizure (probable) in the setting of blood-injection-injury phobia

answer_key.diagnosis: Psychogenic non-epileptic seizure (probable) in the setting of blood-injection-injury phobia
answer_key.etiology: Panic-triggered conversion/dissociative phenomenon secondary to severe blood-injection-injury phobia
answer_key.next_management_step: Modify cognitive behavioral therapy exposure rationale to avoid provoking maximum anxiety; incorporate distress tolerance skills (e.g., porcupine ball, ice pack, verbal self-instructions), continued applied muscle tension, and breathing techniques, with gradual re-exposure starting from finger prick
required_findings: Seizure triggered exclusively by blood/injury/injection stimuli
Seizure semiology: eyes closed, opisthotonic posture, no convulsions, slow asynchronous arm movements, incomplete Trousseau sign, brief perioral cyanosis, urinary incontinence, no tongue bite
Loss of consciousness ~2 minutes with immediate postictal reorientation and confusion <3 minutes
Postictal neurological examination normal
Same-day EEG: no interictal epileptiform discharges or focal abnormalities
Cranial MRI: no pathological findings
Repeat EEG with hyperventilation normal
Background of fainting and intense fear of blood/injury/injections, with childhood neurological workup negative for epilepsy
optional_findings: MBPI-K sum 61.25 confirming severe BII phobia
PHQ-D showing panic symptoms limited to BII situations
Absence of seizures with typical epileptic triggers
Prior occurrence of myoclonic jerks during fainting
Family history of needle fear and circulation problems

provenance.title: Combined Fainting and Psychogenic Non-epileptic Seizures as Significant Therapy Hurdles in Blood-Injury-Injection Phobia: A Mini-Review and Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 62-year-old woman noticed tremor and gradual loss of dexterity in her right limbs in 2017. Since 2018, she had difficulty turning over in bed; levodopa therapy was initiated but was unhelpful. Within 2 years, she developed left-hand tremor, walked with small steps, had slow turning, freezing, and occasional falls. Since 2020, she had difficulty arising from a chair and was barely able to walk; symptoms did not improve with levodopa. In July 2020, she was admitted with severe akinetic rigidity and difficulty walking.

Neurological examination: severe bradykinesia and rigidity, moderate postural and resting limb tremors, severe freezing of gait, spontaneous falls. Pull test positive, Romberg negative, oculomotor examinations normal, no dysmetria or ataxia. Neuropsychological evaluation: MMSE 27, MoCA 24, HAMA 15, HAMD 18 – no dementia or major depression. Residual urine volume 98.6 ml. Supine blood pressure 132/87 mmHg, standing (3 min) 100/65 mmHg. Acute levodopa challenge test: MDS-UPDRS-III motor score improved 11% (91 vs 81) with no obvious on/off.

Preoperative brain MRI showed mild cerebral atrophy and a left lateral putaminal rim. 18F-FDG PET showed hypometabolism mainly in the left putamen. 18F-fluoropropyl-dihydrotetrabenazine PET revealed decreased VMAT2 distribution mainly in the left putamen.

What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the next appropriate step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Multiple system atrophy with predominant parkinsonism (MSA-P)

answer_key.diagnosis: Multiple system atrophy with predominant parkinsonism (MSA-P)
answer_key.etiology: Neurodegenerative disorder of unknown etiology characterized by glial cytoplasmic inclusions containing alpha-synuclein, leading to neuronal loss in the striatonigral and olivopontocerebellar systems.
answer_key.next_management_step: Consider combined bilateral subthalamic nucleus deep brain stimulation and low-thoracic spinal cord stimulation for severe levodopa-resistant rigidity, bradykinesia, and freezing of gait, after thorough risk-benefit discussion.

Orthostatic hypotension (supine 132/87 mmHg to standing 100/65 mmHg, drop of 32 mmHg systolic)
Urinary retention (residual urine volume 98.6 ml)
Negative Romberg and normal eye movements, no cerebellar ataxia
Brain MRI: left lateral putaminal rim
18F-FDG PET: hypometabolism mainly in the left putamen
VMAT2 PET: decreased vesicular monoamine transporter type 2 distribution mainly in the left putamen
Normal cognition (MMSE 27, MoCA 24)
optional_findings: Rapid eye movement sleep behavior disorder (not mentioned)
Dysautonomia beyond orthostasis and urinary retention (not fully assessed)

provenance.title: Case report: Combined therapy of bilateral subthalamic nucleus deep brain stimulation and spinal cord stimulation significantly improves motor function in a patient with multiple system atrophy with predominant parkinsonism

challenge_prompt:
The patient is a 41-year-old right-handed female with a history of drug resistant IGE manifesting as typical absence seizures, myoclonic seizures, GTCS. She had a family history of epilepsy (father with focal epilepsy). There were no other epilepsy risk factors. Her seizures began at the age of 4 years old. Over time, she continued to experience frequent GTCS that were often followed by non-convulsive status epilepticus (NCSE, absence status epilepticus). At the time of her initial evaluation, she would have at least 1 GTCS per year but often would have 1 GTCS every 2–3 months. From 2018 to 2020, she had 11 GTCS that were consistently followed by 1–2 days of NCSE, requiring hospital admissions. Throughout her epilepsy course, she failed medication trials of levetiracetam, topiramate, perampanel, and ethosuximide.

In light of her DRE, she was evaluated for epilepsy surgery. A brain MRI was unremarkable for discrete epileptic lesions. Inpatient video-EEG monitoring revealed generalized, frontally predominant, 3–4 Hz, spike- and polyspike-and-wave discharges as well as bursts of GPFA.

What is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Drug-resistant idiopathic generalized epilepsy (IGE) with multiple seizure types

answer_key.etiology: Genetic predisposition with presumed thalamocortical network dysfunction
answer_key.next_management_step: Bilateral centromedian thalamic responsive neurostimulation (RNS) implantation

Video-EEG: bursts of generalized paroxysmal fast activity (GPFA)

challenge_prompt: An 83-year-old man with a history of hypertension, hyperuricemia, and inguinal hernia, no substance abuse, presented with visual hallucinations and delusional infestation. Starting at age 75, he had REM sleep behavior disorder with limb movements while sleeping and falling out of bed. At 83, he began seeing insects (5 mm to 1 cm) that moved when he tried to touch them, felt them crawling on his body and biting him, and believed they sometimes came out of his anus; he used insecticides but had some insight, saying 'I think that this is a strange experience'. He also reported visual hallucinations of people and mild amnesia. On admission, his delusional infestation resolved without new medication, but he developed visual and tactile hallucinations of people (e.g., 'a man painted me with a slimy thing', 'a person in white clothes like a nurse touched me'). No skin abnormalities or parkinsonism were found. MMSE scores fluctuated between 18 and 23. Blood, CSF, and EEG were normal. Cranial MRI showed diffuse very mild atrophy including bilateral hippocampus. 123I-IMP SPECT revealed hypoperfusion in bilateral occipital and parietal lobes. Cardiac 123I-MIBG scintigraphy showed reduced myocardial uptake (heart-to-mediastinum ratio early 1.55, delayed 1.26). What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Probable dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) with delusional infestation

answer_key.diagnosis: Probable dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) with delusional infestation
answer_key.etiology: Neurodegenerative disorder with Lewy body pathology and striatal dopamine transporter dysfunction.
answer_key.next_management_step: Initiate acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (e.g., donepezil hydrochloride 5 mg/day); avoid antipsychotics due to severe neuroleptic sensitivity in DLB.

challenge_prompt: We present the case of a right-handed 77-year-old female patient who developed a right thalamic infarction at the age of 74 years. She developed numbness in the left upper and lower extremities, which gradually improved but did not completely dissipate. Approximately 1 year after the onset of the infarction, she developed a tremor in her left upper extremity, which gradually worsened. She had no family history associated with tremor, and no medical history of diseases associated with tremor, such as hyperthyroidism. On examination, the tremor was rough and severe, with a frequency of approximately 3 Hz, and appeared in arm-stretching or wing-beating posture and goal-directed movement. Resting tremors were also observed. Dystonic postures and movements were accompanied by tremors, especially when writing letters. Additionally, she had mild and chronic numbness in her left upper and lower extremities, without other neurological deficits. Parkinsonism was not observed. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a small, old infarction in the ventral-posterolateral area of the thalamus. A dopamine transporter (DaT) scan showed a normal pattern. Treatment with atenolol, levodopa, and clonazepam failed to control her tremor. Based on the presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate next step in management?
gold_diagnosis: Holmes tremor

answer_key.diagnosis: Holmes tremor
answer_key.etiology: Right thalamic infarction (stroke)
answer_key.next_management_step: Refer for stereotactic surgery (e.g., deep brain stimulation of the ventral intermediate nucleus or posterior subthalamic area, or MR-guided focused ultrasound thalamotomy)

Tremor onset 1 year after stroke

challenge_prompt:
Here is a published clinical case. Based only on the presentation below, what is the most likely diagnosis or etiology, and what next diagnostic or treatment step is most appropriate?

A 51-year-old man with a 2-year history of Parkinsonism presented with motor retardation, sleep disturbance, and slurred speech. He could not rise from a seat independently; his gait was slow and insecure, with poor postural responses. He had mild rest tremor in both upper limbs, predominantly on the right. Slurred speech, frequent drooling, and difficulty opening his mouth were present. His wife reported that he kicked and shouted during sleep, snored, urinated 3–4 times per night, and felt fatigued, sleepy, and drowsy during the day. He frequently dozed off during conversation.

Symptoms began at age 47 with a slight tremor in the right hand, which was initially ignored. By 2020, speech and movement were affected, making daily tasks difficult. He was diagnosed with Parkinsonism and treated with Levodopa/Benserazide 0.125 g twice daily and Pramipexole 0.5 mg three times daily. Despite medication, his symptoms persisted.

Physical examination: clear consciousness, slurred speech, free eye movements, no nystagmus, reduced facial expression, difficulty opening mouth and protruding tongue, forward-leaning gait, normal muscle strength, cogwheel-like increase in muscle tone in both upper limbs, active biceps reflex, weak knee and ankle reflexes, stiff neck and limbs, left Babinski sign positive, jaw jerk positive. PDSS score 93, PSQI score 11, ESS score 16.

Cranial MR+MRA: significant narrowing of the bilateral posterior cerebral artery P2 segments; multiple ischemic foci in both frontoparietal lobes.

Cranial PET-CT: decreased dopamine metabolism in both striata, more prominent on the left; decreased glucose metabolism in both basal ganglia, more prominent on the left. The pattern was interpreted as not consistent with idiopathic Parkinson's disease.

Polysomnography (PSG): reduced sleep efficiency, prolonged wake after sleep onset (WASO), minimum oxygen saturation 83.3%, sleep architecture: N1 35.4%, N2 57.2%, N3 1.0%, REM 6.4%. A total of 792 periodic leg movements were recorded during sleep, with an index of 135.2, considered severe.
gold_diagnosis: Vascular Parkinsonism with severe periodic limb movement disorder, excessive daytime sleepiness, probable REM sleep behavior disorder, and nocturia

answer_key.diagnosis: Vascular Parkinsonism with severe periodic limb movement disorder, excessive daytime sleepiness, probable REM sleep behavior disorder, and nocturia
aliases: Secondary Parkinsonism due to cerebrovascular disease
answer_key.etiology: Cerebrovascular disease causing multiple ischemic lesions in the brain, leading to parkinsonian symptoms and sleep disorders
answer_key.next_management_step: Electro-acupuncture (EA) therapy as a non-pharmacological intervention for sleep disorders and motor symptoms
required_findings: MRI shows multiple ischemic foci in frontoparietal lobes
MRA shows narrowing of bilateral posterior cerebral artery P2 segments
PET-CT shows bilateral striatal dopamine metabolism decreased, not consistent with idiopathic Parkinson's disease
Physical exam: cogwheel rigidity in upper limbs, positive left Babinski sign, pseudobulbar signs (slurred speech, difficulty opening mouth)
Polysomnography: 792 periodic leg movements during sleep, index 135.2 (severe)
Gait impairment: difficulty rising from chair, slow and insecure gait, poor postural responses
Persistent symptoms despite levodopa/benserazide and pramipexole treatment
History: patient kicked and shouted during sleep (suggestive of REM sleep behavior disorder), excessive daytime sleepiness (ESS 16), nocturia 3-4 times/night
optional_findings: Minimum oxygen saturation 83.3% during sleep (moderate hypoxemia)
Sleep architecture: N1 35.4%, N2 57.2%, N3 1.0%, REM 6.4%
Active biceps reflex, weak knee and ankle reflexes, stiff neck and limbs

provenance.title: Electro-acupuncture on Vascular Parkinsonism with multiple sleep disorders: A Case Report

challenge_prompt:
A 75-year-old woman presents with a 1-year history of short-term memory disturbances, word-finding difficulties, and depressive symptoms. She has multiple comorbidities: essential tremor, previous lung empyema, hepatic steatosis with liver cysts, cholecystolithiasis, coronary artery disease, hypertension, mitral valve insufficiency, hyperlipoproteinemia, hypothyroidism, and polyneuropathy. She smoked 60 pack-years but quit in 2019. Her mother had dementia. Current medications: pantoprazole 20 mg/day, atorvastatin 20 mg/day, L-thyroxine 50 μg/day, fexofenadine 180 mg/day, bupropion 150 mg/day. Psychopathological exam shows reduced drive and slowed psychomotor speed. She meets criteria for moderate recurrent major depression (Geriatric Depression Scale score 6). Neurological exam: pallanesthesia in legs. Cognitive screening: MMSE 29/30. Neuropsychological testing reveals amnestic mild cognitive impairment (MCI) with deficits in information processing speed, visuospatial cognition, verbal and figural memory, consistent with multi-domain MCI. Activities of daily living are mildly impaired (Bayer ADL scale mean 3.3). Brain MRI: cerebral microangiopathy (Fazekas grade 1). Lumbar puncture: CSF analysis shows elevated S100 protein (4.7 μg/L; pathological >2.7 μg/L), elevated phosphorylated tau 181 protein, and evidence of intrathecal IgG synthesis. What is the most likely underlying diagnosis, and what next diagnostic test is most appropriate?

Serum testing revealed positive neurofascin 186 IgG antibodies.
gold_diagnosis: Amnestic mild cognitive impairment associated with neurofascin 186 autoantibodies (autoimmune-mediated MCI with CNS involvement)

answer_key.diagnosis: Amnestic mild cognitive impairment associated with neurofascin 186 autoantibodies (autoimmune-mediated MCI with CNS involvement)
aliases: Autoimmune mild cognitive impairment with neural autoantibodies
answer_key.etiology: Autoimmune: anti-neurofascin 186 antibodies causing central and peripheral nervous system involvement
answer_key.next_management_step: Test serum and CSF for anti-neural autoantibodies, specifically neurofascin 186 antibodies, using immunofluorescence (e.g., BIOCHIP-mosaic with brain tissue and recombinant cells)
required_findings: Amnestic mild cognitive impairment involving multiple domains (information processing speed, visuospatial, verbal and figural memory)
Polyneuropathy (pallanesthesia in legs, known diagnosis of polyneuropathy)
Intrathecal IgG synthesis in CSF
Elevated CSF S100 protein (4.7 μg/L)
Elevated CSF phosphorylated tau 181 protein
Mild cerebral microangiopathy (Fazekas grade 1) insufficient to explain cognitive profile
Recurrent major depression
Family history of dementia
optional_findings: Multiple vascular risk factors (hypertension, hyperlipoproteinemia, coronary artery disease)
Bupropion therapy (possible but less likely contributory)

provenance.title: Case report: Amnestic mild cognitive impairment in multiple domains associated with neurofascin 186 autoantibodies: Case series with follow-up and review

mend_provenance.added: Serum testing revealed positive neurofascin 186 IgG antibodies.
missing_discriminators: Neurofascin 186 autoantibody serology result

challenge_prompt:
A 33-year-old Black male with history of schizoaffective disorder – bipolar type, generalized anxiety disorder, stimulant use disorder (methamphetamine type), phencyclidine use disorder, and cannabis use disorder presents with decompensated psychosis. At baseline he has residual auditory hallucinations without suicidal/homicidal ideation. Two months prior to admission his outpatient regimen included valproic acid 1000 mg daily, buspirone 15 mg three times a day, trazodone 50 mg daily, gabapentin 900 mg daily, quetiapine 300 mg daily, and paliperidone palmitate 156 mg intramuscular every 4 weeks. His outpatient team reported that he frequently nasally ingested his buspirone and had difficulty with medication adherence.

He was found after abandoning his domicile 4 days prior, alcohol-intoxicated and making suicidal statements. On involuntary psychiatric hold, admission labs were unremarkable except urine toxicology positive for amphetamines and tetrahydrocannabinol. He endorsed bizarre, paranoid delusions (e.g., people around him were aliens and demons) and refused hospital food believing it poisoned.

In the inpatient psychiatric unit he developed increasing agitation. He refused oral haloperidol 5 mg, lorazepam 2 mg, and diphenhydramine 50 mg, requiring intramuscular administration after escalating violence. He refused oral risperidone 2 mg daily and diphenhydramine 25 mg nightly, but later accepted chlorpromazine 150 mg daily with some decrease in agitation.

On hospital day 7, the patient requested restarting his home medications for uncontrolled anxiety. He was started on valproic acid 1000 mg daily and buspirone 5 mg three times a day. Within 48 hours he developed increased bizarre and paranoid delusions, agitation, homicidal ideations, and disorganized thinking. He refused oral medications claiming alien abduction and had frequent verbal outbursts. Chlorpromazine was titrated to 300 mg daily but he often refused doses; he was switched to quetiapine XR 300 mg, later titrated to 800 mg without response, then to oral haloperidol 10 mg twice daily. Valproic acid was discontinued due to inability to cooperate with lab monitoring. After 8 days on buspirone, it was discovered he was hiding it in his mouth and he admitted reserving it for nasal ingestion. Buspirone was discontinued. Two days later he was calm, in behavioral control, and cooperative.

He was continued on haloperidol 30 mg daily and benztropine 4 mg daily while awaiting transfer to a locked psychiatric rehabilitation center. His average daily meal intake during that period was 76%. His medications were later switched from haloperidol to oral risperidone and finally to long-acting injectable paliperidone palmitate 234 mg.

On hospital day 62, the patient insisted on restarting buspirone for anxiety, and it was re-initiated at the previous dose. Two days later his paranoid delusions about food being poisoned returned, oral intake declined significantly, and he only accepted pre-packaged food. After 4 days he exhibited increased aggression; the Clinical Global Impressions – Severity scale scored 6 (severely ill). Behavioral notes indicated that he had been found nasally ingesting buspirone again.
gold_diagnosis: Buspirone-induced worsening of psychosis (exacerbated by intranasal administration)

answer_key.diagnosis: Buspirone-induced worsening of psychosis (exacerbated by intranasal administration)
answer_key.etiology: Administration of buspirone, likely compounded by intranasal use leading to increased bioavailability, in a patient with schizoaffective disorder
answer_key.next_management_step: Discontinue buspirone and avoid future use; consider alternative anxiolytic therapy and continue antipsychotic treatment; monitor for improvement.
required_findings: He frequently nasally ingested his buspirone (outpatient history)
Within 48 hours of initiating buspirone 5 mg three times a day, he exhibited increased bizarre/paranoid delusions, agitation, homicidal ideations, and disorganized thinking
After 8 days, buspirone was discontinued... Two days after discontinuation, patient was calm, in behavioral control, and cooperative
On hospital day 62, buspirone re-initiated. Two days later paranoid delusions about food poisoning returned, oral intake declined significantly
After 4 days, exhibited increased aggression; CGI-S scored 6 (severely ill)
Behavioral notes indicated he had been found nasally ingesting buspirone again
Urine toxicology positive for amphetamines and tetrahydrocannabinol
Baseline residual auditory hallucinations (underlying psychotic illness)
optional_findings: Valproic acid 1000 mg daily (part of outpatient regimen)
Chlorpromazine titrated to 300 mg daily but often refused
Haloperidol 30 mg daily and benztropine 4 mg daily after first trial
Average daily meal intake 76% during stable period before rechallenge
Only accepted pre-packaged food during second buspirone exposure
Admission labs unremarkable except urine toxicology positive for amphetamines and THC
Outpatient regimen two months before admission included buspirone 15 mg three times a day

provenance.title: Worsening psychosis associated with administrations of buspirone and concerns for intranasal administration: A case report
